# Igros Kodesh

## Volume 13

## 4273

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, engaged in communal needs,
R' Shlomo, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters from the 13th and 27th of Nissan, along with their enclosures. May it be the will of Hashem that matters develop and progress as much as possible, in accordance with the demands of the hour, and may this be with outstanding success. It is surprising that you do not mention in your latest letter the results of the visit of... may he live, and most importantly, regarding the *b'po'al mamash*.

It is understandable that the state of affairs regarding the postponement of the wedding of the *avreich*... may he live, is distressing. I believe that already last year I wrote to the aforementioned that I do not favor delaying the wedding for such a long time, and if it is possible for me or for the administration of the *Reshet* to do something about this, please inform me, as it is the proper thing to do.

Surely you will utilize the upcoming day of Lag B'Omer in an appropriate manner, especially since it falls on a day when the *talmidim* are exempt from secular studies.

With blessings for good tidings and outstanding success,

**M. Schneerson**

Due to circumstances, the sending of this letter was delayed, and in the meantime, letters from the 5th and 11th of Iyar were received, along with the enclosures.

- From Moreinu HaRav R' A. may he live, Chaikin, there is no letter at all.

- In exchange for the villages that are being emptied, there is surely a possibility to increase the quantity (and quality) of the *talmidim* in the existing Ohalei Yosef Yitzchak institutions.

#### ***4273***

**That matters develop:** In the institutions of the Reshet Ohalei Yosef Yitzchak in Morocco.

## 4 Adar

4 Adar

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Following your long silence, your letter from Sunday was received. Regarding what you write about the elder, it is clear that your words are refuted, for our holy Nasi states that the *Yud-Gimmel Tikkunei Dikna* are a conduit for *hashpaah* for a person and his household, and this *hashpaah* comes with *rachamim*, and with great *rachamim* (see the explanation of the terms by the Tzemach Tzedek on the verse "And He is merciful," also printed in the *miluim* to *Tehillim Yahel Ohr*)...

With blessings that you recognize your true situation—namely, that He who feeds and sustains all will provide enough for your livelihood and the livelihood of your entire household, may they live, in a kosher and Chassidic manner, and especially through the aforementioned *Yud-Gimmel Tikkunei Dikna*. Consequently, you yourself will be amazed, and you will also regret the fact that you waste nerves and health on baseless worries. It is understood that this is not a contradiction at all to what is written, "in all that you do" [specifically through effort], but rather it must be understood according to the precision of the language of our Sages, "When you eat the labor of your hands"—meaning only the labor of your hands, also in the spiritual sense, which is the power of action and *hishtadlus*, but not the labor of your head, meaning the worries and anxieties of the mind regarding this. There is no need to elaborate on a matter that is simple and explained in many places...

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

#### ***4 Adar***

**That your words are refuted:** See also above, Vol. 10, letter 3,263.

**The labor of your hands:** See also above, Vol. 12, letter 4,009, and the references in the notes there.

## Letter 4385

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...The reports indicating a lack of coordination between the administrators and the teachers are distressing, to the point that in some places it has reached a state where "everyone does what is right in their own eyes." It is imperative to put an absolute stop to this lack of order. It is already well-known how much our holy Nesi'im insisted on discipline in such matters, especially since it inevitably affects the discipline of the students toward their teachers and the administrator...

It is understood that since the administrator or the members of the general administration are also flesh and blood, they are also liable to make mistakes.

However, since a person has been appointed as administrator or to the general administration—and anyone who believes in the words of our Sages also believes in their statement that "even the head of a water-ditch is appointed from Heaven"—it follows that (while one may submit a complaint regarding an instruction from the administrator or the administration overseeing them, accompanied by all the arguments against that instruction and the like, and request that it be discussed as soon as possible if the matter is urgent), one must not destroy an institution of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, by warring against the administrator or by acting contrary to his instructions immediately on one's own initiative and the like. There is no need to elaborate on a matter that is understood and simple. May it be Hashem's will that I receive good news regarding the rectification of the above as soon as possible...

Awaiting good news regarding all that has been mentioned.

With blessings.

## 4276

4276

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...And I have come to the main point regarding what you wrote concerning the students... that you treat them with rebukes, etc., and even to the point of striking them... It is simple that such behavior is unacceptable regarding older students, and were I not afraid, I would say the same even for students who are not older. I believe I also told you this explicitly during your visit here, and it is appropriate that the aforementioned should change immediately...

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned.

#### ***4276***

**And even to the point of striking them:** In continuation of this — see letter 4310.

## 417

417

B”H, Rosh Chodesh Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings,

In response to your letter (which does not indicate the date it was written) in which you ask for guidance in the paths of Chassidus:

It is understood that such guidance depends on the character traits of the individual, their talents, and their personal circumstances. Therefore, it is proper and correct that you connect with the Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in your area. Through them, you can become acquainted with the elder Anash who are there, and you should act according to their guidance. Naturally, this is in addition to the general points (which are, furthermore, in addition to the three daily study portions established by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the well-known daily study of Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya). This includes studying the teachings of Chassidus in depth at least several times a week, and following its guidance and customs.

Just as the acceptance of the Torah in its entirety was through the precedence of "we will do" over "we will hear," so too is it regarding every individual part of our holy Torah. That is to say, one should not begin with questions and the like, but rather accept in advance these instructions that have already been proven effective for such matters. Then, in a short time, the understanding will also come.

May Hashem grant you success, and may you have good news to report regarding all of the above.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

#### 417

**Just as the acceptance of the Torah:** See also above, Vol. 11, Letter 3550, and the references in the footnotes there; Vol. 12, 3865; 457; below, 471.

## 4278

4278

B"H, 1 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
R' Shmuel, may he live, Shochet and Bodek

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the note from the members of his group from Rosh Chodesh Adar. It is surprising that since then, no news has been received from him. Surely this is a sign that all is in order and in peace, and likewise with all his family members, may they live. May it be His will that he does not settle for this, but rather fulfills the dictum of our Sages that "we elevate in matters of holiness," and may the promise of our Sages be fulfilled in them: "Whoever adds, they add to him." This is especially true when one engages in matters of Torah and mitzvos with others—and in particular with those who "have nothing of their own"—for then, in the measure of the Holy One, blessed be He, it is surely "measure for measure," and even many times over, added to the one who exerts himself and to his family. For before the Holy One, blessed be He, everyone is in the state of "having nothing of their own." These days are days of *segulah*, as discussed at one of the *farbrengens*: there is a special *segulah* for the days of the month of Ziv (Iyar), in that every single day has a mitzvah unique compared to the rest of the months of the year—the mitzvah of Sefiras HaOmer. Its totality is the process of coming from the level of an animal (the barley omer, which is animal fodder) to the level of a human (the Two Loaves of Shavuos, wheat, which is human food), as explained in the *maamarim* on Sefiras HaOmer in *Likutei Torah* and elsewhere.

With blessings to hear good news regarding all the above, from him and from each of the *anash*, may they live, who are in his camp.

#### ***4278***

**R' Shmuel:** Grossman, London. Another letter to him—see above, Vol. 2, p. 85.

**As discussed at one of the farbrengens:** This matter was explained at a *farbrengen* of the Rebbe, shlita, on Shabbos Rosh Chodesh Iyar 5709—see above, Vol. 3, letter 478. See also *Likutei Sichos*, Vol. 1, p. 263; Vol. 13, p. 171.

## 499

B"H, 1 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed and distinguished, who engages in the work of Heaven,
R' Menachem Yisrael, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

It is surprising that since your letter containing the photograph of the building was received, no further news has been heard from you. Surely you have long since received my letter addressed to the esteemed R' Chaim Shlomo, may he live, Brok, and you will reply to it with the necessary details.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the financial situation, the debts, and so on: While it is understood that this is difficult and distracting, unfortunately, this is the situation in the Tomchei Temimim Yeshivos even here in the United States. According to the stories of the elder Chassidim, the material situation was similar throughout many of the years of the Yeshiva’s existence. Nevertheless, not only did they hold their ground, but on the contrary, it grew and developed. Surely, through the awakening of the hearts of the leaders and directors of the Yeshiva, it will grow and develop even further, as will its branches in general. Happy is the portion of each and every one who strives and performs this holy work, as you have already seen the outstanding success in that you have managed to begin the construction, a significant portion of which has already been completed. It will surely be completed in its entirety in the near future. The reward for those who strive is explained in many places as being immeasurable; it is not merely in the measure of the Ribono shel Olam of "measure for measure," but rather many times over, in an abundance of true nachas—nachas that is Chassidishe—from sons and daughters.

Regarding what you wrote about the symbolic sum I sent: It does not belong to the funds of the Yeshivos or educational institutions; rather, it is from one of the funds of my father-in-law, the Rebbe. He writes in one of his letters regarding such matters that his fund is maintained according to the words of our Sages: "Whoever takes a prutah from Iyov is blessed." From this, it is understood that the quantity is not relevant at all, and it is possible that also...

It is my hope that you utilized the days of Pesach, in addition to the auspicious days preceding it, in an appropriate manner, and that you are preparing a suitable plan to utilize the day of Lag B'Omer to influence the students. May it be His will that you succeed in your holy work to make them Chassidim, yirei Shamayim, and scholars.

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned, and regards to all Anash, may they live, and in particular the esteemed Rav and Chassid, etc., etc., R' Chaim Shlomo, may he live.

## 480

480

B”H, 1 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter in due time, along with the letter from the esteemed, G-d-fearing man, who is occupied with communal needs and performs many deeds for the sake of mitzvos and Chassidus, [Name], may he live. Due to various reasons, the response has been delayed until now.

Regarding the problems you write about in your letter: from now on, you must internalize the truth—which is applicable to **every** Jew—that within Klal Yisrael, **each** of us is a shaliach of Hashem Yisborach, the Creator of the world, to carry out a holy task in this Olam Hazeh. This avodah, in general, has already been indicated in the Mishnah: "I was created to serve my Maker." And as to how this can be done, the ruling in Shulchan Aruch is: "In all your ways know Him" (Orach Chaim, sec. 231)—meaning that in every matter that is permitted and that one does, one can, through a good kavanah, "know Him."

In order to know what one must do and what one must not do, this has been indicated in our Torah, Toras Chaim. From this, it is also understood that the shlichus given to each of us is not uniform, since everyone must utilize their own strengths and capabilities, and according to those strengths and capabilities, the shlichus differs as well.

Since Hashem Yisborach has granted success to the Jewish community in [City] such that, for some time now, the Yeshiva [Name] has been established there, one of the primary shlichus-tasks for every Jew who finds themselves in [City] is to do everything possible—not only to strengthen the Yeshiva, but also to see that it is expanded more and more, and that it is able to accept and encompass all the youth and children, and through them, also influence their parents with Yiddishe warmth and Yiddishe light.

In accordance with what has been said above, you should consult with the administration of the Yeshiva as to what is best, and in what manner you can be of service to the Yeshiva.

And when you **throw yourself** into this work—(and it goes without saying that for the sake of the utility of the work itself, this must be done in a way that, G-d forbid, it should not harm your health, and also to maintain the parnassah matters that Hashem Yisborach will provide you, for Hashem Yisborach gives enough strength and time so that one can perform His shlichus with a healthy body and with parnassah as is fitting)—then, as a shaliach of the Creator of the world, just as a soldier in an army has his private worries and provisions taken over by the high command, so too, being in the service of Hashem Yisborach, those matters that cause you worry will be removed from you.

To wipe away the impression of the past, you should ask for mechilah in the presence of ten Jews from those whom you left regarding the shidduch—without specifying to them whether the fault was on your side or not entirely on your side. Also, immediately have your tefillin checked, as well as the tzitzis of your tallis gadol and tallis katan, and the mezuzos in your home, and the mezuzos in the rooms or office of the factory if you eat there sometimes. You should see to it that you sleep in a tallis katan, and every weekday, before davening in the morning and before Mincha, give a few small coins into a tzedakah pushkah, and do this until the coming Rosh Hashanah, may it be for good. Every day after davening, recite the shiur of Tehillim as divided by the days of the month.

It goes without saying that, in addition to the above, you must have a *kavius ittim* for learning, including the learning of Chassidus. And let your heart be confident in the Creator of the world and its Guide, that He watches over you with *hashgacha pratis* and guards you in all your affairs, and you have nothing to overthink. May Hashem Yisborach help that very soon I should receive good news from you regarding all that was mentioned above.

With blessings.

## 4,280

B"H, 1 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Ze'ev Tzvi, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Rosh Chodesh Adar and the one preceding it, which was delayed in transit. Surely you utilized the auspicious days of the Yomim Tovim and festivals to increase in your students a spirit of fear of Heaven, love of Heaven, and a path of walking in the ways of Chassidus and its conduct, and may Hashem grant you success.

Enclosed is a check for your personal expenses.

With blessings for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

Regarding your note:
a) Regarding what you wrote concerning the difference of opinion among our Sages as to whether it was Michael who saved Lot or Raphael (Bava Metzia 86b; Bereishis Rabbah 50:2) — it is possible to say that this corresponds to the two opinions of our Sages: One opinion holds that Lot was a tzaddik. If so, it is appropriate for Michael, who offers up the souls of the tzaddikim (see Chagigah 12b and elsewhere; Zohar Vol. I, 107b; the Midrash cited in Rashi 19:17). And according to the opinion that he was a rasha, it is appropriate for Raphael, [who represents] teshuvah, which brings healing (see also Zohar Vol. II, 147, bottom of page). Therefore, in Rashi’s commentary, [it is noted] that Lot was a rasha, and it was Raphael who saved him.

b) Regarding what you wrote that "and they brought him out" (Vayotziuahu) refers to an angel created from the mitzvos of Lot — it is not possible to say so, for the verse says "the two angels" (ha-malachim) with the definite article.

***

**R' Ze'ev Tzvi, may he live:** Weber, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, 3,852, and in the notes referenced there.

## 4181

[4 Iyar, 5716]

Rabbi Gerlik, Kfar Chabad

I acknowledge receipt of your telegram. The fast on Thursday is until after midday for healthy adults.

It is appropriate that the healthy adults in all Chabad educational institutions and yeshivos in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, do the same. May Hashem protect each and every one of them, as per the words of our Alter Rebbe, with the attribute of mercy—which is greater than the attribute of kindness—an inheritance without boundaries, passing from one end to the other, etc.

With blessings,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 4282

B”H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and diligent in communal affairs,
R’ Yechiel Michel Yehuda Leib, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

It was a pleasure to read your letter regarding the distribution of the Shmurah [Matzah]. Although it is somewhat surprising that a larger quantity was not distributed—and even though, in the inner dimension of things, quantity in such matters is not of primary concern, especially regarding the matters of Pesach, where "a good measure is greater, etc.," and since *chametz* is nullified even in the slightest amount (*mashehu*), it is understood that even a *mashehu* of good is effective—nevertheless, regarding the arrangements, the *shiur* is at least a *kezayis*, especially when the recipient does not yet understand the quality that is independent of quantity.

It is surprising that you write that because it is *bein hazmanim* in the yeshivos, the number of participants in the *shiurim* of *Da"Ch* (Chassidic thought) has decreased. On the contrary, when it is *bein hazmanim*, they are more available, and it is within their ability to increase these *shiurim*, whether in the place they have been until now or in the place to which they are traveling, by connecting them there with appropriate people. Just as a *mashehu* of these days is of greater significance—for it is so in the physical realm because it is so in the spiritual matters—it is understood how significant the aforementioned is: that they should utilize the days of Pesach with study that strengthens the power of *emunah*. And even though on the face of it the matter is not relevant now, nevertheless, there is a similar concept from time to time on days of vacation, etc., and the aforementioned points can serve as a lesson even then, and even before the upcoming holiday of Pesach.

I am certain that you are making the most vigorous preparations for the upcoming day of Lag B’Omer, to utilize it to the maximum extent, and I will urge *anash* to participate in the expenses involved in this to a certain degree.

With blessings for good tidings in all that is mentioned.

I am sure you are continuing and even increasing your activities within the framework of N’shei U’Bnos Chabad.

## 483

483

B.H., 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Mr. Shlomo, known as Dr. Idelberg
Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter of 27 Nissan, together with the volumes of the *Teshuvos* of Rabbeinu Gershom Meor HaGolah, as well as the booklet by Dr. Chaim Beinart. As you requested, I will confirm receipt of the booklet to him, and I thank you for your efforts in this matter.

As time permitted, I spent some moments looking through the pages of the *Teshuvos* of Rabbeinu Gershom. It is evident that you have invested great effort and labor into this work. May it be Hashem’s will that the teaching of our Sages be fulfilled in you: "Great is study, for it leads to action." Since both study and action are "longer than the earth and broader than the sea," there is always room for infinite growth and further improvement, no matter how good the present state may be. It is surely unnecessary to encourage you to maintain a *tov ayin* (a generous eye), so that your eye may be favorable toward others, influencing them as well to study our Torah—a study intended to teach, to observe, to perform, and to fulfill. This is especially true in our current era, where there appears to be an awakening to seek the truth following the disappointment in the ideologies of various parties and other nations. There is no time more opportune than the present, especially for one who has been gifted with the power to influence his surroundings.

Surely you have heard the story told by many, which has already been printed in various places in the name of a great figure, regarding the reason why specifically Rabbeinu Gershom was given the title *Meor HaGolah* (Light of the Exile). It is because the two enactments of Rabbeinu Gershom are what illuminate the Jewish people in exile. This is the "Light of the Exile": "He enacted that one may not take a second wife in addition to his wife, and that one may not divorce against her will." Thus, we need not fear that Hashem will replace us with another nation, for "there is no divorcing against her will." As the prophet says, "Where is the bill of your mother's divorce?" Therefore, we are secure in what He wrote, "And you shall be to Me a treasure," that He will not choose another, for "there is no taking a second wife in addition to His wife," so to speak, and this is [the meaning of], "And I will betroth you to Me forever."

In this lies the fundamental error of the *Chochmas Yisrael* (Jewish Studies) movement during that well-known period. Their approach was akin to the study of cuneiform, the Code of Hammurabi, and dead cultures. Consequently, it is understood that they could not arrive at correct conclusions, for it is simple that a living subject requires a different approach and a different preparation.

Although, on the face of it, logically speaking, these two matters—the study of *Teshuvos* and the approach to Torah—do not seem related, we see the opposite in practice. This is hinted at in the brief adage of our Sages: Why is the *halacha* established according to him? Not because of his unique depth, understanding, or superior logic compared to his colleague, but because "the *Havayah* (Hashem) is with him" (*Sanhedrin* 93b). Indeed, even regarding the holy ones of Israel themselves, it is said that although both these and those are the words of the living G-d, those who are "gentle and humble" are the ones who merit having the *halacha* established according to them, even if the other side is sharper (*Eruvin* 13b). Based on this, we can understand the instruction of our Sages, which seems puzzling at first glance, that Torah study must take place after *tefillah* (*Berachos* 5b), even though Torah is the Torah of life and the Torah of truth. This is explained in *Likkutei Torah* by our Rebbe, the author of the *Tanya* and *Shulchan Aruch*, in the section on *Devarim* (96c).

With blessings for success in all the above and for good tidings in this.

## 4284

4284

B”H, 4 Iyar, 5716

Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, engaged in communal affairs,
R’ Chaim Zusia, may he live and be well,

Peace and blessing!

Following your long silence, I have received your letter which you began writing on 3 Adar. You write about several matters, yet it is surprising that you omitted others. Perhaps the reason is that others were already dealing with them. My request to anash is already well-known: in addition to what you write from your side regarding what you know, it is appropriate to stir the other activists so that each one reports in his own area...

Regarding what you wrote about the necessity of increasing the number of students in Tomchei Temimim and in Chabad educational institutions in general: this is far more necessary than people realize. I have already written many times that we must "grab them now" (*araynchappen*), because in a short time, they will be searching for students with a flashlight (*mit licht*), and people will be fighting over them (*un men vet reissen zich iber zey*). Consequently, we are currently losing the opportunity to add hundreds of students to Chabad educational institutions, simply due to negligence and a lack of attention to even the most basic, highlighted, and emphasized matters.

Regarding your question about the *Igud Talmidei HaYeshivos* (ET”H), it has already been answered, and may it be G-d’s will that it comes to fruition. My hope—even though you do not mention it—is that you held a gathering regarding the affairs of the *Merkaz L’Inyanei Chinuch* (ML”CH), and that the discussions were recorded, or at least will be recorded now. Consequently, you will be able to publicize them in an appropriate manner. After I see the material and receive proposals regarding this, perhaps it would be worthwhile to print it in the form of a booklet, or to publish it in the *Bitaon* or in the *Kovetz* here...

It is alarming and distressing that the matter of the construction of the *mikveh* is dragging on for so long, and I do not know what else can be done about it.

Regarding what you wrote about your own *avodah*: specifically, you should be occupied head and heart with the matter of *hafotzah* (dissemination), but at the same time, you must stir everyone involved to the necessary and appropriate activity in all other matters. "Stirring as it should be" means that shortly after the stirring, people come, ask, and demand to know what is being done about it—and the next day again, and the day after that again—and that is how, in the end, one achieves results.

The *pidyon nefesh* enclosed in your letter will be read at an auspicious time at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and may it be G-d’s will that you bring good tidings.

With blessing,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

#### ***4284***

**R’ Chaim Zusia:** Vilimovsky, Tel Aviv. Further letters to him: above, Vol. 12, 3880, and the references in the notes there. Below, 4500.

**Question of ET”H:** *Igud Talmidei HaYeshivos* (Association of Yeshiva Students).

**Construction of the mikveh:** In Kfar Chabad. See also above, Vol. 11, 3998; Vol. 12, letter 4271; below, 4318.

4508*. 4651. 4734.

**Regarding the matter of hafotzah:** Of Kehot publications. See above, Vol. 12, letter 3880.

## 485

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs, etc.,
R' Menachem Zev Halevi, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters from the 1st and the eve of Rosh Chodesh Iyar, along with the enclosures. Since you have once again established contact with R' Yaakov Zev Margalit, it would be worthwhile to urge him to ensure that a letter be published as soon as possible in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) that provides a well-founded, absolute refutation of the alarming arguments of the Rabbi who wishes to permit the exhumation of graves, as if this were based on the Zohar and the writings of the Arizal, etc. It is understood that a letter of refutation must include citations and references, etc.

His letter attempting to permit this was printed in *Kol Torah*, which is published in Jerusalem (may it be rebuilt and established), and it is certainly known there to anyone who wishes to know. It is truly surprising that, as of yet, a proper response to this has not been printed.

Thank you for the good tidings regarding those for whom you wrote a *pidyon nefesh*. May Hashem grant them success in always bringing good news, all the days, and may this also apply to you, your family, and those you influence, may they live.

With blessings to hear good news regarding all that was mentioned.

***

**R' Menachem Zev, may he live:** Gringlas, Montreal. Additional letters to him: see above, Vol. 12, 3816, and the references in the notes there. Below, 4423.

**Since you have once again established contact:** See above, Vol. 11, letters 3551, 3741.

**Exhumation of graves:** In continuation of this: see below, letter 4424.

## 485

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 29 Nissan: It is already known and explained in many places that the expulsion of evil in the spiritual sense follows the principle of what is written, "Little by little I will drive them out from before you." This is especially true in our generation, where the tzaddikim are few, and we would be fortunate if we were even at the level of beinonim. Regarding them, our Alter Rebbe elaborated at length in Tanya, beginning with Chapter 27. On the contrary, one should rejoice, and the sadness regarding this stems from not recognizing one's place, etc., for this is the nature of the beinonim and their avodah.

When you study that chapter with the necessary hisbonenus, applying it to yourself, you will see tangibly that the mood you describe is one of the stratagems of the Yetzer Hara, intended to prevent you, chas v'shalom, from the avodah of Hashem appropriate to your level. Increase your hasmadah and shekidah in the study of our holy Torah and in the performance of its mitzvos with hiddur. Move away from the path of debate and intellectualizing that you write about in this letter. May Hashem grant you the merit to influence others as well, drawing them toward Chassidus in all its aspects, for through this, the measure of Hashem—which is "measure for measure," only many times over—will increase in what you require.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4,287

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

It was a pleasure to receive your letter of 25 Nissan with the enclosed excerpt. Regarding what you write—that you think I interpreted your words as meaning you fast until midnight—that is not the case; I understood that you fast until midday. Regardless, this is not the way of Chassidus, as I wrote to you in my previous letter. I also wrote about what can be substituted for these fasts, and on the contrary, etc., and my hope is that you will conduct yourself accordingly and be successful. It is understood that what was said above regarding fasts applies only to fasts one wishes to add upon one's own initiative, not to the fasts mandated in the Shulchan Aruch; this is obvious.

Regarding what you write about Rabbi Goldschmid, may he live: while it is necessary and correct to study the teachings of Chassidus with someone in the capacity of a Rav, it is even more necessary to find a few people—or at the very least one person—with whom you can arrange to study as a chaver, or at the very least a talmid-chaver. This is in accordance with the precision of our Sages, of blessed memory, in the Mishnah: "Make for yourself a Rav and acquire for yourself a chaver." This implies that acquiring a chaver must be like a "purchase," which involves spending money; similarly, it is necessary to expend effort in order to acquire a chaver. When the opportunity arises, I will also write to Rabbi Goldschmid, may he live, but at the same time, it is highly appropriate that you find a chaver in the manner described above, and then your success in your studies will increase.

It is surprising that you do not write about what your wife, may she live, is occupied with, and that she is continuing in the field of kosher chinuch. May it be the will of Hashem that she succeeds in instilling true yiras shamayim in her students.

With blessings for success in all of the above, and for good tidings regarding yourself, your wife, and your entire family, may they live.

#### ***4,287***

**That you fast until midnight:** See Vol. 12, Letter 4,196.

## 4288

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Although it is a presumption that a shaliach fulfills his mission, especially when it concerns the mechanisms of the kingdom such as the postal service, I nevertheless explicitly confirm receipt of your letter and its enclosure. I read it on Erev Rosh Chodesh Iyar at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be the will of Hashem that very soon the wishes of your heart and the heart of your wife—may she live and be well—be fulfilled for the good regarding the shidduch for your daughter—may she live and be well. May it be a Chassidishe shidduch, in the path of your forefathers and their forefathers, in a way of life consistent with their desires, and may you see from them much nachas, in health and with peace of mind.

It is already known what is explained in many places that Iyar* is an acronym for *HaMerkavah* (which corresponds to the exposition of our Sages regarding *Ziv* as being related to the term "the radiance of the world"), as is explained in many places regarding the avodah of the path of *Merkavah*.

With blessings.

***

*) The month in which we currently find ourselves. See Zohar Vol. III, 117b: "May He bless... Aaron, for they are the ones who bless... the house of Aaron, and so too their wives... the second month, which is the radiance (*ziva*) of the blessings of the world, from which emerges... for the radiance of everything emerges from it..."

**Acronym for HaMerkavah:** Avraham, Yitzchak, Yaakov, Rachel. See also above, Vol. III, Letter 479.

**Corresponds to the exposition of our Sages:** See also above, Vol. XI, Letters 3626, 3636, 3655.

## 489

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from before the holiday of Pesach, in which you write that you take upon yourself to study with consistency and diligence and to observe the *sedarim* of the *yeshiva* here (although, on the face of it, this contradicts the beginning of your letter, where you admit that you do not observe the *sedarim* of the *yeshiva*, especially in recent times, etc. And I am concerned that even when you travel from there, the *yetzer hara* will travel with you and will surely continue its incitements even after that. And if in the land upon which the eyes of Hashem your G-d are from the beginning of the year until the end of the year, you do not struggle against it as you ought, then what will be in *chutz la'aretz*? It is already known the ruling of the Rambam (*Hilchos Melachim*, ch. 5) that it is forbidden to leave for *chutz la'aretz*, and when they permit it for the sake of *talmud Torah*, it must be *talmud* in the manner that the Torah defines as *talmud Torah*, in all respects), if you will truly, sincerely, and with the firmest resolve fulfill what you have stated, you may begin the efforts toward this, and the *Ribono shel Olam* will guide you in the best way for you, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe *shlita*,

Secretary

## 490

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Following your long silence, your letter from Motzaei Shabbos was received. May it be the will of Hashem that in the near future you will bring good news regarding the drawing together of hearts and the unity between them, which is steadily growing. As it is written, "as water reflects the face to the face," so too is the heart of man to man. When one relinquishes his own personal traits and personal matters, the other person begins to feel the same way, and they draw closer to one another all the more. It is then that the promise of our holy Torah is fulfilled—that peace is the vessel that holds the blessing of the Ribono shel Olam, a blessing that is boundless and infinite, for its source is the true Infinite. Consequently, an additional blessing is drawn down into one’s activities and those who are influenced by them. May it be the will of Hashem that you bring good news regarding this very soon.

With blessings for success and for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

## 491

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote about the division of hearts: I have written many times that one should study the maamarim of Rabbeinu HaZaken and the Nasi’im who followed him. In doing so, one will naturally understand the source of such division, and will see how critical these matters are. If this is true even in times of absolute peace, then certainly it is so when there are two sides involved. In this world, it is almost impossible for one side to be one hundred percent right and the other one hundred percent at fault; rather, each side is at least partially at fault. This is especially true according to what is explained in Chassidus regarding the verse, "As water reflects the face to the face..." It is clear that until now, much good has been squandered due to a lack of closeness of hearts, all for the sake of imagined honor. Let them not deceive themselves that this is out of fear of Heaven or for the sake of the "Chabad point," for Rabbeinu HaZaken and every one of the Nasi’im in their respective generations wrote the exact opposite. This is explained in the maamar *Hechaletzu* by Rabbeinu HaZaken, and continues through to the maamar of our Nasi, the Rebbe Rayatz, printed in the supplement to the *Kuntres Hechaletzu*; see it there.

The tragedy is that despite the fact that people are stirred to action, there are some who, even after being stirred, switch their priorities. Instead of doing teshuvah for their own matters and judging their fellow favorably—to the point of fulfilling "be humble before every person," in the words of Rabbeinu HaZaken, "be truly humble" (as explained elsewhere, where Rabbeinu cites the version "before *the* person," with the added *hei*)—they do the opposite. They passionately demand that others do teshuvah, while with no less passion, they find justifications for themselves, "adorning" their behavior with the verse, "Love covers all transgressions," and demanding that those around them be humble before them. It is already well known from the Mishnah that in matters between a person and their fellow, it does not help that one eats specifically round *shmurah* matzah, or that one stays up all night on Shavuos, etc., until one appeases their fellow—only then do these things become effective.

We conclude with a positive note: according to the verse, which Rabbeinu HaZaken cites as a practical halachic ruling in *Hilchos Talmud Torah*, "that no one shall be cast away from Him," it is a certainty. May it be His will that this happens soon, with kindness and mercy, and that very soon the wicked will abandon their way and the man of iniquity his thoughts. As is brought in the *maamarei chassunah* of the Tzemach Tzedek regarding this: just as a "wicked person" in the literal sense must abandon his way, so too, literally, must the "man of iniquity" abandon his thoughts—*on* (iniquity) being from the root *on* (strength/power) [see there regarding the concept, that when they spread throughout the entire beis medrash, they push against him at every step]. Consequently, everyone will unite as one man. Through this—together with our holy Nasi’im, who stand as a bridge connecting us to Hashem, our G-d—this peace and unity will protect each person and their household within the entirety of Klal Yisrael like a fortified wall, as it is written: "Bless us, our Father, all of us as one, in the light of Your countenance, and in the light of the countenance of the King of Life."

## 4,292

4,292

B"H, 4 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, engaged in communal needs,
R' Yochanan, shochet u'vodek,
Gabbai of the Beis Medrash of Lubavitch in Lubavitch.

Peace and blessing!

It was a pleasure to receive your note along with the enclosure. Enclosed herewith is a receipt from the institutions named after my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in Eretz Yisrael—the land which Hashem your G-d seeks out from the beginning of the year to the end of the year. They—and those like them—are the ones who protect it. Everyone who supports them and strives to increase their activities, both in the number of students and in the hours of study, is a cause and a conduit for Hashem to build the house and Hashem to guard the city, etc. As the opening explanation on this matter by Rabbi Abba bar Kahana and the elucidation by R' Assi and R' Ammi state: the scribes and the teachers who study and review the Torah day and night, etc. (Midrash Eichah Rabbah, at the beginning). May it be the will that very soon, with kindness and mercy, a spirit from on high will be poured out upon each and every one within the entirety of Klal Yisrael. Then one will see with his eyes, hear with his ears, and understand with his heart that we invoke the name of Hashem our G-d, and He will send His help from the Sanctuary. May His promise be fulfilled: "He will answer us on the day we call," with the true and complete Geulah.

With blessing.

***

**R' Yochanan:** Gordon, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him—see above, Vol. 12, 3,444, and the references in the notes there.

**Hashem to guard the city, etc.:** See above, Letter 4,281, and the references in the notes there.

## 493

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from the middle of the winter. Since I have not received any further news from you regarding this, my hope is strong that you are continuing your studies in the yeshiva, and that from time to time the matter becomes more and more beloved to you. This, in turn, naturally strengthens your resolve to continue these studies for at least the amount of time I advised in my previous letter.

Regarding what you write—that you have no intention of becoming a rabbi or the like, and that you are not qualified for it—my reason for suggesting that you study in the yeshiva for the time I wrote was not specifically so that you would become a rabbi. Rather, it was so that you would be a Jew befitting the substance of that title. In our current era—a period of confusion and bewilderment of the mind, where people call light darkness and darkness light, and winds of non-mitzvah-observance blow against true Yiddishkeit—every single person must fortify themselves with everything necessary. The place for this is within the four amos of Torah and the four amos of tefillah. My hope is that by remaining within these four amos for the aforementioned duration, you will, with the help of Hashem, be able to stand firm against the surging waves outside and conduct yourself in a true, traditional path of life.

Surely you are strengthening yourself in the observance of the three known daily shiurim—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—and in addition to this, you have a set shiur in the study of Chassidus. May Hashem grant you success and bring you good tidings.

With blessings.

#### ***493***

**In my previous letter:** See Vol. 12, 3952.

## 494

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, engaged in communal needs,
R' Shmuel Elazar, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 27 Nissan and those preceding it, as well as the newspaper clippings. Many thanks for your thoughtfulness in sending these clippings, and for the spirit and manner in which you wrote your article in *HaModia*—with warmth, inspiration, and so on. Surely, you will continue to utilize this opportunity afforded by the *HaModia* platform to disseminate the wellsprings outward—the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs. Every such action, in addition to bringing the Geulah closer—as explained in the *Iggeres HaKodesh* of the Baal Shem Tov, "When your wellsprings spread outward, the Master will come"—also connects the inner, hidden dimension of the Jewish soul with the hidden dimension of the Holy One, blessed be He, as the holy *Zohar* famously states.

As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at an *eis ratzon* at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding each and every one of them.

It is my hope that you are making the necessary preparations to utilize the upcoming Lag B'Omer within the framework of *Tzeirei Agudas Chabad*, in order to take full advantage of the opportunity to disseminate the teachings of the Rashbi, the master of the day, which have been revealed in recent generations through the teachings of Chassidus and through the understanding and comprehension of the teachings of Chassidus Chabad. May Hashem grant you the merit that it be with outstanding success.

With blessings,

#### 494

**R' Shmuel Elazar:** Shulzon, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, 3946, and in the notes referenced there.

## 494

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from some time ago—which was delayed in transit—in which you write that it is the custom in your family to strive to fulfill the words of our Sages to marry at eighteen, and since you have already passed this age, you are being presented with suitable proposals and are in doubt as to whether to pursue this, as you wish to continue learning in tranquility for some time longer.

In my opinion, it is correct that you should take an interest in the aforementioned. Certainly, under the conditions in Jerusalem, the Holy City, may it be rebuilt and established, you will be able to continue learning for a significant amount of time even after the wedding. It is already well-known how highly our Sages praised the virtue of studying Torah in purity. As for your concern that marriage will interfere with your consistency in learning, this depends entirely on your own will. If you truly desire it, you will succeed in your study of the revealed Torah and the teachings of Chassidus both before the wedding and after the wedding. May the Ribono shel Olam provide you with a shidduch that is suitable for you in both a physical and spiritual sense, to the point that He will help you transform any matters that stand in your way into something good.

It is surely unnecessary to awaken you to the absolute necessity of establishing a daily study session in the inner dimension of Torah, which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus. This is in addition to the three known daily shiurim in Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya. Especially since you are in the Holy City of Jerusalem, may it be rebuilt and established, you should strive for the perfection of yirah. It is impossible to attain this except through hisbonenus in the greatness of the Blessed One, which comes through studying the teachings of Chassidus, as is evident in a tangible way. May the Ribono shel Olam grant you success in bringing good tidings regarding all of the above, as well as in your influence upon your friends, to bring them closer to the Torah and to the light of the Torah—which is the teachings of Chassidus, its guidance, and its customs.

With blessings.

## 4,296

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and distinguished scholar, who engages in communal needs, R' Dovid, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Sunday, and I was pleased to read in it about the *shiurim* in the *beis knesses*.

Allow me to remark on this: in all times and in all places, there has always been a necessity for knowledge, and consequently, for a *shiur* studying the *halachos* required for daily life. As our Alter Rebbe, author of the *Shulchan Aruch* and *Tanya*, elaborated in *Hilchos Talmud Torah*, this is especially true in our time and in this country, where the *beis knesses* must fulfill the role of a *beis medrash*—not only for the study of Torah *lishmah*, but also regarding knowing "the practical deeds that they shall do and that they shall not do."

In accordance with the above, it is surprising that in your *beis knesses* there is no such *shiur*. It is appropriate, necessary, and even mandatory that, at the very least, from here on out you arrange a suitable *shiur*. Since the time available for study is limited, in my opinion—and as I have written to several of *anash*—one should first study selected *simanim* from the *Kitzur Shulchan Aruch*. I do not mean the *simanim* in order, but rather to go over, once or twice, the *simanim* that discuss matters common in daily life. Specifically in the *Kitzur Shulchan Aruch*, where things are presented concisely (and if explanation is sometimes needed, it is up to the one giving the *shiur* to say it orally), in order to utilize the time at their disposal for *halacha lemaaseh* in practice.

As it is written, "Fear G-d, etc., for this is the whole of man"—meaning that the study must be one that leads to the fear of G-d, and the study of Torah must lead to the knowledge of the *mitzvos*, and "great is study, for it leads to action." May it be His will that you bring good news regarding this as well, in addition to the other matters you write about.

With blessings for increased strength in your holy work in the *beis knesses* to strengthen Judaism in general, and in spreading the *maayanos* (until they reach) *chutzah* in particular, with proper health.

#### ***4,296***

**R' Dovid:** Winchester, Chicago. Another letter to him—below, 4,364.

## 4297

B"H, 5 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the 2nd of Iyar, along with the enclosure. I am certain that the founder and director of the village, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, is arousing great mercies that guard and protect the village and each and every one of its residents—and with even greater intensity and strength from here on out. As our Alter Rebbe expresses it, which is also quoted in my telegram: "In the attribute of mercy, which is greater than the attribute of kindness, which initially was an inheritance without boundaries, extending from one end to the other, etc." (see *Derech Mitzvosecha*, p. 114b).

However, the tragedy is, as I have written many times, that the lack of closeness of hearts—and even more so, the division of hearts—hinders, Heaven forbid, the drawing down of the aforementioned blessing and success to "below ten handbreadths."

You have surely received my letter responding to your previous letters, which also relates to the above. It is understood that I am not coming to justify the other side, as I do not know the details of the dispute, which is likely rooted in imagined honor, or at the very least in trivial matters—certainly matters not significant enough to forfeit, in the words of our Alter Rebbe in the holy *Tanya* (Chapter 32), the indwelling of the Holy One, blessed be He. As it is written, "Bless us, our Father, all of us as one, with the light of Your countenance"—see there. This is explained based on the *maamarim* of *Hechalot* and the startling letters regarding this matter in the *Sefer HaMitzvos* of the Tzemach Tzedek, in the *mitzvah* of *Ahavas Yisrael*. It is clear that in such disputes, to a certain extent, both sides are at fault, even if they differ in degree, and a word to the wise is sufficient... The matter depends solely on his will, for the powers have been granted to him, and the desire is also present. For the desire (the interiority of the will) has already been established: that every person of Israel truly and completely desires, with all their heart, etc., to cleave to Hashem, etc. (*Tanya*, Chapter 41, see there). It is the *kelipah* of Midian that opposes this, as explained in the aforementioned *maamarim*. May the blessing and success be drawn down to "below ten handbreadths," and may you bring me good tidings in the near future of good that is revealed and apparent.

With blessings.

#### ***4297***

**With the enclosed *pidyon nefesh*:** See above, letter 4281, and the references in the notes there.

**In my telegram:** Mentioned above, ibid.

## 4290

4290

B"H, 6 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs, a man of many accomplishments, R. A. S. [may he live and be well],

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter of 23 Nissan, in which you write about the farbrengens of the auspicious days, noting that they were characterized by a large attendance in quantity and an elevation of spirit in quality. May Hashem help that, as explained at length in Likutei Torah, the joy of these festivals should be drawn down into all the days of the year. This is especially true regarding the festival of Matzos—the exodus from Egypt—which is recalled constantly, and specifically on Shabbos Kodesh, and in even greater detail on every Yom Tov, as this represents the departure and liberation from slavery to freedom, and the preparation for Matan Torah. This preparation must be conducted in a manner appropriate to the goal one is striving for; may everyone do what is necessary to free themselves from worries and distractions. This will make possible the receiving of the Torah on the upcoming festival of Shavuos with joy, and even more so, with inwardness. May the merit of your taking upon yourself a portion of the expenses for the dissemination of the wellsprings—the guidance of the teachings of Chassidus and its Torah (until it reaches even) the outermost reaches—bring even more success in this beloved, holy avodah. May we all merit to celebrate the true liberation from the final exile through Mashiach Tzidkeinu.

With blessings and regards to all those who daven and the members of the shul [may they live and be well].

#### ***4290***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 17, p. 457, and completed based on the secretariat's copy.

**R. A. S.:** R. Avraham Shlovsky, Petach Tikvah. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 11, 3585, and in the references noted there. Below, 4309.

## 499

499

B.H., 6 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, involved in communal needs,
R’ Dovber, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos: I was pleased to read about the naming of your son, shlita, and that you aligned with my opinion, even though, as mentioned in my previous letter, it is not customary to give instructions in this matter. I reiterate my blessing that you and your wife, may they live, raise him to Torah, chuppah, and good deeds, with peace of mind.

Regarding what you wrote about the tonsils, etc.: Generally speaking, even for those who catch colds occasionally due to their tonsils, this is relevant during the autumn and winter months. This is not the case in the summer; therefore, on the face of it, there is no need to discuss this question now. Consult with your doctor again, and if his opinion remains the same, there will be time to raise the question and write about it in a few months. In the meantime, may each one of you, and all of you together, have a healthy summer.

I was pleased with what you wrote regarding the distribution of matzah shemurah. Regarding your concluding remark that "all beginnings are difficult," it is a bit difficult to understand what the difficulties are, since this is not something that requires great toil. Furthermore, the second point is the very beginning of the Tur and Shulchan Aruch (in the Rema’s glosses): "Do not be ashamed before those who mock," especially since in our times, the opponents are decreasing both in number and in influence.

Certainly, you should not be satisfied with the shiurim you are currently giving, but should add to them, as you concluded in your letter. This includes shiurim for the youth and, in general, speaking about Chassidus, which sometimes accomplishes a great deal. In the same vein, work on a suitable plan for how to utilize the day of Lag B’Omer, especially since this year it falls on a day when the students are free from secular studies, etc. May Hashem grant you success in bringing good tidings in all the above.

And also through the activities of your wife, may she live.

Regarding your question about the location for the Chassidus shiur: The sicha of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, is well known—that it is better in a place where many people are present or passing through, similar to a physical business, etc. Certainly, outsiders will not interfere in the management of the affairs. And if they do try, you always have the ability to move the shiur to your home. Surely, the mere threat of doing so will be enough to stop the interference. And to the wise, a word is sufficient.

With blessing.

## D'sh

B"H, 7 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 6th of Iyar, in which you write regarding the thirteen-and-a-half-year-old boy who is experiencing difficulties in his conduct regarding yiras shamayim, etc.

In such a case, the best course of action is to fulfill the instruction of the Mishnah: "Exile yourself to a place of Torah." This way, there is no daily friction between the parties; there is only the one-time effort required to gain their consent for him to travel to a place of Torah. As is evident in practice, the benefit of this is clear in many respects—especially in the United States, where it is already customary and appealing to the youth to leave their parents' home for a period of time. Surely, one can find individuals who have influence over the parents so that, in the end, they will agree to the aforementioned. May Hashem grant you success to see good fruit from your labor and to make him a yirei shamayim, a Chassid, and a lamdan.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above.

## D'sha

B"H, 7 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Mr. Schneur Zalman, may he live and be well,

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 13 Nissan. I would like to take this opportunity to reiterate my warm thanks for the handwritten notebooks of *maamarim* of *Chassidus* that reached me through your son, may he live and be well. May the merit of the many who will benefit from the light of Torah concealed within these notebooks—which include *maamarim* that have not yet been published—stand for your son and for the entire family in all that each of them requires.

It is understood that this includes—as we discussed with your son—that the one who brings the notebooks, your son, as well as his parents, his uncles, and the entire family, will be among those who study and engage in this Torah. For as it is said regarding our Torah in general, it applies here as well: "Great is study, for it leads to action." This is especially true given that the merit of their ancestors and the rock from which they were hewn will assist in further success in this endeavor.

With regards to the entire family, and in closing as I began, with thanks for the notebooks you sent in the past and the notebook you are sending now.

#### ***D'sha***

**Schneur Zalman:** Shmerling, Tel Aviv.

## D'shav

B"H, 7 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter:

Be meticulous in reciting the Shema before you go to sleep. Furthermore, while sleeping, ensure you are wearing a tallis katan, and check that its tzitzis are kosher and that there are eight threads on each corner. Before reciting the Shema—except on the nights of Shabbos and Yom Tov—make a note for yourself if you missed any of your studies during the day, so that you do not forget to make up the deficiency the following day. When you do all of this, you will lie down without fear, and your sleep will be pleasant.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 433

[7 Iyar 5716]

To each and every one of Anash, the residents of Kfar Chabad, the institutions located there, and especially the vocational and agricultural schools, the students, the teachers, the administrators, the activists, and the parents:

It is my firm hope that with the help of Hashem, who watches with an open eye and oversees with Divine providence, you will overcome every obstacle, strengthen yourselves in your private and public buildings, and expand all the institutions in both quantity and quality. May it be that, with tranquility and peace of mind, the study of our Torah—our fortress, the Torah of life and the words of the living G-d—and the fulfillment of its mitzvos with joy and goodness of heart, will be magnified and glorified, and "you shall live by them." May the fountains of Chassidus, the teachings and practices of our holy Nesi’im, spread forth from Kfar Chabad until they reach the outside as well, to draw all our brothers, the children of Israel, closer to our Father in heaven with kindness and mercy, and to bring the true and complete Redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu speedily.

I participate in your prayers and your fasts, and I am confident in Hashem that very soon I will also participate in your joy—a joy that is true, internal, and complete, in both physicality and spirituality alike.

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### 433

Published in *Bitaon Chabad*, issue 11, at the beginning. Copied in the secretariat letter from Lag B’Omer and below, 439.

For its content, see letter 481 above, and the references in the notes there.

## 484

[7 Iyar 5716]

Yeshiva, Lod.

In response to your inquiry: you should begin the construction of the new Yeshiva building and the other buildings in the village with vigor.

The fundraising campaign should be in the name of the institutions where the murdered victims, *Hashem yikom damam*, studied, for their development, etc. In coordination with the appropriate offices, arrangements must be made for the students to sleep in the orchard under guard.

With blessing,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***484***

**In the village:** See above, Vol. 12, p. 37, and the references in the notes there. Below, p. 319, p. 385.

**The murdered victims:** See above, Letter 481, and the references in the notes there.

## 4305

B"H, 7 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, engaged in communal needs, R' Pinchas, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the beginning of the month of Iyar and those that followed, as well as the telegrams. Surely, you have long since been informed of the telegram I sent to the village and to the Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva, from which my perspective is understood regarding the strange proposal to relocate from Lod, etc. On the other hand, it is imperative that they immediately establish contact with the appropriate offices and individuals regarding additional security. They asked me here why there is no barbed-wire fence around the village, or why there is no searchlight located at the highest point in the village, or guard dogs, and the like. It would also be worthwhile to raise the issue regarding the orchards, since several places have pointed out that they serve as a hiding place, which complicates the search.

Regarding what you wrote about a letter in the international press, etc., I am surprised by this proposal as well, because: 1) Based on past experience, there is no **practical** benefit to this regarding the actual situation. And all the more so, and *kal v'chomer*, that the individuals you proposed approaching paid no heed to what transpired during the terrible years of 5701, etc. 2) There is room to say that pressure of this kind would, on the contrary, increase the sense of victory for the opposing side. 3) And this is the main point: it seems they completely forget that there are thousands upon thousands of students in Chabad schools, who have many teachers and *mashgichim*, such as the Rabbis Tuvia, Lipsker, Pinson, etc., may they live. A public appeal in the aforementioned press would surely reach there immediately, and the concern for matters, *chas v'shalom*, that this could cause is understood. Without such publicity, several actions have been taken here by appealing to the leaders here with a strong and vigorous response; but not in the public domain, and on the other hand, with pressure that is stronger than the pressure of newspapers, and *d'l* (a word to the wise is sufficient)...

#### ***4305***

**R' Pinchas:** Altein, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him — see above, Vol. 12, 4108, and the references in the notes there.

**His letter from the beginning of the month of Iyar:** See above, letter 4281, and the references in the notes there.

**From the telegram I sent:** Above, 4303-4.

## 466

[8 Iyar, 5716]

Chief Rabbi Herzog
Jerusalem

Thank you for your telegram of encouragement and for your efforts in supporting the residents of Kfar Chabad. I am certain that you will continue to assist in the development of the village and its institutions, and I thank you in advance.

Rumors have spread that efforts are being made to have your honor send a letter of endorsement and blessing to institutions in America—institutions whose very names suggest they claim to be standard-bearers and representatives of the religion of Israel, yet which are comprised of representatives and emissaries of Conservative and Reform movements, such as the New York Board of Rabbis and the Synagogue Council of America.

Any such involvement, even if the wording could be interpreted in two ways, will, in the current climate here, cause confusion in the minds of thousands of Jews and is liable to draw them under the influence of the Conservative and Reform movements.

From my acquaintance with various circles, and particularly with the youth, I have observed that the state of the discussion here regarding participation in the aforementioned bodies necessitates a clear stance and a prohibition against such participation, as this is a matter that impacts future generations and other lands.

With respect and blessing,

**Menachem Schneerson**

**Chief Rabbi Herzog:** See additional letters to him in Vol. 10, letter 3119, and the references in the notes there. See also below, 4414, 4519*, 4555, 4580, 4583.
