**A clear prohibition:** See above, Vol. 12, letters 4162, 4204. See below, 4610, 4414, 4458.

## 4,207

B”H, 8 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi and Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs, etc.,
R’ Eliezer, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh.

It is baffling and distressing how all the letters received from the elders of Anash in Eretz Yisrael—may it be rebuilt and established—contain the same theme: a retreat from the battlefield. Your letter, too, is of this nature. Imagine for yourself what the state of Torah matters in general, and Chassidus in particular, would be today if the generations before us, and the previous generation, had adopted this approach.

I have already made my opinion clear in the telegram: on the contrary, all activities in Kfar Chabad must be expanded. If the Guardian of Israel neither slumbers nor sleeps over every single Jew in every place, how much more so regarding Kfar Chabad, which was founded by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, Nasi of Israel, who continues to direct it even now. The same applies to the Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in Lod.

It is understood that destroying the Yeshiva in Lod in order to rebuild it elsewhere is, in fact, destruction. This is not the way to protect and safeguard the students. It is astonishing: all Bnei Yisrael are believers, the children of believers, and in Anash, the Chassidim, and the Temimim, the concept of *emunas tzaddikim* is even more pronounced—especially according to what is explained in the Mechilta on the verse, "And they believed in Hashem and in Moshe His servant" (see there). Yet, when it comes to practical application, and when it touches them personally, suddenly and unexpectedly, all the instructions of our holy Rebbes on this matter slip from their memory—the instructions of the Alter Rebbe, the Mitteler Rebbe, the Tzemach Tzedek, etc., that specifically in this place, etc., the attribute of *rachamim* is revealed, which is greater than the attribute of *chesed* that precedes it, an inheritance without boundaries, extending from one end to the other.

It is not my intent to cause fear, G-d forbid. But the place that is currently most protected in Eretz Yisrael—may it be rebuilt and established—is Kfar Chabad. You have permission to publicize this clear opinion of mine to whomever you see fit, and to Anash in general.

With blessings for health, peace of mind, and to hear good news regarding all the above.

**M. Schneerson**

## 4208

B"H, 8 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished,
R' Nachum, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter of the 3rd of the month, in which you write that you have moved into your new home in Kfar Chabad. May it be the will of Hashem that this change of place brings a change of fortune for the good and for blessing, in a revealed and manifest way, both materially and spiritually. May you, with peace of mind and an expanded consciousness, increase your efforts in all that pertains to the development and growth of Kfar Chabad in all matters, in accordance with the will of its founder and director, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. It is understood that this includes involvement in the greatest and most important matter: the girls' seminary. The attribute of the Ribono shel Olam is measure for measure, only that He acts in this way to increase the channels, as well as the vessels of each and every resident of Kfar Chabad, in their fullness, as it is written: "And He shall sustain you; open wide your mouth and I will fill it." It is surely unnecessary to prompt you to explain to all those you influence in Kfar Chabad the words of the Alter Rebbe, that now is the time when the attribute of mercy will be bestowed there, an inheritance without boundaries, from one end to the other.

With blessings and regards to all the residents of the Kfar, may they live, each and every one, from the smallest to the greatest—may Hashem increase them—may they live.

#### ***4208***

**R' Nachum:** Trabnik, Kfar Chabad.

**Words of the Alter Rebbe:** See the letter preceding this one, and the references in the notes there.

## 490

B.H., 8 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the distinguished, G-d-fearing, and learned man, who occupies himself with the needs of the community, a branch of the tree of the forefathers, etc., R’ Shazar, may he live:

Shalom u’Bracha!

Since I am writing with great sorrow in connection with and in the aftermath of the events in Kfar Chabad, and my heart goes out to those who were killed, it is possible that my letter may contain expressions that are more intense than usual. However, a person is not held accountable for what he says in his grief, and you will surely understand my distress.

You are surely aware of the telegram I sent yesterday to Kfar Chabad, which reads as follows:

"To each and every one of Anash, the residents of Kfar Chabad, the institutions there—and especially the vocational and agricultural schools—the students, teachers, administrators, activists, and parents: My hope is strong that with the help of Hashem, who watches with an open eye and oversees with Divine Providence, you will overcome every obstacle. You will strengthen yourselves in both private and public buildings, and you will expand all the institutions in both quantity and quality. Through tranquility and breadth of mind, the study of our Torah—our fortress, the Torah of life and the words of the living G-d—and the fulfillment of its mitzvos will be magnified and glorified with joy and goodness of heart, and you shall live by them. From Kfar Chabad, the wellsprings of Chassidus—the Torah and deeds of our holy Nesi’im—will spread until they reach the outside, to draw all our brethren, the Children of Israel, closer to our Father in Heaven with kindness and mercy, and to bring the true and complete Redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu speedily.

Participating in your prayers and your fast, and I am confident in Hashem that very soon I will also participate in your joy—a true, internal, and complete joy, in both the physical and spiritual realms alike—M.S."

From the above, my perspective regarding the residents of the Kfar is clear. But the extent to which this telegram and words of encouragement will succeed in calming the spirits that were shaken by this terrible tragedy depends primarily on action. For this is not a question of improving comfort, but a question of *pikuach nefashot*, and that is what occupies and worries the residents of the Kfar.

As for myself, my view was clear even beforehand, and based on this view, I proposed and demanded some time ago a specific plan for the development of the Kfar. As I have emphasized several times, this was not a question of comfort, but an essential and vital need for the entire development and future of Kfar Chabad and the other Chabad institutions that are directly or indirectly influenced by it. And if there were those who doubted the necessity of carrying out the plan I proposed—in its entirety—it is surely clear to everyone now that with mere words, and even with promises and fragmented actions, we will not be able to remove the anxiety from the hearts of the residents of the Kfar. Only the fulfillment of the plan I proposed, in its entirety and more, is what will be able to restore their spirit to continue their way of life—not through *iskafya* (coercion), but with desire and willingness of heart, and with tranquility and breadth of mind, etc., as stated in the aforementioned telegram.

I will briefly reiterate the main points I proposed and demanded, which, to our regret, have not yet been implemented:

1. All this time, I have striven for all measures to be taken to increase the population of the Kfar and the sources of livelihood there. That is why I demanded and urged the establishment of vocational and agricultural schools, etc., in the most expansive manner and with the greatest possible speed. But to my regret, astonishment, and sorrow, due to reasons beyond my control, matters have proceeded slowly and sluggishly to the greatest degree. As painful as it is, I must say that the reason for this is that the appropriate institutions and offices in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established) that are responsible for executing this matter have debated the issue like merchants dealing with monetary affairs: what amount to lend, under what conditions, how much can be skimped, etc., etc. Furthermore, despite the promises given to me here (even if not officially), they did not keep their word and held back their hand where they should have opened it, and with generosity...

The claim of the tight financial situation in the offices is not hidden from me. But knowing and seeing that regarding activities **abroad**, and regarding matters that do not rise in importance—and not just from my perspective alone—above the matters of the vocational and agricultural schools in Kfar Chabad in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established), they do not economize, while at the same time they proceed with cutbacks regarding the affairs of the Kfar, the "I have nothing" claim falls away on its own. Especially since one must take into account the fact that the expansion of the dining hall and the construction of the aforementioned school buildings are not merely matters unto themselves, but a preparation and prerequisite for the development of the entire Kfar, as they would bring strength of spirit and encouragement to all the residents of the Kfar—men, women, and children—and the entire surrounding area.

2. Similar to the above is the question of the buildings in the new Kfar. The approach to this must be—and indeed, this is the reality—that this is not a question of speed in construction and comfort alone; rather, the matter concerns the very development of the Kfar. Furthermore, after all, the old buildings would eventually have been occupied, and more residents would have been added to the Kfar. Had they approached the matter with this perspective, and not with stinginess and cutbacks, we would already have been privileged to be in negotiations regarding the third stage.

3. In connection with section 2 above, this is also the reason I demanded that they arrange the second Kfar in close proximity to the current one. For besides the fact that this would have facilitated the organization of the new Kfar with a way of life similar to that of the old Kfar, it would also have been a great encouragement to the residents of the old Kfar to see that they are serving as a focal point around which other settlements are concentrating, organizing themselves with a way of life like theirs, and "each man says to his fellow, 'Be strong.'" It is needless to say that I understand 100 percent the logic and the effort to settle the new Kfar specifically in the Lachish region or something similar, but if they had calculated the loss to Chabad against the gain of settling one hundred families (which was what was discussed in the first stage) in the aforementioned regions, they would have recognized that it is not comparable at all. I also remember the explanation that the area around Kfar Chabad is under the ownership of others, *mehadrin*, etc., but it is known that delays of this type are dismissed every day, especially in the current situation in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established), and without any great difficulty.

4. I must add one more point, although I do not know how justified it is, but since they have come to me with complaints about this as well, I am passing it on: that the security for the Kfar is insufficient, as the Kfar is like an open field, and in addition, it is surrounded by orchards that serve as a hiding place for infiltrators, etc.

Now, my intention in all of the above—and surely I will not be suspected of this—is not a matter of, G-d forbid, rebuke and moralizing; it is known that the method of moralizing is not the method of Chabad and Lubavitch. Rather, the intention is that, in any case, from here on in—and primarily, immediately—they should begin to feel a fundamental turning point in everything mentioned above. And most importantly, that all the residents of the Kfar, they, their wives, their sons, and their daughters, will also see with their own eyes that a new turning point has been created in the attitude toward the Kfar. And when they see and become convinced that the offices and the supporters have also awakened on the spot with actual deeds to increase their assistance many times over, this will add to their connection to the Kfar, in such a way that the shocking event will not only not cause, G-d forbid, a fall in spirit and weakness of hands, but on the contrary, it will awaken the inner part of their soul—the Chabad soul—to see this as a "descent for the purpose of an ascent," and even an ascent. It is understood that the condition for this, as stated, is actual deeds and speed in increasing the institutions and the population of the Kfar, expanding their means of livelihood, and increasing matters of security, guarding, and protection.

Of course, I know that some of the above matters do not have a direct connection to the offices in which you are directly active. But since Divine Providence has granted you the merit to be among the primary factors in the establishment of the Kfar and you have worked for the benefit of the Kfar with energy, the establishment, development, and future of the Kfar are directly connected to you. Therefore, I am confident that you also have the ability to do and act in all the above matters, for you are a partner in the matter, and the Holy One, Blessed be He, does not ask except according to the strength of each and every one.

And if one asks, "In what way is Kfar Chabad different, that one must act for it beyond the letter of the law and grow and nurture it more than the general public?" The answer is inherent: First, all the above points together and in their entirety do not require a large sum that would be beyond them. Second—and this is the main thing—Kfar Chabad is indeed an exception, and the residents of the Kfar are also unique in their kind. For after more than 25 years of subjugation under the Communists, they emerged whole in their faith and firm in their spirit, and they were able to settle in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established) in a manner of "the triumph of form over matter," and therefore they serve as an example and a model for our generation, an orphaned generation. You surely remember what we spoke about when you were here, how much the Israeli youth need influence and spiritual encouragement so as not to fall into the net of the destructive ideologies that strike at the soul of the young generation, especially in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established), which is also a matter to be proud of.

It should also be added that Kfar Chabad is one of the only settlements, if not the only one, whose residents remained loyal to their place, despite all the delays, concealments, and obstructions, and in this they showed exemplary discipline, even though most of them do not know me face to face. My hope is that it will be so in the future as well, but one cannot ignore the reality that the situation in Kfar Chabad is currently most serious. And if one takes into account that many of them have relatives and friends who settled in other countries in a much more comfortable and easier way, and they are in contact with them through letters and visits, the test is even greater.

My hope is that in the future as well, with the help of Hashem, I will succeed in maintaining the integrity of the Kfar and its development and expansion, but—each of us prays every day and at the beginning of the day, "Do not bring us into the hands of a test."

I will conclude with the emphasis that my intention in the above is not, G-d forbid, a matter of pressure, as is proven by the telegram I sent even before I wrote to you or received your response. That is to say, that regardless, I will do, with the help of Hashem, everything that depends on me in fulfilling the responsibility that rests upon me. But at the same time, my hope is strong that all the supporters of Chabad on the spot—and all the more so those of Chabad lineage, and all the more so those in whom the root of their quarry beats and in a manner of emotion that is expressed in actual deeds that grow from day to day—that they too will recognize their extra ability and the call of the hour and will do everything possible and even more. I am confident that then everything will be with success and in tranquility, and each and every one will be able to say with a full mouth and a happy heart, "I will thank You, Hashem, for You were angry with me... for You have done greatness..."

Awaiting good tidings, and signing with appreciation and blessing.

## 410

410

B"H, 8 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the honorable Rabbi, the Gaon, renowned for his praise, G-d-fearing, man of action, engaged in public affairs, etc., etc., R' Sh.Y. shlita,

Shalom u’Bracha!

Enclosed is a copy of my letter to Mr. Shazar shlita. Since the letter turned out longer than what he is accustomed to receiving, and due to the multitude of pressures, long letters are sometimes set aside for a more convenient time, I am sure that you will seek an opportunity to meet with him soon regarding the content of the letter and to add your own explanations as you see fit. It is unnecessary to emphasize that one must utilize the awakening in the various circles following the event, and on the other hand, one must strive with all strength to prevent any loss of spirit, chas v’shalom, among the residents of Kfar Chabad, in good time, for everything depends on the abundance of action and diligence in this. May the Ribono shel Olam be with his mouth and grant him success. I would appreciate it if you would keep me informed of the progress of matters.

With respect and blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

Since young rabbis from here are traveling to Jerusalem these days, and will endeavor to influence the Chief Rabbinate in general, and Rabbi Herzog shlita in particular, regarding the problem and conflict that has arisen here surrounding the question of whether it is permissible for those who observe Torah and mitzvos to participate in organizations that have a religious character and include representatives of the Conservative and Reform movements—and despite the ruling of prohibition on this issued by the Rabbis and Roshei Yeshivos, there are those who are trying to continue this participation, including some of those traveling—one must strive with all strength that not only should they not find support, chas v’shalom, for their erroneous opinion, but on the contrary, that the Chief Rabbis in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, should express their clear opinion and stance regarding the absolute and total prohibition of this. Since it is difficult to explain all the details of this in a letter, enclosed is a photocopy of the p’sak din of the Rabbonim, as well as my letter and request to the Rabbi, G-d-fearing, man of action, a man of many deeds and energy, Rabbi Dovid shlita Hollander, to visit you at the first opportunity and to convey to you the development of the matter and all the details involved.

It is understood that you have permission to inform the Chief Rabbi that I have written to you regarding this and to share the content of my letter. I thank you in advance for informing me of good news regarding all of the above.

They asked me how my position on the aforementioned matter aligns with my position regarding the elections in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, given that among the candidates for the elections there were also those who, if they were in the United States, would belong to the Conservative movement or the like. I replied that the distinction is simple: regarding elections, the participation of those who observe Torah and mitzvos does not declare that they recognize them as representatives of a **religious** movement. This is unlike participation in the organization of "Rabbis" of New York (the New York Board of Rabbis) and the organization of synagogues (the Synagogue Council of America), where it is **emphasized** in their names that they are religious organizations. Therefore, a Chareidi person participating in them together with a Reform or Conservative rabbi or their temple representative is, in effect, stating that this too is a "synagogue" and this too is a "rabbi"—in contradiction to the ruling of our Torah, and as the Rambam states, as brought in my letter. It is simple.

Of course, if you can introduce the aforementioned Rabbi Hollander shlita to those who have influence in this matter, you will surely do so, and I thank you in advance.

## 4110

B"H, 8 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and distinguished scholar, occupied with communal needs, R' Yaakov, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, in which you write regarding the order of affixing the mezuzah based on what you heard from the Rebbe, may his merit protect us—thank you, thank you for the details regarding it. Since sometimes additional details are recalled after some time, surely if anything else regarding this comes to your memory, please inform me, and I thank you in advance.

It is surely unnecessary to awaken each and every one of anash that the recent event, rachmana litzlan, must reveal within them the hidden strengths, through which the hiskashrus with our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, is magnified. Consequently, each and every one will increase, to the best of their ability, the development of Chabad institutions in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, specifically in their current locations. This is, of course, understood from the well-known maamarim of the Alter Rebbe, the Mitteler Rebbe, and the Tzemach Tzedek. Enclosed is a copy of what I wrote to someone, etc., which may also interest you. It is understood that I am not writing this for the sake of a lecture, but my hope is that you, too, will utilize all your influence in the aforementioned direction.

With blessings to hear good news regarding all the above.

## 4112

B"H, 8 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Uri, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

After your longest silence, your letter of 4 Iyar was finally received. Regarding what you wrote concerning the weakness, Heaven forbid, in the affairs of Kfar Chabad: I am confident in the founder and director of the village, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, Nasi Yisroel, that, on the contrary, the avodah and peace of mind will increase, and the blessing of Hashem will increase—not only in general matters but also in the private matters of each and every one, and specifically in a way that is good, visible, and revealed, as per the ruling of the Alter Rebbe, the Mitteler Rebbe, and the Tzemach Tzedek on this matter, and as I have written in my letters previously.

Thank you for the good news that you are already looking for a suitable lot for the school. However, it is understood that what you are hoping for—that it will be operational by the 5718 school year—is not sufficient at all. For in matters of chinuch, every month, week, and even day is of the utmost value. Certainly, with the appropriate effort and energy on your part, the efforts on the part of the relevant offices will also increase. May it be Hashem’s will that you bring good news regarding this soon.

It is surprising that until now no one had reported on the start of registration until this letter of yours, from which it appears it began the day before yesterday. May it be Hashem’s will that it be with great success, and you should certainly recruit from among the young men of Aguch and the students of Tomchei Temimim as is necessary for this work...

With the blessing that very soon their housing situation will be resolved in a good way, and you will be able to add the energy required for your holy work in the Reshet Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch in Eretz Yisroel, may it be rebuilt and established.

***

**R' Uri, may he live:** Ben Shachar, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him: above Vol. 12, 4311, and in the references in the footnotes there. Below 4528.

**In the affairs of Kfar Chabad:** See above, letter 4281, and in the references in the footnotes there.

**As I have written in my letters previously:** Above Vol. 10, 3262. And see also above, letter 4307, and in the references in the footnotes there.

## 413

413

B"H, 8 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, distinguished, and meticulous in his communal work, R' Yitzchak, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote concerning the handing over of a key during the sale of Chametz: surely you are aware that in many places and towns, even among those who are meticulous in their observance of mitzvos, this is no longer practiced in recent times. It is possible that your intention in your letter was merely for the sake of pilpul, and not regarding practical application...

With blessings and regards to all those who seek your welfare.

#### ***413***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 289, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Yitzchak:** Dubov. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, 3968, and in the references noted there.

See below 421, 621.

## 414

B"H, 8 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

... Regarding what you wrote concerning your son and his wife, may they live: You are surely looking for an opportunity to diplomatically raise the matter of Torah and mitzvos once again. There is nothing that can stand in the way of will, and it is well-known that there is a *chazakah* that *ta'amulah* (outreach) does not return empty-handed. You should certainly speak in a way that is more than mere *ta'amulah*. May the Ribono shel Olam be with your mouth and grant you success in this.

With blessings and regards to all those who seek your welfare,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***414***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 22, p. 436.

## 415

[9 Iyar 5717]

...You are doing well by heeding the guidance of your mashpi’im and rosh yeshivos, especially now that you have begun to involve yourself in the field of kosher and holy chinuch. Surely, as you become more accustomed to this over time, the concealments and obstructions will diminish, and your success will increase. May it be the will of Hashem that you very soon report good news regarding all of the above.

#### ***415***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 365.

## 416

B"H, 9 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter, in which you write that Divine Providence brought you into contact with the wise man...

And [this encounter] awakened within you the sentiments of a Jewish person, for as our Sages of blessed memory have said, Hashem is awake to His Torah and His mitzvos. It is understood and simple that a momentary awakening is not sufficient at all; rather, one must extend it into the days that follow and into daily life. For each and every day, one must act and accomplish something good so that there are no "naked and empty" days. This is the advantage of man over the animal, which has nothing but eating, drinking, and the like. This is not the case for man, the choicest of creation, who possesses an intellectual soul and a Divine soul, and who must illuminate his surroundings—and all the more so himself—with the "candle of a mitzvah and the light of Torah." And when one truly desires this, there is nothing that can stand before the will, and one will succeed in this...

Awaiting good news regarding all of the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4117

B"H, 9 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, and distinguished scholar, who engages in communal needs,
R' Dov Yehuda, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

... It seems to me that the rationale for organizing an Agudas Chabad in Toronto is sound, and surely Agudas N'shei U'Bnos Chabad should also be included within this, whether as a department or in another manner appropriate to the laws of the country. May it be the will of Hashem that their work ascends ever higher, both in quantity and in quality.

In this same vein, it is somewhat puzzling why the publication of the booklet in connection with the N'shei Chabad gathering in Toronto is being delayed for so long. In matters such as these, the sooner the material is publicized, the sooner the results of that publication will follow, in accordance with the dictum, "The jealousy of scribes increases wisdom."

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

#### ***4117***

**R' Dov Yehuda:** Shuchat. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 11, 3785, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

**Publication... of Toronto:** See above, Vol. 12, letters 4148, 4184; below, 4417.

## 494

B"H, 11 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Kfar Chabad, may Hashem protect them,
May they live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 8 Iyar and the telegrams received, and in continuation of my telegram of response.

1) In my opinion, it is imperative—as I have written many times—that all the institutions in Kfar Chabad be expanded as much as possible. The residents of the village and its administration will certainly do what is dependent upon them, and the administration of the institutions will do what is dependent upon them.

2) In my opinion, diligence is essential, both regarding the construction of individual buildings and public buildings—especially the shul and, distinct from it, the mikveh. Certainly, as mentioned above, they will do all that is dependent upon them and will increase their efforts with the appropriate offices so that they, too, will do their part.

3) I reiterate once again what I have written for several years: it is necessary to increase the number of (suitable) residents in Kfar Chabad. It is self-understood that one must assist all those who are striving to be accepted among the residents of the village. When the attitude toward accepting new settlers changes on their part, they will certainly find a solution to the question of the plots, etc., etc.

4) It is simple and understood—and this is the most important point of all the aforementioned—that there must be a growing closeness of hearts among them. Since our Sages have said in this regard that one should "overlook his own standards," this is a clear instruction: even if it seems to one that he is correct in his claims—and even if this is not merely an impression but the truth—nevertheless, he must overlook his own standards. Great is peace, which draws down and sustains the blessings of the Ribono shel Olam, which are "without limit," to draw them down below the ten tefachim and to sustain them even in physical vessels—that is, regarding physical matters: children, life, and ample sustenance. It is obvious that for this, breadth of mind and peace of mind are required and, consequently, granted. All of this is in the hands of the village residents and is their free choice. Surely, the commands of our Torah, the Torah of life, and the instruction of our Nasi, the holy one, will be fulfilled. As per the saying of the Rebbe the Tzemach Tzedek, which we heard from the mouth of my father-in-law, the Rebbe: "The unity of Chassidim will lead until Moshiach." In accordance with what I wrote in the telegram, my hope and confidence are strong that very soon I will be able to participate in your true and inner joy—even if from afar in location—but in true closeness in spirit, and amidst the peace of mind and expansion of yourselves and all your household members, may Hashem protect them, may they live, in Kfar Chabad—a place where the attribute of mercy has been and will be revealed, which is greater than the attribute of Chesed, which is an inheritance without boundaries, extending from one end to the other.

With blessing for all good in revealed and manifest ways, the one who awaits good tidings and detailed reports.

P.S.
Certainly, the appropriate residents of the village will also participate in the trip to Meron on the upcoming Lag B'Omer. Those remaining in the village (or on the Shabbos before Lag B'Omer—also as mentioned—and in proximity to Lag B'Omer) will also continue this joy in Kfar Chabad through farbrengens with words of Chassidus and words of inspiration, including a farbrengen for the women and girls of Chabad, may Hashem protect them, may they live.

## 419

11 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the Administration of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim,
May they live,

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letters dated 25 Shevat, 1 Adar, 10, 16, 18 Adar, 8, 9, 27 Nissan, and 5 and 7 Iyar, along with the enclosures. The photographs of the schools in Kfar Chabad and the maps were also received in due time, as was the letter regarding the vocational school bulletins dated 2 Adar.

In addition to what I wrote in my telegram, regarding what you mentioned concerning the fundraising campaign for the vocational and agricultural schools: your reasoning for doing so is correct—but it must be done in a manner that avoids any concern of "provoking the Arabs." There are several ways to publicize this that negate such a concern. Regarding what you wrote about the campaign for the new building for Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in the village, this is also correct, but it is questionable whether there is room for two campaigns simultaneously. It is highly doubtful that donors will double their contributions. In such a case, it is understood that the vocational and agricultural schools take precedence, as this is a matter of immediate urgency, whereas the aforementioned building can be publicized even after some time has passed—that despite all the events, it was decided to establish yet another new building in Kfar Chabad, etc. Naturally, this is only a general point, and you must consider on the spot whether combining the two campaigns will yield additional benefit—that is, simply in terms of the total sum. It has already been said, "In the multitude of counselors there is safety."

For about two years now, I have been taking every opportunity to urge you—both directly and indirectly—regarding the **greatest** necessity of increasing the number of students, even in the simplest sense of quantity. To my sorrow and regret, you have paid no heed to this. Although I wrote that increasing the number of students would further increase the channels of income, you arrived at the conclusion that the opposite is true—namely, that the more you limit the number of students, the more you will decrease the deficit, or at least that expenses will not increase even if income decreases. Perhaps there are other reasons, but in any case, regarding the practical outcome—the number of students has decreased. It is understood that all the reasons and excuses you wrote to me regarding this—that this type [of student] is not suitable and that type is liable to cause damage—even if we were to assume that the excuses are valid, if only you had exerted yourselves in acquiring students of the types that even in your opinion are as suitable as necessary, the number of students in the Yeshiva now would be at least double.

...It is puzzling that at the end of your letter, regarding the great neglect, you do not connect this at all to your own approach and conduct over this period, but rather attribute it to the conduct of people who lack free choice. In truth, every person—and especially the administration of Tomchei Temimim—should seek in every matter, first and foremost, a lesson in the *avodah* of Hashem, and only afterward interpret things in terms of the ways and matters of this world, in which the *klipas nogah* is mostly on the side of [evil], etc. Consequently, all spiritual matters—and certainly lessons in the *avodah* of Hashem—are concealed and hidden from them as much as possible. It is **clear** that if it were firmly decided in your minds that regarding the students of Tomchei Temimim in the Holy Land, just as their founders and administrators—our holy Rebbes—can provide the necessary expenses for two hundred students (even though, naturally, there was no place for this in the intellect two years ago), they can also provide the expenses for four hundred, six hundred, and even one thousand students—it would be so in practice, without any exaggeration. The "difference" is that eight hundred more students would have had a share and inheritance in the Torah of the Baal Shem Tov, our Rebbe [the Alter Rebbe], etc. Through conduct of *tzimtzum* and *midas hadin*, you do not give them the possibility for this. And another thing: through this, there is a foothold for *tzimtzum* from the outside, such that even with a number of two hundred students, there will be room for reduction.

It is difficult for me to elaborate on a matter where the reasoning is so opposite, because to me it is simple: if you had increased the number of students, the deficit would have decreased accordingly. To you, it is simple that the opposite is true—that there must be a certain number of students in order to be called a "Yeshiva," but beyond that number, one must *tzimtzum* because every student requires an expenditure of a certain amount of Lirot per month, and why should we have additional expenses?

I have written these lines only to urge you once again: the conduct of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, was and is to expand his institutions as much as possible, as is known from his holy *sicha* regarding his institutions: "I have always been a debtor, and I have never, *chas v'shalom*, remained owing anyone."

Once again, I emphasize that no one should say that this is merely talk for the sake of inspiration and not regarding practical matters. My intention is simple: over the past years, thanks to the administration's conduct of *tzimtzum*, you have, *chas v'shalom*, denied hundreds of Bnei Yisrael the ability to study *Chassidus*. The *he'elem v'hester* (concealment) caused by this is great—to the point that even those who had already entered the gates of Tomchei Temimim, some have left. As is explained at length in *Chassidus* in several places regarding what comes down in the *hishtalshelus* from the *gevuros* of *kedushah*, and the wise will understand.

May it be Hashem's will that immediately upon receiving this letter, each and every one of you will *arainvarfen* (throw yourselves in)—fitting for your role in the Yeshiva administration—to acquire the largest possible number of suitable students, and to be lenient in the standard of "suitability." Then, one can hope that even students who left the gates of Tomchei Temimim—due to reasons that seemed real to them—will recognize the absolute truth, just as the Yeshiva administration will also recognize the absolute truth.

I repeat once again that all of the above is meant quite literally: that the Lod Yeshiva could now include many hundreds of students more than it has. But what can be done, as our Sages said, "One does not cry over the past." At the very least, utilize the coming days as much as possible, and then one can hope that by the beginning of the coming term, hundreds of students will be added—without exaggeration, more than two hundred—to the number in the past. As it is written, "And test Me now with this."

I am certain you will write with the necessary details regarding what is stated in this letter and in the telegram preceding it.

With a blessing for immense success,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4220

4220

B”H, 11 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in the Holy City of Jerusalem, may it be rebuilt and established,
May Hashem keep them alive.

Peace and blessing!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter of 24 Nissan, along with the enclosures. You may set your minds at ease, as you have set mine at ease with the good news regarding your activities in Yiddishkeit in general, and in spreading the wellsprings of Chassidus in particular. You will surely continue to increase these efforts, going from strength to strength, as the needs of the hour demand. As is explained and cited in many places, as we draw closer to the moment of the true and complete Geulah and its inception, every single moment becomes increasingly precious and valuable, for we must hasten to prepare for the coming of our Master, King Moshiach. Therefore, one must utilize every moment to the fullest extent possible, and there is no need to elaborate on something so self-evident.

From this, the answer to your question regarding Lag B’Omer is also understood: it is simple and obvious that those who are able should arrange a trip to Meron. Bli neder, I will participate to a certain extent in the expenses involved in this matter. Those who, for valid reasons, are unable to travel should certainly organize a suitable event and farbrengen in Jerusalem, may it be rebuilt and established. Coordinate these activities with all the branches and members, and may it be with success. I look forward to hearing good and detailed news regarding all of the above.

With a blessing for outstanding success.

P.S.
Surely, if there are further matters in continuation of the Shmurah Matzah campaign, you will inform me. I believe I mentioned in one of my previous letters that it is worthwhile to maintain contact with those with whom you come into contact through these activities, whatever they may be, for ultimately, the ruling of the Mishnah is fulfilled: “One mitzvah leads to another.”

#### ***4220***

Printed in Likutei Sichos, Vol. 17, p. 513, and completed based on the secretariat’s copy.

**To arrange a trip to Meron:** See also above, Letter 4218, and the references in the notes there.

## 4,321

B"H, 12 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I received your express letter (in addition to the one sent by airmail), and the haste in this matter requires contemplation.

It appears that you are in your own world and I am in mine—meaning, I am writing my clear opinion once again: one must not squander expenses and act in a way that is the opposite of building by moving from [location]. There is much work to be done there in spreading the wellsprings (until they reach) the outermost reaches. In simple terms, your desire to move from there is a suggestion of the *yetzer*, which is never satisfied with the work of spreading Chassidus in any form. Since my opinion is clear that one must engage in spreading the wellsprings in [location], it is understood that there is no point at all in moving...

I already told you when you were here that I saw from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, that he did not hold by the approach of "money is no problem," even regarding the conduct of those wealthy in physical silver and gold. How much more so was he not satisfied with such conduct from a public figure—that is, a person who is watched by seven eyes, where if he spends one shekel, people say he spent ten. This applies to you and to your conduct, which in my opinion is incorrect regarding the aforementioned (there was no necessity to speak specifically by telephone in connection with... and so on and so forth)...

I found it necessary to use such strong language, even though I am not accustomed to it, because I see that although I hinted and later spoke several times that there must be a report regarding the expenses of all those involved in the public needs, my intention is not an accounting toward others, but rather toward oneself—meaning, to know that "I dwell among my people," and just as they are limited and mindful of their means, so too am I within that measure and limitation. And although expansion and spreading out is necessary, it must be in spiritual matters, whereas your conduct in practice is precisely the opposite: you are constricting regarding the spreading of the wellsprings, because you are "sitting on your suitcases and thinking about America," while regarding the physical, it is as mentioned above.

And although it is superfluous, I reiterate once again that it is not, *chas v'shalom*, my intention that *anash* should live in physical hardship, *chas v'shalom* and *chas v'shalom*. Anyone who observes even for a short while sees that my approach is the exact opposite. However, an orderly life that is not lived in constriction does not mean a life without order and **without** any justification in sound reason...

With blessings to internalize all that has been said above with joy, and consequently, with inwardness.

P.S.
I am not sending my letter express, and may it be the will of Hashem that from now on *anash* will also conduct themselves in such a manner, except in cases of the utmost necessity.

***

**"Dziengi nie patscham":** Money is not a problem.

## 422

B"H, 12 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I was pleased to receive regards from you via the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and distinguished, active in communal needs and multi-faceted in his endeavors, Rabbi Sholom Zalman Hecht, may he live. I also received the *pidyon nefesh*, which will be read at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, during a time of favor. May it be His will that you receive good tidings regarding everything mentioned therein.

Regarding our conduct until now, you, on your part, do not mention anything about establishing a fixed time for the study of Chassidus, while I, each time, awaken you regarding the absolute necessity of this—a necessity for every single Jew in our generation, this orphaned generation. Although I hope that from time to time you do look into a Chassidic text, I fear that it is not done in a manner of *keviyus* (fixedness). And especially not *keviyus* as explained by our Alter Rebbe, author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch: that true *keviyus* is not only in time, but also *keviyus* in the soul. It is already known the ruling of the Mishnah that a wealthy person who offers the sacrifice of a poor person has not fulfilled his obligation. The same applies to spiritual matters, for "there is no wealthy person except in knowledge, and no poor person except in knowledge." One who has the ability to study with *iyun* (depth)—meaning with an increase in quality, and certainly with an increase in quantity—does not fulfill his obligation with the sacrifice of one who is "poor in knowledge." The analogy is understood, and the matter depends solely on his will, for the *cheftza* (the inner core of the will) is present within him, as it is present within every Jew—especially one who is a guardian of Torah and mitzvos, for one mitzvah leads to another.

May Hashem grant you the merit, and grant me the merit, to receive good news from you that, at the very least, from here on out you will have—without a vow—*keviyus* in the soul and *keviyus* in time for the study of the inner dimension of Torah, which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus.

With blessings.

## 4323

B"H, 12 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your notification regarding the purchase of your home.

May it be the will of Hashem that this be in a good and auspicious hour, both materially and spiritually. That is to say, may the acquisition of the home (and as explained in Chassidic discourse, the concept of *kinyan*—that it is specifically in the world of Atzilus that it is called *kinyan*, because "He, His life, and His essence are one," and through this one acquires a home) be realized spiritually, as explained in the *maamarim* on the psalm *Mizmor Shir Chanukas HaBayis* in *Likutei Torah* and in the *Siddur*, and similarly at the end of the *hemshech* *Mayim Rabim*, and elsewhere. And may this *kinyan* also manifest in the purchase of the home in its literal sense—a home that is blessed in every detail.

And may it be His will that you be able to share good news soon regarding a suitable *shidduch* and the building of a home in Yisrael for your daughter, Mrs. ... *tichyeh*.

With blessings,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***4323***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 18, p. 449.

**Regarding the purchase of the home:** See also above, Vol. 3, Letter 608; Vol. 10, 3254.

## 4324

B"H, 13 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

Following your long silence, your letter from the 19th of Omer was received (as this is the phrasing used by the Alter Rebbe in his holy Siddur, and the discussion regarding this is well-known; see also Iggeres HaKodesh, end of Epistle 13). As you requested, I will mention you and your wife, may they live, at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever you may need.

Regarding your mention that you are studying the holy Tanya and also a little Likutei Torah, as you were instructed in the year 5672: it is understood that since many years have passed since then, this is not sufficient at all. For the commandment of our Torah is that "we elevate in matters of holiness" (some say this is a Torah-level obligation, and even if you say it is Rabbinic—as is the implication of the Alter Rebbe’s opinion in his Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, end of section 34—the words of the Sages are stringent, and the words of the Sages are dear to me). Certainly, from here on, you should increase your study as mentioned, and Hashem is surely faithful to fulfill His promise: "Whoever adds, they add to him."

You are surely participating in the farbrengens of Anash. Great is the act of sharing a drink, for it brings close and unites the children of Israel, drawing down the blessings of Hashem in abundance, as it is written: "Bless us, our Father, all of us as one, in the light of Your countenance." See Tanya, Chapter 32, and the Tzemach Tzedek’s Sefer HaMitzvos, Mitzvah of Ahavas Yisrael, and elsewhere.

With blessings,

**M. Schneerson**

Regarding your question on what is written in the Tzemach Tzedek’s Sefer HaMitzvos, Mitzvah of Sefiras HaOmer, Chapter 2, that Moshiach will be greater than Moshe, while the Rambam writes (Hilchos Teshuvah, Chapter 9) that Moshiach will be a prophet close to Moshe Rabbeinu: what is written in the Sefer HaMitzvos is **explicitly** stated in Midrash Tanchuma, Toldos: "And he will be exalted above Moshe" (as it should be read; and so it is in Yalkut Shimoni, Yeshayahu). As for what is written in the Rambam, it can be explained that it refers only to the level of prophecy, in accordance with what is written, "And there has not arisen a prophet [since in Israel like Moshe]." See also Rambam, Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah, Chapter 7. See also the Arizal’s Likutei Torah on the verse, "Until Shiloh comes"; Menachos 29b; and the Alter Rebbe’s Likutei Torah, Shir HaShirim, at the end. And this is not the place to elaborate.

## 4265

B"H, 13 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To Mr. Eliezer Steinman, Tel Aviv

Shalom u’vracha,

I received your letter from 23 Adar, and I was pleased to read that your forthcoming books will also include citations of sources, just as I emphasized their utility in my previous letter.

Regarding point two of my letter and your response—what you call the "matter of study and action"—you are certainly correct in writing that a face-to-face conversation is far better than written words. Yet, it is also understood that the written word has great power, and possesses qualities that spoken words do not. This is not only because, as you noted in your letter, "letters bring wisdom," but also because one can review written words many times, which is not the case with speech. This is self-evident based on what is explained in Chassidus: that the process of *hishtalshelus* and the drawing down of a concept from thought to speech, and even from speech to action, grants it greater permanence and a stronger connection to the world, to the immediate environment, and through that, to the broader environment as well.

I do not know how I should interpret your silence regarding the main point of the aforementioned second point, namely, a Chassidic *orach chaim* in daily life. Seemingly, the most likely explanation is that since you currently fear your response might not align well with the suggestion in my letter, you thought I might take it as an insult or the like. If this is the correct explanation, I wish to dispel this concern. I view the matter of words of *hisorerus* regarding *Torah* and *mitzvos* from one person to another in its literal sense: through this, one awakens the inner point of the listener—which exists within every single Jew—and one only needs to bring it from concealment into revelation. Therefore, when this revelation is delayed, the lack of success on the part of the one offering the *hisorerus* holds no weight compared to the pain of that inner point, which is the essence of the Jewish soul. Its entire descent into this world—a descent from a high peak to a deep pit (since the soul is hewn from beneath the Throne of Glory of the *Ribono shel Olam*, and this world, in the language of Kabbalah as cited by our *Alter Rebbe* (Tanya, end of chapter 6), is the world of *kelipos* and *sitra achra*, and therefore all the affairs of this world are difficult and evil, and the wicked prevail in it)—is all for the sake of the ascent through *Torah* and *mitzvos*. From this, it is understood that every moment the *nitzotz Eloki* is in the world of *kelipos* and *sitra achra* and does not utilize the moment for a wondrous ascent, the pain is most profound. These matters were explained somewhat more in my letter from some time ago, a copy of which is enclosed herewith.

My hope is strong that in your writing, where you enter the garden of Chassidus out of *ahavas Yisrael* and a longing for the "Sukkah of Dovid"—that is, the soul of Israel—you certainly include yourself and your own soul as well. For all souls are compatible, and we all have one Father, only the bodies are divided (Tanya, chapter 32), as are the matters that stem from the body.

In these days, between the time of our freedom and the time of the giving of our *Torah*—the perfection of freedom—we have been commanded and given the ability to ascend each and every day from the freedom of the servitude of Egypt to the true freedom that comes to the people of Israel through the acceptance of the *Torah*. As our Sages hinted: *Charus* (engraved) on the tablets—*cheirus* (freedom) through the tablets.

And as it was in those days, so it is in this time: there is no free man except for one who lives with his trust in the Creator of the world—the ultimate Justice and Good—and His leadership is consistent with this, even if His ways are not always understood, for "My thoughts are not your thoughts, nor are My ways your ways."

With respect and blessing.

## 426

B"H, 13 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs, R' Tzvi, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from Thursday, in which you write regarding the order in the Talmud Torah and the synagogue under your leadership, and most importantly, that it is growing and developing. You are surely utilizing these auspicious days in the appropriate manner, as is explained regarding the great value of this in my general letter from before Pesach, which surely reached you. From this, it is understood that you will utilize the day of Lag B'Omer as necessary, especially since it falls on a day when people are free from their work.

Surely you are preparing in good time for the summer camp arrangements, and may it be G-d's will that this too will be successful. It is surely superfluous to remind you of the verse, "Is it not to share your bread with the hungry..." and "do not ignore your own flesh," which, according to the explanation in Eliyahu Rabbah, also refers to Torah and mitzvos. From this, it is understood that the conclusion of the verse also applies to oneself regarding Torah and mitzvos—that one must increase in this from time to time, and whoever adds, they add to him.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

#### ***426***

**R' Tzvi, may he live:** Shusterman, Chicago. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 12, 3,992, and in the references noted there.

**In my general letter:** Above, Vol. 12, letter 4,197.

## D'shkaz

[14 Iyar, 5716]

Following the hiatus, your letter of 11 Iyar was received, bearing the good news of the improvement in your health. May it be Hashem’s will that it continues to go from good to even better, and not only in physical matters but also in spiritual matters, even though you did not mention this in your current letter. It is understood that this also includes activities regarding others, especially during these auspicious days, the upcoming festivals and holidays that approach us for good.

The text you enclosed in your letter is generally correct. However, it requires further consideration; perhaps it would be worthwhile to replace the phrase "Erev Yom Kippur" with "Erev Yom HaKadosh," as this is a better omen (see Kesuvos 103b).

#### ***D'shkaz***

**The text:** From the tombstone of a [person] who passed away on Erev Yom Kippur.

## 4228

4228

B"H, 14 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, engaged in communal needs, R' Saadia, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 9th of Iyar. It is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding appropriate preparations for the upcoming day of Lag B'Omer, especially given the generally known special connection our Sephardic brethren have to the Rashbi, etc. Furthermore, this year the day falls when the youths are free from their secular studies, etc. My hope is that, in any event, they will utilize the day properly in practice, and that they will also discuss among themselves such matters regarding the upcoming time of Matan Torah. May Hashem grant you success in increasing Torah and glorifying it, as I wrote to someone else, that this matter (to "increase Torah and glorify it") is relevant and finds its place from the lowest depths to the highest heights. This is also proven in the revealed dimension of Torah by the fact that the Rambam cites this verse regarding a teacher of children (in his Yad HaChazakah, Hilchos Talmud Torah, end of chapter 2), and it is also brought in the context of Torah that transcends action (Chullin 66b).

With blessings for success and good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***4228***

**R' Saadia:** Librov, Tzafrou. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 12, 4121, and the references in the notes there. Below, 4465, 5189.

## 429

429

B"H, Pesach Sheni, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To all lovers of Torah and mitzvos
And supporters of institutions of kosher education
May they live

Peace and blessing!

The cry for help from the Bais Rivkah Schools of Brownsville and East New York must evoke an immediate response in every Jewish heart.

This institution, which was founded by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, fifteen years ago, where Jewish daughters study, and which has already, B"H, graduated many hundreds of students, raised in the spirit of Torah and yiras shamayim to take their place as an **akeres habayis** — this institution currently stands in a serious situation due to difficulties associated with the school building, which must be repaired and expanded in order to receive the necessary certification from the city administration.

It is surely unnecessary to elaborate on the importance of the education of girls on taharas hakodesh in general, and in particular regarding the vital importance of the aforementioned Bais Rivkah Schools, which are well known to the broad Jewish public.

I therefore turn to everyone for whom Torah and Torah-true Judaism is dear and holy, to respond generously to this serious cry for help from the Bais Rivkah Schools of Brownsville and East New York, both with the greatest personal support and through personal efforts to involve and grant others the merit of this great mitzvah of helping in the kosher education of Jewish daughters on taharas hakodesh.

And Hashem Yisbarach, whose measure is measure for measure, and many times over, will bless all the supporters and askanim with Jewish nachas from their children, may they live, and for themselves — with abundance, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings for all the best,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***429***

**Fifteen years ago:** See the book *Toldos Chabad B'Artzos HaBris*, Chapter 58.

## 430

B"H, Pesach Sheni, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

I received your letter of the 17th of Nissan in due time, but I did not have the opportunity to acknowledge it sooner. Please accept my apologies.

It is a pity that there was no opportunity to meet with you during your recent visit to America, for the advantage of speech over writing lies in the fact that during conversation, one's facial expression serves as a significant indicator of how the words are being received by the heart, and what needs to be added or changed. Furthermore, the other party to the conversation has the opportunity to respond immediately, so there is no room for resentment, even if the words are not entirely to his liking.

However, since it did not come to pass, I am using the written medium, continuing from what you wrote in your letter regarding your work as a soldier for the strengthening of the ranks of Yisrael. I will suffice with a few points, as for someone like you, lengthiness is superfluous:

In every campaign, there are various roles, and success and victory depend on each and every person utilizing all their strengths in fulfilling their role in the specific place and matter entrusted to them individually. This is especially true when it is a matter that cannot be performed by others. The magnitude of the power of this condition is understood from what our Sages ruled (Moed Katan 9b): Words of Torah—even matters of Heaven, i.e., the mitzvos—cannot compare to them. But if it is a mitzvah that cannot be performed by others, it is not only equal to words of Torah, but it even supersedes them.

Regarding the above: in physical and material matters, there are many who are occupied and active, but in matters of religion, to our great sorrow, there are very few. And even those who are involved in this do so with the utmost caution so as not to offend anyone’s honor, in a manner of "velvet gloves," and only when the opposing side is in a good mood, etc., etc. It is therefore understood how vital it is that one whom Hashem has graced with the strengths and influence to act in matters of religion should not postpone this activity even for a moment for the sake of other matters.

With the hope of hearing good news, and with respect and blessings.

## 4331

B"H, 14 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, involved in communal needs,
R' Refael Nachman, may he live, HaKohen

Shalom u’Bracha!

Following your longest silence, your letter from Erev Shabbos was received. It is surely unnecessary to urge you to utilize your strengths to encourage the spirit of Anash and the younger generation of Anash, especially those in Kfar Chabad. I emphasize that precisely in the place where the attribute of [judgment] was revealed, there will be revealed the attribute of mercy, which is greater than the attribute of kindness that preceded it—an inheritance without boundaries, extending from one end to the other, as I have written to several of Anash.

The explanation according to Chassidus—and even more so, in the spirit of Chassidus—is that this awakening and the absorption of the instructions of our holy Rebbes must be in a manner that is drawn down into action. That is to say, doing everything necessary and everything within one’s ability for the development of the village and the institutions within it, both in quantity and quality. This will surely result in an increase in the dissemination of the wellsprings until they reach even the outermost reaches.

It was pleasant for me to read in your letter that you are striving to utilize your talents in the aforementioned matter through farbrengens and the like. It would be very good to seek out such opportunities in Kfar Chabad and among the students who are there, for this is the demand of the hour and a matter that the time calls for.

Regarding what you wrote in your letter about the maamar of Rabbeinu HaZaken (cited in Torah Or at the beginning) that through tzedakah, one’s mind and heart become refined a thousand times over: I have written to someone else that the golden language of Rabbeinu HaZaken is precise in every word and letter. In the aforementioned statement, it says they become **refined**, meaning they become vessels capable of receiving much more, but it does not say they become filled with knowledge and feelings. From this, it is understood that although the one who gives tzedakah achieves extraordinary success, he is still in need of avodah, study, and exertion. It is just that the fruits are a thousand times over—a literal thousand—as is proven by the glosses of the Tzemach Tzedek printed in Likutei Torah for the three parshiyos there.

With blessings for good tidings,

**M. Schneerson**

***

**R' Refael Nachman, may he live, HaKohen:** Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him: see Vol. 12, 4099, and the references in the notes there.

**In the place where the attribute of... was revealed:** See above, letter 4281, and the references in the notes there.

**I have written to someone else:** See Vol. 12, letter 4126.

## 4332

B"H, 15 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs, R' Avraham Chanoch, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 5 Iyar, I trust you have by now received the notes regarding the material for the Lag B'Omer publication.

Regarding your question about the differing opinions concerning the posture of the Rebbe Rashab, may his merit protect us, while reciting Chassidus: the simplest approach is to note these differences in a footnote.

As for whether to include clichés [photographic plates] of the building plans and the like in the publication:

I do not think it advisable, unless you open a section specifically for clichés and photos. Otherwise, it implies a fundraising appeal within the publication, which is not its purpose—at least not in the current situation. This is not the case regarding the letter about the Lod Yeshiva, which also contains words of inspiration; there is no such objection there. However, you should consult and discuss among yourselves whether it is worthwhile to do so now, or to publish the letter closer to the time of the actual fundraising campaign.

It is surprising that you do not mention your efforts regarding the books by authors published from time to time (aside from those sent by my relative, the esteemed R' Slonim, may he live, which are published by Mosad HaRav Kook), and especially the books by Mr. Friedberg that you mentioned in your letter some time ago.

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

P.S.
Due to a reason, the sending of this letter was delayed, and in the meantime, your letters of 12 and 21 Iyar were received. A package of translated sichos [was received] (and there is no time to review them now).

Regarding several customs in requests: whether to include the mother’s name or the father’s (this is discussed at length in Responsa Shem Shimon, Orach Chaim, section 22). Therefore (contrary to what is written at the end of the letter of 21 Iyar), one should mention both since it is possible, or only the mother’s name, as per the formula in the Siddur. See also Zohar Vol. I, 84a; Pnei Yehoshua, end of Parshas Beha'alotcha.

Publishing the telegrams to Keren Chabad in the next issue—this is correct.

***

**R' Avraham Chanoch, may he live:** Glitzenstein, Lod. Additional letters to him—see above, Vol. 12, 3779, and the references in the notes there.

**Posture:** Facing north or south.

**Aside from those sent:** See above, Vol. 12, letter 3820, and the references in the notes there.

**Books by Mr. Friedberg:** See above, Vol. 12, letter 4188.

**Including the mother’s name:** See above, Vol. 11, letter 3620, and the references in the notes there.

**Telegrams to Keren Chabad:** See above, 3303.

## 4373

B"H, 15 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 12th of the month, regarding what you write concerning forgetfulness: You should have your tefillin checked, and you should be proficient, at the very least, in several chapters of Mishnayos by heart. Likewise, [you should be proficient in] at least one chapter of the Tanya Kadisha, and review them from time to time; through this, the power of memory will be strengthened.

Regarding what you write about dreams, etc.: You should have the mezuzos checked in the apartment where you live, and especially in the room where you sleep. Also, if possible, sleep while wearing a tallis katan whose tzitzis have been checked, and with your head covered.

Regarding both of the aforementioned matters, every weekday before morning davening, you should set aside a coin or two for tzedakah. May Hashem grant you the merit to share good news regarding all that was mentioned above.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 434

15 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished R' Yitzchak, may he live:

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter—which lacks the date of writing:

1) Regarding your question as to why the Ribono shel Olam desired this, etc.:

Our Sages have already stated a great principle: "Why should you concern yourself with the hidden matters of the Merciful One?" This is to be understood even in its literal sense. Furthermore, every single part of creation has a role it must fulfill. Since there is nothing in the world that is in vain, every part is provided with the powers and time necessary to fulfill its role. When one spends his time and powers (which includes his power of *hisbonenus* and his intellect) on matters that are not his role, he will find himself lacking when the time comes to fulfill his specific task.

2) Regarding your question on how to understand the aforementioned, as well as the burning of the *cheder* in the *kibbutz* Sadeh Eliyahu, which included *seforim*, etc.:

The general point regarding such matters is that there is no control over the affairs of a Yid—especially matters of *kedushah*—by anyone, whoever they may be, unless there is a weakness on the part of the Yid in these **very matters** (which provides an opening for harm from the outside). Therefore, all those whom such matters concern must examine their conduct. They will surely find that they were not sufficiently careful regarding the *kedushah* of the *seforim*, or more likely, regarding the study within them—which is the very purpose of their printing—as is required. The same applies to [the individual mentioned]; there was surely some deficiency in the assistance provided to him and his *avodah* in *kodesh* on the part of those with free choice, and through this, a crack was opened for the control of those who do not have free choice. It is understood that the above is a general point common to all events of this type, but in each specific event, one must examine the immediate situation and the time preceding the event; then one will surely find more specific and detailed matters.

3) Regarding what you wrote concerning the conduct after the burning of the *cheder*, etc.:

This depends on the local custom, and therefore you should turn to the *rabbonim* and *morei hora'ah* who are there or in the vicinity. It is worth investigating whether there were mixed-gender dances in this *cheder*.

4) Regarding what you write about reciting a prayer (during the *Mincha* prayer of *Erev Shabbos Kodesh*) concerning the observance of the upcoming *Shabbos*:

This is not the custom of *anash*, nor is it in the *Siddur* of our *Alter Rebbe*.

5) Regarding what you write that at the time of the event in your *kibbutz*, you were studying the *parsha* [of *Bechukosai*] concerning the destruction of the house, etc.:

Look at the *Midrash Rabbah* at the end of that *parsha* (Chapter 17). The matter in its spiritual sense is the concept of *galus*. The inner essence of *galus* is, as the formula established by the *Anshei Knesses HaGedolah* states: "Because of our sins we were exiled from our land." Sin and iniquity constitute a *galus* for the *nefesh elokis* within every single Yid—a literal "part of G-d from above"—and consequently, for the spark of *Shechinah* within him. This is the source from which the *galus* of the body, in the literal sense, also unfolds. This is the connection between the two matters—the *parsha* and the event: they come to awaken the necessity for an increase in matters of Torah and *mitzvos*, especially on *Shabbos Kodesh*, of which it is said, "a *Shabbos* **to Hashem**." Heaven forbid, a deficiency in this is a matter of *galus* and the aforementioned *parsha*. The central point of *galus* is the burning of the *Beis HaMikdash*, which is why they increased the fast of the fifth month; the same applies to a *mikdash me'at*—a place of prayer, G-d forbid.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that you increase your study of Torah and performance of *mitzvos*, and primarily in setting fixed times for the study of the inner dimensions of Torah, which brings about *hislahavus* in *avodas Hashem* (a fire of *kedushah* that consumes the "fire of the other side" in a spiritual sense, and consequently, there is no place for the "fire of the other side" even in the physical realm), and that you influence your surroundings in the direction mentioned.

With a blessing for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe,
Secretary

## 4335

B"H, 16 Iyar, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, involved in communal needs,
R' Nachum Shemaryahu, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 11 Iyar, in which you write regarding the incident in Kfar Chabad, may Hashem spare us and protect us.

It is surely unnecessary to urge you regarding what I have written to many of anash: that it is imperative not only to strengthen the institutions there, but to expand them further—both in quantity and in quality. For "there Hashem commanded the blessing, even life forevermore," in accordance with the instruction of our holy Rebbes that in this place, the attribute of mercy will be revealed—which is greater than the attribute of kindness that preceded it—an inheritance without boundaries, extending from one end to the other.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above, and for your activities in this regard.

#### ***4335***

**R' Nachum Shemaryahu:** Shashunkin, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him: see above, Vol. 11, 3774, and the references in the footnotes there.
