In response to your letter, and continuing our discussion from when you were here on the eve of the time of the Giving of the Torah, regarding the language of the Rash (Negaim, Chapter 14, Mishnah 5), which implies that physical blemishes disqualify an *eglah arufah* (the heifer whose neck is broken), whereas the Mishnah explicitly states (Sotah 45b) that blemishes do not disqualify an *eglah arufah*.

I happened to see a responsum by the late Rabbi Zemba, *Hy”d*, who was asked about this (printed in the sefer *Mikdashei Hashem*, section 130, by Rabbi Meislish, may he live, of Chicago – 5716). I have copied in the margin what he answered regarding this*, and his words require further study.

What appears to me regarding this great wonder is that although in Chullin (21a), the Rash explains that the term "atonement" is written regarding an *eglah arufah*, the Rash should have resolved his difficulty by stating that one cannot exclude an animal missing a limb on the basis that "atonement" is written regarding it, as is the case with *kodshim* (this is according to the opinion in the Yerushalmi, Sotah, ad loc., that an animal missing a limb is disqualified for *eglah arufah*). For if we were to exclude an animal missing a limb because it is a means of atonement, then all blemishes should logically be prohibited for an *eglah arufah*, as there is no reason to distinguish between a missing limb and any other blemish when it comes to the requirement of atonement.

However, this resolution still does not address the difficulty that one could learn that an animal missing a limb is prohibited for a "means of preparation"—namely, the birds of a *metzora*—because it is similar to another means of atonement, the *sa’ir hamishtaleach* (scapegoat), as Rashi explains there in Chullin. Yet, for the *sa’ir hamishtaleach*, blemishes do disqualify it, just as with all *kodshim*, since it must be fit for the Name of Hashem. Therefore, the Rash chose the explanation that **at once** resolves the difficulty by equating the "means of preparation" with the "means of atonement"—the *sa’ir hamishtaleach* and the *eglah arufah*. That is, in any case, for a *chatas ha’of* (bird sin-offering) and *olas ha’of* (bird burnt-offering), blemishes do not disqualify them. Therefore, one must conclude that one cannot derive a *hekkesh* (analogy) in this matter, neither from the *eglah arufah* itself, in which blemishes do not disqualify, nor from the *sa’ir hamishtaleach*.

In the responsa of Rabbi Zemba, he also notes the commentary of Rabbeinu Gershom Meor HaGolah on Chullin 11b, where it is written regarding *eglah arufah* that since "atonement" is written regarding it, as with *kodshim*, it must not have a blemish, like *kodshim*—which is the opposite of the aforementioned Mishnah. See there in the sefer *Mikdashei Hashem* for his forced resolution of this matter.

With blessings.

#### ***4460***

\*) "Regarding the wonderment concerning the Rash... that he wanted to say that a blemish disqualifies an *eglah*... on the contrary, it is not mentioned there as such, but rather as a way of proving that one cannot say the reason of 'atonement like *kodshim*' is to exclude an animal missing a limb, for if so, even all blemishes would also be [excluded]. And in truth, we hold that blemishes do not disqualify even in a *chatas ha’of* and *olas ha’of*."

## 4461

B"H, 18 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished R' Zev Wolf, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the conclusion of Shabbos Parshas Naso, enclosing the lists of anash to be mentioned at the holy gravesite of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, may his merit protect us: the donation from each person may be placed into the fund of the Reshet Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established. For it is known that this work is a heavenly task, and "you shall teach them to your children" is one of the foundations for the existence of the Children of Israel. As our Sages of blessed memory have said, "There are he-goats only if there are kids." May it be the will of Hashem that we soon merit the fulfillment of the prophecy, "The small one shall become a thousand, and the youngest a mighty nation."

With blessings.

P.S.
Surely you are taking part in the shiurim being studied in public, in addition to the shiurim you have for yourself.

#### ***4461***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 26, p. 346, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Zev Wolf, may he live:** Zalmanov, Tel Aviv.

## 4462

4462

B"H, 19 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter, in which you write about setting the wedding date for your daughter, tichyeh, in a good and auspicious hour, in Yerushalayim, the Holy City, may it be rebuilt and established, and that you and your wife intend to be present at the wedding.

It is surely unnecessary to draw your attention to the fact that when one travels to Eretz Yisrael—of which the Holy Torah says, "The eyes of Hashem your G-d are always upon it, from the beginning of the year to the end of the year," and which even non-Jews call the Holy Land—one must obtain a "visa," not only here from people, but **primarily** from the King, for Eretz Yisrael is called His palace. This visa is obtained through an increase in Yiddishkeit, in the study of Torah and the performance of mitzvos with hidur. It is self-understood that this increase should begin as soon as possible, for "we do not know what may be," and every day that passes is an irreversible loss. In truth, an increase in Torah and mitzvos is necessary even when one is not traveling, and it is indeed required, as it is written, "Whoever adds, they add to him." In your case, in particular, this increase is necessary. Surely, with a little reflection, you will also find areas in which your wife, tichyeh, can add to her own Jewish affairs, and also by influencing her surroundings to bring them even closer to Yiddishkeit. May Hashem Yisbarach grant you success.

I reiterate my blessings that the wedding should be in a good and auspicious hour, and that they may build a home in Yisrael, an eternal edifice, upon the foundations of Torah and mitzvos.

With blessings of Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov.

P.S.
In any event, before you travel, you should have your tefillin checked, as well as the mezuzos in your home, to ensure they are kosher according to the law.

## 4463

B"H, 19 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the honorable Dr. Chaim Beinart,

Peace and blessing!

Your letter dated 9 Iyar was received a short while ago, and in due time, through Dr. Shlomo Eidelberg of New York, I received the offprint of your work, "The Trials of the Inquisition in Toledo, etc." Thank you for it. I trust you will also send your other publications to the library under my auspices, as the library serves the public good; I thank you in advance.

Since you are of Chabad lineage and a descendant of the elders of Anash, our Sages have said, "Throw a stick into the air, and it will land on its root." My hope is that this is indeed the case for you in practice—not only during the auspicious days of Shabbos and Yom Tov and the like, but also in your day-to-day life—and that you utilize your influence on others in this direction. For the matter of being "thrown" has been fulfilled in our time for each and every one of us—brands plucked from the fire—which is why the responsibility is many times greater: the responsibility of all those whom Divine Providence has left as a remnant, which is to keep a great nation alive.

This is especially true since your profession is teaching and influencing the youth. All those whom Divine Providence has placed in the field of education, in whatever subject it may be, must influence their students to fulfill their inner and primary purpose: living according to the path of our Torah, the Torah of life, and its mitzvos.

It is well known that youth do not tolerate compromises. It is precisely when they are given the word of Hashem in its fullness and purity that one gains their hearts—unlike the mistaken view of those who think that by being lenient and compromising, etc., they will gain their trust. Further elaboration on this is unnecessary.

Through traditional, uncompromising education, one prepares the student for a truly happy life—that is to say, happiness that is both physical and spiritual combined.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

#### ***4463***

**In due time, I received:** See letter 4283 above.

**Do not tolerate compromises:** See also letter 3889 in Vol. 12 above, and the references in the notes there. See below 4504, 4510.

## 4464

4464

B"H, 19 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 11 Sivan, in which you write regarding a visit together with your children (may they live) to Eretz Yisrael (may it be built and established). It is somewhat surprising, as it is my impression that the medical opinion is that until a baby is a full year old, one should not travel with them outside the country or to a place where there is a change in climate, water, etc. This is especially true since the city where you are currently located, according to reports, has a good and comfortable climate without major fluctuations from week to week or even from season to season—which is not always the case in... and especially during the autumn and winter seasons.

You have surely heard of the response of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, to someone who consulted with him regarding his lumber business. After receiving a response from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, which concluded with a suggestion regarding the performance of practical mitzvos with hiddur, etc., the inquirer began to make excuses, as he did not want to turn away my father-in-law, the Rebbe, empty-handed. My father-in-law, the Rebbe, said to him: "Look, this is a wonder. Surely, even in your opinion, I am a greater expert in matters of Yiddishkeit, Torah, and mitzvos than in the lumber business. Especially since it is known to all that I have dedicated many of my life's years to matters of Torah and mitzvos and the Jewish way of life, and this is what my fathers and forefathers occupied themselves with throughout their entire lives. Yet, it is simple for you to ask me regarding the lumber business, and you immediately accepted my advice with the intention of putting it into practice. But precisely regarding that matter in which I am, in your opinion, a great expert—an expert the son of an expert—not only did you not ask me in this field, but even after I gave you a clear instruction and explained that this is your true benefit, not only in spirituality but also in physicality, it is simple for you to reject it, and you only look for polite expressions to evade the entire matter."

The lesson from all of the above is surely understood, and there is no need to elaborate on it...

With blessings for good news regarding all of the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

## 4465

B"H, 19 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
R' Saadya, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 14 Sivan... regarding what you wrote, that Lag B'Omer and Shavuos passed in a state of isolation: this is puzzling. For I have many times emphasized what is found in the words of our holy Nesi'im—may the merits of the righteous protect us—and in many of their sichos, that each and every one of us, in his own place, must view himself as a shaliach. This applies not only regarding a specific mitzvah or a specific time, and not even only regarding matters of Toras HaChassidus and its guidance, but rather, in all matters of Yiddishkeit where one can exert influence—he is the one effectively in charge—to do so in ways of pleasantness and peace, in that specific place and at that specific time. And *l'fum gamla shichna* (the burden is commensurate with the camel).

Since you have been in Sefrou for several months and years, is it truly possible to say that among all the residents, and certainly among those serving in holy capacities, there are none capable of receiving *a chassidishen vort* (a Chassidic word), and even more than a mere saying—*chassidishe hadracha* (Chassidic guidance)? Surely, further elaboration on this is unnecessary.

With blessings to hear good news regarding all the above... and with blessings for success in your holy work,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***4465***

**R' Saadya:** Librov, Sefrou. Additional letters to him—above 4328, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4456

B"H, 20 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

The Administration of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in the Holy City of Jerusalem,
May it be built and established by Moshiach Tzidkeinu, speedily in our days,
May they live.

Greetings and Blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 28 Iyar, along with its enclosure, which was somewhat delayed in transit. In it, you write of your good activities in the past weeks. I look forward to receiving further letters from you with good tidings regarding the weeks that have since passed, and especially regarding the utilization of the time of the Giving of our Torah for spreading the wellsprings outward in all directions.

My hope is strong that you meet from time to time with the elders of Anash and consult with them, as is known and as the instruction of our Sages is explicit regarding the benefit of this. May Hashem grant you success in increasing your activities with true peace of mind, and surely you will be repaid many times over in the personal matters of each and every one of you.

With blessings for outstanding success and for good tidings.

## 4467

4467

B"H, 20 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, etc., R' Dovid, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 8 Sivan: May it be the will of Hashem that you bring good news, in a way that is good and revealed, regarding your personal matters, as well as in your avodah of strengthening Yiddishkeit and spreading it, and especially in spreading the wellsprings—and it has already been explained in many places that one is dependent upon the other.

Surely my response to those involved with the school and seminary for girls in Kfar Chabad has already been received. It is certainly unnecessary to remind **all anash** that it is the right and duty of **each and every one** to assist in the development of this institution, alongside the other institutions in Kfar Chabad. Likewise, I hope it is unnecessary to remind you of the absolute necessity of developing the framework of N'shei U'Bnos Chabad in Kfar Chabad. Fortunate is the portion of all those who strive and act in this, both materially and spiritually. May you bring good news, with the necessary details regarding all that has been mentioned.

With blessing, and thank you for conveying the above to each and every one.

#### ***4467***

**R' Dovid:** Bravman, Kfar Chabad. Additional letters to him appear above, 4405, and in the notes indicated there.

## 4468

4468

By the Grace of G-d
20 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent in the study of Chassidus,
R' Alexander Zissel, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings,

Having heard much about you and your activities in general, and in the field of education in particular, for a long time, I was pleased to receive your letter, which outlines your past experiences and your current situation.

While I was surprised and even somewhat saddened by its content—and especially by its spirit, which is permeated with sadness—I am writing to you because I am also puzzled by it. My reasoning for this is that since Divine Providence has placed you in a "ray of light," granting you the opportunity to work in the kosher education of Jewish children, this is clear proof that you have been given the strength to fulfill this most exalted task. For the Holy One, Blessed be He, does not deal tyrannically with His creatures; therefore, the matter depends solely on your will. If you truly and firmly desire it, you will certainly overcome the obstacles and hindrances, as well as the concealments and obscurations.

Even if we were to say that your sadness is rooted in the fact that, for the time being, you have not succeeded in improving the aforementioned education as much as is required, beyond what is explained in Tanya and in many other places—that in any case, sadness is rejected and is nothing but harmful—it is well known what our holy Nasi taught: "One action is worth more than a thousand sighs." This is especially true because it is human nature that when a person sighs, he feels a certain satisfaction in the fact that he is distressed by the matter, yet this does not add any energy to the actions needed to rectify the deficiency.

It is understood that in all of this, I am not speaking for the sake of discourse, but to express my clear and absolute opinion: it is a very great privilege that your activities are in the field of education, for not every person merits this, especially in our current era. In all matters of Torah and mitzvos, the ability and strength are given from Above, and we are also commanded, "Choose life." But ultimately, it is the **person** who must be the one to choose of his own good will and free choice. However, for all those who have been within the walls of the Yeshiva Tomchei Temimim, it is said: "A Chassidishe shtikel broit—a piece of bread—does not go to waste, G-d forbid." They are given an added measure of arousal, as well as a sense of dissatisfaction with a lack of activity, and they are encouraged and strengthened from time to time through hints, and sometimes even through direct instruction. Surely, when you reflect on what has happened to you over the recent period, you will find several of these instructions and hints. My hope is that, at the very least, they have already led to tangible actions, and that you will continue to add to them, going from strength to strength.

You are surely aware of the content of my telegrams and letters to the residents of Kfar Chabad in the Holy Land—may it be rebuilt speedily by our righteous Moshiach—and these words of mine are directed to all Anash, and generally to all those who tremble at the word of G-d. It goes without saying that this applies to the Temimim and to those for whom this is their profession. My hope is strong that you will assist as much as you are able, and even more, in supporting Kfar Chabad and its institutions, and in their development, both in quantity and quality. I would be pleased to receive good news from you regarding this as well.

The conclusion of all that has been said regarding you and the end of your letter is that, as mentioned above, you are in a "ray of light," for you are able to ignite the souls of Israel—"The candle of G-d is the soul of man"—of many Jewish children with the light and warmth of Chassidus. It goes without saying that your comment about troubling me with the reading of your letter is the opposite; I am certain that in your future letters, the sadness will decrease more and more, and the joy will increase—specifically the joy of a mitzvah, the joy of bringing the hearts of Jewish children closer to our Father in Heaven.

With blessings for good news in all of the above, soon.

## 4469

4469

B"H, 20 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings,

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from 12 Sivan. It is puzzling that there are several among anash who, despite the fact that I have written and also stated orally several times that in these countries, simplicity is far better—and to speak in the mother tongue, inventions and "twisting" things are not always an advantage for achieving results, nor do they always help with parnassah—yet from time to time, they revert to what they were accustomed to in our former country.

Even though habit becomes second nature, as stated in Tanya, a Chossid should not be affected by this either, and at the very least, should try to change that nature. Sometimes, when one begins to do so, one sees that the matter is not so difficult, and it also becomes tidier and freer in the head and in the pocket. May it be the will of the Creator of the world and its Guide to fulfill your heart’s desires for the good, with an expansion of parnassah, so that you may be able to increase your study of the revealed Torah and Chassidus, with proper health and parnassah.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4470

4470

B”H, 21 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, who engages in communal needs,
Rabbi Sh. Y. [shiy]

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos: Thank you for what you discussed regarding the travel expenses for the bochurim of our yeshiva to Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, for a period of several weeks. It seems I did not explain my words sufficiently. For although it is certain that the room and board expenses in Eretz Yisrael are, at times, equal to the travel expenses—and I believe I wrote this as well in my letter—this applies only if the aforementioned bochurim were to travel through outside parties and stay in the homes of strangers, meaning they would have to pay for their room and board, as is the case with students from other yeshivos who travel to Eretz Yisrael through the department of the Agency that handles this.

This is not the case in our situation. If I were to have the travel expenses covered, I have a strong hope that I would be able to arrange the room and board matters in Kfar Chabad, at the Tomchei Temimim yeshivos in Lod, and so on, in such a way that the expenses would be absolutely minimal. This is not the case with the travel expenses, where I do not have this ability. [Regarding what they told you—that the tickets must be paid for through the Agency specifically in foreign currency—this is also puzzling. For although it is understood that they will not travel on ships, etc., since they travel on Shabbos, they can arrange the travel via El Al airlines. And even though it is my plan that they visit the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in France and the like during their journey, this is also easy to arrange, since El Al passes through London. They can break their journey there from one El Al plane to another, and in the meantime travel from London to France and back at their own expense. And this is simple.]

Certainly, if you have the opportunity to bring the aforementioned question up for discussion once again, please do so and exert yourself in this matter. Especially, as I wrote in my previous letter, because I see in this the greatest encouragement for the people of the Kfar and for its development...

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned.

## 4741

B"H, 21 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter dated the day that was doubled in goodness. After all the lengthiness, I do not understand why it is necessary to conduct matters in a disorderly fashion, at a time when it is possible to do things in an orderly manner.

My intention is that it is the custom throughout the world that when one wishes to inform someone regarding a matter pertaining to the administration of an institution, a school, or the like, one does not rely on the fact that one happened to meet someone from the administration and, amidst all the conversation, "toss in a few words" regarding this matter. Rather, one enters the office specifically for this purpose and states that one wishes to do such-and-such. It is already a known instruction of our Torah that anything said outside of a Beis Din is of no consequence, and the same applies in a case such as this [...] even if we were to say that the office was located at a distance, and all the more so when one passes by its door every single day.

Regarding what you wrote about a "second-class" [status], even if we were to say that the situation is as you describe in your letter, one cannot justify the lack of order on the part of one person by the wrongdoing committed by another—especially since, in the final analysis, it is the students who suffer, and they are certainly not to blame for the lack of instruction they receive.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you of the verse, "As in water, face answers to face, so is the heart of man to man" (*Mishlei 27:19*). From there it is proven, and the plain meaning of the verse is, that "as in water, face answers to face" does not mean that if the other person looks with an evil eye, one must respond in kind; rather, it is precisely the opposite. Furthermore, even in a place of doubt, this is how one’s conduct must be, as is concluded in *Iggeres HaKodesh*, Epistle 32, "and perhaps this will be enough, etc."

With blessings for a true drawing together of hearts and for contemplation of this, in order to bring it into practice with a whole heart.

## 4712

21 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, engaged in communal work, R’ Y. Steinfeld,

Peace and blessing!

The content of your letter was conveyed to me, in which you express surprise that in my conversation with a certain fellow Jew—who is not currently among those who are meticulous in their observance of Hashem’s word—I spoke to him about the concept of *gilgul neshamot* (reincarnation).

I am surprised by your surprise, for the subject of *gilgul neshamot* has already been discussed in printed books in many places and at great length. Its foundation lies with the *Rishonim*, who studied this matter and even published it in their works, to the point that it has moved beyond the category of the inner, hidden dimensions of Torah and is discussed even in the *nigleh* (revealed) aspects of Torah. This is especially true given that only through the concept of *gilgul neshamot* is it possible to resolve the immense difficulties raised by our fellow Jews in our current times—particularly by the young generation of Jews who contemplate what has befallen the Jewish people in recent years. This has had a negative impact on them regarding their faith in *hashgacha pratis* (Divine Providence) and the governance of the world by the Creator of the world, which in turn weakens their actual observance of *mitzvos*, and so on. This is well-known to anyone who comes into contact with the younger generation and youth in the United States in particular.

Whenever it is possible to find a channel and a way to publicize the matters of our holy Torah—the Torah of life—to the masses, it is one’s obligation to do so to the fullest extent possible. Furthermore, regarding the printing of Torah thoughts in newspapers: while a few generations ago people were cautious about this due to the great concern that they would not be treated with the proper respect and might be discarded in the streets, the great Jewish leaders of the generation before ours began to do so. This is well-known from the practice of the *chareidi* newspapers in Poland and in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, and similarly, many Torah topics were printed in American newspapers by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzvkllh"h, nbg"m, zy"a*, the *Nasi* of the Jewish people—all for the sake of reaching even one or two Jews, to set them on the path of truth and in a "corner of light."

If only all those who are meticulous in their observance of Hashem’s word would go out to the "outer" streets and teach Torah to the Jewish people, without making it a prerequisite that they first become observant of *mitzvos* and only then teach them Torah. By teaching in this manner of "scattering," they would save **many** precious souls of the Jewish people for Hashem and His Torah, and with great haste.

Even though the *Zohar* states, and it is explained in many places in *Chassidus*—including in the holy *Tanya*—that "Torah without *dichilu* and *rechimu* (awe and love) does not ascend on high," we see tangibly that the aforementioned approach—namely, to inform every Jew who comes to ask about the matters of our Torah, the Torah of life, with clear explanations and through *kiruv*—does, in the end, bring the listeners closer (though, naturally, not all listeners to the same degree). See also the *Hilchos Talmud Torah* of our *Alter Rebbe*—the author of the *Tanya* and *Shulchan Aruch*—Chapter 4, Section 3, in the *Kuntres Acharon*, which states: "And also for the Rabbi, it can be said that he must bring in..." and so on; see there. This is especially true regarding Torah concepts that are well-known, but are not understood correctly, and consequently are interpreted improperly, leading to conclusions that are the opposite of the truth.

I sense in your letter that the aforementioned surprise is shared by several of your friends. Therefore, I request that you convey the content of this letter to them as well. The main point is to awaken them and explain to them that:

Many of our fellow Jews are drowning in the vast sea of ignorance, and this is the sole reason that many of them are not careful in the actual observance of *mitzvos*. **Anyone** who has the ability to do something about this, yet hides within his own "four cubits" and explains to himself that he is the righteous one and they are [the ones at fault], has already had his situation clarified in the holy *Zohar* in the well-known places regarding the difference between the conduct of Moshe Rabbeinu **after** the giving of the Torah and the conduct of Noach, who lived **before** the giving of the Torah. In truth, we do not even need the explanation of the holy *Zohar* for this; it is a *kal v'chomer* (an *a fortiori* argument) from the saving of a physical life. If someone were walking along a riverbank and saw a fellow Jew drowning in the river, he would certainly interrupt his sleep and do everything in his power to save him from drowning, and he would not ask him if he puts on *tefillin* every day, etc. If this is the case for saving a physical life, how much more so for saving a soul!

Furthermore, even if you were to argue that it is doubtful whether the listener will change his ways when taught Torah, who is the person who can pass judgment on a soul, deciding that someone will not do *teshuvah*, Heaven forbid? In any case, there is a doubt, and in any case, there is a *sfeik sfeika* (a double doubt). The ruling of the **Shulchan Aruch** is that matters of *pikuach nefesh* (saving a life) override everything—even based on a doubt, or even several doubts. And what is stated here is written in the **same Shulchan Aruch** in which it is also written about the laws of putting on *tefillin*, etc.

May it be Hashem’s will that you find the words necessary to explain all of this to your friends, until they change their current conduct and fulfill the teaching of our Sages: "At a time of gathering, scatter"—to spread the wellsprings of the words of the Living G-d until they reach even the "outside."

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned,

**M. Schneerson**

## 473

473

B"H, 22 Sivan, 5716

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what seems to be your surprise in your letter that I did not write immediately following the event: this is a *kal v’chomer* (an argument from minor to major) from Aharon the High Priest, of whom the verse testifies, "And Aharon was silent"—how much more so for people of our stature...

With blessings for success and for good tidings in all that was mentioned.

#### ***473***

**Regarding the event:** In Kfar Chabad, which occurred on Erev Rosh Chodesh Iyar, mentioned above in letter 481, and in the notes referenced there.

See also letter 506 below.

## 4714

B”H, 22 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of Sunday, in which you write that there is a certain *avreich* whose soul desires Chassidus, but his parents are not pleased with this and sometimes even interfere, and you ask what he should do.

It is surprising that there is even a doubt about this, as it is a settled *halacha*, and it is ruled in *Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah*, section 240, subsection 25. Even if the reason for the prevention were a matter of *pikuach nefesh*, and the father is not preventing the study in general but only in a specific place, it is stated in the *halachos* of **kavod** av that one is not required to listen in such a case—see there. How much more so is this the case when they are preventing the study itself; it is obvious that this does not fall under the types of reasons mentioned there.

Regarding what you wrote about how to go about this—whether to do so with a "high hand," etc.—it is not understood what the benefit is of a "high hand," for the study of Torah is not a matter of victory, *chas v’shalom*. This is especially true of the light within Torah, which is called the *ilana d’chayei*, where there is nothing but *sitra d’kedusha*. This is discussed at length in *Ra’aya Mehemna*, *Parshas Naso*, and is explained in *Iggeres HaKodesh* by the Alter Rebbe, section 26. Regarding the arrangement of the aforementioned person’s studies and their location, he should consult with the elders of *anash* in the Holy City of Yerushalayim, *tizkeh lishnah v’tivneh*, to whom he should explain the entire situation in detail.

Regarding what you ask concerning yourself—whether you should continue your studies where you are now or move to the *Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim* in Lod—since this also depends on details and minute particulars, you should detail them before the elders of *anash* as mentioned above, and they will instruct you.

Surely he and his friend are aware of the three daily *shiurim* for everyone, established by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nivg"m, zya"a*, in *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*, which are well-known. They should keep them, or at the very least, they should keep them from this point forward. May *Hashem* grant them success.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, *shlita*,

Secretary

## 4775

B"H, 22 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, in which you write that your neighbors sometimes bother you with their talk, and you ask what to do about this:

It is obvious that you should pay no attention to this at all. Be strong in the faith of all Yisrael—and each one of us in particular—that the Creator of the world and its Guide oversees every individual with *hashgacha pratis*. Since the Creator is the essence of good, everything that the Merciful One does, He does for the good. And what can a person do to you when the Creator of the world stands over you and protects you?

You should maintain your *shiurim* in the study of *nigleh* and in the study of *Chassidus*, and each weekday before morning *davening*, set aside a few coins for *tzedakah*. May the *Ribono shel Olam* grant you success and bring you good tidings regarding all that was mentioned.

With blessings.

## 4716

4716

B"H, 22 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' Boruch, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

After a long interval, I was pleased to receive your letter of Monday, in which you write about the continuation of the matter regarding the shul in your area. Most importantly, you have ensured that in the coming month of Tishrei, matters there will be in order. We must hope that this will be the case for the future as well, and I want to be certain that you will not slacken in your efforts going forward. It is well known, as our Sages have said, that "one who comes to purify himself is assisted." May it be the will of the Creator and Guide of the world that your visit to the convention also brings about positive results, not only regarding the question of the shul, but in matters of Yiddishkeit in general.

You do not mention your family or yourself, so I assume that all is well and in peace.

With blessings, in the style of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, for a healthy and happy summer.

#### ***4716***

**R' Boruch:** Litvin, of Detroit. Further letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, p. 85, and in the notes referenced there. See also below, p. 575.

**The continuation of the matter:** Regarding the *mechitza* in the shul; see above, ibid. The legal proceedings continued until 5719, at which point the Supreme Court of Michigan ruled that the *mechitza* must remain in the shul. In connection with this, he published a large book titled *Kedushas Beis HaKnesses*—a collection of responsa regarding the obligation of a *mechitza* in a shul, in which the court case is recounted.

Regarding the matter of a *mechitza* in a shul, see also above, p. 435, and the notes referenced there.

## 4727

4727

B"H, 22 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 18 Sivan, in which you write regarding the attitude of [Name] which is not as it should be, and therefore leads to undesirable results.

Now, for a yeshiva bochur of your age—and especially one who has been raised in a Chassidic home, and in the home of a *tamim*—it is certainly superfluous to point out that the study of Torah and the performance of *mitzvos* must be done because this is the command of the *Ribono shel Olam*. One is not doing a favor for anyone else by doing so; rather, one’s own benefit in this world and the next—the good of the soul and the good of the body—depends upon it. From this, it is also understood that there is no place to say that since someone else’s attitude toward me is not as it should be, I must therefore cause myself that which is contrary to my own good. Since this concerns you in truth, one should endeavor, through ways of pleasantness and ways of peace, to ensure that all matters are arranged in the best possible manner. If it seems that [Name] is not satisfied with this, I will see to it that I maintain good relations with him until, eventually, this will succeed, as it is written: "As water reflects a face to a face, so is the heart of man to man."

Regarding the practical application, in your case as well, it is in addition to the fact that at your age the primary focus is not the *chavrusa*, but rather **your** *hasmadah* and *shekidah*. If you do that, you will not need to look for a *chavrusa*; rather, the *chavrusas* will come after you to be with you in study. Even if it takes time until you reach that state, and it is uncomfortable and difficult to learn without a *chavrusa*, one must nevertheless do so with energy, and be confident that this is for a short time, relatively speaking.

This is all said assuming that what you write is one hundred percent accurate. However, since a person is biased toward himself, one must assume that it could be otherwise, as it is written: "Love (self-love) covers all transgressions." Therefore, you should once again reflect upon the matters that depend on you. It may be that if there is a change for the better in those areas, the matter about which you write will, in the natural course of events, be rectified—at least to a significant degree.

Surely you are keeping the three *shiurim* that are equal for every soul, as established by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*—and that every weekday morning before *davening*, you give a few cents to *tzedakah*. May the *Ribono shel Olam* grant you success.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4778

B"H, 24 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 18 Sivan, with the good news that you are distributing Chabad books to the extent possible: Even though you do not provide details, in light of the demands of the hour—especially as we approach the end of our exile—and given the well-known response of Melech HaMoshiach regarding "when your wellsprings spread outward," of which the distribution of Chassidic texts is a primary component, it is understood that one must exert effort and increase this activity many times over. It is a teaching of our Sages that "one only encourages the already diligent," meaning that if until now you have distributed to the extent possible, you must now engage in increasing that measure. This is accomplished through personal advocacy and by involving others in the necessity of studying the teachings of Chassidus, etc. This advocacy itself is also essential and vital. Since this duty has been placed upon each and every one of our generation who recognizes their obligation and has merited to be enlightened by the "light of the Torah"—which is the Torah of Chassidus—the strength has certainly been granted to them to fulfill this command and obligation. May Hashem grant you success so that it may be without obstacles, and in a great measure that continues to grow even further.

As you requested, I will mention you at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

With blessings for success in the above.

#### 4778

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 20, p. 613.

## 479

B"H, 25 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 18th of the third month, in which you ask what your wife, may she live, should do:

This has already been addressed in our holy Torah, the Torah of life, specifically regarding the three primary mitzvos—the "Ch-N" (Challah, Niddah, Hadlakas Haneiros)—that were given to women, and in all the mitzvos they are obligated to fulfill, as detailed in the Shulchan Aruch of our Alter Rebbe, Hilchos Talmud Torah, end of Chapter 1. It is already known what our Sages said regarding the verse, "The entire glory of the king's daughter is within," that through conduct of tzniyus and covering the hair, one merits children, etc.

Regarding what you wrote about your future arrangements: it is understood that anyone who can influence the strengthening of Yiddishkeit and its dissemination in [location], and especially among the residents of [location], must do so with all their ability. Certainly, as with all other mitzvos of the Torah—and especially in the saving of Jewish souls for the life of the World to Come—those who engage in this are granted an increase in the blessings of Hashem, and success in "children, life, and ample sustenance." Based on this, it is simple that you and your wife, may they live, since Hashem has graced you with the talent to educate and to act with all your energy in this field among your brethren in [location], should do so with the greatest possible vigor.

It is surprising that you do not mention your study sessions in the teachings of Chassidus, in addition to your sessions in the study of the nigleh, and in addition to the three daily study sessions for every person established by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the well-known Chitas (Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya).

With blessings to hear good news regarding all of the above.

## 480

480

B”H, 25 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn

Agudas N’shei U’Bnos Chabad, London Branch
May Hashem grant you life.

Blessings and peace!

After a long interval, I was pleased to receive your letter of 20 Sivan, although I am surprised that it was written so briefly, and also that it contained no details regarding how Lag B’Omer and the Yom Tov of Shavuos were utilized, etc. Most importantly, [I am surprised there was no mention of] plans for how to utilize the summer months, when the children are free from other studies and the positive influence they receive at that time is not hindered by other matters. [I also expected to hear] about other suitable plans for work during the summer months for those who find themselves at summer resorts with people who have arrived from many different places, where a positive influence upon them—in the sense of strengthening Yiddishkeit, which is the goal of Agudas N’shei U’Bnos Chabad—can have a good effect in many places at once.

At the same time, Hashem Yisbarach will surely bless all those involved in the work of Agudas N’shei U’Bnos Chabad in general, and in the above-mentioned [activities] in particular, with a good summer, strength in health, and success in their work.

With blessings for good tidings in all that has been mentioned,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4781

4781

B.H., 25 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Agudas N'shei U’Bnos Chabad in France
May Hashem grant them life.

Blessings and peace!

After a long interval, I was pleased to receive your letter of 15 Sivan, in which you write about your work in general and, in particular, about the organization of the Lag B’Omer celebration. May Hashem help you to increase your work, both in quantity and in quality—especially during the coming summer period, when the children are free from other studies and the positive influence they can receive will not be hindered by extraneous matters.

Furthermore, through good and appropriate preparation, you should utilize the fact that while on vacation one meets people from many different places. When you exert a positive influence upon them during the few weeks you are together on vacation, that good effect reaches their permanent places of residence. This spreads Yiddishkeit—which is the goal of Agudas N'shei U’Bnos Chabad—in many cities simultaneously. I hope that you will consult regarding the above and proceed with this work in an appropriate manner.

And surely, Hashem will bless all those involved in the work of Agudas N'shei U’Bnos Chabad in general, and in the aforementioned in particular, with a good summer, strength in health, and success in their work specifically.

With blessings for good tidings in all that has been mentioned.

## 4482

B"H, 25 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what I said to him about at least some measure of kabalas ol: if he had accustomed himself to at least a little kabalas ol, he would have had **doubt** in his own judgment, and even regarding his own decisions, he would have considered that perhaps they were not correct. He would have realized that what seemed otherwise to him was due to the teaching of Chazal that a person is biased toward himself, etc. Consequently, before taking a sharp step or a step affecting general matters, he would have consulted with someone else who is not involved in the matter.

As I am accustomed, I must immediately point out and caution that I do not mean to say that in such-and-such a matter he is one hundred percent to blame and so-and-so is one hundred percent in the right. I am not judging in that manner at all in this letter; rather, I am speaking about the general conduct. In other words, when one studies these matters in the seforim of Chassidus and in the sichos kodesh, one accepts them well, but when it comes to practical application, one immediately decides—with absolute certainty—that everything stated in those seforim applies only to others.

As an example, I will suffice with just one point: if what is written in the first lines of the beginning of Chapter 41 of Tanya (page 56 to the end of column a) were engraved in his mind—and in truth, the last line and a half of that page is sufficient: "And behold, Hashem stands over him and looks at him and examines his kidneys and heart"—I am certain that all the harsh words... would not even have crossed his mind to speak, and it goes without saying that they would not have been expressed, even in much less harsh terms...

With blessings.

## 4783

B"H, 27 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc.,
R' Yechiel Michel Yehuda Leib, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 17th of Sivan, and may it be the will of Hashem that you soon bring good news regarding the continuation of the activities you write about in your letter—specifically, the continuation regarding putting them into practice. May it be in a manner of consistency; and through there being consistency in time and consistency in the soul of the organizers, this consistency will be established and will also draw in those who, for the time being, say to themselves that they are not yet part of anash.

You will surely take advantage as much as possible of your presence in a new place and influence those you are with as much as possible and as is necessary, in such a way that even after your return from there, in a good and auspicious hour, the connection will continue. This will serve as a conduit for instilling Torah and mitzvos, illuminated by the light and warmth of the teachings of Chassidus. May it be with immense success, and may your influence act upon those you influence to the point of "moistening in order to moisten" [i.e., inspiring them to inspire others]. This is in line with the well-known saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, that perfection is found in the birth of a grandchild; from which it is understood that it is the same regarding "your children," which refers to the students—that the perfection of the mashpia is when there is a student to his student. And to note from the Tosefta, Eduyos at the end, brought in the Iggeres of Rav Sherira Gaon, copied in the Aruch, entry "Abaye," and Sefer Yuchasin, that when there is a student to one's student, he is called "Rabban."

With blessing.

***

4783

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 23, p. 471, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

R' Yechiel Michel Yehuda Leib, may he live: Zalmanov, Bnei Brak. Additional letters to him—above, 4282, and in the references noted there.

Regarding the saying: See also below, letter 4544.

## 4,484

B”H, 28 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, involved in communal needs, R’ Nissan, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I acknowledge receipt of your letters from the 15th and 22nd of Sivan, regarding what you wrote about sending papers for two more students. One must first estimate the benefit to others, and it goes without saying, the benefit to the travelers themselves in coming here. Just as... who visited here during the holiday of Shavuos—I am interested to know if he reviewed *da”h* (divrei Elokus/Chassidus) upon his return and organized *farbrengens*, etc., and in simple terms: did what he gained during his visit justify the expenses? It is understood that based on this, one must be selective regarding the nature of those traveling here and also set conditions with them from the outset. Although it is also understood that one cannot demand the same from everyone, as their minds are not equal—in the language of Rabbeinu HaZaken, that one person’s intellect is not affected and aroused by what affects and arouses the intellect of his fellow—nevertheless, the common denominator for all must be that there is a benefit to themselves and a benefit to others. I will await news from you regarding this.

Regarding his papers, they have been submitted to the office.

With blessings for a healthy and successful summer, and for proper preparations for the Day of Redemption, 12th and 13th of Tammuz, may it come upon us for good.

#### ***4,484***

**R’ Nissan:** Nemnov, Paris. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 12, 4,383, and where noted in the footnotes there.

Below, 4,585.

**Set conditions with them:** See also below, letters 4,585, 4,628, 4,646, 4,683.

## 4785

4785

B"H, 28 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and pious individual, who is occupied with communal needs and conducts his business with integrity,
R' Yaakov, may he live, Hakohen

Greetings and blessings!

I was pleased to hear that you will see to it to participate and involve yourself in the arrangements for the farbrengen on Shabbos Rosh Chodesh Tammuz, Parshas Korach, in the Anshei Lubavitch shul. You will surely convey my greetings to all the participants of the farbrengen, where words of Torah and inspiration in Torah and mitzvos will certainly be shared. May it be with Siyata Dishmaya and great success, in accordance with the well-known saying we heard from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in the name of the Alter Rebbe: that which a Chassidishe farbrengen can accomplish, even the angel Michael cannot accomplish. In particular, may this find expression in concrete actions to the fullest extent possible to strengthen and expand the Anshei Lubavitch shul in all matters connected to it.

Regarding the theme of the day from the parsha we read this Shabbos: the parsha is dedicated to the Kohanim, both at its beginning and at its conclusion.

In a broader sense, all Jews are called by the name "Kohanim."

This was their title as a preparation for Matan Torah, as it is written: "And you shall be to Me a kingdom of Kohanim and a holy nation."

How is this achieved? It is explained in the Rambam (end of Hilchos Shemitah V'Yovel): "Whoever is moved by his spirit and understands with his wisdom to set himself apart to stand before Hashem, to serve Him and to worship Him, to know Hashem... he is sanctified as holy of holies... and Hashem will grant him in this world that which is sufficient for him, just as He granted the Kohanim and the Leviim."

In other words, through Jews recognizing the purpose of man’s creation—which, according to the Mishnah, is "I was created to serve my Maker"—so that all things become Avodas Hashem ("In all your ways, know Him"), through this they are Kohanim.

And regarding the gifts of the Kehunah, there is a principle—as stated at the end of the parsha—that it must be *min hamuvchar* (from the choicest), and this is eternal, as it says: "The Terumah of Hashem from all its fat." The Ibn Ezra explains: "The most important part of it." And this is a "covenant of salt forever."

It is a promise from the Ribono shel Olam for all time: when Jews conduct themselves in the manner mentioned above, Hashem gives them *kol chelbo*—the very best—in every place and at every time. This is the eternal covenant of salt.

Just as the primary place for the Kohanim to serve and worship Him was in the Beis Hamikdash, so too in our time, these are the shuls and Batei Medrash, which are the *mikdash me'at* (a miniature sanctuary). From there, one takes the holiness and light to one’s own home, to sanctify and illuminate the mundane affairs of life.

May Hashem grant success so that this conduct serves as a near preparation to hasten the service in the Third Beis Hamikdash, which is also eternal, when the verse will be fulfilled: "And restore the service to the inner sanctum of Your House."

With blessings for a good Shabbos and a joyous month to all the participants, and to the Rav, the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and accomplished individual, R' Shlomo Zalman, may he live, at their head.

## 4786

4786

B.H., 29 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...I do not know if there will be any further benefit in my repeating my perspective on this once again, yet I will attempt to do so very briefly:

In our days, and especially in your situation, it is tangibly evident that your material livelihood and your spiritual livelihood—namely, the number of those you influence who participate in listening to shiurim, the increase in the number of those davening in the beis medrash, and the like—are interdependent.

2) Although I endeavor to also encourage the baalei batim of their beis medrash—whom I know—to engage in the development of the beis medrash, it is understood that they are baalei batim and not those who serve in holiness (*meshartei bakodesh*). From this, it is clear that their activities in this area are limited, which is not the case for one who serves in holiness, for whom this is his primary concern.

3) Furthermore, the general conduct of "having one's work done by others," as explained by our Sages, is a mode of conduct for the future era, and even then, it applies only to the plowing of fields, etc., and not to matters connected with Torah and mitzvos.

4) The custom among young rabbis is that in a situation where they need to develop a beis medrash, they do not rely on any baalei batim to do so. Even though they strive to utilize their influence and encourage them to do more and more, **they** are the ones who take the lead. Even in actions performed by the baalei batim, **they** are the ones standing behind the curtain, providing direction from time to time.

5) Regarding your congregation, it has happened—beyond the natural order—that some of the baalei batim are prepared to do more than one could have hoped for, and they are the ones who awaken and demand that you participate in the activities. This is the **complete** opposite of the conduct that should be. To clarify the situation and explain my opinion further, I reiterate what I have told you several times: it is of the greatest necessity that you visit the homes of the baalei batim, or at the very least, meet with them at regular intervals. A telephone conversation is insufficient, and all the more so when even that conversation does not happen, or occurs only very rarely. While it is understood that I am not at all comfortable with the American custom that the rabbi is a "social worker," it is also understood that one cannot wait for the strengthening and expansion of their beis medrash until this American custom changes. Therefore, you must visit and meet with the neighbors of the beis medrash to encourage them to come and participate in the shiurim in the beis medrash, etc., etc. And when can it be said that this will be successful? When the idea is **absorbed** by you that this is your true benefit, even more so than the benefit of the baalei batim. The Chassidic adage from the Alter Rebbe is already known, regarding what one learns in the ways of avodas Hashem from the conduct of a thief—as the Mishnah says, "Who is wise? He who learns from every person"—that even if he does not succeed the first time, he tries again and again.

May it be the will of Hashem that these few lines in quantity will suffice, and you will then inform me that you have **thrown yourself** into the work in all matters of the beis medrash—even those for which there is no immediate reward—and that you have thrown yourself into it with joy and a good heart, without any *marah shechorah*. May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings for good tidings,

#### ***4786***

**The adage:** See also above, Vol. 5, Letter 1229.

## 4787

4787

B"H, 29 Sivan, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 24 Sivan, I was pleased to read that you participated personally in the distribution of *matzah shemurah*. Although it is surprising that you conclude this point by noting that it was barely known that this came about through your initiative—for this is misplaced humility. Surely, had it been known that this activity belonged to you and that you were the catalyst, it would have led people to listen to your words and your advocacy regarding the study of the teachings of Chassidus and following its guidance, customs, and so on.

And although one does not cry over the past, I am writing this because, surely, from time to time, such matters arise. My opinion regarding conduct is as stated above. As is known from the statement of the *tzaddik*, the *gaon*, the *chossid* R. Hillel of Paritch, *z"l*, that everything he does is so that a Chassidic matter may be absorbed within him in a positive way. Likewise, in our generation—the *ikvesa d'meshicha* whose arrival depends on the spreading of the *ma'ayanos*—it is the call of the hour to utilize every opportunity and every matter for the benefit of the aforementioned dissemination, especially when one is located in a place where no one else is engaged in this.

Regarding what you wrote about studying bookkeeping: since you have already begun this and paid the tuition, you should continue and finish it, and may it be G-d’s will that it be without undue burden.

Regarding what you wrote about [Name] *shlita*, and that you are consulting with two expert doctors without either knowing about the other: in order to avoid confusion later on, it is proper that before you proceed, you arrange in a diplomatic manner, if possible, for the aforementioned to speak with each other regarding the treatment. It is understood that if this matter will cause anger or resentment from either of them, you should be content with presenting the opinions of both doctors to your family physician and consulting with him.

Regarding what you wrote about the *shiur* for the religious soldiers—that it depends on the desire of the participants, etc., and that the results of all the efforts are a small number—the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, is already known, precisely regarding the statement of our Sages that one must be **"engaged"** (*osek*) in Torah. This means it must be in the manner of a business (*esek*). A business owner does not sit in his innermost room waiting for someone to come and ask if he has merchandise suitable for him; on the contrary, he is the one who runs and searches for buyers, specifically in a place where many people pass by. Surely, for someone like you, there is no need to elaborate on the parable or the lesson, but only to emphasize the point that Divine Providence has given you the opportunity to light several candles—as it is written, "The candle of Hashem is the soul of man"—and to illuminate not only your own portion, but also their portion in the world with the light of Torah and the "light" within it, which is the inner dimension of the Torah. I wish that you would utilize this opportunity more and more. The magnitude of the spiritual satisfaction that will come from this in the future, the greatness of the reward, and, most importantly, the greatness of the *nachas ruach* caused to the Creator of the world and its Maker, cannot be sufficiently described. According to the interpretation of our Sages on the verse "to bring forth the precious from the vile," it is understood that the soldiers who are currently not religious are included in all of the above, and even more so.

From the above, my perspective is also understood regarding moving your residence from your current location: it is not advisable for several reasons. The matters you write that, in your opinion, argue against the apartment now—that there are no *anash* and no Chassidic *farbrengens*—are, in my opinion, reasons in favor of staying, as mentioned in the previous section. Since the measure of the Holy One, blessed be He, is "measure for measure," but many times over, surely through your activities in your current location, not only will you not be diminished in your spiritual matters, but on the contrary. And this is well understood.

Regarding what you wrote about an X-ray: it is surprising that in a state of pregnancy one speaks of such radiation, but you should consult about treatment via massage or the like.

Regarding whether you should continue your work in the field of kosher education: here, too, my perspective is understood based on the above—that you should continue in this, and on the contrary, you should gain further qualifications. The same applies to your wife when her situation allows, meaning after the appropriate time following the birth, in a good and auspicious hour. Regarding what you wrote that she is studying several subjects and her influence is being nullified by this, etc.: it is already known what is said regarding the Jewish people and all their affairs, that "you are the fewest of all the peoples," and yet, in the end, they succeed and are victorious. As our Sages say on the verse "You are standing today, all of you," that the nations of the world collapse, etc., while you live and endure forever. The same applies to matters of Torah and *mitzvos* in relation to matters belonging (also) to the nations of the world.

Surely your wife is in contact with the *N'shei uBnos Chabad* in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, and is cooperating with them as much as possible. It is my hope that you will write about this in detail. The very fact that she will know that by a certain date she must report here on her activities in this field will add initiative and reveal strengths and possibilities to increase these activities.

With blessings.

## 4,788

B"H, 1 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, engaged in communal needs, Moreinu HaRav Uriel, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 24 Sivan: I believe that in our conversation before you traveled, it was hinted that you would surely encounter difficulties in your efforts to disseminate the wellsprings—the objections raised by this person and that person. Nevertheless, one must engage in this campaign and dissemination, as they are the demand of the hour and cannot be deferred.

Regarding what you wrote about the Responsa *Mishpetei Uziel*: I already hinted at this in my previous letter, noting that it is specifically the Sephardim who are stringent (whereas the Ashkenazim are lenient; my intention was to refer to the Responsa *Minchas Elazar*). Regarding your statement that there are not even ten people who hear the natural voice: it is a known rule that there is no dispute regarding reality. When one does not speak directly into the mouthpiece, it is understood that the sound spreads in all directions and not only into the microphone. As for what you wrote regarding what is done during the *siddur kiddushin*—this is the first time I have heard that there is a microphone in the courtyard here before the *chuppah*, and that it is removed for the *siddur kiddushin*. It is obvious that this is not done by instruction, and in general, it is questionable whether a microphone is even needed in this courtyard for a *siddur kiddushin* that takes place in the middle of the courtyard.

It is surely superfluous to urge you once again regarding the campaign to disseminate the wellsprings. I have only come to emphasize that the prospects for the success of the campaign are greater while it is still a "new face," as opposed to when several weeks have passed, as is known regarding human nature.

With blessings for good tidings in all that was mentioned, as well as in the other matters you write about.

***

**4,788**

**Moreinu HaRav Uriel:** Zimmer, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him: see above 4,747, and the references in the notes there.

**Regarding what you wrote:** About praying in a congregation via a microphone. See above, letter 4,747.

**Responsa *Mishpetei Uziel*:** Vol. 1, Siman 5.

**Responsa *Minchas Elazar*:** Vol. 2, Siman 72.

**During the *siddur kiddushin*:** That I have seen a *maaseh rav* (an established practice by an authority), both in the courtyard of the Rebbe Shlita and in other places, that during the *chuppah*, the one officiating the *kiddushin* and the one reciting the *Sheva Brachos* removes the microphone.

## 4,489

B"H, 1 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' Yissachar Dov, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Tuesday (and the one preceding it), in which you write regarding the vacation time that all the teachers and the principal are taking at once, leaving the students without any supervision, etc.

In such a situation, it would be best for the teachers and the principal to discuss the matter among themselves so that they do not take their vacation time all at once, but rather in shifts. Since studies are reduced during vacation days and the primary concern is supervision, a smaller number of staff may suffice for all those remaining. If they all consult together, they will surely arrange matters of their own good will, and may it be with success.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the teaching of a craft, it is understood that this is a good thing, especially since this provides an opportunity to encourage them to continue with their studies of holiness.

With blessings for good tidings and for success in your holy work,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

#### ***4,489***

**R' Yissachar Dov, may he live:** Gurwitz, Paris. Additional letters to him appear above, 4,397, and in the notes referenced there.

## 4490

4490

B"H, 1 Tammuz, 5716

Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote concerning the avreich... may he live, that he still lacks the necessary desire, etc.: it is already brought down in Chassidic discourse in many places that an obstacle or hindrance requires one to increase the power of "rushing waters." And there is no "water" other than Torah, and in particular the teachings of Chassidus, which are living, rushing waters.

With blessings for good tidings, and regards to all Anash, may they live,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 491

B"H, 1 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Through the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is work for Heaven, R' Avraham Lissan, I received news about you and your request for a blessing regarding the building of a home. At an auspicious time, I will mention you at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that your heart's desires be fulfilled for the good. Since for a Jewish person, the spiritual and the physical go hand in hand, it is surely unnecessary to urge you to strengthen the building of your home in a spiritual sense. I mean that your "four cubits" and your portion in the world should be a place of refuge from storm and rain, which comes about when life is founded upon the foundations of Torah and mitzvos, which are eternal and steadfast at all times and in all places (see also Sotah 44a; the conclusion of *Hemshech Mayim Rabim 5636* by the Rebbe Maharash). This will minimize the difficulties in building a home in the physical sense, in the literal meaning of the word, and will bring the matter closer to fruition.

The aforementioned individual writes to me that his father was among those who traveled to and spent time with the Rebbe Rashab, of blessed memory. I am interested to know more details about this, and whether any writings, notes, or similar items remain in his possession.

I have also learned from the aforementioned individual that you are a teacher in a religious school. It is surely unnecessary to elaborate on the magnitude of the merit, and at the same time, the magnitude of the responsibility that rests upon the shoulders of educators in every era—and all the more so in our current time, and in the land upon which the eyes of Hashem your G-d are from the beginning of the year to the end of the year. Since this responsibility rests upon your shoulders, you have certainly been given the strength to act in this regard as much as is necessary; the matter depends only on one's will. May it be His will that you do everything within your power, and may Hashem grant you success.

With blessings for good tidings in all that has been mentioned.

## 4,492

B"H, 1 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Sarah, may she live,

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter of 26 Sivan, in which you write about the closing assembly before the summer vacation days.

It is already well known that one of the reasons for the Jewish custom of beginning [the reading of the Torah] from *Bereishis* immediately after the conclusion of *L'einei Kol Yisrael* is to emphasize that there is no interruption in the study of Torah, and that the concept of a "conclusion" does not apply to it. From this, it is understood regarding all *avodah* in holiness—strengthening Yiddishkeit and disseminating it—that there is no such thing as a conclusion; there can only be a change in the form and manner of the *avodah*, which must be adapted to each time and period according to its requirements.

I am confident that regarding the N'shei U'Bnos Chabad branch in Toronto, there will be a similar approach. That is to say, at the closing assembly for the *avodah* appropriate for the youth's school year, you will discuss and begin preparations for the *avodah* appropriate for the youth's vacation days from school. It has already been discussed at one of the *farbrengens* that precisely during the days called "vacation," the work of *chinuch* is greater and carries more responsibility, and the prospects for success increase, since there is no matter of secular studies that distracts the students—and consequently their parents as well—from the study of holiness and its matters...

With blessings for good news in all that was mentioned, and regards to all the members and their families, may they live.

#### ***4,492***

**Mrs. Sarah, may she live:** [Sarah] Shochat, Toronto. Additional letters to her appear above in 4,417, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4493

4493

B”H, 1 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and pious, etc., Moreinu HaRav Mordechai, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 20 Sivan, in which you write regarding the custom of wrapping the *tallis gadol* in the manner of the Yishmaelim—specifically whether one must cover the head down to the mouth or not—and you point out the discrepancy between the *Shulchan Aruch* of the Alter Rebbe and his ruling in his *Siddur*, and that seemingly one should raise a question on this from *Yoreh Deah*, section 386, and the places cited there.

This has already been noted in the book *Ketzos HaShulchan* by the esteemed Rabbi Avraham Chaim Naeh, in Part 1 and at the end of Part 2, and the notes there, as well as in his book *Piskei HaSiddur*, which deals with the differences between the *Siddur* of the Alter Rebbe and his *Shulchan Aruch*.

Regarding the difficulty raised—how it is possible for the wrapping of the Yishmaelim to be in a manner where the eyes are covered, as it would be impossible to walk in such a state—it seems to me that even in our day, many Yishmaelim walk in this manner in various countries in order to protect their eyes from the sand in the air caused by unusual winds, and even by ordinary winds. They are able to see what is happening beneath them and also at a distance of several *amos*, because the *tallis* hangs loosely and is not bound tightly over the eyes. This is similar to the practice of not looking beyond four *amos*.

Our custom, according to the instruction of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, is that at the time of wrapping, one covers the eyes as well with the upper part of the *tallis gadol*, but one does not cover down to the mouth.

Regarding what you write about the text “*Ki vanu vacharta*” in *Kiddush*—in addition to what is written in *Shaar HaKolel* which you noted in your letter—you should also note the *Siddur Otzar HaTefillos* on the subject, the book *Kesser Shem Tov* by Rabbi Shem Tov Gagin, Part 1, and *Eshel Avraham* (by the Rebbe of Buchach) to *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chaim*, section 271 (at length). See also *Mishmeres Shalom* (by the Rebbe of Kaidanov) in the supplement to section 21. Regarding such matters that are practiced every single day or from week to week, it is difficult to say that it should suddenly change. Since the Alter Rebbe wrote it this way in his *Siddur*, **and it has been practiced** this way, generation after generation, without any interruption, he certainly saw this practice from his teacher, etc. And *maaseh rav* (the conduct of a great scholar is a source of law).

With blessing.

## 4494

B"H, 1 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

It is surprising that for a very long time there have been no letters from you, even though from other sources I receive news of the success of your activities in the field of education. If this is the case outside of your own four cubits, then certainly you are surely increasing your courage and effort as much as is necessary in guarding my vineyards... and especially since the merit of our holy Rebbes assists you.

It is understood that this also includes preparing in advance to utilize the days that the public calls "vacation days" (see Zohar, Part II, 20a). On the contrary, during these days one must increase one's courage and utilize the appropriate opportunities to spread the wellsprings outward, since many *avreichim* and students from outside the four cubits of Chabad are wandering about and their time is free from other matters, and a word to the wise is sufficient.

It is understood that these words of mine are directed not only to you alone, but to all those for whom the matter of spreading the wellsprings is as precious as its true value. Consequently, with a little contemplation, they will arrive at the aforementioned perspective, and this will awaken within them ideas and methods of how to bring this matter into practice to the greatest possible extent. May it be Hashem’s will that you explain this aforementioned point wherever you may be, as it is possible that it will bring benefit, and may the Almighty be with your mouth.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned.

There must be discipline not only from the students toward the *Rosh Yeshivah* and the *Mashpiim*, but also the discipline of those who choose, regarding those whom they have chosen to lead them. And ask a Torah scholar what the common practice is.

## 4,495

B.H., 2 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the congregants of the Nusach HaAri Shul,
Anash of Lubavitch, Dorchester
May they live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter inquiring regarding the conduct of the chazzan leading the Kabbalas Shabbos davening and the Mincha davening of Shabbos Kodesh.

We have merited to hear the instruction of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and through the tradition of the Lubavitch-Chabad customs from generation to generation, as follows: The chazzan does not wrap himself in a tallis for Mincha or Maariv, neither on Shabbos and Yom Tov, nor on Rosh Hashanah.

And certainly, on weekdays this is not done.

And even though in some shuls there are different customs regarding this, the instruction of our Torah, the Torah of life, is already known: "Be careful with the customs of your fathers that are in your hands" (Beitzah 4b); "Do not change the custom of your fathers who are at rest" (Yerushalmi, Pesachim, Chapter 4, Halacha 1).

With the blessing that your shul and beis medrash be fitting in every detail to its great name, Nusach HaAri, Anash of Lubavitch.

## 4496

B.H., 2 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 29th of Sivan, with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed, which will be read at an *eis ratzon* at the holy *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

It is truly astonishing to read the tone of your letter, which is entirely inconsistent with the constant command given to every one of the children of Israel—and, one might say, to every creature in existence: "Serve Hashem, the Creator of the world, with joy." This is especially true when these words come from an educator, whose life for decades has been dedicated to observing how the small become great, how lambs become rams, and how those who lack *daas* become possessors of *daas*—and this happens even without any effort on their part. For this is the nature of our Torah, the Torah of life: a minor is not a person of *daas*, but from the moment they reach thirteen years and one day—or for a girl, twelve years and one day—they are possessors of *daas*.
