To the esteemed Chassidim, G-d-fearing and upright, occupied with communal needs,
R' Chaim Shalom, may he live, HaLevi, and R' Yaakov, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter marking the first anniversary of the establishment of the Chabad Beis Medrash in Mazkeret Moshe, which also included an outline of the activities and development of the shul and Beis Medrash.

May it be the will of the Creator of the world and its Guide that for many years to come, He will grant you the merit to report on the expansion of this "miniature sanctuary" you have been privileged to build—an expansion both in quantity and in quality, in all three categories: "The mitzvah is a candle, the Torah is light," and the "light of Torah," its guidance and its customs. It is clear that the Giver of the Torah is faithful to fulfill His promise that those who bring merit to the many are, in the words of the verse, "like the stars forever and ever," and those who stand at the helm naturally and obviously take the primary share in this.

I trust that you utilized the auspicious days of redemption, 12 and 13 Tammuz, in an appropriate manner, and you should inform me of the details as necessary.

Furthermore, it is surely unnecessary to urge you to take advantage of the visit of the shluchim, shlita, who have traveled from here for several weeks to Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, as representatives of Anash and as my own emissaries, so that they may also convene in your shul and Beis Medrash; through them and through you, the Name of the Almighty will be exalted.

With blessings for good tidings regarding all the above, and may increased courage in spreading the wellsprings outward hasten the coming of our righteous Moshiach, in accordance with the well-known promise transmitted to us by the Baal Shem Tov, and may these days be transformed into joy and gladness.

With blessings.

***

**4565**

**R' Chaim Shalom:** Segal, Jerusalem.
**R' Yaakov:** Ornstein, Jerusalem. Another letter to him—above, 4437.
**Visit of the shluchim:** See above, letter 4446, and the references in the notes there.

## 4566

B"H, 25 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn

To the G-d-fearing, esteemed, and faithful worker in the vineyard of Hashem, R' Moshe Zev, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I received your letter with pleasure, along with the enclosed results of your visit to the Chabad educational institutions and your impressions of Chabad affairs in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, in general. It is my firm hope that you will continue to write in the future, with the necessary detail and without any pretense or embellishment, as this concerns practical matters and affects many people. As is evident in a tangible way, through your efforts thus far, several details have been rectified, even though the beginnings were difficult. From this, it is understood that in the future it will be easier, provided that all those involved cooperate and engage in this work with a single intention: to expand these matters and develop them in both quantity and quality, in accordance with the will of our Nasi, the Rebbe, may his merit shield us. Great is the merit of each one of those who strive in this, and even greater is the merit of those who have caused movement and brought attention even to neglected matters; and this is easy to understand.

It is my hope that you will continue your visits to Chabad institutions and take an interest in Chabad affairs, despite the strange tactic of the Yetzer Hara that calls these "vacation days." To our sorrow and regret, this is accepted even by some of those who are G-d-fearing throughout the year, who say that "vacation days are different," and that there is no place for toil and exertion then. They do not feel that this is the opposite of the Torah, which has informed us that "man is born to toil," and "if one says, 'I have not labored but I have found,' do not believe him." "Days are fashioned, and for Him is one of them" (meaning there is not even one day, nor even one hour, that is superfluous, for they were given in a measured allotment—no less than what is required for a person to fulfill his inner purpose, which is as the Mishnah states: "I was created to serve my Maker," but also no more than is necessary). Therefore, every moment that is not utilized—and this applies to the aforementioned days—is a loss that cannot be recovered; they are "empty days," in the language of the Zohar. To elaborate on a matter that is understood by any upright person is unnecessary.

In these days, the Shluchim, may they live, who traveled from here for several weeks as representatives of Anash and others, will arrive in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. It is certainly unnecessary to urge you to meet with them and, to the extent possible, to accompany them on their visits; the merit of the many will assist you.

I await good and detailed news regarding all of the above.

## 4567

B"H, 25 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the avreich, Nachman, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your note, along with the letter you received from your friend, may he live.

At an auspicious time, I will mention him at the holy gravesite of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for the awakening of rachamei shamayim in whatever he requires.

It is already known what the Creator and Guide of the world has instructed: "If one says, 'I did not labor but I found,' do not believe him." Furthermore, regarding each and every person within the totality of Klal Yisrael, it is said, "See, I have set before you today life and good... therefore choose life"—you must choose it yourself, specifically. However, "he who comes to purify himself is assisted." May it be the will of Hashem that he finds the appropriate words to explain to his friend that he has been given the **full** ability to serve as a beacon of light in his current location, and as a conduit through which the light of Torah and the vitality of fulfilling mitzvos can reach all those in his surroundings. But, based on what was said above, he must choose this path; then he will succeed not only in fulfilling his true role, but also in attaining additional blessing and success in his personal affairs. And if in every place and at every time this role increases one's responsibility—and also one's merit—how much more so in the place where he is located and in his current situation. His responsibility is many times greater, for the souls of his father and the generations before him stand and demand of him: "Is this what we sacrificed ourselves for—even every single day—for the sake of Torah and mitzvos, so that our children, etc., would spend a significant portion of their time on trivial matters and justify themselves with reasons that he himself knows are not valid?" May it be the will of Hashem that the merit of his ancestors stands by his friend to place him in a position of light and to illuminate his surroundings as well.

With blessing,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4568

4568

B"H, 25 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' Shimon, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your notification that you and several of your colleagues have decided to establish an "Ezras Achim" (Brotherly Aid) fund, and have already begun practical activities in this regard:

May it be the will of Hashem that this beginning be in a good and auspicious hour, and may the activities proceed and expand as necessary. May your assistance be successful, in accordance with the words of our Sages, of blessed memory, regarding the verse, "May Hashem bless you and guard you" — "Bless you" with wealth and assets, and "guard" the wealth and assets; meaning, that the wealth and assets should be utilized in the best possible way, and in a manner that is good, visible, and revealed (see Sifrei and Midrash Rabbah in their respective places).

Fortunate is the portion of all those who exert themselves in this, and may it be the will of Hashem that, in accordance with the saying of the esteemed Rabbi, Chassid, and Tzaddik, etc., R' Hillel of Paritch, it be fulfilled in each and every one of them: that every enhancement in mitzvos and good deeds should be done so that a *vort* (a point) of Chassidus may be "deposited" (settled) within him. This "deposit" will lead to an increase in the study of the revealed Torah and Chassidus, and to an enhancement in the fulfillment of mitzvos and in the *avodah* of *tefillah*.

With blessings and regards to all those involved in the above, to whom, of course, all the aforementioned is also directed.

#### ***4568***

**R' Shimon, may he live:** Goldman, Brooklyn.

**Saying:** See also above, Letter 4487, and the references in the notes there.

## 4560

B"H, 26 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned Gedaliah, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 17th and 18th of Tammuz: As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for whatever they may need, in accordance with what you wrote.

And since it is you who are requesting on their behalf, know that to the extent that you increase your involvement in matters of Torah and mitzvos—which are the conduits and vessels for drawing down and receiving the blessings of Hashem—so too will Hashem increase the fulfillment of your request.

Based on what is explained in many places regarding the immense virtue of tzedakah, it is appropriate that your mother, may she live and be well, set aside a few coins for tzedakah each weekday morning, and that the mezuzos in the apartment where she resides be checked.

I found a particular point of interest in your writing that you were among the leaders of the Yavneh religious student union. Surely, you continue to maintain connections not only with your former colleagues in this union but also with its current members, even though you are already a university graduate, as you wrote. For although the great principle of the Torah, "Love your fellow as yourself," applies to everyone—and if this is true regarding the physical and material well-being of one’s fellow, how much more so does it apply to their spiritual well-being, which leads to the life of the World to Come and the life of this world—this command is especially incumbent, and to a greater degree, upon people of the same type and similar upbringing, etc. As the Sages concisely put it, "Your own poor take precedence," and "poor" refers only to one who is lacking in knowledge. Naturally, one is called "poor" in this sense if they are lacking in one discipline, even if they are wealthy in the knowledge of other disciplines.

There is an even greater obligation when the person in question is a young friend who is preparing to organize his life. Frequently, the approach to this organization and the initial start leave their mark on the entire duration of the young person’s life and the home he will eventually establish. This means that it is an impression for generations and generations. From this, it is understood that every exertion and effort is worthwhile, since their results span decades. This is especially true regarding influencing the sphere of religion, which is the life of the Jewish people, and the service of Hashem, which is the purpose of man’s creation, as the Mishnah states (end of Kiddushin), "I was created to serve my Maker." I would be pleased to receive good news regarding your activities in this area.

With blessings for good tidings.

P.S.
It is customary in various circles to send to the library [of Agudas Chasidei Chabad] whatever is printed and published by them in all subjects and in all languages, as this library serves the public good. I am sure you will also act in this manner, especially since your fields, as you wrote, are philosophy, education, and mathematics. Thank you in advance.

***

**Gedaliah:** Rachman, Tel Aviv.

## 4575

B"H, 27 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Shmuel Dober, may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the envelope from [3;7amp&] along with its contents.

It is surprising that you do not report on your efforts in spreading Yiddishkeit, and especially in spreading the wellsprings (*hafatza*), for these are the very purpose of a person's existence, as the Mishnah states: "I was created to serve my Maker." This service is accomplished through Torah and mitzvos—the great principle of which is the matter of "love your fellow as yourself"—and by helping fellow Jews in matters of Torah and mitzvos, one adds to the success of one's own *avodah* in this regard.

Although it is true that one must establish every member of Bnei Yisrael upon the presumption (*chazakah*) that he is a *shaliach*—that is, every Jew performs the mission of the "Supernal Man," as explained in *Likutei Torah* at the end of *Parshas Vayikra*—nevertheless, it is a known halachic ruling that in a place where one can clarify the facts, one does not rely on a *chazakah* (*Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah*, at the beginning). This is especially true given that all Jews are responsible for one another, and particularly in our generation, the generation of the footsteps of Moshiach, who is standing behind our wall. Surely, for someone like you, further elaboration on this is unnecessary.

With blessings, and in the language of my father-in-law, the Rebbe: for a healthy summer, and may you bring good tidings regarding all that has been mentioned.

#### ***4575***

**R' Shmuel Dober:** Gonnels, N.Y. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 2, 193, 215.

## 4571

B"H, 27 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and diligent in communal affairs, R' Chaim Shaul, may he live and be well.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 22 Tammuz, in which you write regarding the hiring of a *madrich*, etc. — it is understood that in such matters, one decides on the spot, after seeing his success with the *talmidim*. May it be the will of Hashem that there be extraordinary success, and that together you may raise "plantings" about whom you can rightfully say, "See the plantings that I have raised." May those who stand at the head of this most holy *avodah* take a leading share in longevity and good years — "good" in every sense of the term, as expressed by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe — and may you succeed in fulfilling your inner and primary task: to make for Him, may He be blessed, a dwelling place in the lower worlds.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

***

**R' Chaim Shaul:** Brook, Rishon LeZion. Further letters to him — see above 4007, and the references in the notes there.

**Madrich:** For the Yeshivas Achei Temimim in Rishon LeZion.

## 4512

4512

B”H, 29 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 17 Tammuz: Thank you for writing regarding the farbrengens during the auspicious days that have passed, even though you were brief regarding the importance of the matters (and surely you will elaborate on the next occasion). My hope is strong that, in accordance with the teaching of the Mishnah that "it is not the study that is the essential thing, but the action"—from which it is understood that while study is also great, action is even more essential—each person will strive, according to his standing and situation, to translate the immediate hisorerus into a positive change in the actions, speech, and even thoughts of the participants and those they influence in the days that follow. May it be the will of Hashem that this be with extraordinary success, and just as you bring good tidings regarding the farbrengen, may Hashem grant you the merit to bring good tidings in this as well.

Based on the above, my bewilderment regarding the beginning of your letter is also understood: you write that your heart has begun to burn to study, yet you are delayed—at a time when the Creator of the world and its Guide commands every single day regarding the study of Torah in all its parts, as is known from the ruling on this in the Laws of Talmud Torah by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch, Chapter 1, Chapter 4, and Chapter 10. And even more so according to our Sages (Eruvin 64a) regarding one who says, "This teaching is pleasant and that teaching is not pleasant" (even if he admits that the latter is also a teaching, just that it is not "pleasant" for him, and the implications are understood). In the language of the Tanya Kadisha (beginning of Chapter 41), Hashem stands over him, watches him, and examines his kidneys and heart to see if he is serving Him as is fitting. Further elaboration on such a matter is superfluous. Surely you will inform me in your reply—perhaps you were not precise in your aforementioned writing, and your intent was not the literal meaning of the words. Or, at the very least, you have corrected the matter immediately, with a sense of regret and teshuvah for the past, such that if you were accustomed to reading one page (notwithstanding the necessity of correcting the past), you will now read two pages, etc.—see Iggeres HaTeshuvah, end of Chapter 9.

It is understood that all of the above applies not only to yourself, but to all those you influence. It is already known that "love your fellow as yourself" is a great principle in the Torah, and I will await good tidings in this as well.

As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

I trust you are keeping the three known daily shiurim in Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

A. Quint
Secretary

Your letter of 24 Tammuz has been received. Regarding your question about your health—cease thinking about this entirely. Instead, turn to a doctor and practically fulfill his instructions regarding your daily schedule, etc., without occupying your power of thought with this. The same applies to what you wrote regarding your sister, tichyeh—she also should have consulted with a doctor. For our Sages have said that the Torah grants permission to the physician to heal, and for every matter, our Sages have said that one must have a hold in the natural order.

May the Healer of all flesh send their healing.

## 4573

B"H, 29 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and industrious, engaged in communal needs,
R' Yechiel Michel Yehuda Leib, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

Following a hiatus, your letter from Motzaei Shabbos was received, in which you write the main points of your activities in general, and during the bright days of the Chag HaGeulah in particular. It is regrettable that the group was small in number, and may it be the will of Hashem that each one of the group be "moist enough to moisten others" among his peers. Surely, as you wrote, you remain in connection with them even after the farbrengen.

I was very pleased to read about your lecture at the N'shei u'Bnos Chabad branch. You should certainly do so in the future as well, and urge the rest of Anash to do the same, especially since recently they have been complaining that they are not being assisted through study lectures given to them, etc.

According to the plan, the avreichim shluchim, may they live, will arrive in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, at the beginning of the month of Menachem Av. May it be the will of Hashem that they succeed in their shlichus, the central point of which is the spreading of the wellsprings outward until they reach the furthest reaches, for this is the demand of the hour—a preface and preparation for the coming of Moshiach Tzidkeinu, speedily in our days, for then "the Master will come" when the wellsprings have spread. Fortunate is the one who strives in this, for he takes a leading role.

Awaiting good news in all the above.

P.S.
The *Machanayim* booklet from Shavuos was received, as well as booklet 83. It is strange that the booklets in between are missing—that is, from booklet 80 until the one that was received. To the extent possible, you should surely endeavor to fill the gap, and thank you in advance.

Subsequently, booklets 84–85 were also received, and in copy form, booklets 1–7, 9–11, and 15. Were they only published in copy form?

The shaliach was delayed due to a certain reason, and in the meantime, your letter to the secretariat dated 3 Menachem Av regarding the plan for welcoming the aforementioned was delivered to me. I will await the continuation with good news. It is understood that if there are additional expenses for this for which there is no source of payment, let me know and I will urge Anash, may they live, to contribute toward it. And surely they have brought the resolutions of their meeting into action. May it be with success and in the desired direction.

Just now, *Machanayim* booklets 86–87 were received; thank you for them. Surely, if possible, send also the Tanach published by the IDF, and thank you in advance.

#### ***4573***

Part of this letter was printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 23, p. 439, and part on p. 469; it was completed based on the secretariat's copy.

**R' Yechiel Michel Yehuda Leib, may he live:** Zalmanov, Bnei Brak. Additional letters to him—above, 4282, and in the references noted there.

**The avreichim shluchim:** See above, letter 4546, and the references noted there.

**Complaining:** See above, letter 4429.

## 4574

B”H, 29 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your urgent letters of the 22nd, 24th, and 26th of Tammuz, I am sending this urgent reply as you requested, although it is not clear why there is such urgency a few days before the end of the school term.

In them, you write regarding the conduct of [Name] and [Name], two of the students, which is not as it should be, and that there are differences of opinion between you and the administration regarding how to deal with them; therefore, you wish for a ruling to be issued from here.

Now, (aside from the well-known principle that one does not issue a ruling based on the report of only one side), does it make sense in your opinion that we should intervene from across the ocean regarding individual students in an institution that has an administration consisting of several members? You surely know that in the Lubavitch institutions located throughout the world, there are several thousand students. If the decision regarding every question of imposing a fine or the like were to be directed from the center, it would be in the manner of the verse, "You will surely wither" — and to an even more alarming degree. One should not follow procedures that do not belong to the world of *tikkun*. When there are differences of opinion in the administration of an institution, the practice is to convene a meeting of all the members, where all sides present their arguments, and then decide by a majority vote. After all, the intention of all members of the administration must be the welfare of the institution and the students.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above and that matters will be resolved for the best,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

#### ***4574***

Printed in *Likutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 408, and completed based on the secretariat’s copy.

## 4595

B"H, 29 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, occupied with the needs of the community in faith,
R' Boruch, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 5 Tammuz and the previous one, in which you write regarding your recent visit to... concerning the shul in your camp.

From your description, it appears that you have been granted a merit from Above. According to the teaching of our Sages, "No person touches what is prepared for his fellow," therefore, they will not allow this to be taken away from you. While it is understood that you must do everything possible to continue with this—including regarding the finances—the lack of proper interest that you perceive should not dampen your work; on the contrary, it should have the opposite effect, as stated above.

Regarding your question as to how you can manage to carry this matter through:

It is well known that one of the foundations of the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov is that from every single thing, one can and must derive a lesson for one’s avodah and shlichus in this world. One of the lessons that can be learned from the popular concept of the "Atomic Age"—that a tiny particle in a quantum can destroy an entire city and hundreds of thousands of people, Heaven forbid, because Hashem has placed an extraordinary energy within that tiny particle—is as follows: If this is true in the measure of retribution and destruction, Heaven forbid, and even in physical matter, then how much more so—a *kal v'chomer* of a *kal v'chomer*—that such a power exists within the *neshamah* of a Yid, and can accomplish great things in the realm of goodness, in *tikkun* and building. I am certain that these few lines will give you courage and illumination in your work.

Regarding experts who should participate in the trial on the side of the truth: According to how things are handled here, you need individuals who speak good English, and such people are certainly to be found among the Agudas HaRabbanim, the Igud HaRabbanim, the Hisachdus HaRabbanim, etc.

In anticipation of good news, that you may go out to the army of Hashem to exact the vengeance of Hashem and not be weakened, and may it be His will that it be with great success.

## 4506

B"H, 29 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

To my dear friend, the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, who engages in the needs of the community,
R' Avraham Dovid, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

...It was pleasant for me to read in your letter that you have reviewed maamarim of Chassidus many times. May it be the will of Hashem that the teaching of the Mishnah, "one mitzvah leads to another," be fulfilled—even regarding a single mitzvah, as the language implies the singular. How much more so regarding a mitzvah that is repeated many times, which leads to far more than the number of mitzvos that cause it, according to what is explained in Shaar HaYichud VeHaEmunah, beginning of Chapter 12: "Moreover, that which rises above all..."—the power that comes through the combination of several letters. My intention is simple: that you should increase this many times over in the dissemination of Chassidus. It will surely be with success, and may it be the will of Hashem that you do this with peace of mind, expanded livelihood, and expanded intellect.

With blessing.

#### ***4506***

**R' Avraham Dovid:** Yanovsky, Ramat Gan. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 12, 3857, and in the notes referenced there. Below, 4545.

## 4517

B"H, 29 Tammuz, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from 5/7, and although you write that in your opinion it is difficult for you to influence a group, and that your influence is much easier on an individual:

This, too, is addressed by the well-known expression: "From this and from that, do not withdraw your hand." In time, you will certainly be able to influence groups of people as well, and not only individuals—even though all beginnings are difficult, as is the case in all matters. It is obvious that you must continue your activities and efforts with individuals. The saying of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, is already well-known: "An individual is a multitude." This is seen tangibly, as sometimes through one individual, many are rectified, who later come under the influence of that individual.

I was particularly pleased to read in your letter about the day camp. My hope is that you will not be satisfied with the number of children you mention, but will strive to increase them. This should be easier now that the quality of the camp's conduct has become known. The same applies to the Shabbos gatherings conducted by your wife, may she live and be well. May Hashem grant you success in increasing your efforts in all the aforementioned, to succeed in an exceptional manner, through which the blessing of Hashem and success in your personal matters will also increase.

With blessings.

#### ***4517***

**Note:** See also letter 4498 above.

## 4578

4578

B”H, 29 Tammuz, 5716

Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

... Regarding your question in your last letter as to whether one should postpone the fulfillment of the mitzvah for a month—it is obvious that this should not be done. Especially since in matters such as these, the full consent of the other party is required, and according to what is explained in the poskim, since it involves *tza'ara d'gufa*, it is difficult to ascertain whether the agreement is without reservation, and *d'l* (a word to the wise is sufficient).

And I have already written to several people regarding such matters, that a *chumra* in this area is actually a *kula*, and even worse than a *kula*. For in our generation, a "generation of orphans," few are those who are suited for *perishus* without stumbling, *chas v'shalom*. May it be the will of Hashem that you cease delving into these matters, as such preoccupation is not desirable, and it is certainly not required. Instead, utilize your power of deep thought in *hafatzas hama'ayanos chutzah* as much as possible within the framework of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad and in other areas as well, and may Hashem grant you success.

With blessings.

#### ***4578***

**And I have already written:** See above, Vol. 9, letter 2862; Vol. 12, letter 3876.

## 4579

*[Late Tammuz, 5716]*

Regarding what was written concerning the matter of *Chanukas HaBayis* [housewarming/dedication] from the *She'iltos* at the beginning—(and it is the same in *Tanchuma*, as noted there)—and *Magen Avraham*, *Orach Chaim* 568, subsection 6.

## 4580

4580

B”H, Rosh Chodesh Menachem-Av, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to the telegram: the reply was delayed because the telegram arrived without a signature, and only after a special inquiry was it learned that the telegram should have been signed by your honor, shlita.

I trust that in the meantime, your honor, shlita, has received my letter dated 23 Tammuz, which already contains an answer to the question of whether a ship can operate automatically for an entire twenty-four-hour period without chillul Shabbos. Nevertheless, I will add a few details here.

On a ship (and similarly on a train, or in factories), there are three types of machines: (a) machines that provide propulsion, (b) machines that generate energy, (c) machines for communication between different departments, the transmission and receipt of instructions, and so on. These are for matters that are not as fundamental, yet are nonetheless **essential**.

For example, on a ship: the transmission of orders to the helmsman, or to the engine room, or to the radio room, and the like. All of this is done via telephone, or in writing, and in many places, a specific type of command or instruction is conveyed by turning on an electric light. Likewise, the transmission of telegrams and receiving them from outside—and regarding the matter at hand, I am referring primarily to telegrams related to the navigation of the ship, where the content of the telegram **determines** the ship’s course and its details, and the sending and receiving of the telegram thereby becomes an **essential** part of the ship’s operation, its direction, and so on.

Without all of these, a modern ship cannot function.

In addition to the three types of machines mentioned above that have a direct connection to the actual operation of the ship, there are many machines on a ship that provide services for the passengers and crew—services of all kinds (desalinating seawater by **boiling** it, supplying water, light, heat, fresh air, moving food from one deck to another, and so on). Even though these machines are not directly connected to the navigation of the ship.

Of all the types mentioned above, it is understood that the steering machines, the machines for transmitting and receiving telegrams, and the like, cannot **possibly** be operated automatically.

- Note: In exceptional cases—that is, when the ship is traveling in **one precise** direction without any changes—it is possible to set the steering machine to operate automatically and rely on supervision. But this is only possible **for a short time**, since the ship’s course changes due to the winds, which are variable, requiring a change in the helm to the opposite side to negate their effect. And the main point is that **in reality**, a ship does not travel in one direction for an entire twenty-four-hour period, especially when taking into account the winds and currents in the sea and ocean, information received regarding other ships on the route, etc.

Regarding the machines that generate energy, **in practice**, they also do not operate automatically for many hours without interruption, and it is **obvious** that this cannot be the case for an entire twenty-four-hour period. For the supply of necessary materials, such as water, fuel, oil, and the like, is **not possible** through mere supervision; there must be adjustments from time to time—adjustments for various factors that change from time to time, such as the water level in the boiler, changes in the amount of fuel, changes in pressure, and so on. In accordance with this, one **adds or subtracts** oil, water in the boiler, and so on (i.e., igniting, extinguishing, etc.).

The same applies to the removal of waste materials (fuel residue, water, etc.), which also requires action and cannot be accomplished by mere supervision.

Furthermore, even the machines that actually propel the ship—although seemingly there is room **in theory** to say that they could operate automatically for an unlimited time—this is not the case in practice, because **several times during the twenty-four-hour period, they require** adjustments, changes, etc., all of which are done by human hands.

The common denominator among all the aforementioned types of machines is that every machine requires lubrication with specialized oil to minimize "friction" (smoothing out the tiny pits in the moving parts of the machine), tightening or loosening the joints of the machine, the screws, and more. This is only **partially** done automatically, and in the **large** machines, and even this is done through various adjustments throughout the twenty-four-hour period.

Another common denominator in a large portion of the machines is that in order to know how they are functioning, various measuring instruments are used. In addition to the act of measurement itself (the operation of some of which involves turning on lights, in addition to all this), the results of the measurements are recorded in special logs.

It is also understood that the machines belonging to the service of the crew and passengers cannot be operated for an entire twenty-four-hour period automatically, since changes occur in the service required from the machine throughout the day; there are services that are primary at night and only partial during the day, or vice versa, etc.

And considering **the reality**, granting permission for the ship to sail is granting permission for the **realistic** manner of its sailing and operation, which includes all the types mentioned.

Beyond the discussion regarding the machines, etc.—as I wrote in my previous letter, the issue of the lack of *shevisah* (resting) arises, according to the words of the Ramban, the Chasam Sofer, etc.; the matter of *uvda d'chol* (weekday activity), since a ship in our days is like a small city, except that it is changed for the worse regarding this matter, in that the passenger on it is, to a significant degree, **not** in control of himself, unlike in a city, and more.

* * *

In searching and striving to find some way to permit these ships, I naturally considered the possibility that on Shabbos Kodesh, the machines could be operated by a non-Jew. But aside from the fact that **it is impossible in reality** for a few people to suffice for this—and on a ship carrying hundreds of passengers, a **very large** number of people is needed, and specifically people who are professionals and **experts**—in my opinion, according to *din Torah*, this solution has no place here, since the owners of the ship are Jews, and the majority of the passengers (and in reality, almost all of them) are Jews. And what good would it do (to make it permissible) to have some of the work, or even all of it—which is impossible anyway, as mentioned—done by a non-Jew? And a bill of sale also has no place here, even to fix the matter of ownership alone, for in such a case, a ruse (*arma*) is ineffective, as it is nullified on Shabbos itself at every step by the captain, the crew, etc.

And in my humble opinion, there is no other way out except for the ship to be docked for the duration of Shabbos Kodesh. And since **in any case** the ships are not on their way without interruption, one can find various ways to arrange travel times so that the financial loss caused by the above will be minimal.

One more note. Even though according to *din* there is seemingly room to permit the ship's sailing if it were to anchor for the entire duration of Shabbos, even in the middle of the sea, if the machines were turned off—in which case they would not need the supply of water (for the machines) and fuel, the measurements, etc., etc.—aside from the fact that the transmission of telegrams and the like would still be operated, considering **the reality**, it is clear that they would not uphold the conditions of turning off the machines at the proper time and turning them on at the permitted time, in addition to the operation of the machines serving the passengers and crew, which would not be kept from the prohibition of operation for the duration of time, even if they were to accept it initially.

Therefore, the solution is docking the ships in one of the ports, **several of which** are found along this travel route.

This is, in my humble opinion, the only possible way to arrange these trips.

* * *

I have just been brought a copy of a letter from the management of the "Zim" shipping company here, written specifically in connection with this matter, and in response to the question of those who wish to **be lenient** in this. The response concerns the ships "Israel" and "Zion." It reads:

... These ships are modern turbine driven ships with fully automatic operational features. Actually, the duties of the engine room crew are supervisory rather than functional.

Incidentally, for your further information, the service speed of these ships is 18 knots per hour. It is impossible for either the Israel or the Zion to reach a European port within the given seven (7) day period...

To the question—that seemingly such a letter should be signed by an expert in the field, the captain or the like—I have not received a clear answer, although behind the scenes it is known, etc.

In any case, this very wording proves the reality, that after the pressure, etc., they write only about the machines that propel the ship and not about those that provide the energy—the room where these machines are located is called the "Fireroom," not the "Engine room" mentioned in the letter above—and also not about the radio transmission machines, the measurement, the transmission of orders, the machines for passenger service, etc., in which the primary *melachos d'oraisa* (Torah-prohibited labors) are involved, as mentioned above. And furthermore, even regarding the propulsion machines, it is written that they are automatic and that the role of the people handling them is a matter of supervision **more** than a matter of operation. And nothing more. Likewise in the second paragraph of the letter, that it is impossible to reach a port in Europe within seven days. Even here they were forced to be careful not to be caught in an error and wrote as above. And thereby the relevance to the matter at hand is nullified, since, as I wrote in my previous letter, several islands and ports are found **between** the United States and Europe, and work done several times during Shabbos, even if only in one of the machines, also renders it forbidden. And especially, etc.

Regarding what is written in the telegram about my visiting the ship that is currently docked here: aside from the various difficulties in this, there is no difference between one modern ship and another regarding the questions mentioned, and it is also simple that the operation of the machines while the ship is at rest and anchored is not comparable to their operation while the ship is underway.

And I have seen ship machines while underway. And the matters are also explained in books in this field. And based on this, I wrote as above.

With respect and blessing.

## 4581

4581

B"H, 1 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and accomplished, R' Meir, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

It was with pleasure that I received your letter of 26 Tammuz, in which you write regarding the visit of the shluchim, shlita, to the camp. May it be the will of Hashem that the impression you describe brings forth fruit and fruit of the fruit—in the words of our Sages, "until the end of the world." As explained in Chassidus, the interpretation is "until there is an end to the concealment and the hiddenness," especially among those who dwell in the tents of Torah and the bochurim of the yeshivos. And following the sowing of the shluchim’s visit, surely anash on the spot will continue with the plowing (which follows the sowing, etc.), and so on, until the final blow of the hammer is struck.

This is a continuation of the matters mentioned in the Mishnah regarding the "great principle." The matter depends solely on their will, for the ability has been granted to each and every one of them by Divine Providence, which placed them specifically in a certain place and at a certain time. May it be the will of Hashem that this occur without concealments and hiddenness—and may it be soon.

With blessings and regards to each and every one of anash, them and their families, since the members of the household are also obligated in the aforementioned avodah, appropriate to their own standing and situation. This is similar to what is said regarding the days of our exodus from the land of Mitzrayim, where the women also recited a song of their own. And the future Redemption, may it come soon, is also—"As in the days of your exodus from the land of Mitzrayim, I will show him wonders."

**M. Schneerson**

## 4582

B"H, 1 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad, Tel Aviv branch
May Hashem keep them alive

Peace and blessing!

Following a long interval, the report detailing the activities for the days of redemption, 12th and 13th of Tammuz, has been received. It is surprising that you do not write regarding other matters, as surely there was time and opportunity for various activities between the previous report and this one. Perhaps you will fill this in at the next opportunity, and may it be the will of Hashem that the content be joyous and commensurate with your ability and potential.

It is surely unnecessary to urge you to take advantage of the visit of the shluchim shlit"a, who have traveled from here for several weeks to Eretz Yisrael t"v, acting as representatives of anash and as my own emissaries; through them, the Name Above will be praised.

I look forward to hearing good and detailed news.

With blessings for success.

#### ***4582***

## 4583

B"H, 2 Menachem-Av, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

In response to your letter, which includes a copy of the letter from Zim, which describes only a **part** of the machinery’s operations and fails to mention several fundamental matters. I will mention some of them:

a) The steering gear cannot possibly function automatically for a full twenty-four-hour period, and the operation of the steering gear affects the steam pressure and causes changes in the supply of oil, fuel, and air—which constitutes *mav'ir* (igniting) and *mechabeh* (extinguishing).

b) The transmission and reception of telegrams—which are essential for maintaining the ship’s **course** and **direction**.

c) The sprayers, called atomizers, are changed* several times a day and are cleaned on the same day in a manner that is forbidden on Shabbos and requires re-ignition afterward.

d) The same applies to the filters, referred to in English as oil strainers.

e) The **adjustment** of the oil, fuel, and air supply cannot possibly be automatic for a full twenty-four-hour period, and these changes constitute *mav'ir* and *mechabeh*.

f) The log of the machinery’s operations and more is written—**according to international law**, which the company is not at liberty to change—**immediately** in the ship’s logs.

g) There are several small auxiliary machines on every ship, though they vary from ship to ship, and they are not operated automatically.

h) The transmission of instructions from one department to another involves—in part—prohibited acts (telephone, ringing a bell, and the like).

i) **In reality**, it is impossible that over the course of twenty-four hours there will not be a repair, change, or adjustment needed in the machinery that cannot be deferred and requires human labor.

j) **Clear proof of the above, and regarding several other** *melachos d'oraisa* (prohibited labors of Torah origin) performed on the day of Shabbos, can be found in the entries in the logs of the Shabbos days, which are **required** to be maintained on ships, and these too are written immediately according to **international law**.

I have written at length, and a second letter is forthcoming.

With respect and blessing,

**Menachem Schneerson**

Addition after some time: According to **international** law, the crew handling the machinery changes every four hours. The incoming shift checks whether the machines are operating properly, and this check is performed by moving levers and the like **by hand**, which increases or decreases the supply of fuel, oil, air, etc. This constitutes *mav'ir* and *mechabeh*. Likewise, several details are recorded and written in a special logbook by the outgoing shift at the time of their departure.

## 4584

[From the year 5716?]

Regarding what you wrote concerning the person who traveled on the aforementioned ship, and that on Shabbos he had the ability to make Kiddush over wine, etc.

I am astonished—does this permit the public desecration of Shabbos?! Furthermore, he made Kiddush over wine belonging to the owners of the ship, which travels through the public desecration of Shabbos, and such wine is forbidden (and any [claim of] *hashgacha* in this matter is a mockery, for the prohibition of wine belonging to one who publicly desecrates Shabbos is not because of *maga akum*, as is well known). From this, he became entangled in a doubt as to whether one should recite a blessing over it, as is known regarding the dispute in such prohibitions. From this, it leads to another doubt—if he ate that night without Kiddush, he surely also used—or benefited in some other way—from the electric light on the ship. Since the passengers and crew are *Bnei Yisrael*, any light produced for their sake is forbidden to others as well, even regarding the laws of "a lamp for one is a lamp for a hundred." Likewise, this leads to the drinking of water and the washing of hands with it, for on ships of our time, during the voyage they take seawater and **boil** it to turn it into fresh water. From this, it is understood that the water used at night came from boiling that occurred the previous day; similarly, the water used during the day comes from boiling that occurred a few hours earlier—meaning, the fresh water on the ship in the middle of the day is that which was boiled during the night or the morning of Shabbos, and in this case, the law of "a lamp for one is a lamp for a hundred" does not apply.

#### ***4584***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 6, p. 405. See above, Letter 4555, and the references in the notes there.

## 4585

4585

B”H, 2 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, whose work is the work of Heaven,
Rabbi Nissan [Nemnov]—may he live and be well.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of the 27th of Tammuz: As per your letter, the secretariat has been instructed to send the documents for the students. They will surely be in touch with you, as for several reasons it is appropriate that they know that these matters are being accomplished through you. In particular, this will ensure that they accept and internalize the concept of the benefit of the many regarding their trip, as has been discussed.

You will surely write in detail regarding the stay of the shluchim—may they live and be well—and the impact of their visit on the other students. I am somewhat concerned that you do not mention this in your current letter, as—unofficially—one of the intentions is that their visit should increase the vitality of the students in the various institutions. As is explained in many places, the effect of something new is such that we were specifically commanded that every day they should be in your eyes as new. It appears from your silence that you did not utilize their visit for this purpose, for if you had, you certainly would have mentioned it specifically. Perhaps you will make up for this at the next opportunity. The same applies regarding the avreich—may he live—whom you chose to join the shluchim from among anash in France; his essence in nigleh, in Chassidus, and in avodah. May Hashem grant him success in all the aforementioned, for the good.

With blessing,

**M. Schneerson**
