## 4,586

B"H, 2 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Avreich, Shalom, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 27 Tammuz, in which you inform me that you have been chosen to join the shluchim, may they live and be well, the representatives of Anash, on their trip to Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, for several weeks.

It is surely unnecessary to awaken you to the magnitude of the privilege, and simultaneously, the magnitude of the responsibility that has been placed upon you by this selection. Each of the shluchim must serve as a living example—in his conduct and in all his affairs—of a shaliach of Anash, may they live. However, the burden is commensurate with the camel, and surely the strength is provided in advance to fulfill these requirements; the matter depends only upon his will. May it be the will of Hashem that you should have the desire and succeed in everything necessary.

With blessings.

***

**4,586**

**To the Avreich, Shalom, may he live:** Eidelman, Paris (to Lod). Another letter to him—see Vol. 11, 3,740.

**That you have been chosen to join:** See the previous letter.

## 4587

B"H, 2 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the 24th of Tammuz: It is surprising that there has been a lapse in your correspondence, and likewise that you have been silent regarding your participation in the shiurim taught in public, regarding your own shiurim, and regarding the dissemination of the wellsprings (until they reach) the outside. It has already been explained in many places that all of the above are the channels and the vessels for drawing down the blessings of Hashem, may He be blessed, in all that a person and his household require. May it be His will that, at the very least, from now on you will do everything that is necessary. Furthermore, you should act in accordance with what is stated in Tanya, Igeres HaTeshuvah, Chapter 9: when one requires teshuvah for the past, if one was accustomed to reading one page, he should read two pages, and so on.

Regarding what you wrote—that you have accepted the offer to work as a...—may it be His will that you succeed in this through dedicated and committed avodah, and through this, you will cause nachas ruach to the founder of the village and its director, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Certainly, your reward for this will also be manifest regarding yourself and your household, may they live, in your personal matters.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, with proper health in physicality and proper health in spirituality.

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

## 4588

4588

B"H, 2 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Following the long interval, your letter of 27 Tammuz was received. Regarding what you wrote—that you are traveling there due to matters of physical health—this is nothing more than the "garment of nature," as is explained in many places and in the *Kuntres Uma'ayan* regarding the verse, "And Hashem your G-d will bless you in all that you do," that a person’s affairs require being clothed in nature, especially during the time of *galus*. However, the inner intent is that you serve as a conduit through which the *ma'ayanos* of *Toras HaChassidus*, its guidance, and its customs will reach the people and the *bochurim*, especially those currently in your vicinity. Therefore, you must exert yourself in this as much as possible. Certainly, there is no contradiction here to matters of health, as there is enough time for both. Furthermore, your activities in *hafotzas hama'ayanos* will add to the blessings of the *Ribono shel Olam* in your personal affairs and in your health in particular.

I await good news regarding all of the above.

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4589

4589

B"H, 2 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs, R' Saadia [Librov], may he live.

Shalom u'Bracha,

In response to your letter of 26 Tammuz: It is surprising that the number of students during the vacation period is not as high as it should be. Especially since the time of vacation from secular studies should be utilized even more, as the students are not distracted by other matters from their holy studies, and this is understood.

Regarding what you wrote that the space for the *ashal* (room and board) is insufficient: In addition to the fact that you should certainly increase the number of students from the local residents, you should have discussed with the esteemed Rabbi and Chossid, R' Binyamin Eliyahu Gorodetzky, may he live, the matter of expanding the space in general. The decision regarding this can be implemented now, and consequently, there will be room for many more students. Regarding the difficulty you mentioned: a portion of this is surely in the category of "a candle for one is a candle for a hundred," and for the remaining parts, you must endeavor to find instructors from among our brethren in Morocco itself. In any case, you must search for suitable instructors and teachers **from among them.** In general, it seems that further explanation is still needed even for *anash*, that what we have been commanded—that we must elevate in matters of holiness—applies to all matters of holiness, not only to some of them, and not only to those that do not involve exertion. It is obvious that an educational institution is among the foremost in this commandment, as it affects generations to come. Especially according to what is explained in *Chassidus* that specifically in this—in sons, daughters, and grandchildren—the power of the *Ein Sof* is revealed in this world, and this is understood.

Regarding what you wrote about your engagement and setting a date for the wedding: My perspective on this has surely been known for some time, based on what is explained in many books—especially in our generation, a generation of orphans—that whoever shortens the time between the *tenaim* and the wedding is praiseworthy. May Hashem grant you and your bride success, that you may derive much Chassidishe *nachas* from all your offspring, may they live, while succeeding in your holy work.

With blessings of *Mazal Tov* for your engagement and wedding in a good and auspicious hour,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4590

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and involved in communal needs, R' Dovber, may he live and be well.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter, in which you write regarding the visit of the shluchim, may they live and be well, to your camp and to Gateshead: May it be the will of Hashem that the positive impression and words of hisorerus lead to tangible actions, in accordance with the central point that one must disseminate the maayanos until they reach even the "outside" (chutza). The concept of a *maayan* (spring) in spirituality is understood to correspond to the concept of a *maayan* in physicality—where the status of a *maayan* applies only when the waters are connected to the source, and according to the Rambam, it is also required that they flow outside the place of the spring’s existence; if they do not flow outside, it is nullified from the status of a *maayan*. The analogy is understood. And since it is specifically from our generation that this avodah is demanded, surely the generation is capable of receiving it, and those who are aware of this demand have been given the ability to fulfill this role. May the Ribono shel Olam grant you and your wife, may they live and be well, success in each of you doing what is stated here, according to your respective roles. Beyond the fact that the reward of a mitzvah is a mitzvah, may you also be blessed with an abundance of Chassidishe nachas from all your offspring, may they live and be well, in good health and with peace of mind.

Regarding what you wrote—that from Chapter 35 of Tanya, deeper concepts begin, and therefore, etc.—one should endeavor to study until after Chapter 36, the content of which is a most general concept, understandable even when studied superficially, as is the case for a *baal habayis*.

With blessing.

## 4591

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, G-d-fearing, learned, and accomplished, a man of character, etc.,
R' Yitzchak, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

It gave me pleasure to receive your letter from the eve of Shabbos, regarding the visit of the shluchim, may they live, to your summer camp. May it be the will of Hashem that the words of hisorerus spoken at this gathering bear fruit in practical action for the days to come. As the Mishnah states, "The main thing is not the study, but the action." This is especially true in light of what has been discussed at several farbrengens: that the expressions of Chazal are certainly precise in **every** detail (and this is, of course, according to the teachings of Chassidus, and unlike the opinion of several Jewish philosophers and scholars; see also the introduction to Moreh Nevuchim). Chazal specifically noted "idle words" (*devarim betelim*), not forbidden words, nor even repulsive words. In the subtle sense, "idle words" also include words of hisorerus spoken with the intent of prompting action, if they fail to produce their effect—and the wise will understand. With this, one can also explain the precision of the phrase, "Woe to the one who speaks to ears that do not hear." Note also the commentary of the Rambam on Avos, end of Chapter 1, and Berachos 6b: "Whoever... is G-d-fearing, his words are heard."

I was pleased by the description of the mikveh in your letter, especially in light of the words of the Tashbetz, cited in many places, that regarding a mikveh, one should strive to fulfill the requirements of all opinions.

May it be the will of Hashem that the "Mikveh of Yisrael, Hashem," before Whom the Jewish people are purified, and the physical purification—the taharah of the daughters of Yisrael [and also the taharah of the sons of Yisrael (see She'eilot U'Teshuvot Min HaShamayim, sec. 6), as is known from the words of the Rambam, that the known immersion for purity was never abolished, especially regarding tefillah. (See Rif in the name of the students of R' Yehuda, Berachos Chapter 3, in the name of Rav Hai Gaon, and there), that according to all, tefillah is more readily accepted with immersion. These matters are also explained in Likutei Torah by our Rebbe HaZaken, Parshas Ki Savo, Maamar 3, end of 72b]—will bring about our true redemption. See Tosafos Yom Tov, Yoma, at the end, and Rambam, end of Hilchos Mikvaos. As mentioned above, the expressions of Chazal are precise, and may these days be transformed into joy and gladness.

With respect and blessing,

P.S. Your letter was opened by the postal inspector in England.

## 4592

4592

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

Although it has been some time since I received any news from you, I rely on the presumption (*chazakah*) that a *shaliach* performs his mission. For every person—every Jew—is a *shaliach* from the "Supernal Man upon the Throne" to fulfill the task appropriate to the statement, "I was created to serve my Maker." To do this, one must be healthy and whole, as per the ruling of the Rambam in *Hilchos De'os*, beginning of Chapter 4. I am certain I will receive explicit news to this effect, for where there is an opportunity to clarify, one does not rely on a presumption.

I am writing now because I received a letter from Rav Chaim Axelrod regarding the construction of a *mikveh* in his city and community. He writes that although he approached you regarding this, he did not receive a response, etc. Since he does not provide me with all the details, and I am not familiar with the neighborhood or the writer, I am writing directly to you. I am certain you will act in accordance with the requirement and the situation, and may the Ribono shel Olam grant you success.

May it be the will of Hashem that the "Mikveh of Yisrael, Hashem"—before whom the Bnei Yisrael are purified—and the matters of spiritual purity, lead to purity in the literal sense: the purity of the daughters of Yisrael [and also the purity of the sons of Yisrael, as is well known from the words of the Rambam that the known immersion for purity was never nullified, especially regarding *tefillah* (see *Rif* in *Talmidei Rabbeinu Yonah*, *Berachos* Chapter 3, in the name of Rav Hai Gaon, and there), that according to all, *tefillah* is more readily accepted with immersion. These matters are also explained in *Likutei Torah* by our Alter Rebbe, *Parshas Tavo*, 43, end of side b]. Through this, may we also hasten our true redemption—see *Tosafos Yom Tov*, *Yoma*, at the end, and Rambam, end of *Hilchos Mikvaos*—and may these days be transformed into joy and gladness.

With respect and blessings for success.

## 4593

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the *avreich* Menachem Ben Zion, *sheyichyeh*,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your two letters: At an *eis ratzon*, I will mention all those you wrote about at the *tziyun hakadosh* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *ztzukulleh"h, nbg"m, zya"a*, and may it be Hashem’s will that you bring good news regarding each and every one of them.

Regarding what you wrote concerning your activities within the framework of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad: You certainly will not be satisfied—nor should anyone be satisfied—with what has been accomplished thus far, since the necessity of the hour demands activities on a much larger scale.

And since this is a necessity, the possibilities to fulfill this demand have certainly been provided. It depends only on those working in this field. May it be Hashem’s will that you bring good news, specifically regarding this matter as well.

Regarding your notification of your birthday: You surely observed the customs of this day that *anash* have practiced recently. May it be a year of success for you in the study of Torah, in the performance of *mitzvos* with *hiddur*, and in the *avodah* of *tefillah* in general, as is understood. It is simple that the *avodah* of spreading the *maayanos* is a *klal gadol* in the Torah, as Rabbi Akiva said, "Love your fellow as yourself—this is a *klal gadol* in the Torah."

With blessings,
On behalf of the Rebbe, *shlita*,
A. Quint
Secretary

Regarding what you wrote about the criticisms of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad and the like: Dismiss such talk, for some of the critics would criticize regardless. As for the rest—they do not discern the **fundamental** difference between the Chabad approach of *kiruv* and the approach of others. Chabad acts **according to the command** of our Sages, brought in this context by the Alter Rebbe in *Tanya*, Chapter 32: "Love the creatures and draw them (the creatures) close to the Torah." The approach of the aforementioned is the **opposite**: to "draw" the Torah close to the creatures. And one who does not distinguish between these two **opposite** extremes—is he the one to issue a ruling on this?! Those who criticize the Chabad approach mentioned above do not pay attention to the first part of the aforementioned **command** of our Sages.

One who contemplates the magnitude of the matter of "extracting the precious from the vile" (see carefully *Bava Metzia* 85a) will calmly accept the disparagement, etc.

The *Sefer HaMaftiach* was received, and thank you.

Regarding the order of completing the *Chitas* lessons: It is obvious that the lesson of yesterday takes precedence, for it is correct to fulfill them in order, and not to study the chapter of today before the chapter of yesterday. And this is simple.

Regarding your note as to why *Chitas* lessons should not be postponed *lechatchilah* to the night following Tisha B'Av, since we compare *Chitas* to *kodshim*, where the night follows the day: It is understood that they only said to complete them on the following night in a case of difficulty (*bedieved*), and they found support for this from *kodshim*, etc. But not *lechatchilah*, as his *tefillah* of *Ma'ariv*, etc., is in contradiction to his lessons. And many matters of *tashlumin* (make-up) are only *bedieved*. In other words: among the *tashlumin* in *halacha*, there are several (though not all) where one does not complete the entire essence, but only a part of it. Furthermore, it should be noted: it is known what is written in the writings of the Arizal that one should not study *Mikra* at night. Similarly, one is careful not to recite *Tehillim* at night until *chatzos*.

## 4594

4594

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Yehuda, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

Regarding what you wrote, that in Targum Yonasan to Vayikra 13:1, the words "and to Aharon" were not translated—one should check the early printings (especially the one printed by Dr. Moshe Ginsburger, Berlin 5658, 5663), as it is surely a printer's omission, and those who followed did not notice to correct it.

Regarding the matter of reciting "V'lakachta Solet" on Yom Kippur that falls on Shabbos: 
a) It is not sufficient to merely mention it during the Avodah, since the reason for its recitation is "and we will render [as] bulls our lips" [for one who] occupies himself with the Torah of... (see Shulchan Aruch Admur HaZaken, end of section 1). 
b) It is impossible to say it in the [proper] order, because the Musaf prayer **includes** the Musaf of the day and the Musaf of Shabbos, and the Lechem HaPanim comes **between** them. 
c) Regarding what you wrote that the Avodos of Yom Kippur must be done in the specific order, etc.—this applies specifically to those performed in the white garments (Yoma 60a), and especially to the Lechem HaPanim, which relates to Shabbos and not to Yom Kippur. See also the notes of the Rambam, Hilchos Avodas Yom HaKippurim, Chapter 1, Halacha 2, and Zevachim (91a) regarding "that which is frequent and that which is not frequent, the frequent comes first," and "he slaughtered, etc."

#### 4594

A portion was printed in Likutei Sichos, Vol. 4, p. 1361, and a portion in Vol. 17, p. 308; it was completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**R' Yehuda:** Schmotkin, Tel Aviv. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 9, 2841, 2890. Below, 4743.

**In the printed editions:** Targum Yonasan ben Uziel on the Torah. And there: [and with Aharon].

**Regarding the recitation of V'lakachta Solet:** In continuation of this, below, there.

## 4,495

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Sarah, may she live,

Greetings and blessings!

...It is surely unnecessary to remind you of the primary and essential duty of every teacher and educator: to fortify their students so that they may withstand the trials of life and the winds blowing in the world, without deviating from the path of life—which is the path of our Torah, the Torah of life. Even when teaching subjects that, at first glance, have no connection to the aforementioned, one should utilize them as a conduit to instill in the students an inner faith in the Creator of the world and its Guide, who is the essence of good, and who, in His great goodness, has shown us a way of life that leads to a happy life. With appropriate contemplation, one can find the means to explain this truth to the students in a manner suitable to their age and level of understanding; and there is nothing that can stand before the will.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

## 4596

4596

By the Grace of G-d
4 Menachem-Av, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the honorable, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent [in communal matters], etc.,
Rabbi Shragai, may he live.

Peace and Blessing!

I have just received your letter of 26 Tammuz. Since you did not indicate a different address, I assume you will be staying in Paris for some time, and therefore this letter is being sent there.

Regarding the content of your letter, the question of the ships: You are surely aware of the development and progression of this matter, as you are in contact with your office in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established). Like all matters and problems concerning Eretz Yisrael, this issue is no longer limited to the specific question itself; it has taken on the form of a general problem that reflects the attitude toward our Torah and its mitzvos. This is especially pronounced in the Jewish street in the United States, and particularly among the youth, who do not delve into the subtleties of the matter. Since they receive reports from their friends and acquaintances who have traveled on these ships, and they know about the conduct of the crew—both during their hours of service and during their off-duty hours, etc.—it is understandable that the question takes on a very sharp character in their eyes, one that clearly reflects the attitude in Eretz Yisrael toward our Torah and our religion. This is especially true given that it is known that the owners of the ships are Jewish and the ships are Israeli. The question arises for them: Is the settlement and the regime that of a "kingdom of priests and a holy nation," or a neutral regime where everyone does what is right in his own eyes, or perhaps that of the extreme left?

It seems that the telegram I sent you via Rabbi Zevin (may he live) to Jerusalem (may it be rebuilt and established) was not forwarded to you, as I assumed you were there. A copy is enclosed.

It is superfluous to note that I did not arrive at the decision stated in the telegram—that the current manner of operation on these ships is a total prohibition—based on a superficial discussion. Only after study, and after hesitation regarding my involvement in this issue—especially out of consideration for the honor of the Chief Rabbis (may they live)—did the situation compel me to publicize my clear position, particularly relying on my knowledge of mechanics, as I wrote at length in my letters to the Chief Rabbis (may they live).

The conclusion of the study on this matter is that it is impossible to operate a ship for a full twenty-four-hour period via automatic operation. Furthermore, in the present case, there is no room for the suggestion to employ non-Jews for certain tasks, as you mention in your letter (in addition to the fact that they would need to be experts in the maritime profession, and **many** people are required for this, all of which would cost a fortune. For surely they would not arrange the ships so that the workers and crew members are only non-Jews. And even if there were someone who wanted to do this, the reaction of the leftists to such a proposal is understandable).

However, there is a way to solve the problem, and in a way that is **relatively** easy and simple: that throughout the entire day of Shabbos, the ships should anchor in port. And there are **many** ports located on the route from here to there and from there to here.

Let it be known that even without this, the ships do not travel continuously the entire way, for several reasons.

And although keeping the ship in port for an entire day will increase expenses, it is less than the cost of hiring a secondary crew of non-Jews for one day a week, and incomparably less than the cost of keeping Shabbos in the fields and factories and the like in Eretz Yisrael.

It is understood that I also considered the possibility of stationing the ships in the middle of the sea for the duration of Shabbos. It is simple that there is no danger in this, and it is technically possible, etc. However, this proposal would only slightly alleviate the Shabbos question (in that the machines operating the ship's propulsion would be at rest), but it would not solve the problem. Even if you were to say that the instructions to turn off those specific machines would be followed, many other machines would remain in operation, such as the machines for transmitting and receiving telegrams (regarding weather, winds, the movements of other ships, and the like), which are mandatory when a ship is at sea. The same applies to the machines connected with relaying orders from one department to another, and likewise for machines for passenger services, and much more—in addition to the writing of reports in special logs, which, according to international custom, is done every single day the ship is at sea.

Furthermore, regarding the lack of cessation of work, this is a serious matter. To our sorrow and regret, the crew does not listen to instructions in this regard at all—not only in their private matters, but even in matters concerning the ship, and in public, as is known from hundreds of witnesses who have traveled on these ships, to the point that it has even been publicized in the press.

I am certain that for someone like you, there is no need for the following remark; nevertheless, for the sake of completeness, I am writing this as well:

There are those who argue that out of Ahavas Yisrael and the obligation to support our Jewish brethren everywhere—and all the more so in Eretz Yisrael, the land upon which the eyes of Hashem your G-d are from the beginning of the year to the end of the year—one must strive as much as possible to increase the income of the treasury, since by doing so one is assisting our Jewish brethren in Eretz Yisrael, etc.

—And it seems the one who argues this does not know that this is one of the arguments of those who want to work, G-d forbid, on Shabbos in all the factories and fields in Eretz Yisrael. And by doing so, they earn much more money than through the ships.—

I answered them with the well-known statement of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, who was asked many years ago a similar question. He gave a parable: when extinguishing a fire, one does not check if the water is fit for drinking; one extinguishes the fire even with slop water. My father-in-law, the Rebbe, replied that while they are correct in what they say, one who wants to extinguish a fire with naphtha and fuel oil—since that, too, is a liquid—not only does not bring benefit, but actually increases the destruction. The same applies when one wants to assist a Jew, and all the more so many Jews, in a manner that is forbidden according to our Torah, the Torah of life; this is not assistance, but damage and destruction.

For we have only the notification of our Torah, that through the desecration of Shabbos, not only is there no addition in existence and building, but on the contrary—"people are diminished and the roads become desolate" (Shabbos 33a). And our Sages further said, "Jerusalem was destroyed only because they desecrated the Shabbos therein" (ibid. 119b).

And anyone who believes in Hashem and His Torah desires the existence of Eretz Yisrael and mourns and grieves over its destruction—it is obvious that he will not add to the destruction through actions such as these.

How strange it is that even those who fight against the sale of non-kosher meat in Eretz Yisrael with all their might—even though this abomination is done in public, without deceit, without any necessity, and by private individuals, and there is no issue of providing a *hechsher*—yet regarding the issue of the ships, where most passengers do not even imagine that there is any prohibition involved (since they are accustomed to the idea that sailing on ordinary ships is permitted, especially if they set sail three days before Shabbos, and they have not given thought to the fact that the conditions on these ships are fundamentally different from the ships discussed in the Gemara, the Rishonim, and even the Acharonim), and the ships fly the flag of the Jewish people, and the conduct on the ship is surely known to you as well—in this issue, not only do they not want to know the opinion of the Shulchan Aruch, but they even pressure and threaten, etc., etc. It is difficult to elaborate on such a distressing matter.

And since this is a serious matter, and it is under your responsibility, you will surely utilize all your influence and possibilities (which are much greater than you imagine) to save hundreds and thousands of our Jewish brethren from the desecration of Shabbos, based on the above. May it be the will of Hashem that his efforts be successful.

May it be the will of Hashem that in his days and in our days, the statement of our Sages will be fulfilled in practice: "If Israel would keep one Shabbos according to its laws, the son of David would immediately come" (Yerushalmi, Taanis, Chapter 1, end of Halacha 4). And with the addition in Shemos Rabbah (end of Chapter 25): "Why? Because it is equal to all the mitzvos..."

With respect and blessing, awaiting good news.

*In the Bavli (Shabbos 118b) it says "two Shabbosos according to their laws." This does not contradict the statement in the Yerushalmi of Taanis mentioned above, as explained by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe in his book Likutei Torah, Parshas Behar, in the discourse entitled "Es Shabbososei Tishmoru." See there.*

4597

4597

By the Grace of G-d
4 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, engaged in communal needs,
Rabbi Yaakov, may he live.

Peace and Blessing!

In response to your letter from Erev Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av:

Regarding what you wrote concerning the repetition and variation in reciting the words "v'laharog" and "lifneihem" — it is possible that there was an earlier and a later custom, as the Purim customs were printed during the lifetime of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, in this world, and after he had reviewed them.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the blessing "Harav es riveinu" — I did see my father-in-law, the Rebbe, recite it even when alone, while he was in Paris. Incidentally, at that time I read the Megillah before him with the aforementioned repetition, and he expressed a gesture of satisfaction (naturally, only a gesture, as it was in the middle of the reading; after the reading, the conversation turned to other matters).

Regarding what you wrote concerning "zecher" — whether with five or six vowel points — you have surely seen the conflicting reports on this matter cited in *Ketzos HaShulchan*. I did not have the opportunity to hear a directive on this from my father-in-law, the Rebbe. My reasons and arguments for my suggestion on this are written in the note on this custom in the *Kuntresei Purim*.

There is an additional reason that was not written there, based on the explanation that Rabbi Avahu established in Caesarea (*Rosh Hashanah 34a*) in order to include all the customs, so that the matters would not appear as two separate Torahs.

Regarding your mention that you sent here a copy of the customs known to you — this is not preserved in my memory. It is possible that I forgot, or that it was sent to someone else. Consequently, my suggestion remains in place, for the benefit of the many.

Incidentally, regarding all those customs published in the *kuntreisim* and booklets, etc. — those that were published during the lifetime of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, in this world, it is obvious that he reviewed all the customs. As for those published after that, I noted it: if it was my own reasoning, I also wrote my reasons for it. And if I did not note it, it is from what I heard or saw from my father-in-law, the Rebbe.

Last week, three bochurim from Canada visited me while passing through here on their way to visit Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established). I took the liberty of giving them your address, with the note that through you they might be able to connect with those from whom the situation regarding education and the youth is known, in those details that others might wish to conceal from them. Although I only saw them once, their families are from among *anash*, and in America, they are considered *avreichim* of the *yirei shamayim* type. If they knew more matters, and also more of the value of these matters, they would be even more *yirei shamayim*.

News reached me that you have not been in good health. May it be G-d’s will that you return to your strength, and in the language customary in *maamarim* — to your strength in the literal sense, and to your strength in its deeper meaning, according to the interpretation of our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe in the *maamar* beginning "Maskil L'Eisan HaEzrachi" (printed in *Kuntres Limud HaChassidus*).

With blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4598

4598

B"H, 5 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Kfar Chabad Committee
May Hashem keep them alive

Peace and Blessing!

In response to your letter, which also touches upon the construction of the mikveh—I am compelled to repeat myself, even if only to emphasize a point. My view regarding the manner of construction in Kfar Chabad is that it is being handled with alarming negligence. I only hope that this negligence does not lead to losses and disasters that will be difficult to rectify. This negligence, which is rooted in the will, understandably clouds the intellect; you seize upon any matter or excuse to delay the construction work for as long as possible, and even beyond that. It seems that regarding the mikveh as well, you have latched onto my letter concerning the beauty of the mikveh, the improvements, etc., and because of this, the construction has been cancelled from the outset, and the debate will start from scratch. To negate even this excuse, I hereby undertake—without a vow—to raise funds from anash in the amount of two thousand liras, and perhaps even more. However, I will provide the funds after the completion of the construction; that is to say, after you build the mikveh according to the detailed plan, and if the aforementioned sum is lacking for the completion, then I will provide it. Consequently, it is understood that immediately upon receiving this agreement of mine, you must proceed with the construction of the mikveh, if it is true that the funds are the only obstacle.

It seems that the situation regarding the construction of the shul is the same, as the initial sum that was deposited for it has been lying there like a stone that no one turns over for ten months now. It appears that you are not taking the necessity of the matter to heart.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and may it be His will that it happens quickly and with a detailed and joyous response.

#### ***4598***

From a copy.

## 4599

B”H, 5 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding your personal request to be released from your position: I have already grown weary of receiving such requests from various members of anash. I will state my perspective once and for all, and afterwards, you may do as you see fit. It is as follows:

The positions in Kfar Chabad and in the Chabad institutions held by anash in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) are the very channels and vessels for receiving the blessings of our holy Nasi, may the merit of the tzaddik protect us. This applies not only to spiritual matters but also to material matters in the literal sense, for them and their families [and this is also the case regarding establishing a residence in Kfar Chabad]. However, although our Sages of blessed memory said that one may act to the benefit of another even in his absence, one cannot force a benefit upon a person against his will. It is understood that it is possible for some members of anash to attempt to set up other channels and vessels, and they may even succeed in this, though it is clear that I find no reason or logic in such conduct or such a search. But the adage cited in Chassidic discourse is already known: "One cannot force a head [onto someone else's shoulders]," and people’s opinions are not alike—and all these things apply to you and your holy service...

Regarding what you wrote—that there are those who are not satisfied with your conduct, etc.—surely, as in all matters between a person and his fellow, both sides are at fault, even if not to the same degree. Regarding the portion—even if it is the smallest part—for which you are to blame in the aforementioned, you must improve it. When there is no place for a complaint, and no foothold for a complaint on your part, the complaints will cease altogether.

With blessings that you decide what is appropriate for your benefit, and not what is appropriate for your desire.

#### ***4599***

**Note:** See also above, Vol. 11, Letter 3744, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 4400

B"H, 5 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings,

In response to your letter, in which you write that unfortunately you were unable to attend the farbrengen of the auspicious days of 12 and 13 Tammuz.

Every matter is by Divine Providence, as is known from the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov and the teachings of Chassidus in general. It is explained there at length that one can derive a lesson from everything and utilize it for avodas Hashem. This case is no different: generally, when someone else is able to prevent a Yid from participating in such a farbrengen—even when that person explicitly intends to hinder it—it usually stems from the fact that the desire to participate was not sufficiently strong or not entirely pure. That is to say, the desire was mixed with extraneous matters, and this creates an opening for another person to not only want to prevent that participation, but to actually succeed in doing so. This is the first point.

Regarding the second point—this is along the lines of what is explained in many places in Chassidus: that a concealment (hester) of a holy desire should evoke an even stronger thirst for that holy matter, until the thirst becomes so intense that it influences those who previously hindered it, causing them, too, to recognize the importance and holiness of the matter. So too in this instance: the inability to participate in the farbrengen, the purpose of which is to deepen one’s ahavas haTorah, ahavas Hashem, and ahavas Yisrael, should increase your hasmadah and shekidah in Torah and your hidur in the performance of mitzvos. The purpose of the hester is to evoke this yearning to immediately begin in all these areas as mentioned, at which point, ultimately, the attitude of those who hinder this desire will change. The preparation for this is, quite simply, to learn more and to fulfill mitzvos with greater hidur, and Hashem Yisbarach will grant you success.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the possibility of studying at the Lubavitcher yeshiva in Newark, you should have requested that the administration of Tomchei Temimim in Montreal communicate with the administration of the yeshiva here to determine if this is a suitable proposal.

Surely you are careful to recite the daily portion of Tehillim after davening, according to the monthly division of Tehillim.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe Shlita,
Secretary

## 4301

B"H, 5 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Dovid, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I was pleased to receive regards from you through the *avreichim* traveling to the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established), Mr. Dollefen and Mr. Zand. Your letter of the 25th of Tammuz also arrived. May it be the will of Hashem that you always bring good news, in a way that is good, visible, and revealed. Especially in accordance with the statement of our Sages, "There is no 'good' other than Torah," may you report good news regarding the study of Torah in general, and *Toras HaChassidus* in particular. "And how long will you remain silent?" As is explained at length in the *Shaar HaHakdamos* of R' Chaim Vital, the lack of study of *Pnimiyus HaTorah* delays the *Geulah* (incidentally, the *Gra* writes similarly in his commentary to *Mishlei*). Through the dissemination of this study in one's own "outside" and in the "outside" in the literal sense, the Master comes. May it be the will of Hashem that you begin this immediately and ensure that your surroundings do the same, and may Hashem grant you success.

Enclosed is a *kuntres* that has just been published, and surely you will also merit the public through it. "And the merit of the public depends upon him."

With blessing.

#### ***4301***

**R' Dovid:** Tannenhaus, of Bathurst. Additional letters to him: above, Vol. 8, 2392; Vol. 9, 2339.

**The avreichim traveling:** On a mission for the Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch. See *Sefer Toldos Chabad B'Artzos HaBris*, pp. 279-280.

**The Gra:** See also above, Vol. 9, letter 2886, and the references in the notes there.

## 4002

B"H, 5 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av, in which you write about the business where merchandise was purchased from you, and now there are difficulties regarding the payments, and there is doubt as to whether the matter will come to fruition, etc.

At a time of favor, I will mention you at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for livelihood in a kosher and tranquil manner. May it be His will that, as far as it depends on you, you fulfill the well-known statement of our Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe, author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch: "Hashem gives Jews gashmius (materiality), and they make gashmius into ruchnius (spirituality)." This means that one uses their livelihood for matters of Torah and mitzvos, and the portion for one's own sustenance and that of one's household is also according to the instruction of the Shulchan Aruch, for then that, too, is holiness, as it is written, "In all your ways, know Him," and as is explained at length in Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, section 231. It would be appropriate for you to practice the separation of maaser from your profits for tzedakah, and it has already been said regarding this, "And test Me now with this..."

You surely know of the three daily shiurim that are equal for every soul, instituted by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: 1) Reciting the daily portion of Tehillim—as Tehillim is divided for the days of the month—every day after morning davening; 2) Studying the Chumash portion with the commentary of Rashi for the weekly parsha (on Sunday, from the beginning of the parsha until the second aliyah; on Monday, from the second aliyah until the third, etc.); 3) Studying the daily shiur of Tanya—as Tanya is divided for the days of the year. In any event, you should keep them from here on out.

With blessings for good tidings.

## 4303

4303

B"H, 5 Menachem-Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Temimim students,
Shluchim of the Rebbe Shlita,
May Hashem grant them life.

**Greetings and blessings!**

**Upon your receipt in due time of the telegram from the Rebbe Shlita regarding your successful arrival in Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established, the text is transcribed here:**

May your arrival in the Holy Land be in peace, and may your visit there be for outstanding success in the fulfillment of your shlichus, the inner point of which is the dissemination of the wellsprings outward, immediately to the Geulah. With regards to all Anash.

#### **4303**

## 4304

4304

B.H., 5 Menachem-Av, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the National Conference of the Association of Torah Teachers in Eretz Yisrael — may it be built and established speedily in our days by Mashiach Tzidkeinu —
May they live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your invitation, I hereby offer a few points. I shall be brief, though the subject warrants elaboration, as it is difficult to be lengthy in a letter.

It is understood that these words are directed through you to all teachers and educators as well.

It is the duty of the teacher and educator to always remember that the youth do not believe in compromises, and their minds do not accept "half-truths"—if such a thing even exists. For this reason, a complete education succeeds far better than an education based on half, a third, or a quarter of a compromise, especially regarding Torah and its mitzvos.

This is contrary to the opinion of those who err, fearing that a complete education will frighten the youth, in the sense of "if you grasp too much, you grasp nothing." The opposite is true: any compromise in the fundamental principles and matters of a system arouses distrust in the heart of the student—not only distrust in the educator himself, but also in the entire system that he advocates and represents.

Furthermore, and this is essential: a compromise, which by definition involves concession and deviation from the path—even a slight deviation from the path of absolute truth in the education of youth—is like a scratch on a seed being planted. Even a small scratch will eventually cause a major defect in the tree that grows from it, a defect that is difficult to foresee in advance. The analogy is understood.

On the other hand, one must take into account that all Yidden are believers, the children of believers. As the saying of our Rebbe, the author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch, goes: "A Yid neither wants to, nor is he able to, tear himself away from Elokus." If we see that in practical life there are those whose conduct in many matters does not align with the above—and furthermore, who even turn their conduct into a system contrary to the Torah of truth and its mitzvos—the one who contemplates this will realize the explanation:

A person is flesh and blood and possesses two souls: the nefesh Elokis and the nefesh habehamis. Therefore, he does not always stand up to the test. When he fails in some matter of Torah and mitzvos and does the opposite, one who has the courage of heart will not ignore the reality of the situation—that he did not stand up to the test and transgressed the will of the Creator, as well as his own true will and conscience. He who confesses and abandons his sin will be shown mercy; he returns to the straight path and is healed. But one who is faint-hearted, even toward himself, begins to justify himself and seeks excuses for his inappropriate conduct. Especially since, as our Sages say, one transgression leads to another, he feels an urgent need to justify his behavior—to justify it to himself and to others, and to restore the honor that was tarnished in his own eyes by his weakness. "Love covers all transgressions," especially self-love. And a bribe blinds—the bribe of self-love—it warps his intellect and "invites" a "logical" system, or even a worldview encompassing all of creation, that will justify his conduct and silence his thoughts of teshuvah by "explaining" that his conduct is not a failure or a fall, but rather an ascent; not a transgression, but on the contrary, a mitzvah.

These systems have, of course, also established modes of education that suit them: systems that preserve the "half-way"—education through half-compromise, and so on.

The common denominator in all of them is that they frame the deviation from the path, the fall, and the darkness as an ascent and light.

Therefore, education without compromises—in absolute negation of the approach that "times have changed and therefore one must **adapt** the Torah and mitzvos to them"—

Is not merely a matter of increasing the number of hours spent studying our Torah and increasing the number of mitzvos, laws, and customs in the educational program and the life of the student,

(Which leaves room for calculation and limitation—how much effort is worth investing in this),

But rather—education without compromises is **fundamentally** about grounding the student in the truth: that darkness is darkness and light is light; that the Torah is one and whole from the time it was given until now and forever; and that it is—in its entirety—a Torah of truth and a Torah of life (life in the literal sense, as well, in this world of ours).

May it be the will [of Hashem] that your gathering brings added courage and vitality to your work—the work of Hashem, the work of education and instruction—to bring to the sons and daughters of Yisrael the words of the living G-d, the word of Hashem in its entirety and purity (without mixing in human actions and schemes).

And through this, you will succeed in winning over the youth, to educate and lead them in the way of Hashem, the way of Torah and mitzvos, until "the small one shall become a thousand and the young one a mighty nation."

And in their days and in our days, these days will be turned into gladness and joy, and "no longer shall one teach his neighbor... for they shall all know Me, from the smallest of them to the greatest of them."

With respect and blessings for success,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4705

B"H, 7 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of Reshet Oholei Yosef Yitzchak Lubavitch
in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt speedily,
May Hashem keep them alive.

Peace and Blessing!

My hope is—even though there is no detailed information yet—that the matter of the *kaytanah* (summer camp) is progressing **as much** as is required. Since it seems they began behind schedule, the instruction of our Sages of blessed memory in such a case is well known, as transcribed by the Alter Rebbe in *Iggeres HaTeshuvah*, Chapter 9: "If he was accustomed to reading one page, he should read two pages, etc." This means that the effort in arranging the aforementioned must be double: the effort required for the current matters, plus the effort that should have been made some time ago. I will await detailed reports on this.

2) Although, due to the sheer obviousness of the matter, I did not write to the Village Committee to assist in the *kaytanah* matter **more and more**, for it is their benefit no less than the benefit of the *kaytanah* guests—and perhaps even more so—I am writing this now for added emphasis. Even though these words are addressed to the administration of the Reshet Oholei Yosef Yitzchak, they are trusted to convey these words exactly as they are to the Village Committee. I am confident in the **greatest possible** assistance on their part. And even if there are village matters that, on the face of it, cannot be carried out except at the expense of a deficit in the *kaytanah*, it is already known that a *mitzvah* whose time has passed takes precedence over many things. In this case, too, it is so: the *kaytanah* is a matter of time-sensitive urgency, and because the *kaytanah* guests will later disperse to many different places, there is a special importance to achieving the greatest possible perfection in all its details.

And if these words regarding assistance are said to the Village Committee, all the more so to all members of the Reshet administration, to all Oholei Yosef Yitzchak schools, and to the teaching staff, who must act in this to set an example for everyone else. And if, for whatever reason, some of them have not participated as much as is required, my hope is that upon receiving this letter, they too will involve themselves **in the manner mentioned above**, as the Alter Rebbe wrote in the aforementioned *Iggeres HaTeshuvah*...

4) For the time being, I have not received any information regarding a plan for utilizing the vacation days, or an arrangement for at least meeting with the students a few times during these weeks, or maintaining contact with them in an appropriate manner. Perhaps it is worth utilizing for such purposes the presence of the *shluchim* (may they live and be well) in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt speedily.

With blessings for good tidings and for outstanding success in all matters.

#### ***4705***

**The Shluchim:** See also above, letter 4646, and the references in the notes there.

## 4,706

B"H, 7 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 4 Menachem Av:

It is surprising and also distressing to read that there is no room to accept more students, even though they are requesting to be admitted. It is understood that in such a situation, one must take advantage of the *eis ratzon* of the girls' parents, as well as the girls themselves. A solution for the space must certainly be found, especially since it is now the summer months and, in any case, most of the day is not spent inside a classroom. Even if this entails some additional expense, it is worthwhile—especially since there is reason to hope that some of the summer students will remain to study at the institution afterwards. Through this, they will join the camp of *anash*, and perhaps their families as well; *kal v'chomer*.

I am sending this letter of mine with haste, and it is possible that the above can still be fully rectified. May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4707

B"H, 7 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 3 Menachem Av, in which you write regarding the offer you received from the esteemed Chossid, etc., R' Yitzchak Gansburg, may he live, concerning a position in the Oholei Yosef Yitzchak Lubavitch schools in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. You agreed to this and informed him of your consent in writing, but now a doubt has arisen for you, as your previous place of employment is offering for you to remain with them.

Based on the above, since you have already written your consent, the matter is now in the hands of R' Gansburg, the director of the institution. If they request any change from you, you must direct those making the request to Rabbi Gansburg. It is understood that without the latter’s consent, it is impossible to retract your agreement.

It is surely unnecessary to emphasize to one such as yourself that if in all times and places our Sages stated that fear [of Heaven] must precede wisdom, how much more so in our generation—a "generation of orphans"—and in the land upon which the eyes of Hashem your G-d are at all times, from the beginning of the year to the end of the year. This objective must always be before the eyes of the educator and teacher. Regardless of which subject he is teaching the students, the beginning and the foundation of wisdom is the fear of Hashem. He must utilize every opportunity, and even create opportunities, to instill yiras shamayim and ahavas Hashem in his students. This will serve as a strong foundation for their absorption of their studies as required, and with appropriate hisbonenus, one can always find ways to bring the aforementioned into practice.

With blessings for good tidings and for success.

## 4708

B"H, 7 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of Tuesday, in which you write that the matters of Chassidus and Chassidim have touched your heart, and you ask for guidance on how to study Chassidus and understand it, as one who was born into it, etc.

It is surely known to you the story of our Sages that the receiving of the Torah was through the precedence of *na'aseh* (we will do) to *nishmah* (we will hear), even though the Torah is a profound wisdom, as it is written, "For it is your wisdom and your understanding in the eyes of the nations." Despite this, its reception is possible only in the manner mentioned—that is to say, through *kabalas ol* (acceptance of the yoke). This applies to every part of our Torah, and the teachings of Chassidus are included in this, as is understood.

My intention is that one should not demand at the beginning of one's study that one immediately understand all the nuances and details within this Torah, and that the understanding should come without exertion, without difficulty, and the like. For this is not the way to grasp any wisdom whatsoever, and certainly not the wisdom of our Torah. This is also expressed in the concise and pointed adage of our Sages: "If one says, 'I have not labored and yet I have found,' do not believe him." Yet, they immediately informed us as well: "If one says, 'I have labored and have not found,' do not believe him." This provides a guarantee that one will certainly achieve the goal of understanding this Torah if one only devotes oneself to its study as required. Based on the above, it is understood that the order of study depends on the knowledge and talents of the student. Therefore, you should meet with the elder Chassidim in your vicinity, and they will instruct you in this based on the experience they have had in similar cases.

You are surely aware of the branch of Tzeirei Agudas Chabad in Bnei Brak, and a meeting with them will certainly bring benefit in the matter mentioned as well.

As with all parts of the Torah, where "great is study because it leads to action," so too in the teachings of Chassidus, it is the case that the study must be for the purpose of doing. But even this is "not in heaven," nor is it "far off," and the matter depends only on one's will.

With blessings for success in all the above and for good tidings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4,309

B"H, 7 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, R' A. Shklovsky, shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

I confirm receipt of your letter from Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av, and I thank you for the seforim you sent, as well as for the effort you invested in this.

You mention in your letter regarding Rabbi Zakheim; I would be interested to know if he was a relative of Rabbi Binyamin Zakheim, who was a Rav in Yekaterinoslav (where my father, of blessed memory, also served as Rav).

I hope that you have already met with the avreichim, the shluchim shlita from here, and that they will also have the opportunity to visit you in your beis knesses and beis medrash, even though their time is very limited. I hope to hear good news from you regarding them as well. May it be the will of Hashem that their shlichus, which is primarily intended for raising the stature of anash and raising the stature of Chabad, should be successful. May these days be transformed into gladness and joy in their time and in ours, and as the Gemara relates (Megillah 29a), may all the batei knesses and batei medrash, which are called a "mikdash me'at," be transplanted to the Holy Land, and may we merit the Third Beis Hamikdash speedily in our days.

Enclosed is a kuntres that was published recently; surely you will use it to bring merit to the public, and the merit of the public will depend upon you.

With blessings.

For the time being, the following seforim have been received: Kesem Perachi - B"Ch. Pardes Pischai. Da'as Rashi.

***

4,309

R' A. Shklovsky: R' Avraham Shklovsky. Additional letters to him - above 4,298, and in the references noted there.

The shluchim: See also above, letter 4,246, and in the references noted there.

## 4512

4512

B.H., 8 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Committee for the Establishment of the High School - Seminary in Kfar Chabad
May they live

Greetings and blessings!

It is surprising that for a long time now, there has been no news from you regarding the progress of matters, especially since time is short. Although it is surely unnecessary to remind you of the points below, nevertheless, for the sake of emphasis, I am repeating them once again:

1) Since there will surely be differences of opinion regarding the curriculum, I have already written my **clear** opinion, without any doubt, that the program must be that of the most chareidi Bais Yaakov schools in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established). As is known here, this refers to the Bais Yaakov in Jerusalem (may it be rebuilt and established). It is understood that one must add to their studies, at the very least, a *shiur* in Chassidus. It is also understood that they have the capacity to increase in matters of *yiras shamayim* even more than the aforementioned Bais Yaakov schools. And *kal v'chomer*: if in their countries of origin, where the matter was connected with danger—the danger of the parents and also the danger of the children (may they live)—they nevertheless did not fear anything, and only through this did they save their children for the life of the World to Come and also for the life of this world, how much more so when they are in a place where there is no danger at all, and the land is called by all the nations "the Holy Land," and our Torah testifies that this is "a land which Hashem your G-d seeks out, from the beginning of the year to the end of the year." It is simple that one should not be impressed by the mockers, nor should one be impressed by other concealments and hidden obstacles that may arise. It is essential that the high school-seminary be specifically according to the aforementioned program.

2) In accordance with the above, it is understood and simple that in choosing the teaching staff, first and foremost, one must consider that they should not influence the students negatively, *chas v'shalom*, regarding *yiras shamayim*, Yiddishkeit, and its matters. A positive influence in this regard can be achieved not only through open speech, but even through conduct and the like. And *kal v'chomer*: if regarding the study of Torah, the teacher must be "like an angel," as the Sages instruct, and this applies even to those whose intellect is strong, how much more so in our case.

3) And let the above not be light in your eyes. As is known, the results of the methods of the "compromisers" who delude themselves into saying that by conceding on minor matters they will gain on essential matters, etc.—experience has shown the exact opposite. The concession they make serves as an opening for more and more concessions, and after some time, they are surprised themselves at where they have arrived. There is no need to elaborate on something that is visibly apparent.

4) It is certainly unnecessary to remind you also that it is most appropriate to cooperate with those who are working on the establishment of the continuation school for the Reshet schools, for essentially their goal is one. It is to be hoped that within a short time they will find the way to unite the two institutions as well. Certainly, there are many advantages in this, as "a candle for one is a candle for a hundred," and they will be able to utilize the strengths for strengthening and development, instead of doubling their work. I will await good news regarding all that has been said, as well as information regarding the candidates for teaching positions, who is vouching for them regarding their upbringing and their *yiras shamayim*, and who is vouching for their knowledge. Since all beginnings are difficult, one must invest all the effort and energy required to establish the matter as it should be.

I await good news and sign with a blessing for great success in arranging matters in accordance with the instruction of our Torah, the Torah of life, as explained by our holy Nasiim.

#### 4512

**For the establishment of the High School - Seminary:** See regarding this above, Vol. 12, Letter 3828, and the references in the footnotes there. Above 4308, 4404, 4451, 4467, 4526. Below 4622.

**Methods of the compromisers:** See also above, Letter 4463, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 4611

B"H, 8 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and industrious, engaged in communal needs,
R' Yisroel Sussman, may he live

Shalom u'Bracha,

In response to your letter from Erev Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av, I trust that in the meantime you have received my letter responding to the questions in your previous correspondence.

Regarding what you wrote concerning instructions on how to speak to others, and so on—it would take too long to write it all out, especially since it is unnecessary, as in our generation we have been privileged to have the instructions of our holy Nasi, of blessed memory, published regarding such matters. This is explained in the holy sichos printed in Likkutei Dibburim, Sefer HaSichos, and many other places.

The main point is—and this is something we see tangibly—that words of pleasantness and drawing others near are incomparably more effective than the opposite approach. Although it is clear that this applies only to the "garment"—meaning the manner of approach to others, which must be in ways of pleasantness—the "inner" aspect must be with the appropriate firmness in all matters of Torah and mitzvos. However, regarding this too, it is said, "Little by little I will drive them out," and one should not demand everything at the very first meeting. Surely for someone like you, further elaboration on this is superfluous.

The most essential point of all is that "one action is better than a thousand sighs." It is clear that a lack of action, even if justified by many reasons, will certainly yield no results and bring about no improvement. Conversely, through action, it is highly probable that fruits will be seen in a short time, and certainly over the course of time.

I trust you will meet with the avreichim shluchim, shlit"a, and that they will utilize their stay in Eretz Yisrael, may it be built and established, in the appropriate manner.

With blessings,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4,612

B"H, 8 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and scholarly individual, engaged in communal needs, R' Moshe Tzvi, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 27 Tammuz, in which you write regarding the idea of appealing to the nations of the world concerning teshuvah, etc., and ask for my opinion on the matter:

Without diminishing the value of the concept in and of itself, anyone who observes the current state of affairs in our time will surely reach the conclusion that, for the time being, there is no prospect that such an appeal will find an echo among those who have the capacity to effect change in this regard. And those who would respond to such a call are surely not in need of such an awakening, nor do they have any influence on the course of events. As is brought in various places, the situation in the world is such that, unfortunately, it is currently not prepared for teshuvah out of love, nor even for teshuvah out of fear, but only for teshuvah out of fear of immediate punishment; even punishment that is delayed does not have an effect.

In accordance with the above perspective, it is a pity to expend the energy and effort that would be invested in the aforementioned—at a time when they are required for so many vital matters, including those that cannot be accomplished by others.

From the above, there is also further proof and evidence that all those who say that the redemption of Yisrael will come through the natural order, slowly and gradually, and who rely on the well-known Yerushalmi and on what is written in the commentary attributed to the Ramban on Shir HaShirim, are ignoring **reality** and are also failing to look at the past. As it is written, "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt"—where the redemption also came by way of a leap from the forty-nine gates of impurity to a complete redemption, with a high hand. This is also the answer to the question: if we assume that the aforementioned perspective is correct (and one who observes with integrity cannot arrive at any other conclusion), then what hope is there that the world will be perfected under the sovereignty of the Almighty? The answer to this is that such is the nature of the redemption of Yisrael, through which the redemption of the world will occur; that in the blink of an eye, the hosts of Hashem will emerge from the Egypt of the world’s nakedness and be carried on eagles' wings to become a kingdom of priests and a holy nation. And according to what is explained in Tanya, Chapter 37, in a similar manner, the revolution will come to the entire creation. And this is easy to understand.

With blessing.

## 4613

B"H, 8 Menachem-Av, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the leaders and distinguished members of the Kehilas Nidchei Yisrael
in Mexico
May Hashem preserve them and grant them life,

Shalom u’Bracha!

As the shaliach of the Tomchei Temimim Lubavitch Yeshivos, the esteemed and pious chossid, a man of action who engages in communal needs with great energy, Rabbi Mordechai Dov Teleshevsky, is currently in your camp, I am certain that you will extend to him a helping hand and provide assistance so that he may fulfill his mission in the best possible manner. This is especially pertinent given the pressing situation of the Lubavitch Torah institutions, which are in urgent need of substantial financial support.

In these days of the month of Menachem-Av, which begins with matters of destruction but is immediately followed by matters of comfort and building—for He is the One who comforts Tzion and builds Yerushalayim—the essence of this comfort is our holy Torah. As our Sages of blessed memory stated, "We have nothing left but this Torah," and our shuls and batei medrash are the "miniature sanctuaries" of our time. Therefore, any support given to these halls of Torah, where the perfect Torah of Hashem is studied with yiras shamayim, is an act of comfort and building, and it brings closer the true and complete Geulah, when these days will be transformed into days of joy and gladness, speedily in our days.

And in the measure of the Ribono shel Olam—measure for measure, and many times over—may all those who act and facilitate be blessed with abundant success, both materially and spiritually.

With respect and blessing,

## 4,314

4,314

By the Grace of Hashem, 8 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 27 Tammuz:

I was alarmed by your line of reasoning that you wish to begin a regimen of fasts, relying on what is stated in the *Iggeres HaTeshuvah* of our Alter Rebbe, Chapter 3, with the addition that you have the capacity to do so even during the summer months. If you will listen to me, you will abandon this notion immediately upon receiving my letter. If you have already practiced this three times, you should assemble three of your friends to annul your vow. I have already spoken of this elsewhere: it is not as those who err claim—that they are being "stringent" with themselves by fasting and the like. On the contrary, it is a leniency, because through this they are hindered from *Torah* and *avodah* to the extent required of them. As our Alter Rebbe testified regarding our generations—and as he writes in Chapter 3, that in generations such as ours it is forbidden, etc.—surely no further explanation is needed, for we see the results of these fasts tangibly. Even though not all bodies are the same—in some, the result is seen sooner, and in others later—the common denominator is that, over time, it prevents one from *Torah* and *avodah*.

If the intention is truly to diminish "fat and blood," etc. (though in truth, it is not understood why one should act contrary to the instruction of the Baal Shem Tov, that *avodah* must be in a manner of "you shall surely leave it with **Him**," and not through fasts and self-affliction—and if, nevertheless, you insist on the aforementioned *avodah*), it is already known that the instruction of the Rebbe Maharash is that one can perform many fasts by delaying speech, thought, etc. (see *Sefer Toldos Maharash*, p. 72). We see tangibly that: a) it is more difficult to carry out these fasts than a simple fast; b) one does not derive the same self-satisfaction from this as one sometimes does from a simple fast.

The main point in all of the above is that all the children of Israel who tremble at the word of Hashem are in a state of heavy warfare against the double and redoubled darkness of the *galus* of *ikvesa d'Meshicha*. Especially since each of us remains as a brand plucked from the fire, we must perform even those tasks that were incumbent upon the holy ones who were killed *al kiddush Hashem*, *Hy"d*. Every free moment must be utilized for this—namely, to illuminate the darkness of the world with the candle of *mitzvah*, *Torah*, and the light of *Torah*, which is the *Torah* of Chassidus. We have seen tangibly that success in this is greater when the body does not interfere due to hunger, thirst, or sadness. The rectification of an individual is a private matter, and we do not know how it is weighed in the Heavenly Court. Conversely, efforts to bring the hearts of the children of Israel closer to our Father in Heaven—specifically through joy and love—yield fruits in this world. As the saying of our Rebbes, our holy *Nesi'im*, goes: "There is a strength in *ta'amulah* (outreach) that does not return empty."

I have already written, and I believe you agree, that we should not seek out crooked paths and detours on how to ascend to the House of Hashem, when our holy Rebbes have paved for us a broad path upon which every Jew—from the smallest of the small to the greatest of the great—can walk even in daily life, and make for Him, *yisbarach*, a dwelling place in the lower worlds through the conduct of "in all your ways know Him." May it be Hashem's will that I merit to hear in your return letter the good news that you have abandoned the notion mentioned at the beginning of your letter, and that you see good fruit in your efforts, carried out with joy, to illuminate your portion of the world and your "four cubits," even literally within your home, by having a body that is healthy—literally—and health in your inwardness, so that the soul is healthy as well.

Awaiting good tidings in your reply.

#### ***4,314***

**Instruction of the Baal Shem Tov:** See also above, Letter 4,384, and the references in the notes there.

**Instruction of the Rebbe Maharash:** See also above, Vol. 12, Letter 4,159, and the references there.

415

B”H, 10 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn

To the Administration of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in Lod
May they live,

Peace and blessing!

Following my letter of the 7th of Menachem Av—in the interim, letters from the 4th and 7th of Menachem Av have been received, as have the photographs, maps, and recordings. Many thanks.

I have already written that the fundraising campaign for the printing school, like all campaigns, must utilize the *eis ratzon* as much as is necessary. It is understood that as time passes from the event and the laying of the cornerstone, the enthusiasm wanes, and consequently, it becomes more difficult to return and renew it.

Regarding what you wrote about sending letters and the like: in such matters, the world is one, and merely sending letters serves only as plowing and preparation for the subsequent visit. **It is impossible** to rely on this alone.

From this, it is also understood that announcing now the laying of a cornerstone for a new building in Kfar Chabad for the Lod Yeshiva, etc., is a clear detriment to the printing school campaign (in addition to reducing the income even for the new building, since it comes in close proximity to the printing school campaign). Instead of contemplating the organization of a new campaign, one must contemplate utilizing **all** the possibilities of the printing school campaign.

Regarding your letter of 15 Sivan, section eight: it is understood that it is not relevant who the official borrower is, but rather who will use the loan money—for upon them fall the expenses connected to this.

Regarding section nine there, you understood my opinion regarding the direction of the printing school. I only wish I would merit that you begin with actual operations, and I pray that due to the delay, there will not be a loss that cannot be rectified, G-d forbid.

In the protocol of 7 Sivan attached to the above: there is a decision regarding Ashkenazi students. I am interested in knowing the results of this, or at least a **detailed** description of the steps taken to achieve the aforementioned. For I fear, in the absence of any mention of this in any of your letters, that the decision remained on paper, and in the language currently in vogue—they moved on to the order of the day.

I will also raise another point (and it is a primary one, as it serves to test the interest of the *Roshei Yeshiva* and *Roshei Mesivta* in the affairs of the Yeshiva): what do the students do during the entire twenty-four hours of Shabbos, and who is the *mashgiach* for this?

Regarding your questions from time to time about accepting such-and-such person for a certain position and the like, it is understood that the administration must decide on the spot, according to the candidate's talents after a trial period, or according to his success in his work if he has already served elsewhere in a similar position. But it is understood: a) that one must be wary of those who are *fremde* (alien) in spirit, for several reasons. b) *Mesiras nefesh* for the affairs of Chabad is more likely among those who belong to Chabad, and this *mesiras nefesh* is essential, especially in Chabad institutions. c) Those who have been drawn close to Chabad (*gevorene*, not *geborene*) and have become connected—one must draw them close even more. Especially according to our Sages, who in several matters are more meticulous than those who were born into it. As is evident in practice—and this also serves as an answer to your questions regarding Mr. ... and his family, may they live. From this, it is understood that they must be arranged in a way that they will be satisfied (and certainly there are several ways to arrange this—without the matters suffering)...

With blessing,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

A. Quint
Secretary

***

**415**

**Regarding the event:** See above, letter 481, and the references in the notes there.

**Who the official borrower is:** See also above, letter 441.

## 4116

4116

B.H., 10 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Honorable Rabbi, the Honorable Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, occupied with communal needs, etc.,
Moreinu HaRav Ephraim Eliezer, may he live, HaKohen

Greetings and Blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from the eve of Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av, as well as your previous letter. As you requested, I will, G-d willing, mention the matters you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Nasi of Israel.

Regarding what you noted in the Commentary on the Mishnah, Nazir, Chapter 8, Mishnah 6, concerning the second essential element—that the shaving of the metzora occurs after the immersion—this contradicts what is written in the Torah.

One should also note Nega'im, Chapter 14, Mishnah 2, and Nazir, Chapter 6, Mishnah 6 (and we derive the first shaving from the second)—which implies that if he immersed and then shaved, his immersion was not valid. This requires further study in the Rambam, Hilchos Tumas Tzara'as, Chapter 11, Halacha 4, and the commentaries there.

Initially, I wanted to suggest that the Commentary on the Mishnah originally read "after the immersion" (*achar hatvilah*), meaning "after—the immersion."

When the small line above the word **after** (*achar*) was dragged or omitted, the subsequent printers did not notice it to correct it.

Alternatively, it should read "after the washing" (*achar hakivisah*), as per the wording of the verse. See Tiferes Yisrael and Mishnah Acharonah (Nega'im, Chapter 14, Mishnah 2), and Minchas Chinuch, Mitzvah 173, subsection 7.

However, I saw that this still does not resolve a more fundamental question: What is the Rambam's intent in mentioning the immersion here at all? It is **entirely** irrelevant to the case at hand, as he does not mention the sprinkling, the birds, etc.

Therefore, it seems to me that there is a printing error here. It should read "after the determination" (*achar hahachlat*) or "after the decision" (*achar hahachlatah*). The typesetter, not understanding the meaning and seeing **"and he shall immerse"** (*vetaval*) in the text, "corrected" it.
