## 4353

4353

B”H, 24 Menachem Av, 5716

Brooklyn.

The Administration of Yeshivas Achei Temimim Lubavitch
Rishon LeZion
May Hashem preserve them

*Greetings and Blessings!*

*In anticipation of the building dedication, and in response to the letter and telegram, a telegram from the Rebbe Shlita was sent today, and the text is transcribed here:*

For the dedication of the building of Achei Temimim in Rishon LeZion: May it be the will that it be a source of extraordinary success in magnifying and glorifying the perfect Torah, both the revealed and the inner dimensions, restoring the G-dly and animal souls, and loving [Hashem] with both your inclinations. May you raise students who will disseminate the wellsprings of the words of the living G-d, and may your avodah be with a whole heart and in circles that expand ever further, until the earth is filled with the knowledge of Hashem, with the true and complete Geulah through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

With blessings,

**Menachem Schneerson**

*I trust it was received in due time. Surely you will inform us here with the necessary details regarding what is being done. Thank you in advance.*

*With respect and blessings,*

Secretary

## 4354

B"H, 24 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the members of the Spiritual Committee in Kfar Chabad,
May Hashem protect them and grant them life.

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of the minutes of your meeting from the 13th and 15th of Menachem Av. May it be His will that this serves as a good beginning for a positive change in the state of all spiritual matters in Kfar Chabad, which will, as a matter of course, lead to a change in the state of all material matters in Kfar Chabad. The virtue of resolutions adopted in public is already well-known, as explained in Kuntres Hecholtzu. It is obvious that the members of the committee must serve as a living example—one who preaches well and also practices well—as is known regarding the concept of adding "moistening to cause others to be moistened" over mere "moistening." May the Ribono shel Olam grant you success in improving the situation more and more, for whatever is good is boundless and infinite, since its source is the Essence of the true, boundless Good. I look forward to hearing good news regarding this, with all the details as needed.

With blessings for outstanding success in your holy work.

## 4555

B"H, 24 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 18 Menachem Av, in which you write your reasoning regarding your studies for the coming year.

If you will listen to my opinion, you should study with diligence and perseverance in your current location at the holy Yeshivah Tomchei Temimim in Brunoy. Even if, in your opinion, there is no suitable study partner there for you, you must serve as a living example to the avreichim from Morocco by advancing in your study of our holy Torah from level to level—studying Torah with yiras shamayim, together with the avodah of tefillah. The immense importance of the influence you will have on the younger avreichim from Morocco, so that they too may advance from level to level, is beyond measure. The Giver of the Torah is guaranteed to fulfill His promise—to anyone who gives charity, whether materially or spiritually as mentioned above—that his mind and heart will become a thousand times more refined.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

## 4596

B"H, 24 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, I was pleased to read that despite the many burdens of your livelihood and the long hours of work that entails, you have nevertheless taken upon yourself—*bli neder*—to participate in communal study of *Nigleh* and Chassidus. This is certainly in addition to the three well-known daily *shiurim* in Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya, especially since you can fulfill these while traveling to and from your place of work.

May it be the will of the Creator and Guide of the world that your livelihood matters be eased, so that your current resolution may be implemented without difficulty. May you merit the fulfillment of the directive that one must exert effort in Torah—*yagata u’matzata*—specifically through adding to the aforementioned resolutions. It is already known that our Torah promises that whoever adds, they add to him. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding the above, as well as regarding the departure of your family members from the straits to the expanse, with kindness and mercy.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4557

4557

B"H, 24 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the avreich Yaakov Emanuel, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 21 Menachem Av: It is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding your current activities in Torah and its mitzvos. This is especially so in light of what has been discussed—that precisely during the days referred to by the world as "vacation days," one should increase their Torah study and their meticulousness in the performance of mitzvos. Since, at such times, one is free from many of the distractions that occupy one during the rest of the year, there is an additional reason for those with certain character traits: precisely during these days, when one is not obligated by the yeshiva’s regulations to study Torah for a specific number of hours each day, one demonstrates that out of genuine desire, and without external command, they are adding significantly to that number. Furthermore, you must serve as a living example in your current environment of what a yeshiva student is in general, and a student of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in particular—so that others may see and act accordingly, as is explained in the words of Chazal and our Sages in many places.

In connection with your birthday, when one’s mazal is strengthened and wisdom is also increased—as it is written, "and a multitude of years shall teach wisdom"—you will surely contemplate all of the above and more, and increase your efforts accordingly. May Hashem grant you success for a good and blessed year in the study of Torah and the fulfillment of its mitzvos.

With blessing.

#### ***4557***

Printed in Likutei Sichos, Vol. 8, p. 367; completed based on the secretariat's copy.

**Yaakov Emanuel, may he live:** Shochat, Toronto.

## 4558

4558

B"H, 24 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Nosson Dovid, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Monday: as you requested, once it is received here, a calendar for the coming year featuring portraits of our holy Rebbes, of blessed memory, will be sent to you.

Regarding what you wrote about being occupied with compiling the biography of Rav, etc.: you have surely seen the *Toldos Tana'im V'Amora'im* by Rabbi Hyman, in which he notes the sources where the statements of the Tana'im and Amora'im can be found, etc. It seems to me that decades ago there was also a special booklet regarding Rav, though I do not recall the author's name at the moment. However, regarding any matter that can increase the fear of Hashem, the love of Hashem, and the love of Torah, may the strength of those involved in this be commended.

As for your observation regarding the precision of the language of our Sages—"Rav So-and-so said," or "Rav So-and-so said in the name of Rav So-and-so," or "in the name of So-and-so"—see the sources noted in the footnotes.*

With blessings.

#### ***4558***

*) Chullin 113b, Rashi s.v. Ha; Bava Basra 114b, Rashbam s.v. Mishum; Seder HaDoros, in his introduction (4b), and the sources cited there.

**R' Nosson Dovid:** Rosenblum, Buenos Aires. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, 3443, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

**Toldos Rav:** See also Ma'archos Tana'im V'Amora'im—Rav-Shmuel (Y. Kanovitz, Jerusalem 5734).

## 4459

[24 Menachem Av, 5716]

In response to your letter of 19 Menachem Av, I was pleased to read that you will be a shaliach tzibbur in the shul where they suggested that you speak words of hisorerus before them on Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur. It is surely superfluous to point out that you should do so on Rosh Hashanah, on Yom Kippur, and on the days in between, particularly on Shabbos Shuvah—as you will be there then as well, according to the schedule for the coming year. Though it is understood—in a manner that will not weary the listeners.

Therefore, at times it is necessary to state that this is a discussion of give-and-take, not a moralistic sermon. You will surely find the appropriate methods and the appropriate words. May Hashem grant you success.

#### ***4459***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 543.

4660

B.H., 26 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings,

In response to your letter of 25 Tammuz, in which you write once again regarding something you found written in a certain book—it is astonishing that you remain in doubt as to how to relate to what is written there, at a time when it is the opposite of the simple faith of the children of Israel, and uses appalling expressions regarding that which is found in the words of our Sages, namely, the Torah Shebe'al Peh.

Regarding the author’s attempt to find support in what he found in Seforim of Kabbalah—he did not understand what is written there. That is to say, he took the **words** literally. This is similar to one who learns "the hand of Hashem," "the eye of Hashem," as being literal—Heaven forbid—meaning that Hashem has a hand, like a human being, an eye, like a human being, etc. And the aforementioned has fulfilled (and if only the author would fulfill the directive of the Baal Shem Tov, for) he has entered into the category regarding which there was concern—as is explained in the root of the laws of Tefillah, end of section 2 (by the Tzemach Tzedek): that one should not study Seforim of Kabbalah, for one who does not know how to strip the matters of their physicality becomes very materialized through this study, when he gives a mental image to matters of Elokus, etc. And all the more so, the aforementioned is literally "cutting the shoots."

I will provide one example—comparing what you copied in your letter from what the aforementioned author writes regarding one of the seven names that may not be erased—the name *Elokim*—to what is written, to distinguish, in the holy Zohar, Vol. I, 12a, which states: "And you shall know today, etc., that Hashem is the *Elokim*—to include the name *Elokim* within the name *Havayah*, to know that they are one and there is no separation between them." And in Zohar, Vol. II, 161b: "To know that Hashem is the *Elokim*—this is the inclusion of the entire mystery of faith, of the entire Torah, the inclusion of the upper and the lower," see there. And similarly in Zohar, Vol. III, 264a, and in many other places.

Likewise, regarding what he writes as a difficulty—how there could be evening and morning during the first three days—a question that is **full** in the Midrashim and the commentaries. The language he copies is, "And there came one of our Sages and decided, without hesitation, regarding some of the statements of our Sages and some Kabbalah, that the Creator then created a type of light that served only for three days, etc."—and this is the **exact opposite** of the words of our Sages, of whom it is said, "Everything that a veteran student is destined to innovate **was already said to Moshe at Sinai.**"

Likewise, what you copied in your letter from the aforementioned author: "The view of the Torah of secrets is that the first creation that the Creator created at the beginning of time were the laws of nature." Anyone who flips through the writings of the Arizal, even a **beginner**, would laugh at him (if this were not a matter of "cutting the shoots," Heaven forbid). And if you are copying in your letter from what is written in the book *Leshem Shevo V'Achlama*, one will see there as well how much the aforementioned matters are the opposite of what is stated in the Torah of secrets.

Likewise, what you copied further on: "Those who want to understand that even the first three (days) were also of twenty-four hours of our time, this is a 'stomach-opinion' [a baseless conjecture] and is not based at all on the tradition of our Sages"—this is the **opposite** of what is written in the **Torah Shebe'al Peh.** How wretched is our generation, that those who label the words of our Sages as a "stomach-opinion" are reading this book, at a time when—according to the Shulchan Aruch—its ruling is already clear, and regarding the book itself—if only they had burned the manuscript before it was printed.

Likewise, what you copy—further on—that the Torah begins the count of days from "Let there be light," and before that the earth was already *tohu* and *bohu* and darkness and wind and water, and the creation of all these is not mentioned in the Torah at all—this is the known position of the Karaites, who did not believe in the Torah Shebe'al Peh. For in the Torah Shebe'al Peh it is stated that the heavens and the earth, light and darkness, were created on the first day, and according to our faith, the Torah Shebe'al Peh is also included in the category of Torah.

One merit can perhaps be found for the author of the book: that perhaps the copy you have is not accurate. But if the copy is accurate—it has already been stated above that the aforementioned book should be burned according to the law of the Shulchan Aruch.

Incidentally, regarding what you copy from the aforementioned author about the opinions of scientists, etc.—it seems that the author does not understand what is written in science books either. He relies on the **phrasing** brought in textbooks **for beginners**, for one who knows the **foundations** of science and the conclusions in this area, specifically in recent times, knows that there is **no** contradiction whatsoever to what is stated, that the six days of creation were each twenty-four hours long, etc. And furthermore, scientists say that not only now, but also in the future, **it is impossible** for science to contradict the aforementioned.

As I have elaborated elsewhere.

The conclusion from the above—if he will listen to my opinion, he will not keep the book in his vicinity and will warn others about it.

With blessings,
On behalf of the Rebbe Shlita,
Secretary

## 4651

4651

B"H, 28 Menachem-Av, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

The Administration of Igud Talmidei HaYeshivos
May Hashem grant them life

*Greetings and Blessings!*

*Transcribed herein is the text of the telegram that the Rebbe shlita sent to the Kinus:*

My blessing to the Kinus for success in revealing the strengths of the Chareidi youth in general, and the yeshiva students in particular, to increase Torah Temimah and pure Yiras Shamayim, to glorify it, and to disseminate its wellsprings; and to work with the necessary energy to raise the banner of the elder Israel by infusing Yiddishkeit into all circles of the Children of Israel, and primarily by capturing all the youth for the Torah of life and its mitzvos.

Immediately to Teshuvah, to Torah and mitzvos, immediately to redemption from the physical and spiritual exile to true freedom.

**Menachem Schneerson**

I trust this was received in due time. And surely you will inform us here with the necessary details regarding what was done. Thank you in advance.

With respect and blessing,

A. Quint
Secretary

#### ***4651***

**To the Kinus:** Which was held in connection with the visit of the shluchim to Eretz Yisrael, which is discussed above in Letter 4596, and in the notes indicated there.

In preparation for this Kinus, a letter was sent by the Secretariat to Anash and Chabad institutions, in which it was stated, among other things:

In the itinerary of the mission of the aforementioned shluchim, a Kinus of our students and the students of the yeshivos in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, was scheduled for Sunday, 28 Menachem-Av, upcoming. With this, we wish to awaken and request of all Chabad institutions in every place to send a letter or a telegram of blessing to the aforementioned Kinus, through which they will express personal participation in the Kinus and add much to the essence of the Kinus and to the entire matter of the aforementioned shlichus.

## 4602

B"H, 28 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

My letters in response to your previous correspondence surely arrived in due time, and I hereby acknowledge receipt of your letter from the end of Tammuz, which was somewhat delayed in reaching me.

Regarding what you wrote about a lack of seforim, you should have provided more details. It is understood that as much as possible, these should be seforim from the Kehot Publication Society, as it will be easier to obtain them.

You surely meet often with the shluchim, the avreichim, the rabbanim shlit"a, and you should utilize their visits to Eretz Yisrael t"v for your own avodah in the kerem of Chabad. You should surely report in appropriate detail regarding this as well, and may Hashem grant you success.

A rumor has reached me (and I wish it were false) that there are those who are hindering you in the *hafatzas hamaayanos* among bnei Torah. You should certainly not be impressed by this at all. With "ways of pleasantness," you should continue to disseminate the wellsprings of Torah—for these are its ways—and together with this, it is called *tushiyah*, for it weakens (*metashet*) the strength of all those who oppose its dissemination. And as for anyone who says that this rumor is not fitting—our Sages have already said that even a rumor that is pleasing in his eyes will be lost, chas v'shalom. Fortunate is your portion in this holy work, and in the end, your hand will be on top, in accordance with the well-known ruling of the Beis Din Shel Maalah.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe shlit"a,

Secretary

#### ***4602***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 20, p. 619, and completed based on the secretariat's copy.

**The shluchim:** See also above, Letter 4546, and the references in the notes there.

## 4663

*[28 Menachem Av, 5716]*

In response to your letter from Thursday, in which you write regarding thoughts that wander and intrude even at inappropriate times:

It is well known what is written in Tanya in several places, and specifically in Chapter 28—see there. The same applies to Iggeres HaKodesh, end of Siman 25 (145a). One of the methods for dealing with this is to daven and learn from inside a sefer. Even when one needs to study by heart, one should keep the sefer containing the subject matter open. When you feel your thoughts wandering, look at the letters, for the letters hold and clarify the mind.

#### ***4663***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 403.

## 4654

B"H, 29 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 21 Menachem Av.

At an auspicious time, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Since you are the one bringing the request, you should surely increase your diligence and perseverance in the study of Torah and the performance of its mitzvos with *hiddur*, for through this, Hashem will hasten the fulfillment of your requests.

Regarding what you wrote—that it is difficult for you to hold your thoughts and concentrate during your learning and davening—the advised counsel for this is already known: to learn from the *sefer* and to daven from the *Siddur*. Even when you are contemplating a concept and processing it in your mind, the *sefer* containing the subject you are contemplating should be open before you at that moment. If you feel your thoughts beginning to wander, look inside the text, for the letters hold, illuminate, and explain.

With blessings,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4665

B"H, 29 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned one, who engages in the needs of the community,
Rabbi Yaakov Eliezer, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your letter of 22 Menachem Av, in which you write regarding the arrangements for the Melaveh Malkah for their arrival, and regarding your reasoning concerning the manner of your avodah in the future.

It is surely known to you that the esteemed, multi-faceted activist, R' Moshe Zalman and his son Dovid, may they live, are currently here. From my conversation with them, I have formed the impression that there is much work to be done at Yeshivas Oholei Yosef Yitzchok in Melbourne, especially in the current situation. For surely, if these days and weeks are not utilized to expand the yeshivah as much as is necessary, it is impossible to know if this opportunity will exist later on. From this, it is understood that all those for whom this matter is dear to their heart and soul must work on this with all their strength. Especially since it is imperative to establish immediately a school for girls in the Chabad style (under the name Beis Rivkah or Beis Sarah). In that same conversation, I was also convinced that this opportune time must be utilized not only to found the school, but also for its expansion and possible growth. Therefore, I told the aforementioned that they should not wait to establish the girls' school until they find a building or a house—and certainly not until they build a structure. Rather, since rumor has it that there is a vacant room in the yeshivah building or a women’s section or the like, they should create a separate entrance and exit for the girls (and also a fence in the courtyard so that there will be no intermingling), and immediately upon the return of the aforementioned to Melbourne, they should begin the studies and publicize this accordingly...

From this, my perspective is also understood regarding the reasoning for the manner of your activity about which you write in your letter. My opinion is clear: you should throw yourself into the expansion of Oholei Yosef Yitzchok, and into the founding and expansion of the Chabad girls' school, Beis Rivkah (or Beis Sarah), naturally together with the rest of anash who are in your camp, and with all the necessary vigor. It is already known what the ruling of our holy Torah is regarding how great a mitzvah is when its time may pass. And just as you have seen until now the blessing from Above with extraordinary success (even though, to my regret, not all the possibilities that were given were utilized as much as necessary), I am confident that in the future as well, the work of your hands will be blessed with extraordinary success as mentioned—only that it must be utilized more and more. If only your time and energy will suffice to utilize it as much as is necessary. I will await good news and details that you have begun the appropriate preparations for the aforementioned activities, and the activities themselves, immediately.

With blessings for success in all the aforementioned.

## 4636

B"H, 29 Menachem Av, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 23 Menachem Av, in which you write regarding the dream and the incident, etc.

You should absolutely put all of this out of your mind. It is certain that not all the mezuzos in your home are kosher, and they must be checked. It would also be well to check the tefillin of your husband, shlita. It is appropriate that before lighting the candles every Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov, you set aside a few coins for tzedakah. Your husband should do the same every weekday of Monday and Thursday, setting aside a few coins for tzedakah before davening. You should strengthen your trust in the Creator of the world and its Guide, who is the essence of good and desires to bestow good upon every single one of the Bnei Yisrael. It is only necessary to create a vessel suitable for receiving the blessing, which is a lifestyle consistent with the instructions of our Torah, the Torah of life. The merit of your ancestors will surely assist you. I will await good news regarding all of the above, and may it be soon.

With blessings.

## 4607

*[Menachem Av, 5716?]*

You noted in your letter that the funeral took place in Vancouver on the 28th of Sivan, which, according to the time in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, was the evening of the 29th. It seems your intent is that this makes a difference regarding the recitation of Kaddish (the conclusion of the eleven months), because for us, the Yahrtzeit is calculated from the day of passing (not the burial), even during the first year (Hayom Yom, 13th of Shevat).

In my opinion, since the recitation of Kaddish is for the merit of the departed and relates to their judgment, in every instance we follow the time of the place of passing, not that of the place where the mourners are located.

If there is a practical difference regarding the Yahrtzeit (such as when the passing occurred at the time mentioned above), this is discussed by the later authorities in *Kol Bo* (by Rabbi Grunwald) and others. Some follow the first opinion—that it is determined by the time of the mourners—and they find support in the *Sefer Divrei HaSadeh*. Others say, based on *Responsa Kesav Sofer* (Yoreh Deah, siman 188), that it is determined by the place of passing. This is not the place to elaborate. May we conclude with good tidings.

#### ***4607***

Copied from the Rebbe’s holy handwriting on the margin of a letter from Rabbi Alexander Sender Yudasin, dated 1 Menachem Av, 5716.

Additional letters to him—see above, 4348, and the references in the notes there.

**That there is a practical difference regarding the recitation of Kaddish:** See above, Vol. 3, Letter 536.

## 4608

B"H, 1st day of Rosh Chodesh Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the Administration of the Poalei Agudath Israel Organization in America
May Hashem grant them life

Peace and Blessing!

In response to your letter dated the 23rd of Menachem Av, we are honored to inform you that the Rebbe shlita has seen fit to send a telegram today directly to the location of the conference in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established) — in response to the telegraphic invitation from the administration of Poalei Agudath Israel there. The text is transcribed here:

In response to your invitation, I send my blessings that the conference may be worthy and successful in its true work to strengthen and disseminate authentic Yiddishkeit without compromises, in accordance with the demand of our Sages of blessed memory, "We are laborers of the day." The Tzemach Tzedek explained this as "laboring for the day and the light" through the "candle of a mitzvah and the light of Torah," and the "light within the Torah," until the true and complete Geulah shines forth.

Wishing you a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

Lubavitch

With respect and with the blessing of *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

Secretariat of the Rebbe shlita

## 4659

B”H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 26th of Menachem Av, in which you once again write your reasoning and arguments for moving from Morocco.

I stand by my opinion, firmly rooted in the statement of our Sages, “Your own [needs] come first,” which refers to one’s spiritual well-being. From this, it is understood that this is also for your material benefit—to utilize your talents and knowledge in the field of chinuch al taharas hakodesh in Morocco, among our Sephardic brethren. To the extent that you increase your energy and effort in this, the Creator of the world and its Guide is guaranteed to provide what you and your family—may they live—need in your personal affairs, and certainly success in your studies as well. As our Alter Rebbe, author of the holy Tanya and the Shulchan Aruch, stated, through tzedakah (and it is simple that spiritual tzedakah is included in this), one’s mind and heart become a thousand times more refined.

May it be His will that you choose this beautiful path, as mentioned, and bring good news that you are raising students about whom you will be able to say, in time, “See the great ones I have raised.”

With blessings for a kesivah v’chasimah tovah.

## 4270

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and scholarly individual, involved in communal needs, R' Chaim Hillel, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

Following the longest of interruptions, your letter from the 23rd of Menachem Av was received, along with the two *pidyonos nefesh* enclosed within it, which will be read at an auspicious time at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding what you wrote about the reasons why it is impossible to influence the *avreichim* in [the city], etc.—anyone who contemplates the history of the Jewish people in general, and the history of *Chassidim* in particular, realizes that if they had taken such reasons into account, the situation would be, G-d forbid, [dire], and it is understood. To my regret, I have already become accustomed to such "explanations" that add doubt upon doubt as to whether the outreach will succeed, and therefore [people] do not approach it—even though the instruction of our Sages is well-known and famous: that in matters of *pikuach nefesh*, one is **obligated** to act even upon a doubt, or even a double doubt, etc. In the present case, this is a matter that brings them to the life of the World to Come, and often to the life of this world as well...

I was pleased by what you wrote regarding the outreach and the conduct with the students on Yud-Beis Tammuz, and surely you will continue in this vein. All these matters are like sowing seeds, where one sees literally that the sower throws many grains, even though he knows from the outset that only a few of them will take root and sprout. But it is a matter of fact that those few will take root only if he throws many grains; it would be otherwise if he were to throw only a few. Seemingly, there is a rational difficulty here: why should he "waste" several tens of grains on a field area where only a few will take root? But in practice, when one conducts himself according to this rationalizing logic, even the few do not take root, and it is understood...

At an auspicious time, you will mention all those you wrote about at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be the will [of Hashem] that you bring good tidings regarding all of the above.

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4711

4711

B"H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished chossid, who occupies himself with communal needs,
R' Menachem Mendel, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

It has been some time since I have received any news from you, and I am certain that all is well.

It is a well-known teaching of our Sages that one who gives a gift to his friend should inform him, or at least have him informed through another. It gave me pleasure to see the sense of Ahavas Yisroel displayed by the esteemed and distinguished chossid, who occupies himself with communal needs, R' Dovid Feiglin, may he live, who submitted a note requesting a blessing for the expansion of his livelihood. I mentioned him on Erev Rosh Chodesh of the month of mercy at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Nasi of Klal Yisroel. May it be the will of Hashem that this be drawn down into actuality soon, and may he bring good news in this regard.

I am certain that the guests from the Feiglin family, may they live, will convey to all of Anash, may they live, upon their return to Australia, our conversation regarding the most urgent necessity—not only to expand the Yeshiva Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch in Melbourne, but also to establish, open, and expand a Beis Rivkah school for girls according to the Chabad method of education. It is understood that Anash must increase their energy in their avodah, as a portion of this will be required for this new institution. As I perceived from the aforementioned guests from Australia, their opinion is that your participation and efforts in Beis Rivkah will bring great benefit to the establishment and development of these matters. Although I trust that you would do this on your own accord, our Sages have said, "One only encourages the already diligent." I have come only to urge an increase in quantity and quality in all matters of Anash, including the aforementioned. The exertion of one’s body and personal effort is far more effective than the exertion of one’s money. Since you have already passed the trial of establishing and expanding Oholei Yosef Yitzchok, the matter has moved beyond the stage of "all beginnings are difficult," and it will surely be much easier than in the past. May it be the will of Hashem that you bring good news in this as well.

With the blessing of Kesivah V’Chasimah Tovah.

#### ***4711***

**R' Menachem Mendel:** New, Melbourne.

## 4712

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

I acknowledge receipt of your letter from the 22nd of Menachem Av. Surely, I do not need to point out to you that the matter you write about could lead to similar requests from others—meaning, it would significantly increase the expenses of the mosdos. Nevertheless, I do not mean to state a final decision on this. I have written regarding this to the venerable Rabbi, the venerable Chossid, etc., Rabbi B.A.G. shlita in Paris, and I hope that you will write to him now and receive information on the matter.

It surprises me that you do not mention the work of the Nshei U’Bnos Chabad in your city. If this was of the utmost importance at all times, it is so today to an even greater degree. This is especially true among the Sephardim, for whom the chinuch of girls and women is an entirely new thing, at a time when secular knowledge and anti-Yiddishkeit are reaching them through schools, newspapers, and the like. Consequently, it turns out that they are receiving more and more negative, harmful information, while, unfortunately, very little has been done for them regarding Yiddishkeit until now. It is surely superfluous to emphasize that the future standing of the home, and the chinuch of their children, depends on the proper Jewish chinuch of the daughters.

It goes without saying that you can convey the above to all the Nshei Anash with whom you meet. And the more women who are drawn into this work, the better. For everyone can be of use according to her abilities and possibilities, provided there is a genuine desire for it. May Hashem grant you success in reporting good news in this regard.

With the blessing of Kesivah V’Chasimah Tovah,

On behalf of the Rebbe shlita,

A. Quint
Secretary

#### ***4712***

**Rabbi B.A.G. shlita:** Rabbi Binyomin Eliyahu Gorodetzky.

## 4773

4773

B"H, 1 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 21 Menachem Av, in which you write the reason and the cause for your not participating recently in the work of the Organization of Nshei U’Bnos Chabad and similar matters, which is connected to the opinion of doctors in this regard.

Although it is understandable that one must follow the instructions of a doctor, as our Sages of blessed memory say, "The Torah gave permission to the physician to heal," on the other hand, such work that brings one together with people, and one knows that this brings benefit to a certain circle of people—in ruchniyus or in gashmiyus, or in both—not only does not weaken the person, but on the contrary, it strengthens them. It not only provides spiritual satisfaction, but it literally improves physical health as well, which even doctors acknowledge, and therefore they advise being active, each according to their station.

And taking into account the above, my opinion regarding your question is as follows:

That you should take an active part in the work of Nshei U’Bnos Chabad, and also in matters connected to the benefit of the Yeshiva Achei Temimim in Tel Aviv and similar things. And certainly, it can be arranged that you will not need to walk up stairs, since that is not easy for you according to what you wrote. And I believe that when you consult with the doctor who has treated you better than the first one (and therefore it is advisable to conduct yourself according to his instructions), he will certainly agree to the above in the fullest measure.

And especially since we know that when one does good things for another, one increases through this the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach in that which is needed by the one who does the favor, together with their family. And it is certainly unnecessary to draw your attention to the instruction of Toras HaChassidus that everything must be done with simcha, and then there is also more hatzlacha.

With blessings for good tidings regarding the above, and a *Kesivah V’Chasimah Tovah* for you and all your family members, may they live.

4714

B"H, 1 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned man, who occupies himself with communal needs and conducts his business with integrity,
R' Yaakov HaKohen, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from Erev Rosh Chodesh, the month of mercy.

I was pleased to read in it that during the *ratzon*—*reva d'reva*—at the time of *Mincha* on *Shabbos Mevarchim* Elul, you explained the matters pertaining to the month of Elul among the *Bnei Yisrael*, for "where ten are gathered, the *Shechinah* rests," especially when words of Torah and words of *Chassidus* are spoken. May Hashem Yisbarach help that this *hisorerus* should descend into actual practice, and may all those who were instrumental in this take a leading role.

I was pleased to read in your letter that you will serve as the *shaliach tzibur* during the upcoming *Rosh Hashanah* and *Yom Kippur*, which are approaching us and all of Israel for good.

Surely you are aware of the instruction of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that all those who descend before the *teivah* on these days should, several days beforehand, review the *peirush hamilos*—at the very least the literal meaning—of the *tefillos* and *piyutim* that it is customary to recite, and with which one is not as well-versed.

May it be the will of Hashem Yisbarach, who hears the prayer of His people Israel with mercy, that He be attentive and listen to the prayer of the emissaries of His people Israel, and you are included among them. May He grant each and every one of us, within the entirety of *Klal Yisrael*, a good and sweet year in all matters. This is especially so given that the year begins in a state of readiness—my intention being that the first day after *Rosh Hashanah* this year is *Shabbos Kodesh*, at which time, according to the saying of our Sages, "all your work is done."

Since the month of Elul is already a preparation and, in part, the beginning of the *Aseres Yemei Teshuvah*, when the days of "receiving the face of the King" (the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He) begin—as is written in *Likutei Torah*, *Parshas Re'eh* 33b—may the good things begin already, starting from the first day that it is customary to recite the psalm, "L'Dovid Hashem Ori."

With blessings for good tidings in all that has been mentioned, and regards to your family, may they live, and to all the members of the *beis haknesses*, may they live.

***

4714

R' Yaakov HaKohen: Katz, Chicago. Additional letters to him appear above, 4485, and in the notes referenced there.

The instruction: See also above, Vol. 11, letter 3738, and the notes referenced there.

## 4715

B"H, 1 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 25 Menachem Av, in which you write that you are soon to be married, but that your intended does not conduct herself as is appropriate for a kosher daughter of Israel, and you ask what you should do to bring her to the proper path, etc.

It is somewhat surprising, for surely you have known about her for some time, since you write that the wedding date is soon. You should have acted in the aforementioned direction as early as possible. The method of action is already known and explained in many places: it must be done with pleasant ways, yet it must also be with the appropriate firmness. This is the way of Torah in both these matters—for the ways of Torah are ways of pleasantness, yet its matters are statutes regarding which we have no permission to question, as our Sages of blessed memory have taught. It would be very good if you could also influence her through her friends, since often, influence in such matters is received precisely from those who are not as close, and the wise will understand.

You do not mention anything regarding your *shiurim* in the study of our Torah, the Torah of life, or your meticulousness in the performance of *mitzvos*. Surely you are striving in this as well, as much as is necessary, so that it should be as it ought to be.

Surely you are aware of the three well-known *shiurim* from the *takanah* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*. You should, at the very least, keep them from this point forward.

With blessings for a *ksivah v'chasimah tovah*.

## 4716

B"H, 1 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

To the avreich Yisrael, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

It was with pleasure that I read your letter detailing some of your activities at Camp Gan Israel. May it be the will of Hashem that these activities and their results inspire you to appropriate reflection: If, through your efforts thus far, Hashem has enabled you to bring those you influence closer to Torah and mitzvos, how much more so will this be the case if you increase your efforts in the appropriate manner—as mentioned—which will surely lead to even greater success, both in quantity and quality. This is especially true in light of what is known and explained elsewhere: that a positive change initiated in a youth or child often influences their conduct for weeks and years to come. It is therefore understood that such effort and exertion are worthwhile, provided that the change is brought into actual practice. Naturally, this must be done in ways of pleasantness and paths of peace, for this is the essence of our Torah, as it is written, "Its ways are ways of pleasantness, etc." On the other hand, there is an additional virtue in the positive change effected within the counselors themselves, as they then influence those under their guidance in a different manner. It is a certainty that "propaganda" (outreach) does not return empty, and there is nothing that can stand before the will.

I hope—even though you do not mention it—that you have set times for your own personal study, in addition to the three well-known daily shiurim in Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya, as well as fixed times for study in both Nigleh and Chassidus. May Hashem grant you success.

As you requested, I will mention your parents, may they live, at the holy gravesite, in accordance with your letter.

With blessings for a *kesivah vachasimah tovah* for you, for all your fellow counselors, and for those you influence, may they live.

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

## 4777

B"H, 1 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

...Regarding his career path, you surely recall my opinion: it must be structured in a way that allows him to utilize his talents and knowledge to the greatest possible extent, and in a place where they are most needed. Therefore, in my view, he should settle in a country and a location where the language... is the spoken language. This is especially true since, in that very language, there are very few who are capable of exerting influence in matters of Yiddishkeit, in the laws of Torah and its mitzvos, and so on.

I stand by this opinion even now. However, since it appears that this view is not yet accepted by him—whether due to internal or external obstacles—there is room to accept the job offer in... provided that:

a) He has **full** authority and capability regarding yiras shamayim and the like in the institution where he will work, and that his time will be free to utilize every possibility for the education of adults as well—by which I mean education in yiras shamayim and the actual fulfillment of mitzvos. It is understood that this also includes ensuring that his name is not used to provide a hechsher for undesirable matters, and certainly not for matters contrary to the Shulchan Aruch.

b) That in his current place of work, matters are arranged in such a way that the institution will not suffer as a result of his moving to serve in another position.

As for the concerns he writes that he has regarding accepting a rabbinical position: in our generation, this position is largely a matter of education, especially if the Rav wishes to perform his work faithfully.

Regarding what you wrote at the end of your letter about his beginning to study a little Chassidus: I would like to hope that the initial period has already passed, and that he is consequently increasing both the quantity of his study and its quality. There is nothing that can stand before the will, especially since this part of Torah is called by all "the inner dimension of the Torah," and in the language of the holy Zohar, "the soul of the Torah." It is also evident in a tangible way that through this study, light and vitality are added to all the affairs and actions of a Yid.

With blessings for success in all the aforementioned, and for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good.

## 4778

B"H, 1 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 25th of Menachem Av, in which you write of various reasons for reducing and even ceasing your study of Chassidus.

What benefit is there in reasons and justifications if, in the final analysis, the study is lacking? It is already a known directive of our Torah that we do not say "one who is coerced is considered as one who has performed [the mitzvah]." You must make every necessary effort to set fixed times for the study of Chassidus. It is best to do so every day, even if only for a short time, and on Shabbos and a few times during the week, for a longer duration. **There is nothing that can stand in the way of will.**

Regarding what you wrote about immersion in the mikveh and how it relates to your state of health, it is understood that in such a matter, you must consult an expert physician. You should also ask for his opinion as to whether, in a time of need, placing a cotton plug (dampened with water) in your ear would have any effect.

I trust that you observe the three well-known daily study portions of Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya, and at the very least, you should observe them from this point forward.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

***

 See *Darchei Teshuvah*, section 98, subsection 6.

## 4719

B"H, 2 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned R' Moshe Hakohen, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge with thanks the receipt of the book *She'eris Yisrael*—a collection of the words of the great Torah and Chassidic luminaries—which you have published.

It is a word spoken in its proper time, for while the words of our Rabbis, the luminaries of Israel, have always been like cool water to a weary soul—the soul of every Jew, weary from the hardships of the exile, from the concealment and hiding [of the Divine] where we see no signs—how much more so is this true in our generation and our time. Due to shortness of breath and the hard labor with which the body and the material world enslave the soul and spirit, the voice calling for immediate teshuvah, Torah, and mitzvos is not heard as much as is required, and as is necessary.

The darkness of the exile has grown and doubled, to the point where there are those who call darkness light and light darkness. Only the guidance of the "eyes of the congregation" in every generation—the great leaders of Israel in the fear of Hashem and His Torah—illuminates the darkness of the night of exile, to show us the path in which we should walk, the deeds we should do, and those we should not do.

And as the darkness of the exile and the material world intensified, in Hashem’s compassion for His people Israel, Hashem bared His holy arm and illuminated for us—through our teacher the Baal Shem Tov and his holy disciples, through the Alter Rebbe (author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch), and so on—the Torah of Chassidus and its concepts. This awakens the Divine feeling in the heart of a Jew, and in particular Chassidus Chabad, which provides a path for each and every person, according to their capacity, to approach the holy, to serve Hashem with their mind and heart, and even more than that—to serve Him in a way of "in all your ways know Him," and from a place of joy.

Therefore, the demand of the hour is to publicize this Torah, its sayings and aphorisms, in the widest possible circles, so that these matters reach even those who, for the time being, have not yet merited to enjoy these lights.

Gather the pearls from the sea of Chassidus, which will ignite the "lamp of Hashem, the soul of man" that is within each and every one of the children of Israel, until the lamp becomes a torch illuminating their lives—including their daily lives and their portion in the world.

And they will fulfill in practice what is alluded to in the portion of this week:

"Judges" (*shoftim*—the judgment and the decision) and "officers" (*shotrim*—the fulfillment of the decision, by rebuking the understanding and striking the fool, which is the *yetzer hara*) "you shall appoint for yourself in all your gates"—the eyes, ears, nose, and mouth (*Sefer Yetzirah*, ch. 4; see *Shelah*, end of *Parshas Shoftim*)—of this "small city," the body. "And they shall judge the people"—these are the limbs (*Nedarim* 32b)—"with righteous judgment" (*mishpat tzedek*—*kushut*, for there is no truth but Torah). "You shall not pervert justice" (by favoring yourself), "you shall not show partiality" (to yourself), "and you shall not take a bribe" (of self-love, which covers all transgressions). "And then you shall live and inherit the land which Hashem your G-d gives you."

[This refers to] one's portion in the world—that He will release it from the darkness of the exile, illuminate it, and refine it until even the atmosphere and even the physical earth become holy.

And through this, the redemption of one's own portion will hasten the complete redemption, the redemption of all our fellow Jews in the literal sense, the true redemption to inherit the land which Hashem your G-d gives [us], through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

With blessings for a *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah*.

## 4880

4880

B”H, 2 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 18 Menachem Av, which was received not long ago—it gave me pleasure to read that your wife, may she live and be well, is continuing her holy work at the Chabad school in... You may be confident in the Creator of the world and its Guide that an abundance of blessing and success will be bestowed upon your personal affairs as well, through this most exalted work.

Regarding what you wrote about the conflicts, the inappropriate attitudes, and so on—surely part of this is explained by the financial pressure that necessitates cutbacks (which do not appear as such even to the treasurer), but due to the severity of the pressure, he has no choice and attempts—outwardly—to find justification for it. But ultimately, as we see in all institutions of holiness, the cutbacks diminish. And all those who work in them and take part in their development and expansion receive their reward, not only in spirituality, but also in material reward, quite literally.

From this, my perspective regarding the future is also understood; since Divine Providence has placed you and her in a beautiful role, you must strive in this as much as is necessary. Ultimately, the relationships will also improve, and certainly, from your side, you will do everything that depends on you to bring hearts closer in the aforementioned matter, which is to the benefit of all parties, and this is simple to understand.

The most essential point in all of this is what you wrote—that she has succeeded in her work and the children feel a special connection and bond toward her. This is the most appropriate conduit for instilling in the children the love of Hashem, the love of Torah, and the love of mitzvos. My hope is that you will also find the words to explain to your wife that even if, for the time being, some Chabad customs do not seem right to her, in the course of time she will find sufficient explanation and understanding for them.

Regarding the upcoming school year, which I hope will begin in the Chabad institutions some time before the official date, may it be the will of Hashem that you both succeed in the field of Chabad education with outstanding success, and with health, peace of mind, and livelihood in tranquility.

I will repeat my blessing that Hashem may fulfill the days of your wife’s pregnancy, may she live, properly and easily, and that she may give birth to a healthy child at the proper time, properly and easily.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good,

**M. Schneerson**

P.S.

The *pidyon nefesh* enclosed in your letter will be read at an auspicious time at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Nasi of Israel.

## 4881

B"H, 3 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Yaakov Yosef, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from the first of the month of mercy, it gave me pleasure to read that additional *avreichim* have joined the *shiur* in *Chassidus*. Although, naturally, even with this addition, it is not nearly enough, as is also understood simply from the precision of the phrase **"spreading the wellsprings outward."** Since this is demanded, the possibility to fulfill the requirement has certainly been granted.

It is understood that these words of mine are directed not only to you, but to all *anash* in your camp, just as they are directed to all *anash* everywhere...

With blessing,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,
Secretary

#### ***4881***

**R' Yaakov Yosef, may he live:** Gurkow, London. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 12, 480, and in the references noted there.

## 4822

B"H, 3 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 26 Menachem Av, regarding your practice of reciting *Shnayim Mikra V'echad Targum* on Erev Shabbos and Shabbos with the commentary of Rashi, as explained in the *poskim*.

This does not replace the *takanah* disseminated by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, to study the weekly *parsha* of *Chumash* with Rashi’s commentary every single day. That is, on Sunday from the beginning of the *sedra* until the *parsha* of *Sheni*, on Monday from *Sheni* until *Shlishi*, and so on. For "each and every day must perform its own task," and the content of this *takanah* is not the same as the concept of *Shnayim Mikra V'echad Targum* on Erev Shabbos. The same applies to the daily *shiur* of *Tanya* and the recitation of *Tehillim*. Even if one were to argue that it is possible to make up for a missed day, as with all matters of *tashlumin* (making up for missed obligations), this is only *b'dieved* (after the fact) and under duress. Furthermore, in many such cases, the *tashlumin* does not equal the primary obligation, and this is well understood.

Regarding what you wrote about *tzitzis* spun by machine, and the stories you have heard regarding the instruction of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe:

What I have heard is, in short, that during the years of the First World War, thousands of *Bnei Yisrael* in Russia were exiled to remote areas. It became impossible to bring *tashmishei kedusha* from Poland. Due to the hardship, a spinning factory was organized by Jews, which also spun *tzitzis* for the exiles. I do not know the details of the machines or the *hechsherim* that were provided at that time. However, since this took place in the years 5675-6 in Russia, they certainly did not have the machines that are used today, but rather machines that involved more human manual intervention, although it is understood that even then, the machine did the majority of the work.

With blessings for a *Ksiva V'chasima Tova*,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

#### ***4822***

**What I have heard:** See also above, Vol. 11, Letter 3822, and the references in the notes there.

## 4823

B"H, 3 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned man, who occupies himself with the needs of the community,
R' Shalom Mendel, may he live, Shochet and Bodek.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the beginning of the month of mercy [Elul].

You are surely searching, and you will therefore find, including making acquaintances at the consulate in order to expedite the efforts for the appropriate time.

Regarding what you wrote concerning whether the precision is specifically to repeat [the Chassidus] orally or also from the text: the precision is that the words should be those that emerge from the heart, since the goal is the effect upon the listeners. Words enter the heart of the listener when they emerge from the heart of the speaker; this is to say that the point is that it should be with sincerity and effectiveness.

Certainly, one does not suffice with speaking once with Mrs. ... may she live, and you should continue in this. Especially since there are matters in which she is careful, and the teaching of the Mishnah is well known, that "one mitzvah leads to another." May Hashem grant you success.

With blessings for a *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah*,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

A. Quint
Secretary

## 4824

B"H, 3 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, active in communal affairs,
R' Moshe Pinchas, may he live and be well, HaKohen

Greetings and blessings!

It was a pleasure to read your letter from the second of the month of Elul, the month of mercy, in which you write that you and your wife, may she live and be well, succeeded in influencing others in matters of Torah, mitzvos, and Chassidus during your stay at the hotel.

May it be the will of Hashem that your success in this will increase your desire and yearning to continue in such endeavors in the future, so that this desire and yearning will reveal even the hidden powers, in addition to the revealed powers. And then, not only will there be an increase in activity, but the increase will be incomparable, for there is no comparison between hidden powers and revealed powers. And since the attribute of the Ribono shel Olam is "measure for measure"—except that the blessings of Hashem are increased many times over, incomparably, in specific matters and through the drawing down from the hidden—it is understood and simple that matters of health are included in this, and indeed first and foremost, for a healthy and whole body is one of the ways of Hashem.

You will surely find the appropriate words to explain the above to your wife, may she live and be well, for she too has a share in the aforementioned activities, as you wrote in this regard.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v'chasimah tovah*.

#### ***4824***

**R' Moshe Pinchas:** Katz, Brooklyn. Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 12, 3866, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4885

B"H, 3 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and upright, engaged in communal needs, R' Mordechai Dov, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

... You surely remember what we discussed regarding the search for seforim and manuscripts, etc. Our Sages have already stated regarding the manner of searching that it must be done "in holes and crevices," etc. This is especially true in our generation, where this is a matter of rescuing seforim from decay, Heaven forbid. If you apply yourself to this as well, you will succeed, and thank you in advance.

#### ***4885***

**R' Mordechai Dov, may he live:** Talishevsky.

## 4826

B"H, 3 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the beginning of the month of mercy:

Regarding what you wrote, that "I gave it a twist" [i.e., interpreted a nuance], and therefore wrote regarding such-and-such, etc.—it is a great wonder to me that there are members of anash who, regarding matters about which I write in detail, and others about which I... even though one should not read every possible literal interpretation into what I have written or said, on the other hand, they grasp at hints, hints of hints, and "twists," etc., and delve into these, etc.

With blessings for a *ksiva v'chasima tova*.

4827

B"H, 5 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

To each and every one of Anash, and especially to the administrations of the organizations and institutions of Anash in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established speedily,
May Hashem grant them life,

Peace and blessing!

It gave me pleasure to read the letters from Anash, may they live, from the Holy Land, both from the time of the visit of the shluchim, the young rabbis, may they live, and thereafter. In them, they describe the hisorerus that the visit brought about, the resolutions that were adopted in various places and circles, and the general and individual impression left by their visit, their words, and their activities.

Yet, after all this, it is surely unnecessary to remind you that all these matters—without diminishing their importance and value, for as the adage of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, goes, "there is a chazakah that propaganda does not return empty," especially when it comes from words that emerge from the heart and from those who are found in the "tent of Torah," both Nigleh and Chassidus—after all this, considering the hoped-for goal, these matters are in the category of plowing, and in many places, sowing as well. Just as—to use a parable—we see that many adventures occur even after the plowing and sowing before the matter is brought to its completion, so too is it in the analogy: many "crafts of thought"—and to use the expression of Chazal in a clever way, the "crafts" through which the Mishkan was built, regarding which it is said, "And they shall make Me a sanctuary and I will dwell within them"—must be performed until the matter is finalized.

It is simple that these tasks are the obligation and also the privilege of all Anash among the Bnei Yisrael who are in the Holy Land, and especially and first and foremost, the organizations of Anash, for a mitzvah performed by the many is not comparable to one performed by an individual or a few individuals.

It is surely unnecessary to add that as long as the impression of the visit and its matters is recent, its vitality is greater, and it is easier to continue with all the matters that need to follow the visit in the most efficient and successful manner.

Especially since the time is fitting, as we are in the month of mercy, when, in the words of Rabbeinu HaZaken, the King—the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He—is in the field, and permission is granted to anyone who wishes to go out to greet Him, and He receives everyone with a pleasant countenance and shows a smiling face to all (Likutei Torah, Re'eh 32b). It is understood that just as water reflects the face, so too is the "lower man"—every Jewish man and woman, for you are called "Adam"—awakened.

My suggestion is that if you have not done so until now, immediately upon receiving this letter, you should consult among yourselves on how to approach the activities in the aforementioned direction as soon as possible, to appoint a small committee or even individuals for specific tasks (so that the matter does not become like "a pot belonging to partners"), and proceed immediately to bring the resolutions into action.

May it be the will of our King in Heaven, who is now in the field as mentioned, that He also accept this request of ours for success in the aforementioned direction and in the aforementioned activities—the greatest and most extraordinary success—and may He receive it with a pleasant countenance, so that through this, all concealments and hiddenness are nullified, and you may go from strength to strength, not only in utilizing the visit and its impressions, but also in adding to them many times over in every detail.

I look forward to hearing good and detailed news regarding the above, and I sign with a blessing for extraordinary success in the aforementioned, and for a *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah* for each and every one and their families, may they live.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 4788

[5 Elul, 5716]

...It was a pleasure for me to read the highlights of the activities of the *avreichim* *shluchim*, the rabbis *shlita*, in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established. Surely, he will continue to fill in the details regarding the matters in which he is participating, and he will inspire those who participated in the other activities—about which he writes only the highlights based on hearsay—to do likewise.

The general point in all of this is that after all the *aliyahs*, the visit of the aforementioned is akin to plowing, and to some extent, sowing as well. Now comes the essential *avodah*—which is incumbent upon *anash* in general and *Tzeirei Agudas Chabad* in particular—to perform all the other labors that the *Tanna* lists in the order of bread-making, until it becomes—in the language of *Chassidus*—something fit for the "Supreme Man upon the Throne," rising to the table of Kings, which is the King of Kings, the *Ribono shel Olam*. The analogy is understood. It is appropriate that immediately, while the impression is still warm and alive, they should consult among themselves how to utilize the visit and the activities of the aforementioned in the most effective manner. May the *Hashem* grant them success.

Although I am writing regarding this to the administration of *Tzeirei Agudas Chabad* as well, experience has already shown that sometimes this is received like "a pot belonging to partners." Since he has succeeded in such matters until now in setting the wheels in motion, etc., may it be His will that he succeeds in this now as well, in addition to his own personal activity.

#### ***4788***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 23, p. 470. See also the previous letter.

**The *shluchim*:** See also above, letter 4546, and the references in the notes there.

## 4289

B"H, 5 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Avraham, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

I hereby acknowledge with thanks the receipt of the first booklet of *sichos* for youth, and may his mind be at ease, just as he put my mind at ease when the aforementioned was published.

May it be the will of Hashem that this be a good beginning to conquer the literature in Eretz Yisrael (may it be rebuilt and established), and especially the literature for youth, through the teachings of our holy Nasi, of blessed memory. For these *sichos* as well—ultimately, the hoped-for goal is the spreading of the *maayanos* outward, as is the case with all matters of Torah and *mitzvos* (as it is written, "Hashem commanded us to perform... to fear," and how does one arrive at His love and fear? It is by contemplating His greatness, etc., as per the ruling of the Rambam, which is the essence of the teachings of Chassidus: "Know the G-d of your father"). May Hashem grant him great success in this, and may he also see an increase in his personal matters through this.

With blessings for success in all that is mentioned, for good tidings, and for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*.

P.S.
The *pidyon nefesh* via the *avreichim shluchim*, the rabbis (may they live and be well), was received. Thank you.

#### 4289

**R' Avraham, may he live:** Meislish, Kfar Chabad.

**The first booklet:** See above, Vol. 9, letters 2826, 2834; Vol. 10, letter 3141.

**The shluchim:** See also above, letter 4546, and the references in the notes there.

## 4,390

4,390

B"H, 5 Elul, 5716

Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

... It is certainly unnecessary to remind you that the matter of a *korban todah* (thanksgiving offering) among *anash* and *Chassidim* is an increase in the study of *Chassidus* for oneself and the spreading of the *maayanos* (wellsprings) outward as well. May Hashem grant you success in bringing good tidings in this.

With the blessing of *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*.

## 4,391

B"H, 5 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...I am all the more astonished at him, for he has merited that the Creator of the world, in His greatest kindness, has shown him, his wife, and his children—may they live—open miracles (and if only he would not raise doubts regarding the past, and also regarding the naming, where the miracles were even more complete). Even though no one among us knows the extent of the hidden, it is almost certain that a significant part of this is due to his holy work in the educational institutions of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, Nasi Yisroel. Yet, after all this, he views his work as a matter of necessity and doing a favor for others, as if he is only compelled to engage in it for the sake of material livelihood—and the rest is understood—especially since I do not wish to dwell at length on a matter that causes distress, etc.

May it be the will of Hashem that, since we are in this month when the King is in the field and receives everyone with a smiling countenance (see *Likutei Torah, Devarim* 32b at length), He will also accept the requests of every one of the activists—myself included—that they all dedicate themselves, ultimately, to the efforts of establishing, increasing, and expanding [the institutions] as much as possible, and to raise students, the seed blessed by Hashem.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned, and for a *ksiva v’chasima tova* for him and all his family members—may they live.

## 4,792

4,792

B"H, 5 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I was pleased to receive your letter from Erev Shabbos Parshas Shoftim, with the news that you have successfully signed the contract with the shul. May Hashem Yisbarach help that it should be in a good and auspicious hour. As is known, the saying of the Tzemach Tzedek—which we heard from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—is that he once said to a shamash in the shul: "Call the sun (shemesh) and the light will shine (vizrach or), call the sun and the light will shine"—meaning, bring light into the shul.

May Hashem Yisbarach help that you, too, should act in this way, and that it should be with success. Through this, you will also merit the blessings of Hashem Yisbarach in all that you and your family—may they live—require.

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*.

#### ***4,792***

**The saying:** See also above, Vol. 12, Letter 4,050, and the references noted there.

## 4393

4393

B"H, 5 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

...It is surely unnecessary to remind someone like yourself that while the role of the Charedi school has always been to teach our holy Torah to its students, in our times there is an additional task: to educate them in matters of Yiddishkeit in general, the observance of mitzvos, and even the most basic knowledge. In other words, to create the reality of a Jewish child, a son of Avraham, Yitzchak, and Yaakov. It is understood and obvious that this is even more fundamental than the knowledge of Torah, even if one were to argue that the two could be separated.

Therefore, as I have elaborated elsewhere, it is imperative that in every curriculum, whatever it may be—from the lowest grade to the highest and most advanced—a significant portion must be dedicated to the study of the fundamentals of Yiddishkeit, the knowledge of the halachos required for daily life, the customs of Yisrael, and so on. It is a well-known ruling of our Sages that "serving [the Torah] is greater than studying it."

Surely there is a general curriculum that is followed, but there is certainly enough room for every school director to incorporate the aforementioned study and education to a significant degree. The matter depends solely on the director’s will. Given the magnitude of the matter and its fundamental importance, any effort in this direction is worthwhile, and the merit of the many—the school children—will assist him.

With blessings for success in your holy work, and a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*.

## 4,394

4,394

B"H, 6 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write that while at home your standing in yiras shamayim is better than when you are at Yeshivas Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch in..., even though you are diligent in your study of Torah in general and in the study of Chassidus in particular, and you ask what you should do.

It is simple and understood that the reasoning mentioned is a tactic of the yetzer hara, which seeks to prevent you from being in the "tent of Torah" and the "tent of tefillah." For these are specifically found not at home, as is known from the Mishnah: that Torah is acquired through the companionship of friends, and "go into exile to a place of Torah." The same applies regarding tefillah, and especially regarding the study of Chassidus. You should strengthen your resolve and rouse your yetzer hatov against the yetzer hara, as explained in Tanya, and Hashem will grant you success.

It would be appropriate to have your tefillin checked, and also to ensure that you wear a tallis katan whose tzitzis have been inspected.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above and for a kesivah v'chasimah tovah,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4295

B.H., 6 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from Rosh Chodesh Elul, in which you ask to be shown the gateway to Torah and yirah, the path to guarding one’s eyes and tongue, etc.

It is understood that the above depends on several details and the specific nature of the soul of the inquirer, the conditions of his environment, etc. And although the central point in all of this is as stated—in the holy Tanya, beginning of Chapter 41, to awaken the natural yirah, etc., to contemplate in his mind, etc., that Hashem stands over him, etc., and gazes upon him and examines his kidneys and heart to see if he is serving Him as is fitting (see there)—there are many manners and many ways of conduct following this. He should explain his situation to anash, his friends who know him, and they will guide him based on the experience they have with those they influence in such matters. And the time itself brings success in what was said, according to what is explained in Likutei Torah, Parshas Re’eh 32b, that in the month of Elul, the King is in the field and receives all those who wish to greet Him with a pleasant countenance, etc. (see there).

With blessings for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* and for good tidings regarding the above,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4,396

7 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, meticulous in his mitzvah observance, etc.,
Rabbi S.Y. [Zevin] shlita,

Peace and blessing!

I am writing regarding the main matter. Yesterday, Rabbi Hollander shlita visited me, and in the course of our conversation, he mentioned that while he was in Kiev, he visited the local Rabbi, Panitch, with his friends. There was someone present who told him that he is the brother-in-law (from a first marriage) of Rabbi Zevin shlita (I do not know further details about him).

He appears to be an elderly man, about eighty years old. He added, in confidence, that at night he turns off the lights and listens via radio to broadcasts from Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. Since I understood from Rabbi Hollander shlita that he has not yet informed you of this, and given your many preoccupations, I am doubtful as to when he might inform you, which is why I am writing these lines.

I am sure you have received my two telegrams. To them, I must add that Rabbi Wohlgelernter shlita told me that he, too, will approach the Chief Rabbis—perhaps even by telegram—regarding the situation concerning the ship issue in general, and specifically regarding the ship scheduled to depart [on Erev Shabbos]. After a long conversation with me... he was unable to provide even a single reason for this, not even a flimsy one. Even when I quoted to him in our conversation what was written in the press in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, regarding the "argument" of *pikuach nefesh*—"that if there are additional expenses, the number of passengers will decrease, and over time the voyage might be cancelled entirely, etc."—they brought me another strange reason yesterday: that by "anchoring for a day or two longer, the insurance premiums rise, the prices go up, and the passengers will decrease, etc." It is understood how much of a mockery this is, even as a mere *kas d'aminah* (initial assumption).

Incidentally, one can see here how correct the matter is that "the sharpness [of one's intellect]... and desire misleads the mind," sometimes in an alarming way. Someone here who wants to permit it, and found no logical reason, justifies his *kas d'aminah* with a typical argument (the kind possible among yeshiva students), even though he speaks about it with deep seriousness: "If you assume that passengers are forbidden to travel in such a case because it is possible to anchor the ship, etc., then what is the subject of the debate among the *Acharonim* regarding the permissibility of the journey when the sailors are Jewish? It must be that this itself proves that even then it is permitted." It is understood that I immediately added that this "proof" would also prove that it is permitted for the ship owners to operate their ship on Shabbos, for the *Rishonim* and *Acharonim* who raise the question regarding a ship owned by Jews did not write, nor did they explicitly emphasize, that the ship owners do not obey the law of Yisrael.

In truth, I would not even quote this, but I fear that in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, they will use these arguments as well—and *kal v'chomer* they will argue that it is a matter of *pikuach nefesh* because after a few years the ships might be cancelled, etc., as mentioned above.

Regarding the explanation of what was written in the telegram: as they describe the situation based on information from "behind the curtain," it is clear that without **pressure** from the Rabbinate and the passengers, the Zim management will not comply with the prohibition, using a new argument recently invented in their *beis medrash*: "that it has already been an established practice for several years for these voyages." On the other hand, if the Rabbinate and groups of passengers demand it with the appropriate force—meaning that if their demand is not met, they will not travel on the ships—it is **clear** that the Zim management will endeavor to arrange the matter (at least on some ships).
