Therefore, I emphasized in the telegram that even if there were room to say that one could find a side of leniency for the passengers (if they do not drink or wash in the fresh water on the ship, do not use the amenities, etc.—conditions, of course, that the vast majority will not keep at all), this leniency would—in reality—nullify the prohibition for the ship owners.

I learned something else—with great astonishment—from Rabbi Wohlgelernter shlita: that they are looking for advice on how someone (who understands machinery?) can visit the ship while it is operating in order to examine the manner of its management and the operation of the engines. Naturally, they are encountering difficulties with this (to my great surprise). And that until now, **no one** has demanded the ship's log—from the past few weeks—in which all the actions and labors on the ship are recorded clearly and in detail. When I asked him about this—as it is the simplest verification—he had no answer for me.

Incidentally, the *shluchim*, the *avreichim* shlita, reported here that Rabbi Frank shlita complained to them that he does not know the content of my letters, even though, from what he has heard about them, his opinion also tends in that direction. This led me to request in the telegram that they show my letters to the aforementioned.

For the time being, I am holding back the debate in the press here regarding the ship problems, and only what was written from Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, is being printed here. But if the situation continues, I doubt whether it will not become public knowledge... and this is an additional reason for the necessity of clarifying the situation as much as possible, as soon as possible.

A moral lesson can also be learned from the aforementioned matter, similar to what is written, "Woe to those who draw iniquity with cords of vanity, etc." At first, the ship would depart from here a few days before Shabbos, and apparently, after they saw that all was peaceful and quiet, they began to depart on Erev Shabbos as well. And, of course, not always exactly at "daybreak" (which, incidentally, is impossible, because the departure of a ship from the port is connected to several operations of the port offices, and apart from **extremely** exceptional cases, they—the offices—do not forgo their rest, and they do not add or move up working hours. Therefore, for the most part, the start of the departure from the port is close to midday, and perhaps even later). And when this also passed peacefully and without reaction, they innovated this year that, according to the plan, a ship will depart from here on Erev Yom Kippur. (And in accordance with the above in parentheses, besides the office operations in the ship's departure from the port, the order is, as is known to all sailors, that in ports with heavy traffic, the ship is taken out of the port by an auxiliary ship that leads it a long distance from the shore and the port, and only afterward do they increase the ship's operations and the auxiliary ship returns to the port, and the ship sails on its own engines, which also takes quite some time—usually **several** hours. In addition to the above, after the passengers board the ship, it takes several hours until all the lists, ledgers, writing of receipts for deposited items, changes from one room to another recorded in the ship's books, and so on and so forth are arranged, which in the vast majority of cases lasts for many, many hours. It is understood that when the ship's departure begins after midday on Erev Shabbos, all of the above concludes quite some time after sunset and the appearance of the stars).

As for me, I do not need all of the above, as I am already certain of the matter, and I have only come to prove the deception of the public by the ship's personnel and by all those who traveled on ships departing on Erev Shabbos, as it is impossible that they do not know about the above, for it happened to them, and yet they are the ones shouting loudly that there is no labor and no concern of prohibition, etc. (and their words are the ones accepted).

Rabbi Ganzin shlita asked me—also in your name—whether to make a copy or a photograph of my letters and send them to several Rabbis in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. While in itself it is very proper that they, too, should know about the manner of the ship's management, etc.—and apparently it is **not known at all**—if one were to send the aforementioned by mail or similar, for several reasons one must fear undesirable reactions. In my opinion, it is **easy** to find one of *Anash* in every place where the members of the Chief Rabbinate and the like reside, who will visit them on his own initiative, and on this occasion show him or even give him a copy of my letter. And they should put words in his mouth—the *Anash* member—on how to answer the questions they will ask him about this.

If it is correct, as I understood from Rabbi Wohlgelernter shlita, that one must mobilize the opinion of all the members of the Rabbinate, one must be very concerned with bringing the aforementioned **to fruition**. It is understood that if expenses are involved—photocopying, etc.—they will be covered by the *tzedakah* fund. And it is simple that you have the authority to mobilize the appropriate people from *Anash* in my name, though whether excessive publicity in this matter is desirable is a matter for consideration.

I hope that you have spoken as necessary with Mr. Shazar shlita regarding the travel expenses of the *shluchim* shlita, as it is understood that it is not worthwhile for this to become an old debt. Also, for the aforementioned, it is surely easier to give from the fund close to the time of their visit than several weeks afterward... I still do not know exactly the travel and lodging expenses in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, but it seems it is more than I estimated. May it be the will of the Almighty that the benefit that will come through their visit will justify all the expenses, even taking into account the tight situation in the fund, and furthermore, that it will exceed them by much.

Enclosed is what I have published recently, and surely you will merit to bring merit to the many through its content, and the merit of the many is dependent upon you.

With *tzedakah* and a blessing for a *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah*,

**M. Schneerson**

P.S. A strange rumor has reached me that Rabbi... shlita has removed two *avreichim* from his yeshiva because they are studying *Chassidus*! Is the rumor true?

P.S. Information has reached me that in the coming months (starting from Tishrei)—even though the days are getting shorter—they are arranging for all Israeli ships to depart from here on Erev Shabbos.

## 4,797

B"H, 7 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

Mrs. Mary, may she live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of [date], I am certain you have already received my reply to your previous letter, which also partially addresses the question you raise in this current letter.

Regarding the method of printing this: in my opinion, the main priority is that it should be printed and not remain in manuscript form. Therefore, if there is hope that the entire sefer can be printed in a new edition including all the changes and marginal notes, that is certainly good. However, if due to the expenses involved or other reasons, this is not possible or would take an indefinite amount of time, it is worth considering not reprinting the whole thing, but only the new material. One could then refer—at the appropriate place—to such-and-such a page of the previous edition of Sefer HaRikmah. This would require far fewer expenses and is much easier to implement.

For in any case, Sefer HaRikmah is not a book for everyone; it is only for specialists. Therefore, there is only a slight concern that the minor effort of having to look into a separate volume might discourage its use.

With the wish that you may be able to realize your desire without difficulties and in the best possible way. And for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*.

#### 4,797

**Mrs. Mary:** Vilensky, Cincinnati. Additional letters to her—above 4,344, and in the references noted there.

## 4,898

B"H, 8 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To my dear friend, the G-d-fearing, Torah-learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Yehuda Leib, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from 25 Menachem Av, in which you write of the activities and impressions from the visit of the *avreichim shluchim*, the Rabbis, may they live: You may set your mind at ease, just as you have set mine at ease, for the content of your letter strengthens my hope that *anash* in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, will do everything in their power to utilize this visit—the activities and the impressions—for the primary point: the spreading of the *maayanos* outward. And this should be done in the manner commanded to us in the Torah, so that it is brought into actual practice in daily life—in thought, speech, and action.

This is especially relevant as we are now in the days of *hisorerus*, the month of mercy, when the King is in the field and receives everyone who wishes to greet Him with *seiver panim yafos*, as is written in *Likutei Torah*, *Parshas Re'eh* 32b, see there. May it be His will that you bring good news regarding this as well.

With the blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*.

***

**R' Yehuda Leib:** Slonim, Jerusalem. Additional letters to him: above 4,436, and in the references in the notes there.

**The shluchim:** See also above, letter 4,546, and in the references in the notes there.

## 4,399

4,399

B”H, 8 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 14th of Menachem Av, which took some time in transit before reaching me.

Over the days and weeks that have passed, your talents regarding guidance and education have surely become clearer; in accordance with this, you can estimate your future success and make the appropriate decisions.

Regarding what you wrote about the impressions made upon you by the matters in your environment, etc.—I have already expressed my opinion on this: that diverting one’s attention from these impressions by anchoring one’s thoughts in positive thoughts is the advice and remedy for such a situation. This task will be easier for you if you have committed to memory and engraved in your mind what is written in Tanya, beginning of Chapter 41: “And behold, Hashem stands over him,” etc.—see there.

It is surprising that you do not mention in your letter the checking of your tefillin or the other matters about which I wrote in my previous letter.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above, and for a *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah*.

## 4400

4400

B"H, 8 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter from the month of Menachem Av, in which you write regarding the text of the davening and the knot of the tefillin.

It is already well-known in the Chabad tradition that the Alter Rebbe, author of the Tanya and the Shulchan Aruch, selected this text from among dozens of variations. Since the purpose of davening is to elevate all of a person’s spiritual and physical matters, it is understood that one who wishes to be counted among the Chassidim of the Alter Rebbe should, most appropriately and for the best, daven using the text he established. The same applies to the knot of the tefillin and other matters related to davening.

As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

With blessings for a good and sweet inscription,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 451

B"H, 8 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the beginning of the month of Elul—the month of mercy—filled with complaints regarding your situation, and that you do not see success, including in your influence upon your friends, etc.

It is already a known adage of our Rebbes, the leaders of our people, that regarding any matter that is beneficial or leads to actual avodah, any obstacle that may arise—even if the obstacle is from a seemingly lofty source—is merely a tactic of the nefesh habehamis.

All of these things apply to you: If the aforementioned complaints add vigor to your study of Torah, your fulfillment of mitzvos, and the dissemination of the wellsprings outward, then perhaps—and maybe—they are worthwhile, even though this is not the path of Chassidus, which is to serve Hashem with joy. But if my fear is correct, these complaints weaken you in all of the above. This is the tried-and-tested tactic of the yetzer hara: to say to a person, "Since you are not succeeding anyway and will not defeat me, it is not worth even starting," etc., chas v'shalom. The falsehood of this claim is clear, for each and every one of us has been promised, as part of Klal Yisrael, that he has a share in the World to Come and that "no one shall be cast away from Him." In our generation, an additional point is added to this, as explained in the maamarim of Shabbos Parshas Eikev and Shoftim: "And it shall be, because (eikev) [in the footsteps of Moshiach] you will listen to these ordinances"—certainly, because this is the very last generation and there is no time to delay the birurim. Therefore, "you will listen" is a certainty. Thus, with a little bitisbonenus, one can prove to the "old and foolish king" that he is nothing but a fraud with this cunning.

However, it is even more correct not to engage with him in debate at all, for it is a waste of time, in addition to the principle that "anyone who wrestles with a filthy person, etc." Instead, you should arouse your yetzer hatov against him and explain to yourself that even though it is said regarding the generation of the footsteps of Moshiach that "you shall not go out in haste," this applies when the Geulah and the exodus from galus actually begin. This is not the case in the final moments of the days of galus; on the contrary, every single moment increases in value more and more, for "soon there will come years of which you will say, 'I have no pleasure in them'" (Shabbos 151b). Therefore, there is a necessity to utilize every single moment in the most beneficial way. And since a little light pushes away much darkness, it is simple that avodah in Torah and its mitzvos in a direct manner is more effective than pushing away darkness through debate with the yetzer hara. Based on this, it is also understood that there is a necessity to increase the study of the inner dimension of Torah—which leads to love and fear of Him (see Rambam, Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah, beginning of Chapter 2; the introduction to Sefer HaChinuch, at the end)—and specifically every day, since this "measure" protects the "vessel" of the study of the revealed Torah, as instructed by our Sages (Shabbos 31a, end of side a), "He said to him, 'It would be better if...'" etc. This is explained at length in Likutei Torah, Vayikra (6a); see there.

May it be the will of Hashem that these few lines—small in quantity relative to the subject matter—will influence you to take action, and to also influence your friends in this matter.

Regarding your birthday—on which you surely followed the custom of anash of recent times—may it be a year of success for you in the study of the revealed Torah and Chassidus, and an increase in hidur in the fulfillment of mitzvos and the avodah of tefillah in their entirety and in their essence, for it is the "backbone" that sustains the body of the 248 positive mitzvos—the limbs (see Likutei Torah, Balak, p. 74).

With blessings for a kesivah v'chasimah tovah.

## 4752

4752

B"H, 9 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter—which bears no date—in which you write regarding the excavations at Beis Shearim: surely, in the meantime, you have become aware of the letters from the Rabbonim in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, regarding this matter.

It is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding your standing and situation in the study of Torah and the meticulousness in the performance of mitzvos, for these are the conduits and vessels for all matters of a Yid, and especially for a bochur yeshiva. I am certain you will fill this in at the next opportunity. May it be His will that you also report that you have established times for the study of the inner dimension of Torah—which in our generation has been revealed in the teachings of Chassidus—which brings a person to the love and fear of Hashem (see Rambam, Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah, beginning of Chapter 2). This enables one to fulfill the mitzvah of the unity of Hashem (since then one understands the nature of true unity), etc., and the mitzvos that apply at all times, in every place, and to every Yid. As is brought in the introduction to Sefer HaChinuch, may it be His will that you also increase the consistency of your time [for study], in accordance with the ruling that one must always ascend in matters of holiness, and may you influence your peers to do the same.

With blessings for good tidings and to be inscribed and sealed for good,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

4743

4743

B”H, 9 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, refined in thought, scion of a noble lineage,
R’ Avraham Yehuda, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

Following the long interval, your letter from the eve of Rosh Chodesh, the month of mercy, was received. As you requested, I will mention all those you wrote about at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. It was pleasant for me to read that he has a share in the matter of... may he live. May it be the will of Hashem that this yield fruit, and the fruit of the fruit, and most importantly, that these matters not remain in the realm of speech but be brought into actual practice. That is to say, in his outreach, he should emphasize that the deed is the essential thing. Furthermore, **from him** must come the beginning, contrary to human logic and the two opinions (Shabbos 88a), the opinion of the Tzaduki. The explanation of Rava is that specifically through prioritizing *naaseh* (we will do) over *nishma* (we will hear) are we saved from the distortion of the intellect. Even—and to our sorrow and distress—in these very recent months, it seems the war against religion has intensified, such as the excavation at Beis Shearim, the issue of Shabbos, etc., and it is difficult to elaborate on this distressing matter, as it is truly alarming.

Among the books I received not long ago was the book *Toldos HaGra*. I was astonished to find there a citation in your name regarding what is written in the *Piskei Dinim* of the Tzemach Tzedek at the end, concerning the Gra. If I had found this in the name of someone else, it would not have surprised me so much. But knowing how much you cherish the stories connected to our holy Nasi, of blessed memory, which he heard in the home of his father, of blessed memory, it is a great wonder to me how you could accept a matter that is in complete and absolute opposition to the attitude of the Admur the Tzemach Tzedek regarding Torah matters, for his heart in this was like the heart of a lion. See the *Piskei Dinim* of the Tzemach Tzedek at its conclusion regarding what is written concerning the instruction of the Alter Rebbe. And although "who am I, etc.," nevertheless, "it is Torah, etc." Even though his words were certainly holy regarding the matters of the Tzemach Tzedek, how is it possible to reconcile this with what you wrote there regarding the matter you cited? It is similar to the story told about one of the great ones, that when they told him a certain matter in the name of the Rambam, he said that it is not found anywhere, and added that he does not reject it absolutely because of his own expertise, but because since it is contrary to logic, it is impossible that the Rambam would write such things.

The actual facts of the case are as follows: I heard from the mouth of my father-in-law, the Rebbe: The *Piskei Dinim* was printed in Vilna, where the hand of the *misnagdim* was strong at that time, and especially because it was between one man and another in Lubavitch.

The typesetter added those words that you cited. Immediately when it came out of the printing house and they found the addition, they managed to correct it in most of the copies, and it remained in only a small number, as is proven by the majority of the copies of this very edition. And specifically in these recent months, when some of the *misnagdim*—the remnants of that era—have begun once again to be jealous of the dissemination of Chassidus, and are using alarming means to hinder it (to the point of expelling students from a yeshiva because they were found studying *Tanya* and the like, and as is the custom of recent years, no one takes it to heart), the aforementioned error is distressing, although "who can discern errors?"

I have in my possession a handwritten copy (a transcript; the original manuscript remains overseas) of that part of the *Piskei Dinim*. And, naturally, those two lines are not there.

With blessings for good tidings, a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*, and for you and your household, good health and pleasant sustenance, above all else.

## 4404

B"H, 9 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., R' Aharon Yitzchak, may he live.

Shalom and blessing!

In response to your letter of the 2nd of Elul.

As you requested, I will mention you and your household, may they live—whom you wrote about—at the holy resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Nasi of Israel. This is appropriate to your request. It is proper that you have your tefillin checked, and that from now on, at the very least, you observe the takanah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, regarding the three known daily study portions: Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya. Furthermore, considering your age, we have heard the instruction of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, in such matters: that it is proper to establish a fixed time for the study of Mishnayos, in addition to the other study portions, and if possible, to become proficient in chapters of Mishnayos by heart. May Hashem grant you success in all of the above.

Since you are in an environment with people who have free time, you should view yourself as a *shaliach* of the *Rachmana* to disseminate matters of Yiddishkeit there, in general, and specifically regarding the beauty of performing mitzvos and Torah study, particularly the halachos necessary for daily life. It is already a well-known adage of our Rebbeim, our holy leaders, that there is a *chazakah* for *taamulah* (outreach) that it does not return empty. May Hashem lengthen your days and years in pleasantness, and may you bring good tidings regarding all of the above.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v'chasimah tovah*.

P.S.
I hope that you participate in the *farbrengens* of *anash* that are held from time to time in Tel Aviv as well, and in any case, you should do so from now on.

#### 4404

R' Aharon Yitzchak: Boym, Tel Aviv.

## 4755

B"H, 9 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and energetic, engaged in communal needs,
R' Chaim Shaul, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

Following the long interval, your letter from Motzaei Shabbos was received. It is surprising, as you seem to have become accustomed to omitting joyful news from your letters. While it is understandable that when something is burdensome and distressing, joyful matters are not as readily perceived, nevertheless, the reality exists. Sharing such news with another person in Yisrael who is interested in the matter is the mitzvah of Ahavas Yisrael and "Love your fellow as yourself," which is a great principle in the Torah and a vessel for the mitzvah of "Love Hashem your G-d," as per the well-known saying of the Alter Rebbe. I am referring to the addition to the building—even if it is small, it is still an addition, the laying of the cornerstone, etc.

Regarding what you wrote about "what will be the end of this," I believe I have already written that there is no doubt that if only those involved in this, with your honor at their head, stand firm in their avodah—at the very least no less than until now—it is certain that the institution will continue to develop.

Not being on-site, I am doubtful whether this requires miracles, as you wrote in your letter, or if it is possible without them. However, in the current case, it is similar to what is brought in Chassidus regarding the virtue of the mesiras nefesh of Avraham, who did not seek it out, but rather occupied himself with his avodah of "calling out in the name of Hashem, G-d of the world." If, for the sake of this avodah, he required mesiras nefesh, it was not an obstacle from that perspective. Surely, in the current case, it is the same. May it be the will of Hashem that you view things with a more joyful eye, despite all the difficulties, concealments, and hiddenness; consequently, your avodah will be with more simcha, and "simcha breaks through barriers."

With blessings for good tidings in return from you and from the others participating in your holy work, and for a Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah.

P.S.
Enclosed is a check for the needs of the yeshivah.

456

10 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished R' Naftali HaKohen, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of the 5th of Elul and the one preceding it, in which you write that you are being pressured to change your current place of study—where you learn Tanya—to a location much further away. As you indicate, the reason for this is that it will weaken the shiur currently being studied in your neighborhood, and certainly will not add to it. Furthermore, you mention that on Shabbos during Seudah Shlishis, instead of participating in the farbrengen where a maamar of Chassidus is reviewed, you are being asked to abandon this practice and attend a moralistic talk (sicha musaris). Regarding your question—since many of your friends are not present at the aforementioned talk, you were answered that they are not present at the time the farbrengen takes place where words of Torah are heard, which is not the case for you.

You ask how you should relate to these demands.

It is surprising that you ask this, knowing that you are someone who studies Gemara, Tosafos, and Rishonim, and also engages in deep study with diligence and persistence. Even with a superficial approach, the attitude toward such demands is clear. To proceed from the easier to the more difficult:

1) Even if we were to say that these changes are merely a shift from one custom to another, where the content is equal in terms of Torah and mitzvos, the law regarding the "strap of the shoe" (erkat d'masani) is already known. If the intent and the inner content—the passing over and opposition to a specific aspect of a mitzvah—are present, the definitions of that specific matter one intends to nullify apply to the "strap of the shoe" decree. So it is in this case: since, according to your letter, it is understood that the intent of both these changes is to weaken and distance you from studying a part of our Torah—which is a "perfect Torah" and was given in all its parts to Moshe at Sinai—the attitude toward these demands must be the same as the attitude toward a demand to reduce one’s study of (a part of) our Torah and to influence others in that direction as well.

2) In addition to the above: if the practice itself is being changed for the worse, it is understood that this makes the matter even more severe. Since the things through which Torah is acquired include the bonding of friends and the pilpul of students, the teaching of Chazal applies here as well: "A person should always learn in a place where his heart desires." This applies to his study partners and those with whom he engages in pilpul, as success and a "sign of blessing" are not the same for everyone. Since you have been accustomed to learning with a specific chavrusah and have been successful, it is not clear—if you change to a different chavrusah—whether you will see a sign of blessing there as well. The attitude toward such a demand is understood *a fortiori* (kal v'chomer) from the ruling in Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah, sec. 240, para. 25, that a sign of blessing in study—even if it is only a doubt—overrides the command and the concern, etc. See there. The same applies to the second change—to study a different subject at a specific time than you have been accustomed to until now. Since not all times are equally suited for all studies, and since you have succeeded in your study at a specific time, it is almost certain that this change will be for the worse. This is simple to understand.

3) Regarding the second demand—that during the time of Seudah Shlishis on Shabbos you should exchange the study of Pnimiyus HaTorah for a moralistic talk—the instruction of the Arizal is already known [which has been accepted by all circles of the Jewish people throughout the world, and by the Chassidim as well. In the words of his student, R' Chaim Vital: "G-d illuminated for us... and sent us a 'city and a holy one' descending from heaven, the great, G-dly, and pious Rabbi, my teacher and master, R' Yitzchak Luria Ashkenazi, of blessed memory, who was filled with Torah like a pomegranate, in Mikra, Mishnah, Talmud, pilpul, Midrashim, and Aggados. And in another place (Taamei HaMitzvos by R' Chaim Vital, Parshas Va'eschanan): 'And my teacher, of blessed memory, due to the greatness of his sharpness, would examine six ways in Halacha,' etc."].

He is the one who said that the day of Shabbos is uniquely suited for the study of Pnimiyus HaTorah. It is understood *a fortiori* that there is no place to suggest canceling a good custom you have already established of studying Pnimiyus HaTorah on the holy Shabbos day.

Note: I was surprised and puzzled by the suggestion to give a moralistic talk specifically during the time of Seudah Shlishis on Shabbos. Beyond the general nature of the Shabbos day—where the verse explicitly states, "And you shall call the Shabbos a delight," and it is ruled as Halacha that any distress is forbidden on it, even that which comes through a definite mitzvah (see Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, 282)—it is explained in the holy Zohar, at length, that the time of Mincha on Shabbos is an *eis ratzon* (a time of favor) above all others. Throughout the Jewish diaspora, we say at that time, "And as for me, my prayer is to You, Hashem, an *eis ratzon*," during the Mincha prayer of Shabbos. And specifically at this time—to come before our Father, our King, with a moralistic talk, which in the vast majority of cases consists of "turn from evil," describing the shortcomings of people, etc., etc.! In the words of the Arizal regarding the time of Seudah Shlishis on Shabbos: one should not arouse the aspect of *din* (judgment) there. This is even more severe than saying *Tachanun* on Yom Tov or Shabbos. See Rambam, Hilchos Shabbos, ch. 24, law 7; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, end of 339; and Sefer HaMitzvos of the Tzemach Tzedek, Mitzvas Lo Tiva'aru Esh.

It is already known among the rules of the poskim that we rule according to the words of the Kabbalists if there is no disagreement found in the Talmud or the poskim.

Given the simplicity of the matters stated above, my hope is that there is surely some misunderstanding in the discussion between you and those who spoke with you regarding the aforementioned demands.

With a blessing for good tidings in all the above. There is no "good" but Torah—good in its entirety, meaning good for Heaven and for people. See Iggeres HaKodesh by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, sec. 26. And may you be written and sealed for good.

**M. Schneerson**

## 4757

B.H., 12 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of the 7th of Elul.

I am extremely distressed by the manner of your daughter’s (tichye) travel. This is, of course, not because she acted differently than she was requested, but because of the prohibition involved—a clear Rabbinic prohibition, and there is room to argue it is even a Torah prohibition. Unfortunately, those who permit it have stumbled due to a lack of familiarity with the mechanics of how a ship operates, its management, and the usage of the ship by the passengers.

For example, it seems that until now, no one has paid attention to where fresh water comes from on a ship in the middle of the ocean. In the time of the Gemara, every traveler would take with them enough water until they reached a place where fresh water could be found, or the captain would take that amount for the passengers. This is not the case now. The salt water of the sea is converted into fresh water **every single day** according to the necessary amount by **boiling** the sea water in special boilers (and afterwards, the steam is transferred to another cold place where it turns into fresh water). Due to the lack of space on the ship, this is done daily to meet the day’s needs. It is understood that they increase this amount in accordance with the number of passengers. On the aforementioned ship, where (almost) all the passengers and crew are Bnei Yisrael, this constitutes cooking on Shabbos, etc. The same applies to many conveniences on the ship, in addition to the issue of desecrating Shabbos—a public Chillul Hashem where Torah-prohibited labors are performed for the sake of the ship’s operation by the crew, which would not be done if there were not a sufficient number of passengers on board.

I have written at length about all of this in my letter to the Chief Rabbis, based on what I know—also as a mechanic—regarding the operation of the ship’s machinery and the management of the ship in general. It is a public Chillul Shabbos of the greatest magnitude, committed by hundreds of Jews in public, Heaven forbid. Each of the passengers has a share in this, of course, for the desecration of Shabbos is not the same if there are ten passengers as it is now, when there are hundreds, even though ten is also considered public.

It is understood that I would not have written all this to you had I known it would cause you distress. I have stepped out of my usual bounds to write the above only because of the gravity of the matter, so that everyone in Eretz Yisrael (tichye) may inform everyone in their surroundings that it is an absolute prohibition to operate the ship on Shabbos in the aforementioned manner, and likewise to travel on them.

With a little contemplation, this is understood, and all the more so when compared to the battle being waged regarding the sale of pork. The Reform Temple, and even the excavations in Beis Shearim, are not comparable to the alarming matter mentioned above. For no one is misleading the masses by saying that the Reform are G-d-fearing, that pork is kosher, or that the excavations in Beis Shearim are a great mitzvah. This is not the case with the ship travel, where there are instigators—some of whom are G-d-fearing and pious Bnei Yisrael—who explain to them that they are performing a great mitzvah by traveling on an Israeli ship that also travels on Shabbos, and furthermore, that they are "saving" Bnei Yisrael because the treasury earns a few more shekels thereby. At the same time, our Torah, the Torah of life, informs us that Jerusalem was destroyed only because they desecrated the Shabbos within it, and that if they had kept one Shabbos according to its law, they would be redeemed. How wondrous is the juxtaposition of our Sages (Shabbos 119b), who add right there that this was because they did not rebuke one another and because they did not feel shame before one another.

The saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (ztzvkllh"h, nvgm, zy"a), is already well-known regarding such tactics—where they claim to improve the situation—that it is like someone trying to extinguish a fire with gasoline or kerosene. Even though it is a liquid like water, not only does it fail to extinguish the fire, but on the contrary, it makes it worse. It is difficult to write at length about such a distressing matter to the point of alarm, but I am confident that you, on your part, will do **everything** possible regarding the above. May it be Hashem’s will that we soon merit the raising of the horn of Israel and the observance of Shabbos, and the fulfillment of the promise: "Immediately they will be redeemed."

With blessings for good news regarding all the above and for a good sealing.

## 4758

B"H, 12 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, a man of many accomplishments and fine character, who contemplates matters well, R' Moshe Zalman, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

It was with pleasure that I received the telegram regarding your successful arrival in Melbourne. I am certain that you found all your family members, may they live, in good health, as well as the Anash, may they live, and that they were surely happy to greet you. After all, joy breaks through barriers—that is to say, it overcomes the matters that limit a person in his endeavors; therefore, one strives to begin every matter and undertaking with joy in order to increase success.

Enclosed is a *kuntres* containing the *maamarim* that were delivered during your stay in Brooklyn. It is my hope that you will merit to share their content with the many, and the merit of the many will depend upon you.

I am confident that in addition to conveying the general regards to the Anash, may they live, you will also convey to them, with the appropriate detail, the matter of the school for girls, *Beis Rivkah*, in Melbourne, Australia. Divine Providence has granted you and your family, may they live, the merit to be among its founders, together with your son, *shlita*. You will surely also influence your son, so that regardless of his many preoccupations and general communal affairs, he will involve himself in this school with the greatest energy, commensurate with the magnitude of the matter. May the Creator of the world and its Guide grant you much true, Jewish, Chassidic *nachas* from all your sons and daughters and family, may they live, for long, good days and years.

With the blessing of *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah*, and looking forward to hearing good news regarding the above.

#### 4758

**R' Moshe Zalman:** Feiglin, Shepparton. Another letter to him appears above, 4384.

A similar version was written to his son, R' Dovid.

## 4459

4459

B”H, 13 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the distinguished Rabbi, the renowned Gaon, G-d-fearing, and pious,
[titles of honor]
Rabbi Reuven HaKohen, shlita,
Chief Rabbi, Av Beis Din, and Rosh Yeshiva of Petach Tikvah and the district.

Greetings and blessings!

Following up on my telegram of the 9th of Elul, enclosed are copies of my letters—dated 23 Tammuz and Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av—to the Chief Rabbis, R’ Yitzchak Isaac HaLevi Herzog, shlita, and R’ Yitzchak Nissim, shlita, as well as a copy of my telegram to them from Monday, 2 Menachem Av. In these, I clearly explained my position regarding the alarming matter of the Israeli ships.

I am deeply distressed by the length of the deliberation and the delay in the ruling of the Chief Rabbinate of Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, during which time many of our brethren traveling on these ships are stumbling. My distress is particularly great regarding those traveling on the ship scheduled to depart on the 15th of Elul in order to arrive in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established, for the upcoming Yomim Nora’im, and another ship scheduled to depart on the eve of Yom Kippur.

It is my hope that your honor, shlita, will do everything in your power, urgently, to prevent the public Chillul Shabbos and Chillul Hashem.

With respect and blessings for all the best, and with blessings for a *Kesivah V’Chasimah Tovah*.

Enclosed is the *kuntres* that we have just published, in which you will surely find much of interest.

#### ***4459***

**Rabbi Reuven:** Katz.

A similar letter was sent to Rabbis Frank and Unterman.

**The Kuntres:** See letter 4455 above.

## 4510

B"H, 13 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To our brothers and sisters, the children of Israel,
May Hashem grant them life,

Peace and blessing!

The financial situation of the Yeshivos Tomchei Tmimim Lubavitch compels me to issue a special appeal for assistance to the public.

For decades upon decades, the Lubavitcher yeshivos have served as the greatest fortresses of Torah Temimah with yiras shamayim. It is also well known what a revival and spirit of life the Lubavitcher yeshivos in America have brought into Jewish life in America since their transplantation here in the year 5700—through the efforts of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

And at a time when every opportunity should be provided—so that the Yeshivos Tomchei Tmimim may continue their development and expansion—they are struggling with an ever-growing deficit, in addition to the challenge of meeting the current operating budget.

I turn with a heartfelt appeal to everyone to whom Torah is dear, and who recognizes the vital necessity of true Yiddishkeit in America:

Respond with a generous hand and send in your largest possible contribution for the Lubavitcher Yeshivos Tomchei Tmimim immediately. Your assistance at this moment will be a "word in its time"!

And the merit of this Torah and tzedakah during these days of mercy and favor will bring about that the Almighty—the Giver of the Torah—will grant you and your household, may they live, a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year.

With the blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 4511

4511

B”H, 15 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the Administration of the 44th National Convention
of the Tzeirei Agudas Yisrael Organization
in America
May they live

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your invitation to participate in your annual convention, which will take place, G-d willing, from the 17th to the 19th of Elul.

May it be the will of Hashem that the convention inspire its participants, and those beyond them, to fulfill their essential mission: the infusion of Torah study and the observance of its mitzvos into daily life, particularly within the younger generation, which still stands upon the threshold of life.

The necessity of this activity is many times greater in our current era, which is a period of crisis—a crisis in the world at large and in the life of our people in particular. It is a period that has brought about total disillusionment with secular ideologies and perspectives. As in every period of crisis, it is the younger generation that has been most shaken; those among them who were not educated in the Torah of truth, faith, and religion stand bewildered, searching and groping for a path in life.

It is a special obligation, and at the same time a special privilege, for every member of the younger generation who stands in the radiance of light—the light of Torah and its luminary—to be a living example of a Jew who lives a Jewish life, both within his home and outside of it.

And through this—and, of course, through direct action as well—to set the entire environment, and the youth in particular, upon the correct path, which is the path of Torah and mitzvos.

Our Sages of blessed memory stated regarding the signs of the era of *Ikvesa D’Meshicha*: "Youths will shame the elders," etc. The words of our Alter Rebbe (author of the Tanya and the Shulchan Aruch) regarding the rebukes in *Parshas Bechukosai*—and likewise regarding the rebukes in this week’s *parsha* and the rebukes in the Torah in general—are well known: although they appear to be curses, in truth they are nothing but blessings (*Likutei Torah, Bechukosai* 48a).

May it be the will of Hashem that the youths and young children of Israel in America will not make peace even with those matters that are inconsistent with the complete, perfect Torah—matters against which the elders, the generation preceding them, ceased to struggle.

Rather, they should fight against these matters as well, with a vigorous offensive. With a great voice, they should proclaim and declare that our Torah is a Torah of truth, and in truth there is no room for compromise. It is eternal, and in all eras and in all lands, it is what governs the life of every single Jewish man and woman in their daily life.

We have been promised by the Creator and Guide of the world that with the appropriate strength and the necessary energy, we will ultimately be victorious in this battle. May it be the will of Hashem that this occur without any concealments or hiddenness, and speedily.

For every single moment in this world-altering era of ours, the fate of many Jewish souls depends upon it: whether they will remain faithful to our Torah—the Torah of life—and its mitzvos, or whether they will be swept away, Heaven forefend, into the current of those who hate and ostracize us, who begin with compromises and end with the total negation of the tradition of our ancestors.

With blessings for tangible activities in raising the banner of our ancestral tradition through the infusion of the rule of Torah and mitzvos in ever-widening circles, and with the blessing of *Kesivah V’Chasimah Tovah*.

#### ***4511***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 16, p. 557, and completed according to the secretariat’s copy.

## 4512

B"H, 19 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the avreich, Moshe Chaim, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from the beginning of the month of Elul.

It is understood that you are correct in what you write, that the study of Pnimiyus HaTorah is a preparation for the coming of Moshiach and the complete Geulah. As is well known, the response of Melech HaMoshiach to the Baal Shem Tov was that he would come when "your wellsprings spread outward." However, we have **only** the wellsprings of the Baal Shem Tov, our Alter Rebbe, their students, and the students of their students. As is known, the study of Pnimiyus HaTorah must be through receiving from a Rav, as the Arizal and his student, the Rch"u, elaborated upon at length; see the startling words there regarding this.

You do not write your study schedule in Toras HaNigleh and Toras HaChassidus.

By Toras HaChassidus, I refer to the aforementioned wellsprings of the Baal Shem Tov, etc., and you will surely complete this at the next opportunity.

You surely know of the three daily portions—Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya—and you should keep them, at the very least, from here on in.

With the blessing of Kesivah V'Chasimah Tovah,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita

#### 4512

**Moshe Chaim:** Brandwein, Jerusalem.

## 4513

B"H, 19 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I have received your letter of 14 Elul, as well as the one preceding it.

It is surprising that in matters such as these, the custom is to bring the issues before a Rav who is a *moreh hora'ah* in Yisrael, whether through a *zeh borer lo echad* (a private arbitration) or a *din Torah*, rather than writing about a fellow Jew using the expressions you have used in your letter.

This is especially true given that we are currently in the month of Elul, the month of mercy, when every single one of *Bnei Yisrael*—even the most righteous—is in need of Heavenly mercy. It has already been said, "I will bless those who bless you," and it is alarming that precisely during these days, one would use the opposite language. After all, the principle that "a person is close to himself" applies to everyone. If the concern that one might lack his sustenance is so great that it leads one to use such an alarming expression regarding another person, then surely this concern stems from an impure source, and consequently, it causes a person to transgress the will of his Creator, who commanded us to bless every single Jew, as our Alter Rebbe explains at length in Tanya, Chapter 32.

May it be the will of Hashem that during these days of favor in the month of Elul, your conduct will be transformed from one extreme to the other. It has already been said, "Whoever has mercy on the creatures"—and the precision of this phrasing, "the creatures," is well known—and this applies *a fortiori* when the subject concerns the people about whom you are writing.

With blessings for good tidings in all the aforementioned, and may it be His will that you stand the test and be inscribed and sealed for good,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4514

B”H, 19 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 13 Elul, in which you write regarding your situation concerning the study of Torah, yiras shamayim, and the battle with the yetzer, etc.

The matter has already been addressed in the Tanya. You should study the Iggeres HaTeshuvah therein, as well as at least the first 37 chapters of the first part of Tanya, and also some chapters of Derech Chaim. You should also meet with the young men of Aguch in Jerusalem—may it be rebuilt and established—and your success in this will increase as well.

It is appropriate to have your tefillin checked, to ensure that you wear a tallis katan whose tzitzis have been checked, and that every weekday, before the morning davening, you set aside a few coins for tzedakah.

With blessings for a kesivah v’chasimah tovah,

On behalf of the Rebbe shlita,

Secretary

## 4515

B"H, 19 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 5 Elul:

... 2) Regarding the remedy for one who has committed several sins:

He should study *Iggeres HaTeshuvah* by our Alter Rebbe, as well as Part I of *Tanya*; therein he will find the proper advice for the aforementioned.

3) Regarding the remedy for one who has committed a sin for which there is no [apparent] repentance:

There is nothing that stands before *teshuvah*, and there is no sin that *teshuvah* cannot rectify—to the point that even willful sins can be transformed into merits. However, not all *teshuvah* is the same, and not all efforts in this regard are equal. When he studies the aforementioned *Iggeres HaTeshuvah*, he will see light and know what lies before him to do. And since "a person creates many companions," he should befriend the *Tzeirei Agudas Chabad* and the elders of *anash* in the Holy City of Jerusalem (may it be rebuilt and established), and they will instruct him.

It is appropriate that he have his *tefillin* checked, and likewise that he keep the three well-known daily study portions: *Chumash*, *Tehillim*, and *Tanya*.

With the blessing of *Kesivah V'Chasimah Tovah*,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4516

4516

B"H, 20 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the Administration of the Reshet Oholei Yosef Yitzchok Lubavitch
in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt speedily,
May Hashem preserve them and keep them alive.

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your minutes and the telegrams...

You have surely included in the Reshet’s curriculum special lessons in connection with the upcoming days of Tishrei. It would be most appropriate that during the upcoming days of the Chag, you meet with the students and hold a Simchas Beis Hashoeivah gathering together. Furthermore, it would be excellent if the campaign for the Arba Minim were implemented specifically within the students' homes, by the Reshet’s teachers or at the very least by supporters of the Reshet. Through this, the connection will be strengthened, and the light of the learning will enter their homes as well; and this is easy to understand.

I look forward to hearing good news regarding all of the above and all necessary details, and I sign with a blessing for outstanding success in your holy work, and for a *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah*.

## 4517

B.H., 20 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 14 Elul, in which you write regarding the reasoning for traveling here to study.

My position on this is already well-known: the work in the vineyard of Chabad in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established) is vast, and unfortunately, the neglect is also great. It is incumbent upon every individual who is on the ground there to utilize all of their strengths and possibilities for this work, until the ruins are restored and the deficiencies filled, etc. Every moment that passes without this is an irretrievable loss, and who knows if there will ever be an opportune time like the present. If this applies to everyone, it applies all the more so to the young, whose strength and energy are still with them and who have not yet spent so much time in this "world of falsehood." This is especially true regarding you, as you have seen good fruit from your labor in the field of proper chinuch. Any thought that weakens or confuses one in this is from an impure source, as is known from the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (Rashab) of blessed memory, regarding the test in such matters (see *Hayom Yom*, 23 Sivan, 5703).

The conclusion from the above is that you should contemplate deeply what you must accomplish regarding Chabad affairs in the Holy Land (may it be rebuilt and established), and begin immediately with practical actions in this regard. Among these is also influencing your entire environment, explaining to them that these matters are directed at each and every one of them. There is no need to elaborate on a simple matter, even though sometimes the "bribe" of peripheral concerns blinds even the eyes of the wise. May it be His will that in your next letter you bring good news that you have begun to act in all that has been mentioned, and specifically with joy, and Hashem will grant you success.

With blessings for a *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah*.

#### ***4517***

**As is known:** See also above, letter 4540, and the references in the notes there.

## 4518

4518

B"H, 21 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter, in which you write regarding the incident that occurred at the time of your daughter’s wedding—may it be in a good and auspicious hour.

It is explained in several holy seforim that whenever a person witnesses a miracle—and in truth, there is not a day that does not contain miracles, only that the beneficiary of the miracle does not recognize his miracle, and especially so in the case of an open miracle—the person who witnesses the great wonders performed by the kindness of Hashem must contemplate this and bring a "sacrifice of thanksgiving." My intention is that one should increase their involvement in our Torah, the Torah of life, and its mitzvos. This itself also serves as a vessel to increase the kindness of Hashem toward the beneficiary of the miracle and his household; the implication is understood...

And as we approach the new year that is coming upon us and upon all of Yisrael for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all that is yours: a blessing of *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

With blessing.

## 4519

4519

B”H, 22 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the honorable Menachem Harari, may he live

Peace and blessing!

I am writing to acknowledge receipt of your letter from 13 Tammuz, as well as the manuscripts sent by R’ Pinchas Althaus.

Surely you are aware that these manuscripts are not authored by your late father, but are rather maamarim that he transcribed, which were delivered at various times. Therefore, it is not appropriate to print them as they are, even though maamarim from this period are included in the plan that we hope, eventually, to print for the benefit of the public when their time comes and as time and material means permit. However, even until then, these writings serve to clarify matters in other maamarim that are currently being printed, in accordance with the well-known statement of our Sages: "Words of Torah are poor in one place and rich in another." Thus, the merit of the public already depends on them even now.

I would be interested to know if you recall any stories from your late father regarding his visits to our Rebbes, which might contain content of interest to the public as well.

With renewed thanks for the gift.

With respect and with the blessing of *Kesivah V’chasimah Tovah*.

## 4760

4760

B”H, 22 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 15 Elul, in which you outline the main points of your life’s history.

It would be appropriate for you to study the *Iggeres HaTeshuvah* of our Alter Rebbe with the necessary contemplation and depth. There you will find an answer to your questions. In what is written in Chapter 3 (page 93a), we see that in our generation, even this method [of self-affliction] is harmful to one’s health, and consequently, it also hinders one’s *avodah* in spiritual matters. It should be replaced with the *avodah* of "afflictions" that consist of restraining one’s mouth from speaking inappropriate things, closing one’s ears from hearing them, and averting one’s eyes from seeing them. It is tangibly evident that this is a more difficult *avodah* than the aforementioned fasts, and "according to the effort is the reward."

The general point is that you should increase your involvement in Torah and *mitzvos* in general. This includes the concept of "love your fellow as yourself"—influencing others in ways of pleasantness and peace in this direction as well. One of the ways to exert this influence is to be a living example of how the conduct of *anash* should be in daily life. Then your affairs will go from strength to strength, for even a little light dispels much darkness; through this, the undesirable impressions and matters will diminish more and more.

In order to merit *siyatta d'shmaya* and the blessing of the *Ribono shel Olam* in all of the above, it is essential to increase peace within your own four cubits and in your entire surroundings. I await good tidings regarding all of this.

Regarding your notification about the condition of your wife, *tichyeh*:

May the *Ribono shel Olam* fulfill the days of her pregnancy properly and with ease, and may she give birth to a healthy child at the proper time, in a healthy manner and with ease.

As we approach the new year, which is coming upon us and all of *Klal Yisrael* for good and for blessing, I hereby extend my blessing to you and to all who are yours: a blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

With blessings,

## 4761

B"H, 23 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter of the 20th of Elul, along with its enclosure, has just been received. As you requested, I will mention Mrs. [Name]—may she live and be well—at the holy gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, regarding all that she needs in general, and for the improvement of her health in particular. It is surely unnecessary to remind you that every increase in Torah and mitzvos performed by her family members also adds to her merit, increasing the blessings of the Creator and Guide of the world in all that she requires.

And lastly, your announcement of your marriage, in a good and auspicious hour.

It is surely unnecessary to remind you that in marriage—which is the beginning of a new life for two people who become partners, and subsequently, a unity in building a home in Israel—a firm resolution is required and necessary to establish life upon foundations that withstand the changes of this world of ours, without their strength and vigor being diminished, since the endurance of the entire structure depends upon the strength of its foundations.

For the life of the Jewish people, and of every individual Jew, has endured various and strange periods and changes, yet—in the language of the verse—"You are all standing here today". This is due to the way of life according to the instruction of our Torah, the Torah of life. This is why many nations, great and strong in material terms, have collapsed and are no more, while the Jewish people live and endure. And all these things apply to each and every Jewish person.

Based on this, we can also understand the demand upon each of us: "And let him not be ashamed before those who mock". Even though the Jewish people are "the fewest of all the peoples", and those who observe Torah and mitzvos are not always the majority among the Jewish people, nevertheless, this way of life is the duty and the privilege of each and every one of us, and it is precisely this that leads to a happy life, both spiritually and materially.

May the Creator and Guide of the world grant you and your wife—may you live—the merit to resolve to act in the direction mentioned above, and may you be happy all the days of your lives.

With blessings of Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov, and for a good inscription and sealing.

## 4422

B"H, 23 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Mrs. Z. Althaus, may she live; Mrs. G. Ginzburg, may she live;
Mrs. B. Zalmanov, may she live.

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter of 8 Elul and the attachment, as well as your inquiry via telegram, which was reiterated by the esteemed R. Refael Cohen, may he live.

You yourselves must provide the answer to this, as it depends on the character of the individuals involved. We have seen tangibly, and continue to see, that people begin projects requiring certain sums even when they are guaranteed a much smaller portion of the budget than you have been promised. If they simply act with the necessary energy, without allowing room for doubt or being influenced by others who come asking "why and wherefore," they succeed. Furthermore, the difficulties are far fewer than one anticipates in advance. The same applies to this matter: since a significant portion of the budget is already on hand, if you work without fear, you will certainly succeed in establishing the institution. You must not be discouraged by difficulties, especially at the beginning, and you must not be discouraged by those who mock—for the more necessary a thing is, the greater the number of mockers, especially those who mock out of jealousy that they do not wish to reveal.

Regarding what you write about the nature of the teachers and counselors, etc.:

I have already spoken on this in connection with several events: every Chabad institution influences the others, and weakness in fundamental matters in one is multiplied many times over in the other institutions. A person looks at the eyes and does not take interest in observing what is in the heart. If one sees that a counselor is being accepted from a certain circle, it is, for him, an explicit message that he, too, is permitted to do the same, and he will publicize this—without asking whether there were special conditions or unique merits in the instance from which he wishes to learn. Therefore, it is consistent with what I have written regarding the girls' seminary as well: it is imperative not to compromise at all on matters pertaining to yiras shamayim, especially in an educational institution in general, and in a Chabad institution in particular. For they are being watched with seven eyes (and especially by those who wish to find faults), and the wise will understand.

Since you write in your letter that there are candidates for whom everything is in order, and there will be no need to justify any difficulties, it is understood that they have priority.

With blessings for success in your holy work with peace of mind, and awaiting good news.

## 4763

4763

B”H, 24 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Rabbi Avraham Tzvi HaKohen, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

It is surprising that for a very long time I have not received any news from you. After all, in every period and at all times, one must ensure that "every single day accomplishes its work." This is all the more true in this generation, the heels of Mashiach, for since our Sages of blessed memory have informed us that "there is no day whose curse is not..."—it is understood that the intent of this notification is that the Children of Israel must increase, each day, the matters that bring about the transformation of "Hashem your G-d [turning] the curse into a blessing."

See also *Likutei Torah* by our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe, *Parshas Bechukosai*, on the verse "In my breaking, etc., and ten women will bake," [explaining] that all the rebukes are actually blessings. And while it is understood that writing about one's activities is not the primary thing, but rather secondary to the secondary, nevertheless, we see tangibly that when one knows that at a certain time he must write to someone or tell someone about his activities, this arouses additional energy and courage. And who is greater than the firstborn of Israel, Reuven? Our Sages said, "Had he known, etc.," and the matter does not depart from its literal meaning, despite what the *Yefeh Toar* explains there (*Vayikra Rabbah*, ch. 34) [and to remove his difficulty and wonderment, one can say that it is along the lines of what is explained in a letter of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, printed in *HaTamim*, regarding Avraham Avinu: that all interpretations have a place, since the subject is a soul clothed in a body; see there].

I am writing now in continuation of the information received through the *shluchim*, the young rabbis, may they live and be well, during their visit to Jerusalem, and especially in Meah Shearim.

I wish to suggest that you establish a *shiur* several times a week for the study of *Tanya* in one of the *batei knesios* there. It is understood that I do not mean for those who are already in such a *shiur*, but rather for the *batei knesios* of those who call themselves *Neturei Karta*, who recently printed in their bulletin that they should refrain from studying the Chassidus of Chabad, etc. Since the opposition to the study of Chassidus has grown so much that it found a place in the aforementioned bulletin, and regrettably, some say that one or two people have actually refrained from studying Chassidus because of it, it is understood that all those who drink from the waters of the Baal Shem Tov, his students, and his students' students, must not only protest against the aforementioned lines—for after all, protest is not the ultimate goal—but rather, they must increase the study of Chassidus and disseminate it even "outward," and even within the four cubits of those who oppose this study. I am confident that you will succeed, based on the ruling of the Heavenly Court that in every matter of Torah, fear of Heaven, and good character traits, the hand of those who are connected to him and follow in his footsteps will be on top (see *Kitzurim VeHe'aros LeTanya*, p. 122).

It is known from our Sages that the world cannot exist without a tanner (*bursi*) and without a perfumer (*besam*); happy is he who, etc. It is understood how great the pity is for a tanner who fights or opposes the perfumer. Just as all the words of our holy Torah speak of the higher realms and hint at the lower ones, these words of our Sages also apply to a person’s *avodah* in his world—to make of the world a dwelling place for Him, may He be blessed—where the *avodah* is divided into two lines: *sur meira* (turning from evil) and *aseh tov* (doing good).

The tanner, who processes hides (as explained in *Torah Or*, beginning of *Parshas Mishpatim*, regarding the Hebrew slave who serves G-d, etc.), and the perfumer, who disseminates Torah in general and the inner dimension of Torah—the teachings of Chassidus—in particular, which relates to the matter of scent. As explained by our Sages (see Rashi on *Shir HaShirim* on the verse "Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth") that the reasons and the inner dimension of the Torah will be revealed by Mashiach, whose distinct sign and essence is scent—as in the story of our Sages that through "smelling and judging," they proved the authenticity of Mashiach at a time when, from the perspective of other matters, it was not clear. From this very statement of our Sages, it is proven that the world—meaning, the *avodah*—cannot exist without the *avodah* of the tanner, as explained in many places. But, as mentioned above, it is alarming if the tanner causes opposition to the perfumer. Elaboration on such a simple matter is superfluous, and at the same time, it is distressing to see what we have reached in our generation, where up until now, those who write the bulletin say that they listen to their voice and accept their instructions; this means that they have the power to protest, yet nothing has been heard from them, and this gives room to say that silence, etc.

It is simple that my intention for the aforementioned fixed study is not that it be done secretly and without revealing that this is the study of Chassidus, etc., but rather that it should be done in the manner of the opposition's actions—namely, that it should be stated explicitly that one wishes to establish this study in such-and-such *beis knesses*.

It is appropriate that one of the study times should be on Shabbos Kodesh, which is holy for the study of the inner dimension of the Torah, as explained by the Arizal and in many places, and at length in the *Kuntres Eitz Chaim* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (the Rashab), of blessed memory.

I am sending this letter as urgent, so that you may begin the efforts in this matter as a preparation for the coming year. How good it would be to begin the study on Erev Rosh Hashanah, which would bring merit to the entire past year, or at the very least, to the beginning of the coming year. May it be the will [of Hashem] that this year be the year of our redemption and the coming of Mashiach Tzidkeinu, for which the dissemination of the wellsprings outward is the preface and preparation, and the vessel for this: "For the earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem as the waters cover the sea," through the Torah of Mashiach, which he will teach to all of Israel—a knowledge that is incomparable to the Torah of now, as our Sages say in *Koheles Rabbah*, ch. 11, on the verse "For if a man live many years."

In anticipation of the New Year coming upon us and all of Israel for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all who are yours, a blessing of *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah* for a good and sweet year, materially and spiritually.

With blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

## 4724

4724

By the Grace of Hashem, 24 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed Rabbi, man of action and energy,
R’ Amram, shlita,

Peace and blessing!

It gave me pleasure to learn from the esteemed R’ Uriel Zimmer, who, at my request, visited you and spoke with you regarding the dissemination of the study of Chassidus in those circles and environments where your reach and influence extend. This is especially pertinent given that your conversation touched upon your lineage and background—that you were connected, and surely remain linked, to our Rebbe, the Nasi, and among them, the Tzemach Tzedek. Holiness never departs from its place, and children and grandchildren merit beauty, etc., and wisdom, etc. If this is true regarding physical beauty and wisdom, how much more so regarding spiritual beauty and wisdom—especially through the study of their Torah, which is eternal. Of it, it is said, "Are not My words like fire?"—which purifies from all matters of concealment and hiddenness even more than purification by water, as our Sages have said (Sanhedrin 39a), "The primary purification is through fire."

Since we are at the very end of the year and in but a brief moment we will enter a new year, we rely on the words of the Alter Rebbe in his Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 14, that every single year, a new and renewed light descends and shines from Chochma Ila'ah—a light that has never shone before—to the higher world, etc., and to the lower beings who receive their vitality from it.

I therefore propose that it would be most appropriate for the new year to be inaugurated—specifically from Rosh Hashanah—through the education and study of Chassidus, including in public and in places where the public gathers; that is, in the shuls, batei medrash, and yeshivos where you are able to exert influence. For the Torah in general is the light, and also the vessel for absorbing and uniting with the lights and the influences from Above. It is understood that if it were possible to begin at least one day in the year 5716, how good and appropriate that would be, for a day in a year is considered a year. I am confident that for one such as yourself, further elaboration on this is unnecessary. May it be Hashem’s will that through the study and dissemination of the wellsprings outward—which are the preface, preparation, and vessel for that which is written, "The earth shall be full of the knowledge of Hashem as the waters cover the sea," in the Torah of Moshiach, who will teach knowledge to all of Israel, which is incomparable to the Torah of the present—and as our Sages say (Koheles Rabbah, ch. 11, on the verse "For if a man live many years"), may this year be the year of our true redemption.

Approaching the new year that is coming upon us and all of Israel for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all who are yours: a blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, in both physical and spiritual matters.

With respect and blessing,

P.S.
I understood from R’ Uriel Zimmer that there are matters with which you do not agree, and perhaps even oppose, etc. But surely this should not be linked to the implementation of the aforementioned, nor should it cause any delay, however brief. For the words of the Rambam are well known: "Accept the truth from whoever says it." In this, I am merely transmitting the instructions and matters of our Nasi, the Baal Shem Tov, and the Alter Rebbe, who sacrificed themselves for the study of the inner dimension of the Torah and commanded those who follow them—those who drink of their waters—to do everything possible within the entirety of Israel, and "whoever has the ability, etc."

4525

B"H, Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Nitzavim-Vayeilech, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

I have a strong hope that your health has improved. Based on the statement of our Sages that the Torah grants permission to the physician to heal, you are surely following the instructions of the doctors—not only regarding medicine in the literal sense, but also concerning eating and drinking, which are also included in the realm of healing. One should not be stringent in this matter, for such stringency is actually a leniency; every Jew is commanded to guard their body and its health. When something is permitted according to Shulchan Aruch, and a doctor has ordered it, one must be stringent to fulfill that directive. This is especially true in light of the saying of our Alter Rebbe: "One has absolutely no conception of how precious the body of a Jew is to Hashem."

Therefore, those foods and drinks that the doctor says you must consume, and which a halachic authority permits, you should consume accordingly—and may it be for your health, both physically and spiritually.

With blessings for good news regarding all the above, and for a good and sweet year, inscribed and sealed for good.

#### ***4525***

**Saying:** See also above, Vol. 10, Letter 3190, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4726

B"H, 26 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...Regarding what you wrote, that you remain in doubt: since by nature you do everything *mit a bren* (with fervor), it seemed to you from my letter that you need to calm yourself, because B"H matters are proceeding according to the intention, and that you should not seek out "great and hidden things," etc.

You did not interpret my intention in accordance with what I meant. My intention is that, in my opinion as well, you can indeed accomplish great and hidden things. In my opinion, too, both the general and specific matters provide room for completion and improvement—as is the case with all human endeavors, and all the more so regarding matters connected to chinuch. Furthermore, in my opinion, this *hislahavus* (enthusiasm) is beneficial in all that has been mentioned. However, in my opinion, *hislahavus* does not mean that because of that enthusiasm, one should not look to the right or the left and should act with eyes closed. Rather, it is possible—and indeed necessary—that the *hislahavus* should add energy, and that matters should be carried out through consultation with at least a few others, and not with only one person or without any consultation at all. The statement "in the multitude of counselors there is salvation" applies even to matters of holiness that must be carried out specifically with *hislahavus*. It is obvious that *hislahavus* does not preclude making room for the opinions and advice of others.

...As I have written many times, even Rav Ashi, who possessed both Torah and greatness in one place, would consult with all the butchers in his locale. How much more so, *v'kal v'chomer*, etc. May it be His will that you take all that has been said above according to my intention—that is, the words as they are simply meant—and I am confident that the conduct described is for your benefit and for the benefit of the institution, both materially and spiritually.

And in anticipation of the new year approaching us and all of Yisrael for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all who are yours: a blessing for a good and sweet year, materially and spiritually.

With blessings for good tidings in all the above.

#### ***4726***

**As I have written many times:** See also above, letter 4728, and the references in the notes there.

## 4727

4727

B"H, 26 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing man, a man of energy and many accomplishments,
R' Shimshon Raphael, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I received with pleasure your blessing for the coming year. May all who bless be blessed with the blessing of Hashem, whose addition is greater than the essence...

And although the saying of our Alter Rebbe is well known, that "one does not force advice upon others," nevertheless, when I see the spiritual state of the youth in the United States and the shift that has occurred in recent years—where it is easier to make an impact than in the past, and there is room for even greater efforts—it is understood that anyone who can act in this direction, and certainly one who has already acted and seen the fruits of his labor, must mobilize himself for this holy work.

And although, as with all matters of holiness, there are many concealments and obstructions—one of which is the tactics of the *yetzer* to tempt the person who is active into believing that his efforts are of no use, and that it is a waste of the time and energy that could be utilized for other matters, especially other matters of holiness—

The saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, in the name of his father, the Rebbe Rashab—of blessed memory, may his merit protect us—is well known: that any matter or argument that brings about a weakening in a person’s efforts in Torah and *mitzvos*, even if their external appearance is holy, their source is from the "other side" (*sitra achra*). For since he is an expert in his craft, he sometimes disguises himself and comes to a person in a silk cloak—a *zeidene zupitze*—and the rest is understood.
