It is my hope that you will not take offense at these lines, for as mentioned above, "one does not force advice upon others." But since I see in this the good of many, many members of the young Jewish community and their families—which naturally makes it clear to me that this is also for the good of yourself and your family—I felt it a duty to say what I have said, and I ask for your forgiveness.

And approaching the new year, which is coming upon us and all of Israel for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and all that is yours: a blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, in both material and spiritual matters.

With respect and with a blessing for success in the above.

#### ***4727***

**R' Shimshon Raphael:** Weiss, N.Y. Further letters to him—see above, Vol. 12, p. 44, and the references in the notes there.

**Saying of our Alter Rebbe:** See also above, Vol. 5, p. 407, and the references in the notes there. Above, p. 423.

**The saying is well known:** See also above, letter 4540, and the references in the notes there.

**In a silk cloak:** See also above, Vol. 11, letter 3503, and the references in the notes there.

## 4768

B”H, 27 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished individual, who engages in communal needs with noble character, a scion of noble ancestors, etc., R’ Sh.Z. shlita,

Shalom u’vracha!

As we approach the New Year, which is coming upon us and upon all of Yisrael for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all who are yours: a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

I hesitated whether to write these following lines to you; nevertheless, I will do so, at least briefly.

Based on the information I received from the shluchim shlita upon their return from Eretz Yisrael (and in continuation of our conversation during your visit here regarding the younger generation and the youth in Eretz Yisrael, who have become disillusioned in many respects and, to a significant degree, feel themselves without a desirable anchor in the life of the present), it is to my greatest regret that my concern regarding this has been confirmed, and to an even greater extent. It appears that the aforementioned disillusionment has led a certain segment—also significant in quality—of the youth and young people to a yearning for the pleasures of this world in their literal sense (though, to my satisfaction and in the current situation, for the most part these are not forbidden pleasures, but rather, in the language of the Tanya, those of *kelipas nogah*), such as a nice apartment and fine furniture, a comfortable and quiet position, etc., with all their desires directed toward this, to the point of distancing themselves from achievements in spiritual matters. And it is understood that if a portion of the youth is of the aforementioned type, there is also a minority that has descended even further—from *kelipas nogah* and below.

As I also said during our conversation, in a person’s life, the situation at a specific moment is not as important as the direction and the path one is taking. Therefore, what is distressing and also frightening about the aforementioned situation is that throughout these last years, the path that the youth and young people in Eretz Yisrael have been inclined toward is in the aforementioned direction: from spirituality to less spirituality, from there to physicality, and from physicality to materialism.

It is understood that my intention is not at all to distress you, and for this reason I have been as brief as possible regarding all of the above. I have only written these lines in the hope that you will utilize your influence upon those circles that have it in their power to finally take the appropriate measures to rectify this situation, and it goes without saying that they should distance themselves from anything that pushes the youth and young people in the aforementioned direction.

And it is worth noting that a portion of the shluchim met unofficially also with those who are not from the Chareidi circles—not only the middle-of-the-road circles, but also those who are not currently observant of Torah and mitzvos at all. To my regret, in all of these, the aforementioned decline is felt, although, of course, to a different degree from one circle to another.

With respect and blessing, and for good tidings regarding the above.

## 4729

By the Grace of Hashem
27 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and diligent individual,
Mr. Yitzchak, may he live.

Peace and Blessing!

I received your letter in due time, along with a reprint of your article, "Until When?" I derived great pleasure from the content of your article, and it is my hope that this is the beginning of a series of articles—and, what is even more important, a series of actions—whose goal is the attainment of the hoped-for objective.

I delayed my response to the above because I wanted to see what echo and reaction your article would bring in the circles for which it was intended. Now, however, after Mr. Blizinsky, may he live, came here and conveyed to me the content of the conversation that took place between you, I see to my regret that one of my concerns regarding this has been confirmed: namely, that the statement of our Sages, "The Merciful One desires the heart," is being used out of context. In other words, even though they acknowledge that the demand is generally correct, they refuse to draw and fulfill the conclusions in practice. Consequently, they evade them in every way, with hollow excuses, to the point of confusing the mind.

My intent is simple: The foundation of all foundations is that one must recognize the truth in its complete reality and not begin with compromises. This does not mean, of course, that one should expect that everyone whose conduct is not one hundred percent as it should be will immediately transform their conduct from one extreme to the other. But one must demand that they **recognize** it, and truly as it is, for truth and compromise are two opposites. Even if it is possible that a person, despite recognizing the truth, fails in practice for various reasons, or due to a lack of strength of will, he must still recognize at the time of his failure that he is committing a transgression against his conscience. For then, there is hope that he will strive to correct himself and draw closer and closer to the desired perfection. This is not the case if a person deceives himself into thinking that the compromise is the ultimate perfection and places the compromise in the place of the truth; in doing so, he denies himself the possibility of correcting what is distorted and lacking.

From the above, my perspective is understood regarding the discussion you had with Mr. Blizinsky, may he live, concerning the question of what should be demanded of those who come to seek guidance and an answer to their question, "Until when?"

My opinion is clear, based on experience: one must answer them with firmness. The Torah is a Torah of life, which does not allow for compromises. And consider the logic: if you acknowledge that the Torah is from Heaven, then man, with his limited intellect and narrow understanding, cannot or is not permitted to change anything in it. On the other hand, if human intellect is the final arbiter, then the Torah is stripped of all its value and validity as the Torah of the living G-d, and man is left without a support. As the popular adage goes, a man cannot lift himself up by pulling on his own hair.

If the inquirer recognizes the truth and wishes to reach it, then we provide him with a regimen suited to the nature of his soul and his environment, to show him the best and most appropriate path for him to reach the goal. One person may be inclined toward the path of "turn from evil," while another chooses "do good," etc. These are merely instructions on the most efficient way to reach the desired goal. This is not the case if the beginning itself is a compromise, for then it will not only fail to bring one to the desired goal, but will even lead to results that are the opposite of what was intended.

I would also add, based on what is explained in *Tanya*, that a person’s heart should not fall within him during the heavy battle he has with his *yetzer*, and sometimes it seems to him that it overcomes him every day. For such is the way of our world, that sometimes one falls and one rises, and sometimes, etc. But it is clear that with every good action and good deed, one adds more light to the world, diminishing the darkness that is in the world—and not only in his own world, this small world that is man, but also in the world at large (and see also *Iggeres HaTeshuvah*, Chapter 11).

There are circles here, and surely similar ones in the Holy Land (may it be built and established), who say that if they come with a program of all 613 *mitzvos*, they will frighten those who come to be purified and will also drive away many souls of Israel, etc.

But experience has shown the opposite, for in the end, they feel that they have been given a compromise, and they have no trust thereafter in the words of the guide, even after the explanation regarding the necessity that led to presenting the Torah only partially, etc.

The adage of our *Nasi* is well known: "A campaign does not return empty." I am certain that your appeal and your words have already brought fruits and the fruits of fruits. However, in our current era, one must not be satisfied with the minimum. Effort is required to utilize every moment in this period of the "pregnancy of the world" to save the souls of the children of Israel, for who knows if in the moment that follows it will still be possible to influence them. Therefore, my hope is that you will continue your activities in the aforementioned direction and absolutely reject the fears about not increasing the demands, as well as the doubts as to whether there is any benefit in your campaign. For there is, and there is, especially if you add to it with firmness and in clear terms, since you have merited, through Divine Providence, to awaken the hearts in certain circles that until now were—in the language of the Rambam—"forgetting the truth in the vanities of time, etc., sleeping and slumbering, and their sleep was sweet to them."

I will permit myself to add that one of the methods of campaigning in this and similar matters is to show a desirable, living example in the conduct of the director of the campaign who preaches and awakens. The merit of the many assists him to overcome and win his own battle, and more easily than he imagines.

I fear that for several reasons there will not be as much success with those of middle age and older, but I hope for great success among the circles of youth and young people. It is possible that they will also influence the elders and the veterans, according to the signs of our Sages regarding the footsteps of *Moshiach*, that "youths will shame the elders, elders will stand before the young," etc.—but in the positive aspect of this.

I look forward to good news regarding all that has been said, and I wish you and all that is yours a *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah* for a good and sweet year.

With respect.

Regarding practical instructions:

Since all the children of Israel are believers, the children of believers, in the words of our Sages, and our Sages have said that the matter of the *mitzvah* of *tefillin* is the subjugation of the heart and the brain, and the entire Torah is compared to *tefillin*—and on the flip side, non-kosher foods "clog" the heart and the brain—one must introduce these two matters as an immediate beginning that is equal for everyone, in addition to the essential matter of **starting** the day's affairs each and every day with *tefillah* and giving a few coins to *tzedakah* (of course, the *tzedakah* on weekdays). It is imperative that the *tefillah* be specifically in the text accepted and formulated by the Men of the Great Assembly. Beyond the internal matters involved in this, it has a primary point: that all those who return should know that they are all united in the manner of their *tefillah* and their conduct each day.

If someone specifically wants to add from his own, he should do so after the aforementioned *tefillah*. And as for *tefillah*, according to my aforementioned approach, one must know the text that is in the *Siddur*, and to pray the *Birchos HaShachar*, *Krias Shema*, *Shemoneh Esrei*, and the *Tefillas Sheva* and *Musaf* in general (and *Shemoneh Esrei* on *Motzaei Shabbos* and *Yom Tov*).

One must emphasize every single time that the failure to fulfill any *mitzvah* that may be the case for the time being, and even at the moment of beginning, is also a transgression, and for this, too, one needs the *avodah* of *teshuvah*.

## 4730

B"H, 27 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, etc., R' Meir [Blizinsky]
May he live

Greetings and blessings!

Enclosed is a copy of my letter to Mr. Dmiel, as well as a segment that, for understandable reasons, I did not wish to include in my letter to him. It would be appropriate for you to speak with the aforementioned regarding the content of this segment, whether on your own initiative or based on the fact that you are aware of my perspectives on this matter. I would appreciate it if you would inform me regarding this.

As we approach the new year, which is coming upon us and all of Israel for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and all that is yours: a blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, in both material and spiritual matters.

With blessings.

#### ***4730***

**R' Meir [Blizinsky]:** Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 9, 2919, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 4731

B"H, 27 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the end of Elul, in which you write regarding the suggestion to set aside time each day for *hisbonenus* before *davening* and during *davening*.

It is understood that this is a correct suggestion, and much good is hidden within this practice, in addition to the improvement that is seen openly in all matters of a *Yid*. This is, of course, in accordance with what is explained in many places in *Chassidus*, including *Likutei Torah*, *Parshas Balak*, in the *maamar* beginning with the words "He did not behold" (*Lo Hibit*), that *davening* is like the spinal cord that supports the entire body; see there.

May it be the will [of Hashem] that you also influence your friends in this, and that you see good fruit regarding *davening*, and from the *beis knesses* to the *beis medrash* for the study of Torah. As explained in the prayer of Abba Binyamin, that "their prayer should be adjacent to my bed," the study of Torah after the *avodah* of *davening* is in the most exalted manner.

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* and for the study of Torah with *yiras shamayim*.

## 4732

B"H, 27 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...On the occasion that you mention in your letter the arrangement of the *kiddushin*, I will emphasize once again the wording of the blessing, "a building of eternal witnesses" (*binyan adei ad*). This is because, according to the account of our Sages of blessed memory, it originates from the Men of the Great Assembly. A building in general—its beauty and its splendor—has value, and its endurance depends upon the strength of its foundations, specifically whether they can withstand the changes occurring in the world without themselves being altered.

The same applies to the building of a home in Israel. First and foremost, one must ensure that the foundational materials are tested and proven—these being the foundations of Torah and *mitzvos*, which have sustained the Jewish people through all eras, all times, and all lands, without change. Through them, the Jewish people live and endure. The analogy is understood.

May Hashem grant you success in being a *akeres habayis* (homemaker) to the glory of Israel, and together with your husband *shlita*, may you be happy in both material and spiritual matters.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

On behalf of the Rebbe *shlita*

4733

B”H, 28 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied with communal needs, etc.,
R’ Shazar, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter of 22 Elul from Paris, as well as the *pidyon nefesh* via Rabbi Kirshblum, may he live, and I thank you for your attention to the matter.

Regarding the matter itself, I must admit that my mind is not at all at ease... I will be as brief as possible:

...Knowing as a mechanic the manner in which ships are operated and managed, it is an explicit and well-known fact that leaves no room for doubt regarding the halachic ruling on this... And since you write in your letter yourself that through your pressure you succeeded in fixing several things, it is simple that there is also great hope here that you will succeed if you act with the necessary vigor. After all, for several reasons, you are able to exert more pressure than others can.

As for what you write regarding those who wrote to you about this issue—that it was not the sanctity of Shabbos that touched their souls, but rather a desire to cause harm, etc.—it is understood that this response does not mitigate the prohibition involved (and along with it) the danger to the security of the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, and the security of its inhabitants. There are many statements of our Sages regarding the connection between the observance of Shabbos and the security of the aforementioned, and an entire *sugya* is devoted to this in *Maseches Shabbos* (119b), in addition to many statements of our Sages scattered throughout our Torah. Surely, for someone like you, the references are superfluous.

An additional danger to the matter itself arises from reports in the press and rumors in various circles that there are those who permit the sailing of the ships and traveling on them, since the profits are used for the purchase of weapons, etc. Surely you have also seen the report in *HaTzofeh* regarding the discussion between the Chief Rabbinate and Captain Halperin. From this, one can derive a *gezeirah shavah* and a *binyan av*, and even a *kal v'chomer*, that factories in the Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, are "commanded" to work seven days a week, etc., etc.

By the way, regarding the immigration from Africa, which you touched upon in your letter and which is also carried out by these ships, the arrangement is even easier. Since the voyage from the shores of North Africa to the Holy Land can be completed within six days, this means that if it departs on the first day, the entire problem regarding these trips would be resolved. It is obvious that in this, you have the decisive opinion, since now everyone admits that the immigrants from Africa are all G-d-fearing and observant of His *mitzvos*.

As we approach the new year, which is coming upon us and all of Israel for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all that is yours: a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

With respect and blessing.

Enclosed is a *kuntres* that we have just published, and I am sure you will find it of great interest.

***

4733

R’ Shazar: Shragai. Additional letters to him: above 4531, and in the references in the footnotes there.

Regarding the matter itself: An Israeli ship traveling on Shabbos. See above, letter 4555, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 4734

B"H, 28 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

The Administration of Kfar Chabad
May Hashem preserve them

Peace and Blessing!

I hereby acknowledge receipt of your report from your meeting on Motzoei Shabbos Parshas Ki Seitzei, as well as the previous ones. Regarding the matter of the mikveh, I was surprised to hear that because the plan is to build two mikvaos (one for women and a separate one for men), each costing 12,000–13,000 Lira, and unfortunately there is only about 12,000 in the fund, you are waiting to begin construction until the entire sum is collected, while in the meantime you are using the old mikveh. The difficulties involved in this are understood—walking at night during the approaching winter months, etc.—and even without that, it is incomprehensible why you are waiting so many months for this or that possibility. In my opinion, you should **immediately** begin construction on the women’s mikveh, for which you already have the full amount. The men can continue to use the old mikveh for the time being; after all, men use the mikveh in the morning, not at night, and this is obvious. It is my hope that if you proceed with the necessary vigor, the construction will be completed before the coming winter.

I asked the secretary, R' Y.M. shlita, about the status of the searchlight and the street lighting. He said that according to the plan, the electric lamps should certainly be hanging already, whereas the searchlight has not yet been started. It appears from your report that even the electric lights are not yet in place. I hope that you will hasten this as much as possible, since from the 15th of Menachem Av the days have been growing shorter, etc.

I have received complaints from several of the Sephardim living in the Kfar... who wish to benefit from the radiance of a farbrengen but are unable to do so because they do not understand Yiddish. It would be appropriate for you to discuss how to rectify this at your next meeting. This is a matter that is timely, as the seventh month is the one that is "saturated" with everything.

... Even according to human logic, and even the logic of the *nefesh habehamis*, it is understood that when each person views his fellow as a competitor, it results not only in a lack of assistance from one to the other, but the opposite. This is especially true according to what is explained in the words of our Sages and in the teachings of Chassidus in particular: that peace is the only vessel that holds the blessing of the Ribono shel Olam, "Bless us, our Father, all of us as one," and this is obvious...

With blessings for success in your work and for a *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah*.

## 4735

B"H, 28 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and blessings!

...And although it is understood that I do not mean to say that he alone is to blame for all of the above, on the other hand, it appears that all the administrators and all the members of the board each have a share in the division of hearts. This is also inevitable according to what is stated, "As water reflects a face to a face," and if one side had begun with conduct of peace and closeness, it would, in the end, have turned and brought close the heart of the other side. May it be the will of Hashem that, at the very least, from here on out each of them will act in accordance with the above. And specifically in accordance with what is explained—and the instruction of our Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe, in *Iggeres HaTeshuvah*, end of Chapter 9—that when a rectification of the past is required, one must double the effort. In the language of our Sages, if one was accustomed to reading one page [of Mishnah], [he should now read two]. I await good news.

With the blessing of *Kesivah V'Chasimah Tovah*,

On behalf of the Rebbe, shlita,

Secretary

## 4736

4736

B”H, 28 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

Greetings and Blessings!

In response to your letter of 20 Elul, I am writing to reply promptly. It is obvious that my intention in my previous letter regarding your personal situation was not directed at you specifically. Rather, it was that the Rabbonim, the Roshei Yeshivos, and all those to whom a traditional way of life is dear, must employ the most vigorous means to change the prevailing situation where a wedding is tied to the expenditure of several thousand liras for apartment rental.

Among the immediate means for this, as it appears from a distance, is to reside not necessarily in the two or three locations where "key money" [dmei mafteach] is exorbitant, but to relocate the yeshivos, or at least parts of them, to those places where apartments are cheaper. Although the difficulties in this are understandable, these difficulties fall upon the public collective, whereas the "key money" for an apartment falls upon the individual to find and provide, and this is obvious. According to the reports that arrive, the current situation regarding "key money" is hindering many marriages and shidduchim, and is excessively lengthening the time between the tana’im and the wedding—and the attitude of Chazal regarding this is explained in many places.

It is my hope that when those interested in rectifying the situation who are on the scene gather together, they will certainly find a way to alleviate the situation soon and to rectify it as required, at least over time. After all, the merit of the many will assist them.

Approaching the New Year, which comes upon us and upon all of Yisrael for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all that is yours: a blessing of *Kesivah v’Chasimah Tovah* for a good and sweet year, in both material and spiritual matters.

With respect and blessing.

#### ***4736***

**In my letter to him:** See above, 4723.

## 4737

B"H, 28 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn

Greetings and blessings!

I am in receipt of your letter, in which you write regarding your state of mind, your conduct, and your perspective concerning your near future, and you ask for my opinion on the matter.

It is clear to me that the foundations upon which you are building your perspective and your conclusions do not appear correct to me at all—by which I mean that, in my opinion, they do not reflect reality. That is to say, your current state of mind is a passing one. To the extent that you exert effort, the dissipation of this state of mind will be accelerated. It is certain that you possess strengths and talents that you can utilize not only for your own benefit, but also for the benefit of others and the public at large. However, like everything in creation, one must bring matters from potential into actuality and develop them. In the vast majority of cases, this requires exertion—but it is an exertion that is within a person’s capacity. When a person contemplates that through their exertion for a relatively short period of time, they will achieve a benefit for themselves for many long years—a benefit for others and the public in improving their affairs, whether spiritual or material, or both together—and that this improvement is liable to bring forth fruit and the fruit of fruit, they will easily understand that the effort, the striving, and the exertion are worthwhile.

It is fair to say that, as a rule, it is impossible to change from one extreme state of mind to its absolute opposite. However, this is not an absolute necessity; it is sufficient if you decide, upon receiving this letter, that you must proceed **in the desired direction**—that is, toward an active life—and begin this in practice with the first step. Then, step by step, as they join one to the other and to the third, you will find yourself, within a not-too-long period of time, on the king’s highway—that is, the path that leads to a life of inner satisfaction and contentment. If you follow my opinion, you will continue your studies in the coming year, but together with this, you will involve yourself in the field of education. It would be excellent to do so a few times a week; I mean organizing groups of girls whom you can guide, or even teach them matters appropriate to their understanding, such as Shabbos and Yom Tov parties for them, and the like.

Reflect upon this: there is so much to accomplish in our world, and so many strengths are found within every single person. Because of the magnitude of the work, it is impossible to forgo even one strength or one talent. It is the task of every individual to activate **everything** at their disposal to improve their portion of the world. The beginning, as in all matters, is the study of one’s profession—acquiring the necessary knowledge in general, and the knowledge necessary for the utilization of one’s specific talents and strengths in particular. When you contemplate what has been said here once or twice, I hope that you will immediately begin taking action, will not be deterred by exertion and effort, and will soon see good fruit from your labor.

With blessings for good news regarding all the above in the near future, and for a *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah*.

## 4738

B"H, 28 Elul, 5716
Brooklyn.

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished,
R' Moshe, known as Dr. Taub,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter, in which you write that you are enjoying the neighborhood in which you have settled, as it contains several matters of Torah, yiras shamayim, and also Chassidus.

Surely you and your wife, tichyeh, are also contributing to these matters, for a neighborhood’s reality is defined by the individuals who reside within it and are active in its affairs. Each and every person has a specific and important role in this avodah. This is especially true given that your profession is medicine; in this day and age, everyone acknowledges that the health of the body depends upon the health of the soul and its tranquility. It is already known from the statement of Chazal regarding the peace established in the "lower family" (the earthly realm), which Rabbeinu HaZaken, author of the Shulchan Aruch and the Tanya, explains refers to a person and his two souls—the nefesh elokis and the nefesh habahamis. This is achieved through Torah—meaning, a way of life according to the instruction of our Torah, the Torah of life. Surely, you can find the appropriate language to explain to those who visit you as a doctor matters concerning the health of the soul as well, in accordance with what is explained in many places, including the Shemonah Perakim of the Rambam and the seforim of Chassidus and mussar...

As we approach the new year, which is coming upon us and all of Yisrael for good and for blessing, I hereby extend my blessing to you and to all that is yours: a blessing of *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, in both gashmius and ruchnius.

## 4739

B"H, Selichos Days, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the sons and daughters of Israel, wherever they may be,
May Hashem protect them and grant them life,

Greetings and blessings!

As we draw near to the end of the old year and stand upon the threshold of the new, every person who thinks seriously takes stock of the year that has passed and, on that basis, makes resolutions for the year to come.

In order for this accounting, as well as the resolutions, to be accurate, one must guard against overestimating one’s own virtues and accomplishments, but at the same time, one must also not exaggerate one’s shortcomings and failures.

For a mood of despondency—and certainly, Heaven forbid, despair—is one of the things that weakens the resolve to improve oneself.

However, it may unfortunately happen that even when one does not exaggerate one’s shortcomings and performs a *cheshbon-tzedek* (an honest accounting), the left, negative, and not-good side appears to be quite large, and sometimes even larger and more overwhelming than the right, positive, and good side.

But even in such a case, one must not allow oneself to feel despair.

For together with the deep *teshuvah* (regret) for the past and a firm resolution to change from now on—which a *cheshbon-tzedek* should evoke—one must always remember that the good, *tov*, and *kedushah*—and as our Torah, *Toras Chaim*, explains what is good and holy—are indestructible and eternal.

For they originate and come from the *neshamah*, from the *nitzotz Eloki* (Divine spark) that is within a person.

But the not-good, negative deeds are connected to and come from the *nefesh habahamis* (the animal soul within a person) and the *yetzer hara*; they are essentially limited and can be completely *batel* (nullified) and wiped away.

The bad, the not-good deeds, are merely temporary, and they can—through true and sincere *teshuvah*—be rectified and wiped away.

The above must evoke in everyone, regardless of what their *cheshbon-tzedek* of the past year reveals, a feeling of encouragement and strong hope for the *asid* (future).

Knowing that their good deeds in the past year are eternal and have brought light into their life, into their family, and into *Klal Yisrael*,

For *Kol Yisrael areivim zeh bazeh* (all Jews are connected to one another; they are one whole).

From this, it is also understood that even if one sometimes sees phenomena that point to a *yeridah* (decline)—not everyone is becoming wiser and not everyone is becoming more observant—

In truth and in *pnimiyus* (inwardly), the good in the world is becoming stronger and greater with every year, every day, and every moment,

For every moment, more good *peulos* (actions) are added.

And even at times when the not-good seems to have the upper hand, this is only temporary. Ultimately, the good must take the upper hand and the not-good must be completely *batel*,

For the *Borei Olam* and its Guide has promised that all will do *teshuvah*, and the *Oibershter*, who is *marbeh lisloach* (abundant in forgiveness) even for *aveiros* that have been repeated many times, will accept the *teshuvah*, *ki lo yidach mimenu nidach* (no one will be cast away).

And it is in everyone’s hands to make the coming year a year of a very good accounting of their *peulos*—by turning themselves around and changing—and the *Oibershter*, who desires *teshuvah*, helps in this as well.

And the firm resolution to do so makes it all the more certain that one will have a *shanah tovah u’mesukah*.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 4740

4740

B"H, Days of Selichos, 5716

Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

Your *pidyon nefesh* has been received.

As we approach the new year, which is coming upon us and upon all of Israel for good and for blessing, I hereby express to you my blessing: a blessing of *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

Surely, the instruction of our Sages is engraved in your memory, that every individual who is in the army is under a special commandment, "And your camp shall be holy." This is especially true since you are in the land of which it is said: "The eyes of Hashem your G-d are always upon it, from the beginning of the year to the end of the year." May you also influence your peers in all of this, and may it be His will that you succeed.

## 4741

4741

B"H, Days of Selichos, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Greetings and Blessings!

... Regarding what you wrote—that activities, publicity, and the like can (and perhaps already do) cause a feeling of *yeshus* and the like—the saying of the Mitteler Rebbe is well known: "You may become an onion, but you must review *Chassidus*." And in particular, since, unfortunately, for many—the feeling of *yeshus* is present, but the activities are not. And we conclude with good.

#### ***4741***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 23, p. 546.

**Saying of the Mitteler Rebbe:** See also above, Vol. 8, Letter 2538.

## 4742

B"H, Yemei HaSelichos, 5716
Brooklyn, N.Y.

Rabbi, the G-d-fearing, etc., our teacher Rabbi Menashe Klein

Greetings and Blessings!

Your letter was received, and I thank you. As we approach the New Year, which is coming upon us and upon all of Israel for good and for blessing, I hereby express my blessing to you and to all that is yours: a blessing of *Kesivah V’Chasimah Tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

With respect and blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

Regarding your note in the margin of your letter concerning the fact that the *Sefer Chochmas Shlomo* (by the Maharshak) does not mention the words of the Ramban (on the Torah) and the Ritva (to *Kiddushin* 22a) that a master is obligated to provide for his wife’s sustenance by Torah law—even though a wife’s sustenance is generally only by Rabbinic law—because it is the custom of the land to provide for one’s wife, etc.: That *sefer* is not currently in my hands. However, I have always been troubled by the reasoning of the Ramban and the Ritva, for according to this, many derivations of our Sages would be nullified. For example, from where do we derive all these major *halachos* (*Berachos* 31a)? From where do we derive that a mourner is obligated to cover his head, etc. (*Moed Katan* 15a, ibid. 27b)? And there are others.

#### ***4742***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 6, p. 322, and in its entirety in *Nesi’ei Chabad U’Bnei Doram*, p. 167 [On that same page, his question was also printed. Following it, the continuation of the scholarly discussion on the subject was transcribed, and it will be printed, G-d willing, in the next volume].

**In *Sefer Chochmas Shlomo*:** He questions the position of the *poskim* that a wife’s sustenance is by Rabbinic law, based on the Gemara in *Kiddushin* 22, which states: "And his wife shall go out with him"—from here we derive that a master is obligated to provide for his wife’s sustenance.

## 4743

4743

*[Days of Selichos]*

... Regarding what you wrote, asking from where we know that for *Le-shana haba’a* we do not suffice with the mention of *Le-shana haba’a* in the *piyutim* of the *avodah* — I already noted in my previous letter to the *Shulchan Aruch* of the Alter Rebbe, where it is proven that this is so. And likewise, from the fact that we also recite the **verses** of the *Musafim* (*Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim*, end of section 588).

#### ***4743***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 306. It is addressed to R. Yehuda Schmotkin. Additional letters to him appear above, 4594, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

**In my previous letter:** Above, ibid.

## 4704

4704

*[Late 5716]*

...Regarding what you wrote concerning beginning the avodah of tefillah — this is a very proper thing. However, to begin with, you should study the Kuntres HaTefillah a few times (at the very least, the concepts of avodah contained therein), and for the initial period, you should conduct yourself in this manner during the tefillah of Shabbos Kodesh.

## 4745

4745

*[Late 5716]*

...Regarding what you wrote concerning the two sections of Krias Shema, where you found a contradiction to what is written in Likutei Torah, in the maamar beginning "Ve'ahavta"—see also Shaar HaTefillah in Shaar HaEmunah VeHaBitachon of the Mitteler Rebbe, chapter 25.

See also the aforementioned in Imrei Binah, Shaar HaKrias Shema, chapter 45.

You are surely continuing with the review of Chassidus and adding to this, and may Hashem grant you success in this as well.

#### ***4745***

This letter is to R' Avraham Dovid Yanovsky. Additional letters to him appear above in 4796, and in the references noted there.

**Where you found a contradiction:** In the maamar beginning "Reishis Arisoseichem" 5629, it is explained that the first section is the light of Chesed in the vessel of Gevurah, and the second section is the light of Gevurah in the vessel of Chesed.
